tr:d3c17c1e4afe32a8:
• Unit (most): test individual functions/components. Fast, cheap, stable.
  • Integration (moderate): test multiple parts working together (e.g., component + hook + mock API). Best balance for frontend.
  • E2E (least): test real user flows in a browser (Playwright/Cypress). Slow, prone to flakiness, but catches integration bugs.
Modern Senior Mindset

The current trend (Testing Library) emphasizes: test user-visible behavior, not implementation details (internal state). Many frontend teams lean toward the "testing trophy" — investing most in integration tests because they offer high reliability at reasonable cost. Mentioning this perspective shows you're up-to-date.

🪜 Deep Dive Ladder — Interview Yourself

Don't stop at definitions. Answer each question yourself before viewing the sample answer — where you get stuck reveals your true depth.

  1. 1Why the pyramid shape — why fewer tests as you go up?
    Unit tests: quick to write, fast to run, easy to maintain — having many is inexpensive. Integration tests: slower, more complex — moderate number. E2E tests: slowest, most flaky, resource-intensive — should be fewest. It's a pyramid because of ROI: many unit tests provide broad coverage at low cost.
  2. 2Why does modern frontend lean toward the 'testing trophy' instead of the classic pyramid?
    Frontend tests often mock modules (API, router) too heavily → unit tests are completely isolated → they miss integration bugs. Testing Library emerged to encourage testing at the component + hook + mock-API level (integration) → catches real behavior at moderate cost. Kent C. Dodds' "testing trophy": many integration tests, moderate unit tests, few E2E tests.
  3. 3What causes E2E tests to be 'flaky' and why does it erode team trust in testing?
    Flaky test: sometimes passes, sometimes fails, with no code changes. Causes: timing (animations, async), shared state between tests, environment dependencies, network. Teams start ignoring failed tests → "red is normal" → loses all CI value. Flaky tests are worse than no tests because they create false security.
  4. 4Is 100% coverage a good goal? Why or why not?
    100% coverage does not guarantee quality. Coverage measures which lines are executed, not whether behavior is correct. A test like expect(fn()).toBeDefined() gives 100% coverage but asserts nothing useful. Seniors aim for sufficient coverage on critical paths and high-risk areas, not absolute numbers.
  5. 5When does writing tests cost more than the value they provide?
    When code changes rapidly (early stage, prototype), tests frequently become outdated → maintaining them costs more than writing. When business logic is simple and low-risk. When tests are significantly harder to write due to design issues (tightly coupled code, side effects everywhere) → this signals a need to refactor the code, not abandon testing.
tr:153eb0e1fdd09667: When testing a flashlight, you press the button and check if it lights up (behavior). You don't disassemble it to count how many wires are inside (details). Because if the manufacturer changes the wiring layout but the flashlight still works, your test should not report a "failure."
tr:888364fab8148050:
Tests should verify what the user observes (visible text, clickable buttons, correct form submission), not internal state or private function names.

// ❌ Tests implementation details — breaks on refactor
expect(wrapper.state('count')).toBe(1);

// ✅ Tests behavior — resilient
fireEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button'));
expect(screen.getByText('Liked (1)')).toBeInTheDocument();
Senior mindset

Tests coupled to implementation make developers fear refactoring because tests break even when the app works correctly — this is a "brittle test." Prefer querying by role (getByRole) over test-id, as it both tests behavior and checks accessibility.

🪜 Deep dive ladder — interview yourself

Don't stop at definitions. Answer each question yourself before viewing the sample answer — where you get stuck reveals your true depth.

  1. 1Why do implementation-detail tests make developers fear refactoring — what's the specific mechanism?
    Test coupled to implementation: component uses useState, test checks wrapper.state(). Refactor to useReducer → test breaks even though UI doesn't change. Developer must fix tests before refactoring → refactoring becomes painful → people avoid it → code rots. Behavior tests: test button click → expect UI change → internal refactoring is completely free.
  2. 2Why is getByRole better than getByTestId — what extra does it verify?
    getByRole queries by ARIA role (button, textbox, heading) — this is also how screen readers find elements. If a test passes with getByRole('button'), that element is accessible to screen readers. getByTestId only finds by data attribute — unrelated to accessibility, tests can pass but the element may not be accessible.
  3. 3What are the limitations of behavior tests — what do they NOT catch?
    Behavior tests don't catch: performance regression (component renders correctly but slowly), visual regression (text is correct but layout breaks), screen reader reading order accessibility, SEO (heading structure, meta tags). Need additional: visual testing (Chromatic/Percy), performance benchmarks, manual a11y audit.
  4. 4What category does snapshot testing fall into and why is it controversial?
    Snapshot testing captures serialized component output. It's "implementation detail" because any markup change (even adding a className that doesn't affect behavior) fails the test. Often leads to blindly updating snapshots without review. More useful for: testing pure function output, serialized formats, or combined with visual testing.
  5. 5The line between 'behavior' and 'implementation' is sometimes blurry — give an example of a hard-to-classify case?
    Example: testing a component with animation — testing "button disabled while submitting" is behavior, but should you test whether the CSS class is-loading is added? That's "behavior" (visual feedback) vs "implementation" (class name). The answer depends: if the class name drives behavior (CSS animation), testing it is meaningful. If it's purely styling, it's not.
tr:5f27fab9928bfccb: Rehearsing a play but lacking real actors: a stub is a stand-in who delivers exactly one pre-written line. A spy is someone who sits and records "who called how many times." A mock is a full-fledged stunt actor, both delivering lines and being checked to see if they follow the script correctly.
tr:108a9e7e4e4d5f60:
• Stub: replaces a function with a version that returns a fixed value (controls input).
  • Spy: tracks whether a real function was called, with what arguments, and how many times.
  • Mock: combines both + sets expectations on how it should be called.
const fetchSpy = jest.spyOn(api, 'getUser')
  .mockResolvedValue({ name: 'A' });   // stub the result

await loadUser();
expect(fetchSpy).toHaveBeenCalledWith('123');  // spy on the call
Senior mindset

Over-mocking ⇒ tests "pass" but don't reflect reality (testing the mock, not the real code). Modern trend: mock at the network boundary (use MSW to intercept HTTP) instead of mocking each module, to make tests closer to real behavior.

🪜 Deep dive ladder — interview yourself

Don't stop at definitions. Answer each question before viewing the sample answer — where you get stuck is your true depth.

  1. 1Why does over-mocking make tests 'pass' but worthless?
    Detailed mocking → test couples to the implementation of the mocked module → refactoring the module (rename function, split file) → mock breaks even though logic hasn't changed. A "passing" test no longer proves behavior is correct — it only proves the mock was called correctly. Each test becomes a spec of the implementation, not a spec of the behavior.
  2. 2Why is mocking at the network boundary (MSW) better than mocking each module?
    Module mocking: each test file mocks import { fetchUser } from './api' separately → couples to module structure. MSW (Mock Service Worker) intercepts HTTP at the network layer → tests don't know whether the implementation uses fetch/axios/etc → can completely refactor without changing tests. Closer to real integration, more resilient to refactoring.
  3. 3What problem do fake timers solve for async testing?
    Async tests depend on real time: testing a 300ms debounce must actually wait 300ms → tests are slow. Fake timers (jest.useFakeTimers()) allow manually advancing time (jest.advanceTimersByTime(300)) → tests are instant. Also useful for: setInterval, Date.now(), polling logic.
  4. 4When should you NOT mock (losing what the test needs to verify)?
    Don't mock when: mocking hides important behavior that needs testing (e.g., mocking a database instead of using a real test-db), when the mock is so complex it becomes unrealistic, or when you're trying to test integration (the integration point is what needs testing). Mocking is a tradeoff — faster but further from reality.
  5. 5Heavy mocking vs real integration tests — where's the tradeoff between reliability and speed?
    Mocking: fast, isolated, no external dependencies, but far from reality → false confidence. Real integration: slower, complex setup, but catches real bugs. Balance: mock at the outermost boundary (HTTP, DB) so tests stay fast but internal logic is tested for real. Don't mock in the middle of logic — that's "testing the mock, not the code."

[{"question":"Explain how React's reconciliation process works, including the roles of Virtual DOM and Fiber.","expectedPoints":["Reconciliation is the diffing algorithm that compares the new Virtual DOM with the previous one to determine minimal updates.","Virtual DOM is a lightweight JavaScript representation of the real DOM, enabling efficient diffing.","Fiber is the new reconciliation engine that enables incremental rendering and prioritization of updates.","The process involves creating a Fiber tree, performing diffing, and committing changes to the real DOM.","Key techniques: key props for list reconciliation, memoization to skip unnecessary re-renders."],"followUps":["How does Fiber enable concurrent rendering?","What happens if you don't provide a key prop in a list?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy castle made of blocks. If you want to change a block, instead of rebuilding the whole castle, you compare the new design with the old one and only swap the blocks that need to change. React does the same with web pages using a 'virtual' copy of the page, and Fiber helps it do this smoothly without freezing the screen.","detailedExplanation":"

Reconciliation is the process React uses to update the DOM efficiently. When a component's state or props change, React creates a new Virtual DOM tree (a lightweight JavaScript object representing the UI). It then compares this new tree with the previous one using a diffing algorithm to determine the minimal set of changes needed. Finally, it applies those changes to the real DOM in a process called commit.

Virtual DOM

The Virtual DOM is an in-memory representation of the real DOM. It allows React to batch updates and avoid expensive direct DOM manipulations. By comparing two Virtual DOM trees (the current and the new one), React can compute the most efficient way to update the UI.

Fiber

Fiber is the reconciliation engine introduced in React 16. Each Fiber node corresponds to a component instance and stores information about its state, props, and side effects. The Fiber architecture enables incremental rendering: work can be split into chunks and prioritized, allowing React to pause and resume work, handle high-priority updates (like user input) before lower-priority ones (like data fetching), and avoid blocking the main thread.

How It Works

During reconciliation, React traverses the Fiber tree in two phases: render (or reconciliation) and commit. In the render phase, React calls components, builds the new Fiber tree, and marks nodes with changes. This phase can be interrupted. In the commit phase, React applies the changes to the DOM synchronously.

Why It Matters in Production

Efficient reconciliation reduces unnecessary DOM updates, improving performance and responsiveness. Features like key props help React identify list items correctly, preventing full re-renders. Memoization (e.g., React.memo, useMemo) can skip re-rendering components when props haven't changed.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is using array indices as keys in lists. This can cause incorrect component state updates or unnecessary re-renders when the list order changes. Always use stable, unique identifiers.

"}]

[{"question":"What are the main reasons a React component re-renders, and how can you optimize re-renders?","expectedPoints":["Identifies props, state, context changes, and parent re-render as triggers.","Mentions memoization techniques like React.memo, useMemo, useCallback.","Explains shouldComponentUpdate or PureComponent for class components.","Discusses performance impact and avoiding unnecessary re-renders.","Gives practical optimization examples (e.g., key props, splitting state)."],"followUps":["How does React.memo differ from useMemo?","Can you describe a scenario where a component re-renders even though its props haven't changed?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that lights up when you press a button. The toy re-lights (re-renders) when you press the button (state change), when you give it a new battery (props change), or when you shake it (parent re-render). To save battery, you can add a switch that only lets it light up when really needed—that's like using React.memo or useMemo.","detailedExplanation":"

React components re-render when their props change, state changes, context value changes, or when the parent component re-renders. Understanding these triggers is crucial for performance optimization.

Why Re-renders Matter

Unnecessary re-renders can degrade performance, especially in large component trees or components with heavy computations. React provides several tools to control re-renders:

  • React.memo (for functional components) and PureComponent (for class components) perform a shallow comparison of props/state to skip re-renders if nothing changed.
  • shouldComponentUpdate lifecycle method allows manual control over re-rendering.
  • useMemo and useCallback hooks memoize values and functions to prevent unnecessary re-creation on every render.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is passing inline functions or objects as props, which breaks shallow comparison because a new reference is created each render. For example: <Child onClick={() => handleClick()} /> causes Child to re-render even if handleClick hasn't changed. Use useCallback to stabilize the function reference.

Production Best Practices

Profile your app with React DevTools to identify unnecessary re-renders. Apply memoization selectively—overusing it can add overhead. Also, consider splitting state into smaller units and lifting state up only when necessary.

"}]
qgen_v2:306c6ca7eaf0c318:
[{"question":"Compare and contrast useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo. When would you use each, and how can they be combined effectively?","expectedPoints":["useMemo memoizes computed values; useCallback memoizes functions; React.memo memoizes entire components.","useMemo and useCallback are hooks for functional components; React.memo is a higher-order component.","They all prevent unnecessary re-renders but at different levels: value, function, component.","Combining them: useCallback to stabilize callbacks passed to memoized children; useMemo for expensive computations.","Overuse can hurt performance due to memory overhead; only apply when profiling shows a bottleneck."],"followUps":["What happens if you omit the dependency array in useMemo or useCallback?","Can React.memo replace useMemo or useCallback? Why or why not?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that does a slow calculation. useMemo remembers the answer so you don't have to calculate again unless the numbers change. useCallback remembers how to do a trick so you don't have to learn it again. React.memo is like a sticker that says 'Don't rebuild this toy unless its parts change.' They help your app run faster by avoiding extra work.","detailedExplanation":"

useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo are three tools in React to optimize performance by reducing unnecessary re-renders. They work at different levels and serve distinct purposes.

What they are

  • useMemo: A hook that memoizes the result of a computation. It returns a cached value that only changes when its dependencies change.
  • useCallback: A hook that memoizes a function definition. It returns the same function reference across renders unless dependencies change.
  • React.memo: A higher-order component that memoizes the rendered output of a component. It performs a shallow comparison of props and skips re-rendering if props haven't changed.

How they work

useMemo takes a function and a dependency array. It calls the function only when dependencies change and returns the cached result otherwise. useCallback is essentially useMemo for functions: useCallback(fn, deps) is equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps). React.memo wraps a component and compares new props to previous props using shallow equality; if they are the same, the component does not re-render.

Why they matter in production

In large applications, unnecessary re-renders can cause performance issues, especially when dealing with expensive computations, large lists, or deeply nested component trees. Using these tools judiciously can improve responsiveness and reduce CPU usage. However, they come with overhead (memory for caching, comparison cost), so they should only be applied when profiling indicates a bottleneck.

Common pitfall

A frequent mistake is overusing these optimizations without measuring. For example, wrapping every function in useCallback or every component in React.memo can actually degrade performance due to the overhead of dependency tracking and shallow comparisons. Always profile first and apply optimizations only where needed.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain stale closures in useEffect and how to prevent them.","expectedPoints":["Defines stale closure as a closure capturing outdated variable values.","Describes how useEffect's callback forms a closure over state/props.","Lists solutions: adding dependencies, using refs, or functional updates.","Explains why missing dependencies cause stale closures in intervals or event listeners.","Mentions useCallback/useMemo as related patterns to avoid stale closures."],"followUps":["How would you fix a stale closure in a setInterval without restarting the interval?","What is the role of the dependency array in preventing stale closures?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that says the same number every second, even if you change the number. That's a stale closure. To fix it, you tell the toy to check the new number each time it speaks, or you give it a new instruction whenever the number changes.","detailedExplanation":"

A stale closure occurs when a function captures a variable's value at the time of its creation and continues to reference that old value even after the variable has been updated. In React, this commonly happens inside useEffect when the callback function uses state or props without including them in the dependency array.

How It Works

When useEffect runs, its callback forms a closure over the current values of variables referenced inside it. If the effect only runs once (empty dependency array) or misses a dependency, the closure retains the initial values. For example, a setInterval inside useEffect that logs count will always log the count from the first render if count is not in the dependency array.

Why It Matters in Production

Stale closures lead to bugs that are hard to detect, such as:

  • Timers or event listeners using outdated state.
  • Incorrect data fetching or subscriptions.
  • Unexpected behavior in animations or polling.

These issues can cause data inconsistency, UI glitches, or performance problems.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is omitting dependencies from the array, assuming the effect doesn't need them. This often happens with setInterval or addEventListener. The fix is to either include the dependency (which may restart the effect) or use a ref to store the latest value without causing re-renders. Alternatively, use the functional update form of setState when the closure only needs to update state based on previous state.

"}]
qgen_v2:760c298ef604b56a:
[{"question":"Explain the key differences between Server Components (RSC) and Client Components in React, including when to use each and their trade-offs.","expectedPoints":["Server Components render on the server, reducing bundle size and improving initial load time.","Client Components render in the browser, enabling interactivity and access to browser APIs.","Server Components cannot use state, effects, or event handlers; they are for static or data-fetching content.","Use Server Components for data fetching, static content, and SEO; use Client Components for interactivity.","Trade-offs: Server Components are harder to debug and less customizable, while Client Components increase bundle size."],"followUps":["How would you decide whether a component should be a Server or Client Component in a real app?","What are some common pitfalls when mixing Server and Client Components?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that can be built either at the factory (server) or at home (client). Factory-built toys arrive ready to play, but you can't change them easily. Home-built toys take longer to set up but you can customize them. Server Components are like factory-built toys—they load fast but aren't interactive. Client Components are like home-built toys—they can do more but take longer to load.","detailedExplanation":"

Server Components (RSC) and Client Components are two distinct types of components in React, each designed for different purposes. The core difference lies in where they execute: Server Components run on the server, while Client Components run in the browser.

Key Differences

  • Execution Environment: Server Components are rendered on the server and send only the resulting HTML to the client. Client Components are rendered in the browser and include JavaScript for interactivity.
  • Capabilities: Server Components cannot use useState, useEffect, event handlers, or browser APIs. They are ideal for fetching data and rendering static content. Client Components have full access to React hooks, state, effects, and browser APIs.
  • Performance: Server Components reduce the JavaScript bundle size because their code never reaches the client. This improves initial load time and Time to Interactive (TTI). Client Components increase bundle size but enable dynamic, interactive UIs.
  • SEO and Caching: Server Components are great for SEO because search engines can index the server-rendered HTML. They also benefit from server-side caching.

When to Use Each

  • Server Components: Use for data fetching, static content, layouts, and components that don't require interactivity. They are also beneficial for pages that need fast initial loads and high security (since no client-side code is exposed).
  • Client Components: Use for interactive elements like forms, buttons, animations, and components that rely on browser APIs or user input.

Trade-offs and Pitfalls

A common pitfall is trying to use hooks or event handlers in a Server Component, which will cause an error. Another is overusing Client Components, leading to large bundle sizes and slow load times. Debugging Server Components can be harder because errors occur on the server, not in the browser. Additionally, Server Components cannot be customized at runtime by the user, limiting flexibility.

In production, a typical pattern is to use Server Components for the outer layout and data fetching, then wrap interactive parts in Client Components. This hybrid approach balances performance and interactivity.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain the difference between microtasks and macrotasks in the JavaScript event loop, and how they affect the order of execution.","expectedPoints":["Defines microtasks (e.g., Promise callbacks) and macrotasks (e.g., setTimeout, I/O).","States that microtasks are executed before macrotasks in each event loop iteration.","Describes the event loop's priority: synchronous code first, then microtasks, then macrotasks.","Provides a correct example of execution order (e.g., console.log, Promise, setTimeout).","Explains why this matters for performance and avoiding UI blocking."],"followUps":["What happens if a microtask queues another microtask?","How does requestAnimationFrame fit into this model?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're at a restaurant. The chef (event loop) has two types of orders: small ones (microtasks) like adding ketchup, and big ones (macrotasks) like cooking a steak. The chef always finishes small orders before starting a big one, so your steak doesn't get cold while waiting for ketchup. In JavaScript, promises are like small orders, and setTimeout is like a big order.","detailedExplanation":"

The JavaScript event loop is a mechanism that manages the execution of code, collecting and processing events, and executing queued sub-tasks. It distinguishes between two types of tasks: macrotasks and microtasks.

Macrotasks

Macrotasks include setTimeout, setInterval, I/O operations, UI rendering, and event listeners. They are queued in the macrotask queue and processed one per event loop iteration.

Microtasks

Microtasks include Promise.then/catch/finally, MutationObserver, and queueMicrotask. They are queued in the microtask queue and are processed immediately after the currently executing script and before any macrotask.

Execution Order

The event loop follows this priority: synchronous code first, then microtasks, then macrotasks. After each macrotask, the event loop checks the microtask queue and empties it before moving to the next macrotask. This ensures that microtasks are handled promptly, which is critical for promise-based asynchronous operations.

Why It Matters in Production

Understanding this order helps avoid unexpected behavior, such as UI blocking or delayed promise resolutions. For example, if a microtask queues another microtask, the event loop will keep processing microtasks until the queue is empty, potentially delaying macrotasks like rendering. This can lead to starvation of macrotasks if not managed carefully.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming that setTimeout(callback, 0) runs immediately. In reality, it is a macrotask and will run after all microtasks (e.g., promise callbacks) have been processed, even if the timeout is zero.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain what a closure is in JavaScript and provide a real-world example of its use.","expectedPoints":["Closure is a function that retains access to its outer function's scope even after the outer function has returned.","It 'closes over' variables from its lexical environment.","Example: a counter function that increments a private variable each time it's called.","Closures enable data privacy and function factories.","Each closure instance has its own separate environment."],"followUps":["How would you use a closure to create a private variable?","What is a common pitfall when using closures in loops?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a magic box that remembers a number even after you close the lid. Every time you open it, you can add more to that number. A closure is like that magic box: it's a function that remembers the variables from where it was created, even after the outer function finishes. So you can keep using those variables later.","detailedExplanation":"

A closure is a fundamental concept in JavaScript where an inner function retains access to the variables of its outer (enclosing) function even after the outer function has finished executing. This happens because the inner function maintains a reference to its lexical environment (the scope where it was defined).

How it works

When a function is defined inside another function, the inner function forms a closure over the outer function's variables. The JavaScript engine keeps the outer function's variables alive as long as there is any reference to the inner function. This allows the inner function to read and modify those variables.

Why it matters in production

  • Data privacy: Closures enable the creation of private variables that cannot be accessed from outside the closure, which is useful for encapsulation.
  • Function factories: You can create functions with preset arguments or behavior, like a multiplier function that remembers a factor.
  • Event handlers and callbacks: Closures are commonly used to preserve state in asynchronous code, such as in setTimeout or event listeners.

Common pitfall

A classic mistake is using closures inside loops without proper scoping. For example, when creating multiple closures in a for loop that reference a loop variable, all closures will share the same final value of that variable unless you use let (which creates a new binding each iteration) or an IIFE to capture the current value.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain the difference between arrow functions and regular functions in JavaScript, particularly regarding the 'this' keyword.","expectedPoints":["Arrow functions do not have their own 'this'; they inherit it from the enclosing lexical scope.","Regular functions have their own 'this' which depends on how they are called (e.g., method call, constructor, etc.).","Arrow functions cannot be used as constructors and will throw an error if used with 'new'.","Arrow functions have implicit return when the body is a single expression without curly braces.","Regular functions have their own 'arguments' object; arrow functions do not."],"followUps":["How would you fix a common bug where 'this' is undefined inside a callback in a regular function?","Can you give an example where using an arrow function as a method on an object leads to unexpected behavior?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a remote control that changes the TV channel. A regular function is like a remote that always points to the TV it came with, no matter where you use it. An arrow function is like a remote that points to whatever TV is nearby when you press the button. So, 'this' in arrow functions is like the nearby TV, not the original one.","detailedExplanation":"

Arrow functions and regular functions in JavaScript differ primarily in how they handle the this keyword. A regular function has its own this context, which is determined by how the function is called (e.g., as a method, as a constructor, or standalone). In contrast, an arrow function does not have its own this; it captures the this value from its enclosing lexical scope (the surrounding function or global context).

Key Differences

  • this binding: Arrow functions inherit this from the parent scope; regular functions define their own this based on invocation.
  • Constructors: Arrow functions cannot be used with new; they throw a TypeError. Regular functions can be constructors.
  • Arguments object: Arrow functions do not have their own arguments object; they rely on rest parameters (...args). Regular functions have the array-like arguments object.
  • Implicit return: Arrow functions with a single expression body (no curly braces) implicitly return that expression. Regular functions require an explicit return statement.

Why It Matters in Production

Understanding this difference is crucial to avoid bugs, especially in event handlers, callbacks, and object methods. For example, using an arrow function as a method on an object will cause this to refer to the outer scope (e.g., window in browsers) rather than the object itself, leading to unexpected behavior.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is using an arrow function when a dynamic this is needed, such as in a prototype method or a constructor. Always choose the appropriate function type based on whether you need lexical or dynamic this binding.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain how the prototype chain works in JavaScript and how ES6 classes are built on top of it.","expectedPoints":["Describes prototype chain as a mechanism for property lookup through parent objects.","Explains that ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over constructor functions and prototypes.","Shows how methods defined in a class are added to the class's prototype object.","Demonstrates inheritance via extends and super, linking child prototype to parent prototype.","Mentions that the chain ends at Object.prototype, and property lookup stops when found or reaches null."],"followUps":["What happens if you define a method with the same name in both parent and child class?","How would you create an object that inherits from another without using class syntax?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine a family tree where a child can ask their parent for something they don't have. If the parent doesn't have it, they ask their own parent, and so on. In JavaScript, objects are like children, and they can ask their 'prototype' parent for properties or methods. ES6 classes are just a tidy way to set up this family tree.","detailedExplanation":"

The prototype chain is a fundamental mechanism in JavaScript that enables objects to inherit properties and methods from other objects. When you access a property on an object, JavaScript first checks if the object itself has that property. If not, it looks up the object's internal [[Prototype]] (accessible via __proto__ or Object.getPrototypeOf) and continues up the chain until it finds the property or reaches an object whose prototype is null (typically Object.prototype).

How ES6 Classes Relate

ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over the existing prototype-based inheritance. When you declare a class, JavaScript creates a constructor function and a prototype object. Methods defined inside the class are added to that prototype. For example:

class Animal { constructor(name) { this.name = name; } eat() { ... } }

This creates Animal.prototype.eat. When you instantiate with new Animal('Bella'), the new object's prototype is set to Animal.prototype, so it can access eat via the chain.

Inheritance with extends

The extends keyword sets up the prototype chain for inheritance. For a child class Dog extends Animal, Dog.prototype’s prototype is set to Animal.prototype. The super() call invokes the parent constructor. Thus, a Dog instance can access both bark (on Dog.prototype) and eat (on Animal.prototype).

Why It Matters in Production

Understanding the prototype chain is crucial for debugging property lookup issues, optimizing memory usage (shared methods on prototype vs. per-instance), and implementing patterns like mixins or polyfills. It also helps when working with legacy code or frameworks that manipulate prototypes directly.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is confusing the prototype of the constructor (Class.prototype) with the prototype of instances (instance.__proto__). Also, modifying built-in prototypes (e.g., Array.prototype) can lead to unexpected behavior in loops or third-party code.

"}]
qgen_v2:0982f1efd5f855fc:
[{"question":"Explain how Promises work in JavaScript and how they differ from callbacks.","expectedPoints":["Promise is an object representing eventual completion/failure of async operation.","Promises avoid callback hell by chaining .then() and .catch().","Promises have three states: pending, fulfilled, rejected.","Async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises, using try/catch for error handling.","Error handling with .catch() or try/catch in async functions."],"followUps":["What happens if you don't return a promise in a .then() chain?","How would you handle multiple independent async operations concurrently?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you order a pizza. A callback is like giving the restaurant your phone number and waiting for them to call you back. A Promise is like getting a receipt with a number; you can check the status online, and when it's ready, you get notified. Async/await is like having a friend wait for the pizza while you do other things, then handing it to you when it arrives.","detailedExplanation":"

A Promise in JavaScript is an object representing the eventual completion (or failure) of an asynchronous operation and its resulting value. It provides a cleaner alternative to callbacks for handling asynchronous code.

How Promises Work

A Promise can be in one of three states: pending, fulfilled, or rejected. Once settled, it cannot change state. You attach callbacks using .then() for fulfillment and .catch() for rejection. Promises can be chained, where each .then() returns a new Promise, allowing sequential async operations without nesting.

Difference from Callbacks

Callbacks are functions passed as arguments to be executed after an async task. They lead to deeply nested code (callback hell) and make error handling cumbersome. Promises flatten the code and provide a unified error handling mechanism via .catch().

Async/Await

async/await is syntactic sugar built on Promises. An async function returns a Promise. The await keyword pauses execution until the Promise resolves, making asynchronous code look synchronous. Error handling is done with try/catch blocks.

Why It Matters in Production

Using Promises and async/await improves code readability, maintainability, and error handling. It prevents callback hell and makes debugging easier. In modern JavaScript, Promises are fundamental for handling network requests, file I/O, and any async operations.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is forgetting to return a Promise inside a .then() callback, which breaks the chain and leads to unexpected behavior. Always ensure that .then() callbacks return a value or a Promise to maintain the chain.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain the difference between the equality operator (==) and the strict equality operator (===) in JavaScript.","expectedPoints":["== performs type coercion before comparison, === does not.","=== checks both value and type, == checks value after coercion.","Examples like 5 == '5' (true) vs 5 === '5' (false).","Pitfalls with falsy values: 0 == false is true, 0 === false is false.","Best practice: prefer === to avoid unexpected coercion bugs."],"followUps":["Can you give an example where using == leads to a bug in production?","How does the coercion algorithm work for ==? (e.g., ToPrimitive, ToNumber)"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have two boxes: one with the number 5 and another with the word '5'. The == operator is like a friend who says 'they are the same because both have 5 inside', ignoring that one is a number and the other is a word. The === operator is a strict friend who checks both the inside and the label on the box, so they say 'they are different because one is a number and the other is a word'. Always use === to avoid confusion.","detailedExplanation":"

The equality operator (==) and the strict equality operator (===) are both used to compare two values in JavaScript, but they behave differently regarding type coercion.

How they work

== performs type coercion before comparing. If the types differ, JavaScript tries to convert one or both values to a common type using an internal algorithm (e.g., ToNumber, ToPrimitive). For example, 5 == '5' converts the string '5' to the number 5, so the result is true. In contrast, === checks both value and type without any coercion. Thus 5 === '5' returns false because one is a number and the other is a string.

Why it matters in production

Using == can lead to unexpected bugs due to coercion rules. For instance, 0 == false is true because false is coerced to 0. Similarly, '' == false is true. These can cause subtle logic errors in conditionals. The best practice is to always use === (or !== for inequality) unless you explicitly need type coercion, which is rare.

Common pitfall

A frequent mistake is using == to compare a value to null or undefined. While null == undefined is true (a special case), null === undefined is false. Relying on == for such checks can hide type mismatches. Always prefer === for clarity and safety.

"}]

[{"question":"What are Core Web Vitals and how would you optimize them in a production web application?","expectedPoints":["Defines LCP, CLS, and TBT with target thresholds (LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, TBT < 100ms).","Explains optimization techniques: image optimization, CDN, lazy loading for LCP; fixed-size elements for CLS; efficient JS and reducing long tasks for TBT.","Mentions real-world impact on user experience and SEO (Google ranking factor).","Describes measurement tools like Lighthouse, PageSpeed Insights, or web-vitals library.","Identifies common pitfalls: not accounting for third-party scripts, dynamic content causing layout shifts."],"followUps":["How would you debug a high CLS caused by a third-party ad widget?","What trade-offs exist between optimizing LCP and TBT?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy website. Core Web Vitals are like three rules to make sure your website loads fast (LCP), doesn't jump around (CLS), and responds quickly when you click (TBT). You can fix them by making pictures smaller, giving things fixed sizes, and not using too many heavy scripts.","detailedExplanation":"

Core Web Vitals are a set of real-world metrics that measure user experience on the web. They consist of three key indicators:

  • Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) – measures loading performance. Target: < 2.5 seconds. Optimize by optimizing images, using a CDN, reducing render-blocking resources, and implementing lazy loading for non-critical content.
  • Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) – measures visual stability. Target: < 0.1. Optimize by setting explicit dimensions for images and ads, using font-display: swap, and avoiding inserting dynamic content above existing content.
  • Total Blocking Time (TBT) – measures interactivity. Target: < 100 ms. Optimize by breaking up long JavaScript tasks, using code splitting, deferring non-critical scripts, and minimizing main thread work.

Why It Matters in Production

Good Core Web Vitals directly improve user satisfaction, reduce bounce rates, and are a ranking factor in Google Search. Poor vitals can lead to lost revenue and lower search visibility.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is ignoring third-party scripts (e.g., analytics, ads). They can significantly degrade LCP and CLS. Always audit third-party impact and consider loading them asynchronously or after the main content.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain how virtualization (windowing) works in the context of web performance, particularly for rendering large lists.","expectedPoints":["Virtualization renders only visible items in a scrollable list, not all items.","It reduces DOM nodes and improves memory usage and paint performance.","Implementation uses scroll position to calculate which items to render.","Common libraries: react-window, react-virtualized.","Trade-off: complex layout calculations and potential blank spaces during fast scroll."],"followUps":["How would you handle dynamic item heights in a virtualized list?","What are the performance implications of virtualization on accessibility?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge list of toys, but you can only see a few at a time through a small window. Instead of building all the toys, you only build the ones you can see right now. When you scroll, you build new toys and remove the ones you can't see anymore. This makes everything faster and uses less memory.","detailedExplanation":"

Virtualization (windowing) is a performance optimization technique used to render only the visible portion of a large list or grid, rather than rendering all items at once. This is critical for web applications that display thousands of rows, such as data tables, feeds, or logs.

How It Works

The technique calculates which items are within the current viewport based on the scroll position. It then renders only those items, while the rest are replaced with empty placeholder elements that maintain the correct scroll height. As the user scrolls, items are dynamically added and removed. Libraries like react-window and react-virtualized provide efficient implementations.

Why It Matters in Production

Rendering thousands of DOM nodes causes significant performance issues: slow initial load, high memory consumption, and janky scrolling. Virtualization reduces the DOM size to only visible items (e.g., 10–20 nodes), leading to faster rendering, smoother scrolling, and lower memory usage. It is essential for any data-heavy UI.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming all items have the same height. When items have dynamic heights, the virtualizer must estimate or measure each item's height, which adds complexity. Incorrect height estimation leads to scroll jitter or blank spaces. Solutions include using a cache of measured heights or a fixed estimated height with re-measurement on render.

"}]
qgen_v2:46197f34c37f3a15:
[{"question":"Explain the difference between debounce and throttle, and provide a use case for each.","expectedPoints":["Debounce delays execution until after a pause in events; throttle limits execution rate.","Debounce is used for input autocomplete; throttle is used for scroll or resize handlers.","Debounce resets timer on each call; throttle ensures at most one call per interval.","Both improve performance by reducing unnecessary function calls.","Implementation differences: debounce uses clearTimeout/setTimeout; throttle uses a flag or timestamp."],"followUps":["How would you implement a debounce function that also supports immediate execution?","Can you think of a scenario where using throttle instead of debounce would be a mistake?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're pressing a button that makes a sound. Debounce means you only hear the sound after you stop pressing for a while. Throttle means you hear the sound at most once every second, no matter how fast you press. Both help avoid doing too much work too quickly.","detailedExplanation":"

Debounce and throttle are two techniques used to control how often a function is executed in response to frequent events (e.g., typing, scrolling, resizing). They are essential for optimizing performance in frontend applications.

What is Debounce?

Debounce ensures that a function is called only after a specified period of inactivity. Each time the event fires, the timer resets. The function executes only when the events stop for the given wait time. A common use case is autocomplete search: you want to send a request to the server only after the user stops typing, not on every keystroke.

What is Throttle?

Throttle ensures that a function is called at most once in a specified time interval. No matter how many times the event fires, the function executes at a steady rate. A typical use case is scroll or resize handlers: you want to update the UI or fetch data periodically while the user scrolls, but not on every pixel change.

How They Work

Both are higher-order functions that return a wrapped version of the original function. Debounce uses clearTimeout and setTimeout to postpone execution. Throttle can be implemented using a timestamp or a flag (e.g., setTimeout with a lock).

Why They Matter in Production

Without these techniques, expensive operations (like API calls, DOM updates, or heavy calculations) can be triggered hundreds of times per second, causing jank, high CPU usage, and poor user experience. Debounce and throttle help maintain smooth performance and reduce server load.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is using debounce for scroll events, which can cause the UI to feel laggy because updates only happen after scrolling stops. Throttle is usually better for continuous events like scrolling or resizing, as it provides regular updates without delay.

"}]
qgen_v2:398421dd32206ead:
[{"question":"Explain code splitting and lazy loading strategies for optimizing web application performance.","expectedPoints":["Code splitting divides the application bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand.","Lazy loading defers loading of non-critical resources until they are needed.","Both techniques reduce initial bundle size and improve load time.","Common tools include Webpack dynamic imports and React.lazy with Suspense.","Trade-offs include increased complexity and potential for loading delays."],"followUps":["How would you implement code splitting in a React application?","What are the potential pitfalls of lazy loading and how would you mitigate them?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big toy box with all your toys inside. If you want to play with just one toy, you have to dump out the whole box. Code splitting is like having smaller boxes for each toy, so you only open the box you need. Lazy loading is like waiting until you actually want to play with a toy before you open its box. This makes starting to play much faster because you don't have to deal with all the toys at once.","detailedExplanation":"

Code splitting and lazy loading are performance optimization techniques that reduce the initial load time of a web application by breaking the JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks and loading them only when needed.

What They Are

Code splitting is the process of splitting the application code into separate bundles that can be loaded on demand. Lazy loading is the practice of deferring the loading of these bundles until they are actually required by the user (e.g., when navigating to a route or interacting with a component).

How They Work

Modern bundlers like Webpack and Rollup support dynamic import() syntax, which returns a promise and creates a separate chunk. In React, React.lazy() combined with Suspense allows lazy loading of components. For example:

const LazyComponent = React.lazy(() => import('./LazyComponent'));

This chunk is fetched only when LazyComponent is rendered.

Why It Matters in Production

Large JavaScript bundles can significantly slow down initial page loads, especially on slow networks. By splitting code, you can reduce the initial bundle size, improve Time to Interactive (TTI), and enhance user experience. Lazy loading also reduces bandwidth usage by loading only what the user interacts with.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is over-splitting, which can lead to too many small network requests and degrade performance due to request overhead. It's important to find a balance—split at natural boundaries like routes or heavy components, and avoid splitting trivial code.

"}]
qgen_v2:7c7ef5f07366a86e:
[{"question":"How do you decide between using local state, Context, a state management library like Redux, and server state for a React application?","expectedPoints":["Local state for component-specific, non-shared data (e.g., form inputs).","Context for shared state across a subtree with moderate frequency of updates (e.g., theme, user).","State management library for complex, global state with many actions and middleware needs.","Server state for data that must be synchronized with a backend, often using caching and refetching.","Consider trade-offs: performance, scalability, and developer experience."],"followUps":["What are the downsides of using Context for frequently updating state?","How would you handle optimistic updates with server state?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box. Local state is like keeping a toy in your pocket for just you. Context is like putting a toy in the living room so everyone in the family can play with it. A library like Redux is like a big toy warehouse with a manager who keeps track of all toys. Server state is like a toy that lives at your friend's house, and you have to ask them to play with it.","detailedExplanation":"

Choosing the right state management approach in React depends on the scope, complexity, and synchronization needs of the state.

Local State

Use local state (via useState or useReducer) for data that is only relevant to a single component. Examples include form inputs, toggle switches, or UI state like dropdown visibility. It is simple and keeps the component self-contained.

Context

Context is ideal for sharing state across a component tree without prop drilling. Use it for global data that doesn't change frequently, such as theme, locale, or authenticated user. However, avoid using Context for state that updates often (e.g., real-time data) because it can cause unnecessary re-renders of all consumers.

State Management Libraries

Libraries like Redux, Zustand, or MobX are suitable for complex global state with many actions, side effects, or middleware requirements (e.g., logging, undo/redo). They provide a predictable state container and devtools for debugging. However, they add boilerplate and should not be used for trivial state.

Server State

For data fetched from or synchronized with a backend, use tools like React Query, SWR, or Apollo Client. These handle caching, background refetching, and optimistic updates, reducing the need to manually sync state with the server. Avoid storing server data in Redux unless you need to share it across many components with complex transformations.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is overusing Context for state that updates frequently, leading to performance issues. Similarly, using Redux for simple local state adds unnecessary complexity. Always start with the simplest solution and scale up based on actual needs.

"}]

[{"question":"How does normalizing state reduce complexity and improve performance in frontend applications?","expectedPoints":["Explains that normalized state stores each entity in a separate object, avoiding duplication.","Describes how updates to one entity do not affect others, reducing complexity.","Mentions that normalized state enables efficient lookups and updates using IDs.","Connects to performance improvements by minimizing re-renders and data fetching.","Provides a concrete example (e.g., Redux normalizr, relational data)."],"followUps":["What are the trade-offs of normalizing state compared to keeping it denormalized?","How would you handle optimistic updates in a normalized state structure?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big box of toys, but each toy is in its own small box. If you want to change one toy, you only need to open that small box, not the whole big box. Normalizing state is like that: each piece of data is stored separately, so updating one piece doesn't mess up the others, and finding things is faster.","detailedExplanation":"

Normalizing state is a design pattern where data is stored in a flat, normalized structure, similar to a database schema. Each entity type (e.g., users, posts) has its own object keyed by ID, and references between entities use IDs instead of nested objects. This approach reduces complexity by eliminating data duplication and ensuring a single source of truth.

How It Works

Instead of storing nested data like { post: { id: 1, author: { id: 5, name: 'Alice' } } }, you store { posts: { 1: { id: 1, authorId: 5 } }, users: { 5: { id: 5, name: 'Alice' } } }. Updates to a user only happen in one place, and any component that needs user data can look it up by ID.

Why It Matters in Production

  • Performance: Normalized state reduces unnecessary re-renders because updating one entity doesn't cause other entities to re-render. It also makes memoization and selectors more efficient.
  • Maintainability: Changes are localized; you don't need to update multiple copies of the same data. This is crucial in large applications with complex data relationships.
  • Optimistic Updates: With normalized state, you can optimistically update a specific entity (e.g., change a user's name) without worrying about affecting other parts of the state. If the server fails, you can roll back that single entity.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is over-normalizing or under-normalizing. Over-normalizing can lead to excessive joins and complex selectors, while under-normalizing causes duplication and sync issues. Use tools like normalizr to define schemas and strike a balance based on your app's access patterns.

"}]
qgen_v2:88c40abbda9af692:
[{"question":"Compare and contrast Polling, Long-polling, WebSocket, and Server-Sent Events (SSE) for real-time data updates. When would you choose each?","expectedPoints":["Explains Polling: client sends requests at fixed intervals, simple but wasteful.","Explains Long-polling: server holds request until data available, reduces unnecessary requests but still uses resources.","Explains WebSocket: full-duplex, low latency, requires server and client support.","Explains SSE: server pushes events, unidirectional, simpler than WebSocket, uses HTTP.","Considers trade-offs: performance, resource usage, browser support, use case (e.g., chat vs. notifications)."],"followUps":["How would you handle reconnection in WebSocket vs. SSE?","What are the scalability implications of each approach?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to know when your mom bakes cookies. Polling is like asking every 5 minutes. Long-polling is asking once and waiting until she says 'done'. WebSocket is like a phone call where you both talk anytime. SSE is like a radio where she announces when cookies are ready, but you can't reply. Each has pros and cons depending on how often you need updates and how much effort you want to spend.","detailedExplanation":"

Real-time data updates are crucial for modern web applications like chat, live feeds, and notifications. Four common techniques are Polling, Long-polling, WebSocket, and Server-Sent Events (SSE). Each has distinct characteristics and trade-offs.

Polling

Polling involves the client sending HTTP requests at fixed intervals (e.g., every 5 seconds) to check for new data. It's simple to implement but inefficient because many requests return no new data, wasting bandwidth and server resources. It's suitable for low-frequency updates where simplicity matters more than performance.

Long-polling

Long-polling improves on polling by having the server hold the request open until new data is available or a timeout occurs. This reduces the number of empty responses but still uses a persistent connection per client, which can strain server resources under high load. It's a fallback when WebSocket is not available.

WebSocket

WebSocket provides a full-duplex communication channel over a single TCP connection. Both client and server can send messages anytime with low latency. It requires server support and a handshake upgrade from HTTP. Ideal for applications needing bidirectional, low-latency updates like online gaming or collaborative editing.

Server-Sent Events (SSE)

SSE enables servers to push events to clients over HTTP. It's unidirectional (server to client) and simpler than WebSocket, using standard HTTP and supporting automatic reconnection. However, it has limitations: no client-to-server messaging, limited browser support (not in IE), and a maximum number of concurrent connections per browser (typically 6).

Choosing the Right Approach

Consider performance: WebSocket and SSE are efficient for frequent updates. Resource usage: Long-polling and SSE are lighter than polling but heavier than WebSocket. Browser support: WebSocket is widely supported; SSE lacks IE support. Use case: For chat, WebSocket is best; for live news feeds, SSE works well. Common pitfall: Overusing polling can degrade performance; always consider fallback strategies for older browsers.

"}]
qgen_v2:c63f059427684668:
[{"question":"Explain the Critical Rendering Path (CRP) and its steps.","expectedPoints":["CRP converts HTML/CSS/JS to pixels on screen","Steps: parsing HTML to DOM, layout, paint, composite, draw","CRP runs in event loop via rendering engine","Optimization techniques: lazy loading, code splitting, caching"],"followUps":["How does a blocking script affect CRP?","What is the difference between layout and paint?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a LEGO castle. First, you read the instructions (parsing HTML). Then you figure out where each brick goes (layout). Next, you color the bricks (paint). Finally, you put all the pieces together to see the castle (composite and draw). The Critical Rendering Path is like that process for a webpage.","detailedExplanation":"

The Critical Rendering Path (CRP) is the sequence of steps a browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into pixels on the screen. Understanding CRP is crucial for optimizing page load performance.

Steps of CRP

  • Parsing HTML: The browser parses HTML to build the DOM (Document Object Model) tree. CSS and JavaScript can block or delay this step.
  • Layout: The browser calculates the position and size of each element based on the DOM and CSSOM (CSS Object Model). This is also called reflow.
  • Paint: The browser fills in pixels for each element (e.g., colors, images) into layers.
  • Composite: Layers are combined into a single image, often using the GPU for efficiency.
  • Draw: The final image is displayed on the screen.

How it works

CRP runs within the browser's event loop, managed by the rendering engine. The engine repeatedly processes tasks like parsing, layout, and paint. Any change to the DOM or styles can trigger a reflow or repaint, which may be costly.

Why it matters in production

Optimizing CRP reduces First Contentful Paint (FCP) and Time to Interactive (TTI). Techniques include lazy loading images, code splitting JavaScript, and caching resources. Minimizing render-blocking resources (e.g., CSS in <head>) is critical.

Common pitfall

Developers often forget that JavaScript can block DOM construction if placed before CSS or without async/defer. This delays CRP and hurts performance.

"}]
qgen_v2:99e1d19a15ecf4a4:
[{"question":"What are the differences between cookies, localStorage, and IndexedDB, and when would you use each?","expectedPoints":["Cookies are small (4KB), sent with every HTTP request, used for session management and tracking.","localStorage is larger (5-10MB), synchronous, persists until cleared, suitable for simple key-value data.","IndexedDB is asynchronous, supports large structured data (unlimited), uses object stores and indexes.","Cookies have expiration and security flags (HttpOnly, Secure), localStorage and IndexedDB are domain-specific.","Choose cookies for server-required data, localStorage for simple client-side persistence, IndexedDB for complex offline apps."],"followUps":["How would you handle storing a user's shopping cart?","What are the security implications of using localStorage vs cookies?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a notebook (cookie) that you carry everywhere and show to your teacher each time you talk. You have a drawer at home (localStorage) where you keep things you want for a long time. And you have a big filing cabinet (IndexedDB) for lots of papers you need to find quickly. Each is good for different things.","detailedExplanation":"

Cookies, localStorage, and IndexedDB are client-side storage mechanisms with distinct characteristics.

Cookies

Cookies are small text files (max 4KB) stored by the browser. They are automatically sent with every HTTP request to the same domain, making them ideal for session management (e.g., session ID), personalization, and tracking. They can have expiration dates and security flags like HttpOnly (prevents JavaScript access) and Secure (only sent over HTTPS). However, they increase request size and are limited in storage.

localStorage

localStorage provides a simple key-value store (5-10MB per origin) that persists even after the browser is closed. It is synchronous and only stores strings. It is not sent to the server automatically, making it suitable for client-side preferences, cached data, or offline state. However, it is not secure for sensitive data and blocks the main thread during operations.

IndexedDB

IndexedDB is a low-level, asynchronous NoSQL database that can store large amounts of structured data (files, blobs, etc.). It supports indexes for fast queries and transactions for data integrity. It is ideal for complex offline applications (e.g., Progressive Web Apps) that need to store and query large datasets efficiently.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is using localStorage for sensitive data (e.g., tokens) because it is accessible via JavaScript and vulnerable to XSS attacks. Use HttpOnly cookies for authentication tokens instead.

"}]
qgen_v2:3415fe953e537af1:
[{"question":"Explain how CORS works and why it is necessary for web security.","expectedPoints":["CORS is a browser security mechanism that controls cross-origin HTTP requests.","It uses HTTP headers like Access-Control-Allow-Origin to specify allowed origins.","Preflight requests (OPTIONS) are sent for non-simple requests to check permissions.","CORS prevents malicious sites from accessing sensitive data from other origins.","Common pitfalls include misconfigured headers or overly permissive origins."],"followUps":["What is the difference between a simple request and a preflighted request?","How would you debug a CORS error in a production application?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a secret clubhouse. Only kids with a special wristband can enter. CORS is like that wristband for websites. When your website wants to get something from another website, the browser checks if the other website gives permission. If not, the browser stops the request to keep your data safe.","detailedExplanation":"

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security mechanism that controls access to resources from different origins. An origin is defined by the scheme (protocol), host (domain), and port. For example, https://example.com and https://api.example.com are different origins.

How It Works

When a web application makes a cross-origin request, the browser adds an Origin header to the request. The server responds with an Access-Control-Allow-Origin header specifying which origins are allowed. If the origin matches, the browser allows the response to be read by the application. For requests that may have side effects (like PUT, DELETE, or requests with custom headers), the browser first sends a preflight request using the OPTIONS method. The server must respond with appropriate CORS headers (e.g., Access-Control-Allow-Methods, Access-Control-Allow-Headers) to indicate that the actual request is permitted.

Why It Matters in Production

CORS is critical for preventing Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) and data theft. Without CORS, a malicious site could make authenticated requests to a banking API and read the response. Proper CORS configuration ensures that only trusted origins can access your API. In production, avoid using Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * if credentials (cookies, authorization headers) are involved, as this is insecure.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is forgetting to handle preflight requests. If the server does not respond to OPTIONS requests with the correct headers, the browser will block the actual request. Another pitfall is misconfiguring allowed headers or methods, causing legitimate requests to fail. Always test CORS with tools like curl or browser developer tools.

"}]
qgen_v2:464a077444425613:
[{"question":"Explain the difference between XSS and CSRF attacks, and describe how you would defend against each in a modern frontend application.","expectedPoints":["XSS involves injecting malicious scripts into a web page, while CSRF tricks the user into performing unintended actions.","XSS defenses include output encoding, input validation, and Content Security Policy (CSP).","CSRF defenses include anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies, and double-submit cookie pattern.","Understanding that XSS can be used to bypass CSRF protections if not properly mitigated.","Practical implementation details like setting CSP headers and using framework-provided CSRF tokens."],"followUps":["How would you implement a Content Security Policy to mitigate XSS without breaking existing functionality?","Can you explain how SameSite cookies help prevent CSRF and what the different SameSite values mean?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a secret diary. XSS is like someone sneaking a note into your diary that makes you write things you don't want to. CSRF is like someone tricking you into opening your diary and writing something while you think you're doing something else. To protect against XSS, you check every note before putting it in. To protect against CSRF, you make sure only you can open your diary by using a special key.","detailedExplanation":"

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) and CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) are two distinct web security vulnerabilities that exploit different aspects of web application trust.

What is XSS?

XSS occurs when an attacker injects malicious scripts (usually JavaScript) into a web page viewed by other users. The injected script executes in the victim's browser, allowing the attacker to steal cookies, session tokens, or perform actions on behalf of the victim. There are three main types: Stored XSS (persistent), Reflected XSS (non-persistent), and DOM-based XSS.

What is CSRF?

CSRF tricks an authenticated user into executing unwanted actions on a web application where they are logged in. The attacker crafts a malicious request (e.g., via an image tag or form) that leverages the user's existing session cookie to perform state-changing operations like transferring funds or changing email.

Defense Mechanisms

  • XSS Defenses:
    • Output Encoding: Encode user input before rendering (e.g., HTML entity encoding).
    • Input Validation: Reject or sanitize malicious input (e.g., strip script tags).
    • Content Security Policy (CSP): Restrict sources of executable scripts via HTTP headers (e.g., Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self').
  • CSRF Defenses:
    • Anti-CSRF Tokens: Include a unique, unpredictable token in forms and verify it server-side.
    • SameSite Cookies: Set cookies with SameSite=Strict or Lax to prevent cross-site requests from including them.
    • Double Submit Cookie: Send a random value both as a cookie and a request header; server checks they match.

Why It Matters in Production

Both attacks can lead to account takeover, data theft, or unauthorized actions. A senior frontend engineer must implement layered defenses, as XSS can be used to steal CSRF tokens, rendering CSRF protections ineffective. CSP is a powerful tool but requires careful tuning to avoid breaking legitimate functionality.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on client-side validation for XSS or assuming that HTTPS alone prevents CSRF. Always enforce server-side validation and use security headers.

"}]
qgen_v2:d8d20b1e9f5016b7:
[{"question":"Explain the testing pyramid and the purpose of each layer: unit, integration, and end-to-end tests.","expectedPoints":["Describes the testing pyramid as a hierarchy of test types.","Explains unit tests: test individual units in isolation, fast, many.","Explains integration tests: test interactions between units, fewer than unit tests.","Explains end-to-end tests: test full system from user perspective, slow, few.","Mentions trade-offs: speed vs. confidence, cost vs. coverage."],"followUps":["Why is it recommended to have more unit tests than end-to-end tests?","What are common pitfalls when following the testing pyramid?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine building a LEGO castle. Unit tests check each brick is good. Integration tests check if two bricks fit together. End-to-end tests check if the whole castle stands. The pyramid says: have many brick checks, fewer fit checks, and just a few whole-castle checks.","detailedExplanation":"

The testing pyramid is a metaphor that describes the ideal distribution of automated tests in a software project. It consists of three layers: unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end (e2e) tests.

Unit Tests

These test the smallest pieces of code—functions, methods, or classes—in isolation. They are fast, cheap to write and maintain, and provide quick feedback. The goal is to verify that each unit behaves correctly on its own. They form the base of the pyramid, meaning you should have many of them.

Integration Tests

These test how multiple units work together, such as a function calling a database or an API endpoint. They are slower and more complex than unit tests but catch issues that arise from interactions. They sit in the middle of the pyramid, so you have fewer integration tests than unit tests.

End-to-End Tests

These simulate real user scenarios, testing the entire system from the UI to the backend and database. They are slow, brittle, and expensive to maintain. They sit at the top, meaning you have the fewest of them. They provide high confidence that the system works as a whole.

Why It Matters in Production

Following the pyramid ensures a balanced test suite that gives fast feedback (unit tests) while still catching integration and system-level bugs. Over-reliance on e2e tests leads to slow, flaky builds; too few unit tests misses low-level bugs early.

Common Pitfall

Developers often write too many e2e tests and too few unit tests, making the test suite slow and fragile. The pyramid reminds us to prioritize unit tests for speed and reliability.

"}]
