[{"question":"Why should we prefer behavior-driven testing over testing implementation details?","expectedPoints":["Improves understanding of customer requirements","Reduces dependency on implementation details, increasing flexibility","Enhances maintainability when code changes","Avoids duplicate tests, saving time and effort","Increases confidence in deployment"],"followUps":["Can you give an example where testing implementation details caused maintenance issues?","How would you balance BDD with unit tests in a real project?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're testing a vending machine. Behavior-driven testing checks if you get the right snack when you press a button, no matter how the machine works inside. Testing implementation details would check if a specific gear turns, which might break if the machine is redesigned. BDD focuses on what the user cares about, making tests more reliable and easier to update.","detailedExplanation":"

Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) focuses on testing the external behavior of an application from the user's perspective, rather than testing internal implementation details. This approach aligns tests with business requirements and improves collaboration between developers, testers, and non-technical stakeholders.

Key Benefits

  • Enhanced Understanding: BDD tests are written in a language that describes how the application should behave, making it easier for everyone to understand what the system does.
  • Reduced Dependency: Tests are decoupled from internal code structure, so refactoring or changing implementation does not break tests as long as the behavior remains the same.
  • Better Maintainability: When requirements change, only the behavior tests need updating, not a suite of unit tests tied to specific functions.
  • Less Duplication: BDD encourages testing scenarios rather than individual methods, reducing redundant tests.
  • Increased Confidence: A robust set of behavior tests ensures that the application works correctly from the user's standpoint, giving confidence during deployment.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is to over-rely on BDD and neglect unit tests entirely. While BDD is great for high-level scenarios, unit tests are still valuable for verifying complex algorithms, edge cases, and internal logic that may not be covered by behavior tests. A balanced testing strategy uses both approaches.

"}]
qgen_v2:eae35f353fc11c49:
[{"question":"Explain the differences between mock, stub, and spy in testing, and describe scenarios where each is most appropriate.","expectedPoints":["Mock: pre-programmed expectations, used to verify interaction behavior.","Stub: provides canned answers to calls, used to control indirect inputs.","Spy: wraps a real object, records calls, allows partial mocking.","Mock is best for behavior verification; stub for state verification; spy for legacy code.","Overusing mocks can lead to brittle tests; prefer stubs for simple return values."],"followUps":["How would you decide between using a mock and a stub for a given test?","Can you give an example where a spy would be more useful than a mock?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're testing a robot that delivers packages. A mock is like a fake person who only says 'I got the package' so you can check if the robot handed it over correctly. A stub is like a fake door that always opens when the robot knocks, so you can test the robot's next step. A spy is like a camera that watches the real door and records if it was knocked, but lets the real door work normally.","detailedExplanation":"

In testing, mocks, stubs, and spies are test doubles used to replace real dependencies. They serve different purposes and are chosen based on the test's goal.

Mock

A mock is an object that is pre-programmed with expectations about which methods will be called, with what arguments, and how many times. It is used to verify behavior — i.e., that the system under test interacts correctly with its dependencies. For example, in a payment service test, you might mock the payment gateway to assert that charge() is called exactly once with the correct amount.

Stub

A stub provides canned answers to calls made during the test. It is used to control indirect inputs — i.e., to make the system under test follow a certain path. For example, stubbing a database call to return a specific user object allows you to test the logic that processes that user without needing a real database.

Spy

A spy wraps a real object and records information about calls made to it. It allows you to optionally stub some methods while letting others run real code. Spies are useful for partial mocking, especially in legacy code where you want to test a method that calls other methods on the same object without fully isolating it.

When to Use Each

  • Mock: Use when the primary goal is to verify that certain interactions happened (e.g., a callback was invoked, an event was emitted).
  • Stub: Use when you need to provide controlled responses to make the system under test execute a specific scenario (e.g., error handling, edge cases).
  • Spy: Use when you want to keep most of the real behavior but need to verify or override a few methods, often in integration tests or when refactoring legacy code.

Common Pitfall

Overusing mocks can lead to brittle tests that break when implementation details change, even if the behavior remains correct. Prefer stubs for simple return values and reserve mocks for interactions that are truly critical to the system's correctness.

"}]

[{"question":"Walk me through how you would design the frontend for a real-time collaborative document editor like Google Docs. Use the RADIO framework to structure your answer.","expectedPoints":["Requirements: Identify key features like real-time sync, offline support, conflict resolution, and accessibility.","Architecture: Propose a component tree, state management (e.g., CRDT or OT), and data flow (WebSocket, local cache).","Design: Discuss UI layout, toolbar, cursor presence, and responsive design.","Implementation: Mention tech stack (React, WebSocket, IndexedDB) and key libraries (e.g., Yjs, Quill).","Optimization: Talk about debouncing updates, virtual scrolling for long docs, lazy loading, and performance monitoring."],"followUps":["How would you handle conflict resolution when two users edit the same paragraph simultaneously?","What tradeoffs would you consider between using Operational Transformation (OT) vs CRDT?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are drawing on the same digital whiteboard from different computers. You need a plan so that both of you can draw at the same time without messing up each other's work. The RADIO framework is like a recipe that helps you think step by step: first, figure out what you need (Requirements), then decide how to build it (Architecture), then sketch the look (Design), then actually build it (Implementation), and finally make it faster and smoother (Optimization).","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to apply the RADIO framework to a complex, real-world frontend system design problem. The interviewer wants to see structured thinking, awareness of tradeoffs, and depth in areas like real-time collaboration, state management, and performance.

Requirements

Start by clarifying functional and non-functional requirements. Key features include real-time multi-user editing, offline support, conflict resolution, version history, and accessibility. Non-functional requirements: low latency (<100ms for keystrokes), high availability, scalability to thousands of concurrent users, and consistency of document state.

Architecture

Propose a client-server architecture using WebSockets for real-time communication. On the client, use a component tree (e.g., React) with a state management layer (e.g., Redux or Zustand) that holds the document model. For conflict resolution, choose between Operational Transformation (OT) or CRDTs (e.g., Yjs). OT is mature but complex; CRDTs are simpler for peer-to-peer but may have higher memory usage. Data flow: user input → local update → send operation via WebSocket → server broadcasts to other clients → apply operation.

Design

UI design includes a toolbar (bold, italic, etc.), a document canvas, cursor presence indicators (colored cursors with user names), and a sidebar for comments or version history. Responsive design ensures usability on mobile and desktop.

Implementation

Tech stack: React for UI, WebSocket (or Socket.IO) for real-time, IndexedDB for offline storage, and a library like Yjs or ShareDB for collaboration. For the editor, use Quill or Slate with custom plugins. Implement debouncing for local saves and virtual scrolling for long documents.

Optimization

Optimize perceived performance: optimistic updates (show changes immediately), lazy loading of images and non-critical assets, code splitting for the editor bundle, and service workers for offline support. Monitor with Lighthouse and Web Vitals.

Common pitfall: Ignoring conflict resolution complexity. Many candidates assume simple last-write-wins, which leads to data loss. Always address how concurrent edits are merged.

"}]
qgen_v2:117d026393cfff14:
[{"question":"Walk me through how you would design a real-time collaborative document editor (like Google Docs) from a frontend perspective, using the RADIO framework.","expectedPoints":["Clarifies requirements: real-time sync, conflict resolution, offline support, user roles.","Proposes architecture: component tree, data flow (e.g., WebSocket, CRDT/OT), state management.","Defines data model: document structure, cursor positions, version vectors, client vs server state.","Specifies interfaces: API endpoints, WebSocket messages, component props (e.g., Editor, Toolbar, CursorOverlay).","Discusses optimizations: debouncing, virtual scrolling, optimistic updates, accessibility, security (XSS, rate limiting)."],"followUps":["How would you handle conflict resolution if two users edit the same paragraph simultaneously?","What trade-offs would you consider between using Operational Transformation vs CRDT?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are drawing on the same digital whiteboard at the same time. You need a plan so that both of your changes show up correctly without messing up each other's work. The RADIO framework is like a checklist that helps you think about all the important parts: what you need to build, how to organize the pieces, what data to store, how parts talk to each other, and how to make it fast and safe.","detailedExplanation":"

The RADIO framework provides a structured approach to frontend system design interviews. Let's apply it to designing a real-time collaborative document editor.

Requirements (R)

First, clarify the problem: multiple users editing the same document simultaneously, seeing each other's changes in real-time. Key requirements include real-time sync, conflict resolution, offline support, user roles (view/edit), and device compatibility. Ask the interviewer to confirm assumptions about scale (e.g., 100 concurrent users) and features (e.g., comments, history).

Architecture (A)

Propose a component tree: App → Editor (with Toolbar, ContentArea, CursorOverlay), CollaboratorsPanel, CommentSidebar. Data flows via WebSocket for real-time updates, with a state management layer (e.g., Redux or Zustand) holding the document state. Use CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) or OT (Operational Transformation) for conflict resolution. The architecture should separate server state (persisted document) from client state (cursor positions, pending operations).

Data Model (D)

Define the document as a list of operations or a tree of nodes (paragraphs, text runs). Each node has an id, type, content, and attributes. For collaboration, include version vectors or Lamport timestamps to order changes. Client state includes cursorPosition, selection, pendingOps.

Interface/API (I)

Define WebSocket messages: joinDocument, leaveDocument, operation, cursorUpdate. REST endpoints for fetching document history, user info. Component props: Editor receives documentId, userId, onOperation callback. CursorOverlay receives remoteCursors array.

Optimizations (O)

Discuss performance: virtual scrolling for large documents, debouncing cursor updates, optimistic updates for low latency. Accessibility: ARIA roles for editor, keyboard navigation. Security: sanitize HTML to prevent XSS, rate-limit WebSocket messages. A common pitfall is over-engineering the conflict resolution algorithm; start with a simple OT approach and only add complexity if needed.

"}]
qgen_v2:0121a8fe8cd53d79:
[{"question":"Explain the difference between frontend system design and backend system design.","expectedPoints":["Frontend focuses on user experience, client architecture, rendering, and perceived performance.","Backend focuses on throughput, database, server scaling, and data consistency.","Both require trade-offs and scaling considerations, but frontend is more about client-side optimization.","Frontend design involves component organization, state management, network optimization, and user-perceived performance."],"followUps":["How would you approach designing a real-time collaborative editing feature on the frontend?","What trade-offs would you consider when choosing between client-side rendering and server-side rendering?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you are building a toy car. Frontend design is like making the car look cool, have comfortable seats, and drive smoothly for the person inside. Backend design is like building the engine and making sure the car can go fast and carry heavy loads without breaking. Both are important but focus on different things.","detailedExplanation":"

Frontend system design and backend system design serve different purposes in a web application. Frontend system design focuses on the client side: how to structure components, manage state, optimize rendering, and deliver a smooth user experience. It deals with perceived performance, such as load times, animations, and responsiveness. Backend system design focuses on server-side concerns: handling requests, managing databases, scaling services, and ensuring data consistency and throughput.

Key Differences

  • User Experience vs. Throughput: Frontend prioritizes how the user feels and interacts; backend prioritizes how many requests can be handled efficiently.
  • Client Architecture vs. Server Architecture: Frontend designs component trees, state management (e.g., Redux, Context API), and caching strategies (e.g., service workers). Backend designs API endpoints, database schemas, load balancers, and microservices.
  • Perceived Performance vs. Actual Performance: Frontend optimizes for perceived speed (e.g., skeleton screens, lazy loading); backend optimizes for actual response times and resource utilization.

Why It Matters in Production

Understanding these differences helps engineers make informed trade-offs. For example, a frontend developer might choose client-side rendering for interactivity but must consider SEO and initial load time. A backend developer might choose a NoSQL database for scalability but must handle eventual consistency. Both need to think about scaling, but the metrics and constraints differ.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming frontend design is just about UI. In reality, it involves complex decisions about data fetching, caching, state synchronization, and performance monitoring. Ignoring these can lead to poor user experiences even if the backend is perfectly scaled.

"}]

[{"question":"You are asked to design a real-time collaborative document editor like Google Docs. Walk me through how you would clarify the requirements before proposing a solution.","expectedPoints":["Ask about functional requirements: supported features (text formatting, comments, version history, etc.).","Ask about non-functional requirements: latency, consistency model, offline support, number of concurrent users.","Clarify scope: what is in/out (e.g., mobile support, real-time sync vs. periodic sync).","Identify constraints: browser storage limits, network reliability, security (auth, encryption).","Define success criteria: e.g., conflict resolution rate, time to sync, user satisfaction metrics."],"followUps":["How would you prioritize these requirements if the timeline is tight?","What trade-offs would you consider between eventual consistency and strong consistency?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend want to draw on the same digital whiteboard from different houses. Before deciding what markers to use, you need to agree on what you'll draw, how fast it should update, and what happens if you both draw at the same time. That's what clarifying requirements means.","detailedExplanation":"

Clarifying requirements is the most critical step in system design interviews. It prevents building the wrong solution and demonstrates your ability to think holistically.

What to Clarify

  • Functional requirements: List features like real-time editing, comments, version history, formatting, and access control.
  • Non-functional requirements: Define latency (e.g., <500ms), consistency model (e.g., eventual vs. strong), offline support, and scalability (e.g., 10k concurrent users).
  • Scope: Explicitly state what is in scope (e.g., web only) and out of scope (e.g., mobile native apps).
  • Constraints: Identify technical limits like browser storage (e.g., IndexedDB size), network reliability, and security (e.g., end-to-end encryption).
  • Success criteria: Metrics like conflict resolution rate, time to sync, and user satisfaction.

Why It Matters in Production

Without clear requirements, teams waste time on features that don't matter. For example, building strong consistency for a collaborative editor might cause high latency, frustrating users. Clarifying early avoids costly rework.

Common Pitfall

Jumping straight to a solution (e.g., using CRDTs) without understanding the problem. Always start with questions like 'How many users?', 'What's the acceptable delay?', and 'Do we need offline support?'

"}]

[{"question":"In a frontend system design interview for a social media feed, how would you categorize and prioritize requirements? Give examples of functional, non-functional, and constraint requirements.","expectedPoints":["Functional: features like posting, liking, commenting, infinite scroll, image upload.","Non-functional: performance (load speed, smooth scrolling), offline support, accessibility, SEO.","Constraints: device types (mobile/desktop), browser support, network conditions (3G), multi-language, framework choice.","Prioritization: core functional first, then non-functional based on user impact, constraints as boundaries.","Trade-offs: e.g., offline support vs. complexity, performance vs. feature richness."],"followUps":["How would you handle conflicting requirements, like rich animations vs. low-end device performance?","What metrics would you use to validate non-functional requirements?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a playground. Functional requirements are the swings and slides (what kids can do). Non-functional are how safe and fast the equipment is. Constraints are like the size of the playground and the materials you can use. You need to decide what's most important first.","detailedExplanation":"

In a frontend system design interview, categorizing requirements is crucial to ensure a clear, structured approach. The three main categories are functional, non-functional, and constraints.

Functional Requirements

These define what the system does. For a social media feed, examples include: user can post text/images, like posts, comment, scroll infinitely, and upload images. These are the core features that directly deliver user value.

Non-Functional Requirements

These describe how well the system performs. Key aspects: performance (initial load time under 2s, smooth 60fps scrolling), offline support (cached content), accessibility (ARIA labels, keyboard navigation), and SEO (server-side rendering for crawlers). These often differentiate a good product from a great one.

Constraints

These are boundaries the design must respect: device types (mobile-first, desktop), browser support (latest 2 versions), network conditions (3G/4G), multi-language (i18n), and framework (React/Vue). Constraints limit technical choices but also guide prioritization.

Prioritization & Trade-offs

Start with core functional requirements (MVP). Then address high-impact non-functional like performance and accessibility. Constraints are non-negotiable but may force trade-offs: e.g., offline support adds complexity but improves UX on slow networks. A common pitfall is over-engineering non-functional requirements early, delaying delivery. Instead, iterate: launch with basic features, then enhance performance and accessibility based on user feedback.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you decide between CSR, SSR, and SSG for a new web application? Walk me through the key factors you'd consider.","expectedPoints":["Identifies SEO requirements as a primary factor (SSR/SSG for SEO-critical pages).","Considers time-to-interactive (FCP/LCP) and user experience (SSR for faster initial paint).","Evaluates data freshness needs (SSG for static content, SSR for dynamic, CSR for highly interactive).","Mentions scalability, caching, and server costs as trade-offs.","Asks clarifying questions about audience, device types, and offline support."],"followUps":["What if the app needs real-time data? Would you still use SSR?","How would you handle a mix of static and dynamic pages in the same app?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a lemonade stand website. If you want everyone to see your menu instantly (even if they have slow internet), you'd bake the menu into the page before they arrive (SSR). If the menu never changes, you could print it once and hand it out (SSG). But if you have a live game where scores change every second, you'd let the browser handle updates (CSR). The choice depends on what's most important: speed, freshness, or interactivity.","detailedExplanation":"

When choosing a rendering pattern (CSR, SSR, or SSG), the decision hinges on a few critical requirements. The most impactful factor is SEO: if search engines must index your content, SSR or SSG is almost mandatory because CSR delivers an empty shell to crawlers (though Google can now execute JavaScript, it's slower and less reliable). Next, time-to-interactive metrics like FCP and LCP matter: SSR sends fully rendered HTML, so users see content sooner, especially on slow networks. SSG pre-builds pages at build time, offering the fastest load but only for static content.

Key Considerations

  • Data freshness: SSG is ideal for blog posts or marketing pages that change infrequently. SSR handles dynamic data (e.g., user dashboards) by rendering on each request. CSR excels for highly interactive apps (e.g., real-time collaboration tools) where server round-trips would hurt UX.
  • Scalability & cost: SSG can be served via CDN cheaply. SSR requires server resources for each request. CSR shifts rendering to the client, reducing server load but increasing client-side processing.
  • Offline support: CSR with service workers enables offline functionality more naturally than SSR.

Common Pitfall

Many developers default to SSR without considering whether the app truly needs it. For example, an internal admin tool with no SEO requirements and frequent data updates would be better off with CSR. Over-engineering with SSR adds complexity and cost. Always start by asking: “Does this page need to be indexed by search engines? How quickly must content appear on slow devices?”

"}]
qgen_v2:c7ed8f8240f06fd3:
[{"question":"How would you architect a large-scale React application to ensure separation of concerns and maintainability? Walk me through the key layers and their responsibilities.","expectedPoints":["Divides the app into distinct layers: UI components, state management, data fetching, and business logic.","Explains how each layer communicates (e.g., custom hooks for logic, context/redux for global state).","Mentions component decomposition: small, reusable components with single responsibilities.","Discusses performance considerations like lazy loading and memoization.","Addresses folder structure and module boundaries."],"followUps":["How would you handle cross-cutting concerns like authentication or logging in this architecture?","What trade-offs do you consider when choosing between Context API and Redux for state management?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a giant LEGO castle. You don't just dump all the bricks in one pile; you separate them into groups: walls, towers, gates. Each group has a job. In a React app, we do the same: we split the code into layers like UI, data, and logic, so each part is easy to change or fix without breaking the whole castle.","detailedExplanation":"

When architecting a large-scale React application, the goal is to achieve separation of concerns so that each part of the system has a clear responsibility and can be developed, tested, and maintained independently. A common approach is to divide the application into several layers:

1. UI Component Layer

This consists of presentational components that are purely concerned with rendering HTML and styling. They receive data via props and call callbacks for user interactions. They should be small, reusable, and stateless where possible. For example, a Button or UserCard component.

2. State Management Layer

This layer manages the application's global and local state. For global state, tools like Redux, Zustand, or React Context are used. Local state is handled with useState or useReducer inside components or custom hooks. The key is to keep state as close to where it's needed to avoid unnecessary re-renders.

3. Data Fetching & Side Effects Layer

This layer handles API calls, caching, and synchronization with external services. Libraries like React Query or RTK Query are popular because they provide built-in caching, deduplication, and background refetching. Alternatively, custom hooks can encapsulate fetch logic.

4. Business Logic Layer

Complex business rules and computations are extracted into pure functions or custom hooks. This keeps components clean and makes logic testable without rendering a UI. For example, a useCart hook that calculates totals and applies discounts.

5. Infrastructure Layer

This includes routing (e.g., react-router), authentication, error boundaries, and performance optimizations like lazy loading with React.lazy and memoization with React.memo or useMemo.

A common pitfall is mixing concerns, such as putting API calls directly inside a presentational component. This makes the component hard to test and reuse. Instead, use a custom hook or a dedicated data-fetching module. Another pitfall is over-engineering: not every app needs Redux or a complex architecture. Start simple and refactor as the app grows.

"}]
qgen_v2:6b453e6a2bb5e678:
[{"question":"In a layered frontend architecture, how would you test each layer independently, and what tradeoffs exist between testing the View Model layer versus the View layer?","expectedPoints":["Describes testing strategies for each layer (e.g., unit tests for View Model, integration tests for View).","Explains tradeoffs: View Model tests are faster and more isolated but miss UI rendering issues; View tests catch visual bugs but are slower and more brittle.","Mentions dependency injection or mocking to isolate layers during testing.","Discusses real-world impact: View Model tests catch logic errors early, View tests ensure correct user experience.","Acknowledges that over-testing the View layer can lead to high maintenance cost due to frequent UI changes."],"followUps":["How would you decide which layer to test more heavily in a large-scale application?","What tools or frameworks would you use for testing each layer?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy car with separate parts: the engine, the steering, and the body. To make sure the car works, you can test each part alone before putting them together. Testing the engine alone is like testing the View Model—it's fast and checks if the logic works. Testing the whole car is like testing the View—it shows if everything looks and works right, but it's slower. You need both to be confident the car is good.","detailedExplanation":"

In a layered frontend architecture, each layer has a distinct responsibility, and testing them independently is crucial for maintainability and reliability. The View Model / Controller layer contains business logic, event handlers, and state transformations, while the View / UI Components layer handles rendering and user interaction.

Testing the View Model Layer

This layer is typically tested with unit tests using frameworks like Jest or Vitest. You can mock the State / Store Layer and Data / Network Layer to isolate the logic. Tests focus on verifying that given certain inputs (e.g., user actions, state changes), the View Model produces the correct outputs (e.g., updated state, API calls). These tests are fast, deterministic, and easy to write, making them ideal for catching logic errors early in development.

Testing the View Layer

The View layer is tested with component tests or integration tests using tools like React Testing Library or Cypress. These tests render the actual UI and simulate user interactions, verifying that the correct elements appear and behave as expected. They catch visual regressions and ensure the UI matches the design. However, they are slower, more brittle (prone to break on minor UI changes), and require more setup (e.g., rendering with mocked data).

Tradeoffs

  • Speed vs. Coverage: View Model tests run in milliseconds and cover logic thoroughly; View tests take seconds and cover rendering but miss edge cases in logic.
  • Maintenance: View tests often need updates when the UI changes, even if the logic stays the same, leading to higher maintenance cost.
  • Confidence: View Model tests give confidence in correctness; View tests give confidence in user experience. Both are needed for a robust application.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is over-testing the View layer with too many snapshot tests or testing implementation details (e.g., internal state). This leads to brittle tests that fail on trivial changes. Instead, prefer testing behavior (what the user sees and does) and rely on View Model tests for logic.

In production, a balanced approach is recommended: unit test the View Model heavily (80% of tests), integration test critical user flows (15%), and smoke test the View layer (5%) to catch major visual issues.

"}]
qgen_v2:92d742f7567812f5:
[{"question":"Explain the Container vs Presentational pattern in React and describe a scenario where you would choose to use it over hooks.","expectedPoints":["Container handles state, side effects, and data fetching; Presentational only renders UI via props.","Separation of concerns improves reusability, testability, and maintainability.","Hooks can replace containers but pattern still useful for complex logic or team conventions.","Example: UserProfileContainer fetches user data and passes to UserProfile presentational component.","Trade-off: extra abstraction vs. simplicity with hooks."],"followUps":["How would you test a presentational component in isolation?","When would you avoid this pattern and use hooks directly in a component?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that can both fetch a story from the internet and show it on screen. The Container part is like the internet-fetching robot that gets the story, and the Presentational part is like a picture frame that just displays whatever story you give it. By keeping them separate, you can reuse the picture frame for different stories without changing the robot.","detailedExplanation":"

The Container vs Presentational pattern (also known as Smart vs Dumb components) is a design pattern in React that separates logic from UI rendering.

What it is

A Container component manages state, side effects (e.g., API calls), and event handlers. It passes data and callbacks as props to a Presentational component, which only renders the UI based on those props. The presentational component contains no business logic or state of its own.

How it works

In practice, the container uses hooks like useState and useEffect to fetch data and manage state. It then renders the presentational component with the necessary props. The presentational component is a pure function of its props, making it easy to test and reuse.

Why it matters in production

  • Reusability: Presentational components can be reused across different containers or even projects.
  • Testability: You can test UI in isolation (e.g., with Storybook) without mocking API calls.
  • Maintainability: Changes to data fetching logic don't affect UI rendering, and vice versa.

Common pitfall

Overusing this pattern can lead to prop drilling and unnecessary abstraction, especially with the advent of hooks. In modern React, you can often use hooks directly in components, but the pattern remains valuable for complex state logic or when you need to reuse UI components across different data sources.

"}]
qgen_v2:79b79fc3372474b0:
[{"question":"What are the main benefits and drawbacks of using micro-frontends in a large-scale application?","expectedPoints":["Benefits: independent deployment, team autonomy, scalability, performance optimization, easier maintenance of large codebases.","Drawbacks: duplicate dependencies/bundle size, integration complexity, UX inconsistency, increased build complexity.","Suitable for large, complex projects with multiple independent teams.","Trade-off between team independence and technical overhead.","Not recommended for small projects or teams lacking DevOps maturity."],"followUps":["How would you handle shared dependencies across micro-frontends to avoid duplication?","What strategies can ensure consistent UX across independently deployed micro-frontends?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge Lego castle that many friends want to build together. Instead of everyone working on the same pile of bricks, you split the castle into separate towers. Each friend builds their own tower separately, and then you connect them. This way, each friend can work at their own speed and use different Lego styles. But sometimes, you might end up with duplicate bricks (like having two copies of the same special piece), and the towers might not fit together perfectly, making the castle look a bit messy.","detailedExplanation":"

Micro-frontends extend the concept of microservices to the frontend world, decomposing a monolithic frontend into smaller, independent applications that can be developed, tested, and deployed by separate teams. This architecture is particularly beneficial for large-scale projects with multiple teams, each owning a distinct business domain.

Benefits

  • Independent deployment: Teams can release updates to their micro-frontend without coordinating with other teams, enabling faster delivery cycles.
  • Team autonomy: Each team can choose its own technology stack (e.g., React, Vue, Angular) and work independently, reducing cross-team dependencies.
  • Scalability: The application can scale horizontally by adding more micro-frontends, and performance can be optimized by loading only the necessary parts.
  • Codebase management: Large codebases are split into smaller, more manageable pieces, making it easier to understand and maintain.

Drawbacks

  • Duplicate dependencies: Each micro-frontend may bundle its own version of libraries (e.g., React), leading to increased bundle size and memory usage.
  • Integration complexity: Combining multiple micro-frontends into a cohesive user experience requires careful orchestration (e.g., using a shell application or iframes).
  • UX inconsistency: Without a shared design system, different teams may produce visually inconsistent interfaces, harming the user experience.
  • Build and testing overhead: Setting up CI/CD pipelines, end-to-end tests, and shared infrastructure adds complexity.

When to Use

Micro-frontends are ideal for large, evolving products with multiple autonomous teams, such as e-commerce platforms or enterprise dashboards. They are not recommended for small projects or teams lacking strong DevOps practices, as the overhead may outweigh the benefits.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is neglecting to establish a shared design system and communication protocol early on. Without these, teams may produce inconsistent UIs and struggle with integration, leading to a fragmented user experience. It's crucial to invest in a common component library and define clear integration boundaries.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you decide between CSR, SSR, SSG, and ISR for a new e-commerce product listing page? Walk through the trade-offs.","expectedPoints":["Considers SEO requirements (SSR/SSG/ISR better for indexable content).","Evaluates time-to-first-content (TTFC) vs interactivity (CSR may be slower initially).","Discusses server cost and complexity (SSG/ISR cheaper than SSR for static content).","Mentions update frequency (ISR for semi-dynamic, SSG for rarely changed).","Weighs user experience (CSR for highly interactive, SSR for fast first paint)."],"followUps":["What if the page has user-specific content like 'Recommended for you'?","How would you handle a product page that updates inventory frequently?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy store website. CSR is like building the store inside a truck and driving it to each customer – they have to wait for the truck to arrive before they can see anything. SSR is like having a factory that builds a toy for each customer when they order – it takes a bit of time but they get a ready toy. SSG is like building all toys in advance and putting them on shelves – customers grab them instantly, but if a toy changes, you have to rebuild. ISR is like having a shelf that automatically updates some toys every few minutes – fast and fresh.","detailedExplanation":"

When choosing a rendering pattern for an e-commerce product listing page, you must balance SEO, time-to-first-content (TTFC), server cost, and content freshness.

Client-Side Rendering (CSR)

CSR sends a minimal HTML shell and relies on JavaScript to fetch data and render the page. This can lead to poor SEO because search engine crawlers may not execute JavaScript fully. It also increases TTFC as users see a blank page until JS loads. However, CSR is cheap on the server (static hosting) and great for highly interactive apps like dashboards.

Server-Side Rendering (SSR)

SSR generates the full HTML on the server per request. This improves SEO and first paint because the browser receives ready content. The downside is higher server load and cost, especially under heavy traffic. It also increases time-to-first-byte (TTFB) if the server is slow.

Static Site Generation (SSG)

SSG pre-builds HTML at build time. It offers the best performance (CDN cached) and SEO, with minimal server cost. But it's unsuitable for frequently changing content – you'd need to rebuild the entire site to update a product price.

Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)

ISR combines SSG's speed with the ability to update specific pages after a set interval (e.g., every 60 seconds). It's ideal for product pages where inventory changes moderately. However, it adds complexity (cache invalidation) and may serve stale data briefly.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming SSG is always best for performance. For a product listing with real-time stock, ISR or SSR might be better to avoid showing out-of-stock items. Always measure TTFC and SEO impact with real crawlers.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain the key differences between CSR, SSR, SSG, and ISR in terms of when HTML is generated, SEO impact, and user-perceived performance.","expectedPoints":["CSR generates HTML on the client after JS loads, poor SEO, slow initial paint.","SSR generates HTML per request on server, good SEO, fast FCP but slower TTFB.","SSG generates HTML at build time, best SEO and fastest FCP via CDN.","ISR generates HTML at build with periodic revalidation, good SEO and fast.","Trade-offs: CSR for dynamic apps, SSR for personalized content, SSG for static content, ISR for hybrid."],"followUps":["How would you decide between SSR and SSG for a product listing page?","What are common pitfalls of hydration in SSR?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to show a picture book to your friend. CSR is like sending an empty book and then drawing each page while your friend waits. SSR is like drawing the page on your computer and then sending the finished page. SSG is like drawing all pages before your friend arrives and just handing over the book. ISR is like having most pages ready but updating a few pages every now and then.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your understanding of rendering strategies in modern web development. The core difference lies in when and where HTML is generated, which directly impacts SEO and user-perceived performance.

Client-Side Rendering (CSR)

In CSR, the server sends an empty HTML shell. The browser downloads a JavaScript bundle, executes it, fetches data, and then renders the UI. This means HTML is generated on the client after JS runs. SEO is weak because crawlers may not execute JS. First Contentful Paint (FCP) is slow because the user sees a blank page until JS finishes. CSR is best for dashboard apps or apps behind login where SEO is not critical.

Server-Side Rendering (SSR)

With SSR, the server generates the full HTML for each request. The browser receives ready HTML, so the user sees content quickly (fast FCP). However, TTFB (Time to First Byte) is slower because the server must render. SEO is good because crawlers see the full HTML. After HTML loads, hydration attaches JavaScript for interactivity. SSR is ideal for personalized pages or content that changes frequently.

Static Site Generation (SSG)

SSG generates HTML at build time and serves it via CDN. This yields the fastest FCP and best SEO because HTML is pre-built. However, content is static until the next build. SSG is perfect for blogs, documentation, or marketing pages.

Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)

ISG combines SSG with periodic revalidation. Pages are built at deploy time but can be regenerated on demand or at intervals. This gives fast performance with fresh content. ISR is great for e-commerce product pages that change occasionally.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming SSR always improves performance. For highly dynamic apps, SSR can increase server load and TTFB. Also, hydration mismatches in SSR can cause UI flicker. Always measure real-world metrics like LCP and TTI.

"}]

[{"question":"Compare SSR, SSG, and ISR in terms of hydration cost and use cases. When would you choose one over the other?","expectedPoints":["SSR hydrates on each request, higher server load but fresh content.","SSG pre-renders at build time, zero hydration cost for static pages.","ISR revalidates incrementally, balancing freshness and performance.","Choose SSR for dynamic user-specific content, SSG for static blogs, ISR for content that updates periodically."],"followUps":["How would you handle hydration for a dashboard that shows real-time data?","What are the trade-offs of using React Server Components vs traditional SSR?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that needs batteries to work. SSR is like putting batteries in every time you play, so it's always fresh but takes time. SSG is like having a toy that already has batteries from the factory, so it works instantly but can't change. ISR is like a toy that checks if it needs new batteries only when you shake it, so it's fast but can be a little old.","detailedExplanation":"

What is Hydration?

Hydration is the process of attaching JavaScript event handlers to server-rendered HTML, making a static page interactive. It can be expensive because the browser must download, parse, and execute JavaScript for the entire page before the user can interact, delaying Time to Interactive (TTI).

SSR vs SSG vs ISR

Server-Side Rendering (SSR) generates HTML on each request. It hydrates fully on the client, which can be slow for large pages. Best for dynamic, user-specific content (e.g., social media feeds).

Static Site Generation (SSG) pre-renders HTML at build time. No server work on request, and hydration is minimal because the page is mostly static. Ideal for blogs, marketing sites.

Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR) combines SSG with on-demand revalidation. Pages are served statically but can be updated after build. Good for e-commerce product pages that change occasionally.

Why It Matters in Production

Choosing the wrong strategy can hurt performance or user experience. For example, using SSR for a static blog wastes server resources and increases latency. Conversely, using SSG for a user dashboard would serve stale data.

Common Pitfall

Assuming hydration is free. Even with SSR, the client must hydrate the entire page, which can block interactivity. Techniques like progressive hydration or islands architecture can mitigate this by hydrating only critical parts first.

"}]
qgen_v2:4d639b04d1fc158e:
[{"question":"You are building a news blog that needs great SEO and fast loading, with content updated several times a day. Which rendering pattern is most suitable and why?","expectedPoints":["Identifies SSG/ISR as the best fit","Explains that SSG provides fast CDN delivery and good SEO","Mentions revalidation to handle content updates","Contrasts with SSR (slower, higher server cost) and CSR (poor SEO)","Acknowledges that content changes multiple times a day, not every second"],"followUps":["How would you implement revalidation in Next.js for this scenario?","What trade-offs would you consider if the blog also had user-specific content?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a newspaper that you want to deliver to people's homes as fast as possible. If you print all copies ahead of time (static generation), they can be stored in many local warehouses (CDN) and delivered quickly. But news changes during the day, so you need to reprint some pages every few hours (revalidation). That's faster than printing a fresh copy for every single request (SSR).","detailedExplanation":"

Hydration is the process of attaching JavaScript event handlers to server-rendered HTML, making a static page interactive. In the context of a news blog, choosing the right rendering pattern is critical for SEO and performance.

Why SSG/ISR is the best choice

Static Site Generation (SSG) pre-builds HTML at build time, which can be served from a CDN with minimal latency. This gives excellent SEO because search engines receive fully rendered HTML, and fast loading because the content is already on the edge. However, for a news blog updated several times a day, pure SSG would require a full rebuild for each update. Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR) solves this by allowing pages to be revalidated and regenerated on demand or on a schedule without rebuilding the entire site.

How it works

With ISR, you set a revalidate time (e.g., 300 seconds). When a request comes in, the CDN serves the cached page. If the revalidate time has passed, the server generates a new version in the background and updates the cache. This ensures content is fresh while maintaining CDN speed.

Why not other patterns?

  • SSR (Server-Side Rendering) generates HTML per request, increasing server load and TTFB (Time To First Byte). It's unnecessary for content that doesn't change every second.
  • CSR (Client-Side Rendering) hurts SEO and initial load time because the browser must download and execute JavaScript before rendering content.
  • ACSR (Adaptive CSR) or pure JSON delivery would not provide the SEO benefits of pre-rendered HTML.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is using SSR for all pages out of habit, even when content is mostly static. This wastes server resources and increases latency. Always evaluate update frequency: if content changes less than once per minute, ISR or SSG is almost always better.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you improve the Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) of a React application that displays a hero image and a large block of text?","expectedPoints":["Optimize image size and format (e.g., use WebP, compress, lazy load)","Use a CDN to reduce server response time","Minimize render-blocking resources (CSS/JS) and inline critical CSS","Consider server-side rendering or static generation for faster initial paint"],"followUps":["How would you measure LCP in production?","What if the hero image is dynamically loaded based on user data?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy car. LCP is how fast the car's biggest piece (like the body) appears. To make it appear faster, you can use a lighter material (smaller image), build it closer to the starting line (CDN), or pre-assemble some parts (inline CSS).","detailedExplanation":"

Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures the time it takes for the largest visible content element (e.g., an image or text block) to render on the screen. A good LCP is under 2.5 seconds.

How to improve LCP in a React app

  • Optimize images: Use modern formats like WebP, compress images, and serve appropriately sized images via srcSet. Lazy load below-the-fold images, but ensure the hero image is eagerly loaded.
  • Use a CDN: A Content Delivery Network reduces server response time (TTFB) by serving assets from edge locations closer to the user.
  • Minimize render-blocking resources: Inline critical CSS and defer non-critical JavaScript. Use rel=\"preload\" for the hero image and key fonts.
  • Server-side rendering (SSR) or static generation: Pre-render the page on the server so the browser receives HTML with the hero image already present, reducing client-side work.
  • Optimize web fonts: Use font-display: swap to ensure text is visible immediately while fonts load.

Why it matters in production

LCP directly impacts user experience and SEO. Google uses LCP as a ranking factor. A slow LCP can lead to higher bounce rates and lower conversions.

Common pitfall

Over-optimizing images by using extremely low quality can hurt user experience. Balance compression with visual quality. Also, avoid lazy loading the hero image, as that delays LCP.

"}]
qgen_v2:b2af9b0484730053:
[{"question":"How would you debug and improve a page that has a poor LCP score (e.g., > 4s) and a CLS score of 0.25? Walk me through your approach.","expectedPoints":["Identify the LCP element (e.g., hero image, heading) and optimize its loading (e.g., preload, compress, lazy-load below-fold).","Reduce render-blocking resources (CSS/JS) and use efficient caching/CDN.","For CLS, add explicit width/height to images/videos, avoid inserting content above existing content, and use font-display: swap.","Use tools like Lighthouse, WebPageTest, or Chrome DevTools Performance panel to measure and pinpoint issues.","Consider server-side rendering or static generation for faster initial paint.","Mention trade-offs: preloading may increase bandwidth, fixing layout shifts may require design changes."],"followUps":["What if the LCP element is a dynamically loaded image from a slow API?","How would you prioritize fixing LCP vs CLS if both are poor?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"debugging","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy castle. LCP is how fast the biggest tower appears—if it's slow, kids get bored. CLS is like if the castle suddenly jumps, spilling the toy knights. To fix it, you'd make sure the big tower loads quickly (like having it ready in your hand) and that all pieces have fixed spots so nothing slides around.","detailedExplanation":"

Core Web Vitals are user-centric metrics that measure real-world experience. LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) marks when the main content is visible; a poor score (>2.5s) often stems from slow server response, render-blocking resources, or unoptimized images. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) quantifies visual stability; a score >0.1 indicates elements shifting after load, usually due to missing dimensions on images/ads or late-loading fonts.

Debugging Approach

  • Identify the LCP element using Chrome DevTools Performance panel or Lighthouse. Common culprits: hero images, large headings, or videos.
  • Optimize LCP: preload the LCP resource (<link rel=preload>), compress images (WebP/AVIF), use a CDN, and eliminate render-blocking CSS/JS (inline critical CSS, defer non-critical JS).
  • Fix CLS: set explicit width and height on all images/videos, reserve space for embeds (e.g., aspect-ratio), and use font-display: swap to prevent invisible text shifts.
  • Measure with field data (Chrome User Experience Report) and lab tools (Lighthouse, WebPageTest).

Production Considerations

Always test on real devices and network conditions. Preloading too many resources can hurt performance. For CLS, avoid inserting dynamic content (ads, banners) above existing content without reserving space. A common pitfall is forgetting to set dimensions on lazy-loaded images—they cause layout shifts when they finally load.

"}]
qgen_v2:745dd050aa918c5d:
[{"question":"Explain the difference between useMemo and useCallback in React, and when you would use each for performance optimization.","expectedPoints":["useMemo memoizes a computed value, useCallback memoizes a function reference.","Both prevent unnecessary re-computation or re-creation on re-renders.","useMemo is for expensive calculations; useCallback is for passing stable callbacks to child components.","Overusing them can hurt performance due to memory overhead.","They should be used only when profiling shows a bottleneck."],"followUps":["Can you give an example where useCallback is necessary even if the function is not passed to a memoized child?","How would you debug a performance issue caused by unnecessary re-renders related to these hooks?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that does a big math problem every time you shake it. useMemo is like writing down the answer so you don't have to do the math again if you shake it the same way. useCallback is like keeping the same instruction card for a friend, so they don't get a new card every time you move.","detailedExplanation":"

useMemo and useCallback are React hooks that help optimize performance by memoizing values and functions, respectively. They are part of React's built-in optimization toolkit, but they must be used judiciously.

What They Do

useMemo takes a function and a dependency array. It returns the memoized result of that function, recalculating only when dependencies change. useCallback takes a function and a dependency array, returning a memoized version of the function itself, so the same function reference persists across renders unless dependencies change.

How They Work

Both hooks store the previous value (or function) in memory. On each render, they check if dependencies have changed using Object.is comparison. If unchanged, they return the stored value; otherwise, they recompute (for useMemo) or recreate (for useCallback).

Why They Matter in Production

In large applications, unnecessary re-renders or expensive calculations can cause jank. useMemo prevents recalculating derived data (e.g., filtering a large list) on every render. useCallback ensures that child components wrapped in React.memo don't re-render due to new function references. However, overusing these hooks adds memory overhead and comparison costs, which can degrade performance if applied prematurely.

Common Pitfall

Using useMemo or useCallback for trivial operations or without profiling. For example, memoizing a simple arithmetic operation or wrapping every callback in useCallback can be counterproductive. Always measure performance with tools like React DevTools Profiler before adding memoization.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you implement code splitting and lazy loading in a React application to improve initial load performance?","expectedPoints":["Uses React.lazy() with dynamic import for component splitting","Wraps lazy components in Suspense with a fallback UI","Explains that code splitting reduces bundle size and improves FCP/LCP","Mentions route-based splitting as a common pattern","Handles loading states and errors gracefully"],"followUps":["What happens if the lazy component fails to load?","How would you decide which components to lazy load?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big toy box with all your toys inside. Instead of carrying the whole box everywhere, you only take out the toy you want to play with right now. Code splitting and lazy loading do the same for websites: they only load the parts of the website you need at the moment, making it start faster.","detailedExplanation":"

What It Is

Code splitting is the technique of breaking your application's JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks that are loaded on demand. Lazy loading is the practice of deferring the loading of these chunks until they are actually needed. In React, this is achieved using React.lazy() and Suspense.

How It Works

You replace a static import with a dynamic import inside React.lazy():

const Dashboard = React.lazy(() => import('./Dashboard'));

Then wrap the lazy component in a Suspense boundary with a fallback prop (e.g., a loading spinner or skeleton). React will automatically load the chunk when the component is rendered.

Why It Matters in Production

Without code splitting, the entire application bundle is downloaded on the first visit, increasing First Contentful Paint (FCP) and Largest Contentful Paint (LCP). By splitting by routes or heavy components, you reduce the initial bundle size, leading to faster load times and better user experience, especially on slow networks.

Common Pitfall

Over-splitting can lead to too many small network requests, which may degrade performance due to request overhead. A good rule of thumb is to split at the route level or for components that are not immediately visible (e.g., modals, tabs). Also, always handle the error state when a lazy load fails (e.g., using an error boundary) to avoid a blank screen.

"}]
qgen_v2:7da9e8b0fbcef6c3:
[{"question":"How would you implement a virtualized list that renders 10,000 items efficiently, and what are the key considerations for ensuring smooth scrolling?","expectedPoints":["Only render items in the viewport plus a small overscan buffer","Reuse DOM nodes as the user scrolls (windowing)","Use a library like react-window, react-virtuoso, or @tanstack/virtual","Measure performance before optimizing; avoid premature optimization"],"followUps":["How would you handle variable item heights in a virtualized list?","What metrics would you use to verify the performance improvement?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge list of toys, but you can only see a few at a time through a small window. Instead of building all the toys, you only build the ones you can see right now. As you move the window, you build new toys and put away the old ones. This makes everything fast and uses less memory.","detailedExplanation":"

List virtualization (also called windowing) is a technique to efficiently render large lists by only creating DOM nodes for items that are currently visible in the viewport, plus a small overscan buffer to handle fast scrolling. This drastically reduces memory usage and improves rendering performance compared to rendering all items at once.

How It Works

A virtualized list calculates which items fall within the visible area based on the scroll position. It then renders only those items, reusing DOM nodes as the user scrolls. The container has a fixed height, and a spacer element maintains the correct scrollbar size. Libraries like react-window, react-virtuoso, and @tanstack/virtual provide ready-made components for this.

Why It Matters in Production

Rendering 10,000 DOM nodes simultaneously can cause jank, high memory consumption, and long load times. Virtualization keeps the DOM small (e.g., 10–15 nodes), ensuring smooth scrolling and a responsive UI. It is essential for feeds, tables, chat logs, and any large dataset displayed in a scrollable view.

Common Pitfall

Premature optimization: Developers often implement virtualization without measuring if it's needed. Always profile with Lighthouse, Performance tab, or React Profiler to identify real bottlenecks. Also, handling variable item heights requires extra logic (e.g., measuring each item or using a library that supports dynamic sizes).

"}]
qgen_v2:a82395595989dca7:
[{"question":"How does list virtualization (windowing) improve performance when rendering large lists, and what are its trade-offs?","expectedPoints":["Virtualization renders only visible items in the viewport, reducing DOM nodes.","It improves scroll performance and reduces memory usage.","Trade-offs include complexity of implementation and potential layout shifts if item heights are not fixed.","Requires careful handling of focus, keyboard navigation, and dynamic heights."],"followUps":["How would you handle items with variable heights in a virtualized list?","What are the differences between libraries like react-window and react-virtualized?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge list of toys, but you can only see a few at a time. Instead of building all the toys, you only build the ones you can see. When you scroll, you quickly swap them out. This makes the list fast and doesn't use too much memory.","detailedExplanation":"

List virtualization (also known as windowing) is a performance optimization technique for rendering large lists of data. Instead of rendering all items in the DOM, it renders only the items that are currently visible within the viewport, plus a small buffer. As the user scrolls, the component dynamically replaces the rendered items with the new ones that come into view.

How It Works

The virtualized list calculates the total height of the list based on item count and estimated item height. It then positions a container with that total height and renders only the subset of items that fall within the visible scroll range. When the user scrolls, the component recalculates which items should be visible and updates the DOM accordingly. Libraries like react-window and react-virtualized provide reusable components for this pattern.

Why It Matters in Production

Rendering thousands of DOM nodes causes significant performance issues: slow initial load, high memory consumption, and janky scrolling. Virtualization keeps the DOM small (e.g., ~10–20 nodes regardless of list size), leading to smooth scrolling, faster rendering, and better user experience. It is essential for applications like social media feeds, product catalogs, or logs.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming all items have the same height. If items have variable heights, the virtualized list may miscalculate scroll positions, causing items to jump or overlap. Solutions include measuring item heights dynamically or using a library that supports variable heights. Additionally, keyboard navigation and focus management can break if not handled properly, as the DOM nodes are recycled.

"}]
qgen_v2:3945065094957a9a:
[{"question":"How would you handle race conditions when fetching data in a React application, and what strategies do you use to ensure the latest response is used?","expectedPoints":["Identify race conditions: multiple async requests where order of responses may cause stale data.","Use cancellation: AbortController with fetch or axios cancel tokens.","Use state flags or unique request IDs to ignore outdated responses.","Leverage libraries like React Query that handle deduplication and stale-while-revalidate.","Consider debouncing or throttling for rapid user input."],"followUps":["How would you implement a custom hook to cancel previous requests when a new one is made?","What are the trade-offs of using AbortController vs. ignoring responses with a flag?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're asking a friend for the time, but you ask again before they answer. If you listen to the first answer, it might be old. To avoid that, you can tell your friend 'never mind' when you ask again, so you only hear the latest answer. In code, we cancel old requests or ignore their responses.","detailedExplanation":"

Race conditions in client-side networking occur when multiple asynchronous requests are sent, and the responses arrive in an unpredictable order. The classic scenario is a search input: the user types 'apple', then 'applesauce'. The request for 'apple' might return after 'applesauce', causing the UI to show stale results. This degrades user experience and can lead to data inconsistency.

How to Handle Race Conditions

1. Cancellation: Use the AbortController API with fetch or CancelToken with Axios. When a new request is made, abort the previous one. This ensures only the latest request completes.

2. Ignoring Stale Responses: Maintain a counter or unique ID per request. When a response arrives, compare its ID with the current request ID. If they don't match, discard the response.

3. Library Support: Libraries like React Query or SWR handle deduplication, caching, and stale-while-revalidate strategies out of the box, reducing manual effort.

Why It Matters in Production

In production, users interact with dynamic UIs that trigger frequent network calls. Unhandled race conditions cause flickering, incorrect data display, and even form submission errors. For example, a paginated list where the user clicks page 2 then page 1 quickly might show page 2's data on page 1 if the requests resolve out of order.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is to only cancel requests but not handle the AbortError gracefully. If you don't catch the error, it may propagate as an unhandled rejection. Always wrap fetch calls in try-catch and ignore aborted errors.

"}]

[{"question":"In a production frontend application with complex UI screens that display deeply nested relational data, would you choose REST or GraphQL? Justify your choice, including trade-offs in caching, versioning, and data fetching.","expectedPoints":["Recognizes GraphQL's advantage for complex, nested data with single endpoint and precise field selection.","Discusses REST's simpler HTTP caching vs GraphQL's need for normalized caching (e.g., Apollo).","Addresses versioning: REST uses URL versioning; GraphQL evolves schema with deprecation.","Mentions over/under-fetching: REST often over-fetches; GraphQL avoids it.","Considers team expertise and tooling maturity as practical factors."],"followUps":["How would you handle caching for a GraphQL endpoint in a high-traffic app?","What if the backend team is more experienced with REST? Would you still push for GraphQL?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're ordering a pizza. REST is like a menu with fixed combos—you might get extra toppings you don't want. GraphQL lets you build your own pizza with exactly the toppings you like. For a complex order with many customizations, GraphQL is better because you get only what you need, but it's harder to remember past orders (caching).","detailedExplanation":"

When choosing between REST and GraphQL for a production frontend with complex UI screens, the decision hinges on data fetching patterns, caching requirements, and team capabilities.

Data Fetching and Over/Under-fetching

REST exposes multiple fixed endpoints, often leading to over-fetching (retrieving unnecessary fields) or under-fetching (requiring additional requests). GraphQL allows the client to specify exactly the fields needed via a single endpoint, eliminating these issues. For deeply nested relational data, GraphQL reduces the number of round trips and payload size.

Caching

REST benefits from built-in HTTP caching based on URL and headers, which is simple and effective for public resources. GraphQL caching is more complex because all requests hit the same endpoint. Solutions like Apollo Client use normalized caching (storing objects by type and ID) to avoid redundant fetches, but this requires careful configuration and can be error-prone.

Versioning

REST commonly uses URL versioning (e.g., /v1/users), which can lead to code duplication and maintenance overhead. GraphQL evolves the schema by adding new fields and deprecating old ones, allowing backward-compatible changes without breaking existing clients. This is more flexible for rapid iteration.

Production Considerations

For a complex UI with many interdependent components, GraphQL's flexibility often outweighs its caching complexity. However, if the team lacks GraphQL experience or the API is simple and cache-heavy, REST may be more pragmatic. A common pitfall is underestimating the effort to implement a performant GraphQL layer, including resolver optimization and query cost analysis to prevent abusive queries.

[{"question":"For a social media feed where new posts are constantly added, which pagination strategy would you choose and why?","expectedPoints":["Recognizes cursor-based pagination as the best fit.","Explains that offset pagination can cause duplicate or missed items when new data is inserted.","Mentions that cursor-based pagination provides a stable anchor point.","Notes that infinite scroll often pairs with cursor-based pagination for feeds.","Acknowledges trade-off: cursor-based pagination does not support arbitrary page jumps."],"followUps":["How would you implement cursor-based pagination in a React component?","What are the downsides of using offset pagination for a real-time feed?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a long list of toys, and new toys keep being added at the top. If you use page numbers (offset), when you go to page 2, some toys might have moved or disappeared. Instead, you use a special bookmark (cursor) that marks exactly where you left off, so you always get the next toys correctly.","detailedExplanation":"

When building a social media feed where new posts are continuously added, the choice of pagination strategy is critical to avoid data inconsistency and poor user experience. The three common strategies are offset-based, cursor-based, and infinite scroll (which often uses cursor-based underneath).

Why cursor-based pagination is preferred

Cursor-based pagination uses a unique identifier (cursor) from the last item in the current page to fetch the next set. This ensures that even if new items are inserted at the top, the cursor remains stable and points to the exact position where the user left off. In contrast, offset-based pagination (e.g., ?page=2&limit=20) calculates the starting point based on a fixed number of items per page. When new items are added, the offset shifts, causing the same items to appear on different pages or some items to be skipped entirely.

How it works in production

In a React application, you would typically store the cursor from the API response and pass it as a parameter in the next request. For infinite scroll, you can use the IntersectionObserver API to detect when the user scrolls near the bottom and trigger a fetch with the cursor. This pattern is common in feeds like Twitter or Instagram.

Common pitfall

A frequent mistake is using offset pagination for real-time feeds without considering data mutations. This leads to duplicate or missing items when users navigate pages. Always evaluate the data volatility: if items are frequently added or removed, cursor-based pagination is more reliable.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you handle race conditions in an autocomplete search component when the user types quickly?","expectedPoints":["Recognizes the race condition: later responses overwrite earlier ones.","Mentions AbortController to cancel in-flight requests.","Suggests debouncing to reduce request frequency.","Considers checking if the response corresponds to the latest query before updating state.","Discusses potential pitfalls like abort errors or stale closures."],"followUps":["How would you handle the case where the user clears the input?","What if the API doesn't support cancellation? How would you handle it client-side?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're asking a friend for directions, but you keep changing your mind. If you ask for 'store' then 'supermarket', your friend might answer for 'store' after you already asked for 'supermarket'. To fix this, you can tell your friend to ignore the first question when you ask a new one. In code, we use a special tool called AbortController to cancel old requests, and we also wait a tiny bit before asking to avoid too many questions.","detailedExplanation":"

Race conditions in autocomplete occur when the user types quickly, causing multiple network requests to be sent. The response for an earlier query may arrive after a later one, overwriting the correct result. This is a classic stale closure problem.

How It Works

The solution involves three key techniques:

  • AbortController: Create a new AbortController for each request. Before sending a new request, call controller.abort() to cancel the previous one. Pass the signal to fetch so it can be aborted.
  • Debouncing: Use a debounce timer (e.g., 300ms) to delay the request until the user stops typing. This reduces the number of requests and minimizes race conditions.
  • Latest query check: After receiving a response, compare the query that was sent with the current input value. If they don't match, discard the response.

Why It Matters in Production

Without handling race conditions, users will see incorrect suggestions, leading to a poor experience. In production, network latency varies, and users type at different speeds. Proper handling ensures that the UI always reflects the most recent user intent, improving reliability and trust.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is forgetting to handle the AbortError thrown when a request is aborted. This can cause unhandled promise rejections. Always catch the error and check if it's an abort error before ignoring it. Also, ensure that the debounce timer is properly cleared on component unmount to avoid memory leaks.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a resilient autocomplete component that handles network failures and race conditions?","expectedPoints":["Debounce input to reduce request frequency","Use AbortController to cancel outdated requests","Implement retry with exponential backoff for failed requests","Set a timeout for each request to avoid hanging","Graceful degradation: show cached results or error message"],"followUps":["How would you ensure idempotency if the autocomplete uses a POST endpoint?","What if the user types very fast? How would you prioritize the latest query?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're typing a search term and the website suggests words. If you type fast, many requests go out. You want to show suggestions for the latest word you typed, not an old one. So you wait a tiny bit after you stop typing (debounce), cancel old requests (AbortController), and if a request fails, you try again but wait longer each time (exponential backoff) so the server doesn't get overwhelmed.","detailedExplanation":"

Resilience in frontend systems ensures the application can recover from errors gracefully. For an autocomplete component, the main challenges are race conditions (outdated responses overwriting newer ones) and network failures (timeouts, server errors).

Debouncing

Use a debounce mechanism (e.g., 300ms) to delay sending a request until the user stops typing. This reduces the number of requests and prevents flooding the server with every keystroke.

AbortController for Race Conditions

When a new request is sent, cancel any pending previous request using AbortController. This ensures that only the response for the latest query is processed, preventing stale data from overwriting fresh results.

Retry with Exponential Backoff

If a request fails (e.g., network error, 5xx), retry with increasing delays: 1s, 2s, 4s, etc. This avoids hammering the server and gives it time to recover. Set a maximum retry count (e.g., 3) to avoid infinite loops.

Timeout

Set a timeout (e.g., 5s) for each request using AbortSignal.timeout() or a custom timer. If the request takes too long, abort it and treat it as a failure, triggering retry logic or graceful degradation.

Graceful Degradation

When all retries fail, show a cached result (if available) or a user-friendly error message like “Suggestions unavailable” instead of a blank screen or spinner. This maintains a good user experience.

Common Pitfall: Forgetting to handle race conditions properly. Without canceling outdated requests, a slow response from an earlier query can overwrite the correct suggestions for a later query, causing confusion.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you decide between using local component state, Context API, and a global state management library like Redux for a new feature?","expectedPoints":["Classifies state by scope: local UI state vs. shared state vs. global app state.","Explains trade-offs: simplicity vs. scalability, re-render performance, debugging.","Mentions specific criteria: frequency of updates, number of consumers, team size.","Gives a concrete example of when to choose each approach.","Acknowledges that over-engineering with global state is a common pitfall."],"followUps":["What would you do if a deeply nested component needs to update state that affects many siblings?","How do you handle stale closures when using hooks for state management?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box.
