[{"question":"Why does adding a property to an object after construction degrade performance in V8, and how would you avoid it?","expectedPoints":["Adding properties after construction changes the hidden class (shape).","Different shapes cause V8 to deoptimize inline caches.","Solution: initialize all properties in constructor or use Object.assign to set them at once.","Avoid delete as it forces a new hidden class.","Use monomorphic code paths for consistent shapes."],"followUps":["What happens if you delete a property from an object?","How does using a Map instead of an object affect hidden classes?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box with special slots for each toy. If you add a new toy after the box is made, you have to change the whole box, which takes time. It's better to decide all toys upfront so the box stays the same and you can grab toys quickly.","detailedExplanation":"

Hidden classes (also called shapes) are internal metadata that V8 uses to track the structure of JavaScript objects. When you create an object, V8 assigns it a hidden class based on the order and types of its properties. Objects with the same property order and types share the same hidden class, enabling inline caching (IC) — a technique where V8 caches the memory offset of a property for fast access.

Why adding properties after construction hurts performance

When you add a new property to an object after it has been created, V8 must create a new hidden class that includes the new property. This transition invalidates any existing inline caches that assumed the old shape. Subsequent property accesses on that object (or other objects that shared the old shape) become slower because V8 has to fall back to a slower lookup path (megamorphic or dictionary mode).

How to avoid it

  • Initialize all properties in the constructor — assign every property you will ever use, even if it's undefined or null.
  • Use Object.assign to set multiple properties at once, which can create a single hidden class transition.
  • Avoid delete — deleting a property also forces a new hidden class, and can even degrade the object to dictionary mode (slow property lookup).
  • Prefer Map for dynamic key-value pairs, as Map does not use hidden classes and is optimized for frequent additions/deletions.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is to create objects with a constructor but then conditionally add properties later. For example:

function User(name) { this.name = name; }
const u = new User('Alice');
if (someCondition) { u.age = 30; } // bad: changes shape

This causes u to have a different hidden class than other User objects without age, breaking monomorphic inline caches. Always define all possible properties in the constructor, even if they start as undefined.

"}]
qgen_v2:7f81bd3ef2705c07:
[{"question":"How does generational garbage collection improve performance in JavaScript engines, and what are common memory leak patterns in frontend applications?","expectedPoints":["Explains the generational hypothesis: most objects die young.","Describes young vs old generation collection strategies (scavenge vs mark-sweep-compact).","Identifies common frontend memory leaks: unremoved listeners, uncleared intervals, closures, detached DOM nodes, missing cleanup in React useEffect.","Mentions using DevTools Memory panel for heap snapshots to detect leaks.","Avoids premature optimization; emphasizes writing clear code and profiling first."],"followUps":["How would you use Chrome DevTools to find a detached DOM node leak?","What is a hidden class and how does object shape affect performance?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine a library where most books are borrowed and returned quickly (young generation), so we clean that shelf often. Old books that stay long (old generation) are cleaned less often. In apps, forgetting to return borrowed items (like event listeners) causes clutter that slows things down. We use special tools to find that clutter.","detailedExplanation":"

Generational garbage collection is a memory management strategy used by JavaScript engines (like V8) based on the observation that most objects die young. The heap is divided into generations: Young Generation (new objects) and Old Generation (objects that survive multiple GC cycles).

How it works

Young generation collection uses a fast scavenge algorithm (copying live objects to a survivor space). Objects that survive several scavenges are promoted to the old generation. Old generation collection uses mark-sweep-compact, which is slower but runs less frequently. This design reduces pause times because most collections are quick scavenges.

Why it matters in production

Efficient GC prevents jank and memory bloat. However, memory leaks can still occur if references are unintentionally retained. Common frontend leaks include:

  • Event listeners not removed (addEventListener without removeEventListener)
  • Intervals or timeouts not cleared (setInterval / setTimeout)
  • Closures holding large objects
  • Detached DOM nodes still referenced by JavaScript
  • React useEffect missing cleanup functions

Common pitfall

Prematurely optimizing code without profiling. Engines are highly optimized; writing clear, consistent code (e.g., initializing object properties in the same order to share hidden classes) is usually sufficient. Use Chrome DevTools Memory panel to take heap snapshots and identify leaks before optimizing.

"}]
qgen_v2:e0f27c393fbc0c17:
[{"question":"Explain how HTTP caching works in the browser and how you would configure cache headers for a static asset that changes rarely but must be immediately updated when it does.","expectedPoints":["Describes browser cache check (Cache-Control, Expires) and validation (ETag, Last-Modified).","Proposes a strategy like long max-age with versioned URLs (e.g., content hash in filename).","Mentions cache busting via query string or file rename for immediate updates.","Distinguishes between public, private, no-cache, no-store directives.","Explains how the browser uses cached responses to avoid network requests."],"followUps":["What happens if you set Cache-Control: no-cache but omit ETag?","How would you debug a caching issue where the browser is serving a stale asset?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a favorite toy that you keep in a special box. The first time you play with it, you take it out. But if you want to play again later, you can just grab it from the box instead of asking your mom to buy a new one. HTTP caching is like that box: it saves web files so your computer doesn't have to download them again. But if the toy changes (like a new paint job), you need a way to know it's different—so you might put a sticker on the box that says 'new version' to force getting the fresh toy.","detailedExplanation":"

HTTP caching is a mechanism that stores copies of web resources (HTML, CSS, JS, images) either in the browser (client-side) or on intermediate proxies. It reduces latency, bandwidth, and server load by serving previously fetched content without a full round trip.

How It Works

The server controls caching via response headers. The most common are:

  • Cache-Control: Directives like max-age=31536000 (seconds), public (any cache), private (only browser), no-cache (must revalidate), no-store (never cache).
  • Expires: An older HTTP/1.0 header with an absolute date (superseded by Cache-Control).
  • ETag: A unique identifier (e.g., file hash) for the resource version. The browser sends If-None-Match; server replies 304 Not Modified if unchanged.
  • Last-Modified: A timestamp; browser sends If-Modified-Since for validation.

Production Strategy for Rarely-Changing Assets

For static assets (e.g., app.a1b2c3.js), use a long max-age (e.g., one year) combined with content hashing in the filename. When the file changes, the hash changes, forcing the browser to request the new URL. This avoids cache invalidation complexity. For immediate updates (e.g., hotfix), you can also use a query string like ?v=2, but hashing is more reliable.

Common Pitfall

Setting Cache-Control: no-cache without an ETag or Last-Modified forces the browser to revalidate every time, but if the server doesn't support conditional requests, it still returns the full response, negating caching benefits. Always pair no-cache with a validator.

"}]
qgen_v2:efa2a28f75dfd500:
[{"question":"Explain the difference between Cache-Control: no-cache and Cache-Control: no-store, and describe a scenario where each would be appropriate.","expectedPoints":["no-cache means must revalidate with server before use, not 'no caching'.","no-store means absolutely no caching anywhere.","no-cache suitable for HTML files that change frequently but need fresh content.","no-store suitable for sensitive data like banking details or personal info.","Mention that no-cache still allows caching with revalidation, while no-store forbids any storage."],"followUps":["How does the ETag header interact with no-cache to enable efficient revalidation?","What happens if you accidentally set no-store on a static asset like a JavaScript file?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a library book. 'no-cache' means you can borrow it, but you must check with the librarian each time before you take it home to make sure it's the latest version. 'no-store' means you are not allowed to borrow the book at all; you must read it only in the library and never take it out. For websites, 'no-cache' is used for pages that change often, like news articles, while 'no-store' is used for private data like your bank balance.","detailedExplanation":"

Cache-Control: no-cache and Cache-Control: no-store are two distinct directives that are often misunderstood. Despite the name, no-cache does not mean the response is never cached. Instead, it instructs the browser (or any intermediate cache) that before using a cached copy, it must revalidate with the origin server to ensure the content is still fresh. This is typically done via conditional requests using headers like If-None-Match (with ETag) or If-Modified-Since (with Last-Modified). If the server responds with 304 Not Modified, the cached copy can be used, saving bandwidth. This directive is ideal for resources that change frequently but where serving a slightly stale version is unacceptable, such as an index.html file in a single-page application.

On the other hand, no-store is a strict directive that forbids any caching of the response. The browser must not store the response in any cache, whether on disk or in memory. This is critical for sensitive data like personal information, authentication tokens, or financial records, where even a stale cached copy could pose a security risk. For example, a bank's account balance endpoint should use no-store to prevent any accidental exposure.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is using no-cache when no-store is intended, or vice versa. Using no-store on a static asset like a JavaScript file would defeat the purpose of caching and increase server load and latency. Conversely, using no-cache on sensitive data might still allow caching in intermediate proxies if not combined with private directive, leading to potential data leaks. Always choose based on the resource's sensitivity and update frequency.

"}]
qgen_v2:7ba0c75374495750:
[{"question":"A frontend developer sees a CORS error in the console when making a PUT request to an external API. They try to fix it by adding `mode: 'no-cors'` to the fetch options. Why won't this work, and what is the correct fix?","expectedPoints":["CORS is enforced by the browser, not the server; client cannot bypass it.","'no-cors' mode restricts response to opaque (unreadable) and only works for simple requests.","PUT is a non-simple request, requiring a preflight OPTIONS request.","Correct fix: server must respond with appropriate Access-Control-Allow-Origin and related headers.","Alternatively, use a proxy to make the request same-origin."],"followUps":["What would happen if the server receives the PUT request but doesn't include CORS headers?","How does a preflight request differ from a simple request?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're at school and want to borrow a book from another classroom. The teacher (browser) checks with the other classroom's teacher (server) first: 'Can this student borrow a book?' If the other teacher says no, your teacher stops you. You can't just pretend the other teacher said yes. The only fix is for the other teacher to allow it. That's CORS.","detailedExplanation":"

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security mechanism that restricts web pages from making requests to a different origin than the one that served the page. The error occurs when the server does not include the required CORS headers in its response.

Why mode: 'no-cors' doesn't fix it

Setting mode: 'no-cors' in a fetch request tells the browser to make the request without CORS checks, but it severely limits the response: the response becomes an opaque response that JavaScript cannot read (status, headers, body are all inaccessible). Moreover, no-cors only works for simple requests (GET, HEAD, POST with certain content types). A PUT request is a non-simple request that triggers a preflight OPTIONS request regardless of the mode. The browser will still block the actual request if the preflight fails.

Correct fix

CORS errors must be resolved on the server side. The server must include the appropriate Access-Control-Allow-Origin header (e.g., Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://yourdomain.com or * for public APIs). For non-simple requests, the server must also respond to the preflight OPTIONS request with headers like Access-Control-Allow-Methods and Access-Control-Allow-Headers. Alternatively, you can set up a proxy server that runs on the same origin as your frontend, forwarding requests to the external API, thus avoiding cross-origin issues entirely.

Common pitfall

Many developers mistakenly think CORS is a client-side issue and try to disable it in the browser or use browser extensions. CORS is a browser-enforced policy; the client cannot override it. The request still reaches the server, but the browser blocks the JavaScript from reading the response. Always fix CORS at the server or use a proxy.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a component library to ensure consistency across multiple teams while allowing for product-specific customization?","expectedPoints":["Define a clear API for components with props for customization.","Use a design token system for theming (colors, spacing, typography).","Implement versioning and semantic versioning for the library.","Provide documentation and Storybook for discoverability and usage.","Establish a governance process for adding or modifying components."],"followUps":["How would you handle a team that needs a variant not supported by the current library?","What strategies would you use to encourage adoption and contribution from other teams?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big box of LEGO bricks that everyone in your class uses to build houses. To make sure all houses look similar but can still be unique, you decide on a few basic brick types and colors. You also write a rulebook so everyone knows how to use them. If someone wants a special brick, they ask the class leader to add it to the box. That's like a design system for websites.","detailedExplanation":"

A component library is a collection of reusable UI components that enforce consistency across an organization. To design one for multiple teams, start by defining a clear API for each component. Use props to allow customization (e.g., variant, size) while keeping the core behavior fixed. This prevents teams from forking the library.

Design Tokens

Implement a design token system that abstracts visual properties like colors, spacing, and typography. Tokens (e.g., --color-primary) are defined in a central theme file and consumed by components. This allows teams to create brand-specific themes without changing component code.

Versioning and Distribution

Publish the library as an npm package with semantic versioning. Use a monorepo (e.g., Nx, Lerna) to manage multiple packages (core, theme, utilities). Provide a changelog and migration guides to handle breaking changes smoothly.

Documentation and Tooling

Use Storybook to document each component with live examples, prop tables, and usage guidelines. This serves as the single source of truth and reduces miscommunication between designers and developers.

Governance

Establish a design system committee with representatives from design and engineering. Define a process for proposing, reviewing, and approving new components or variants. This prevents bloat and maintains quality.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is making components too rigid or too flexible. Over-customization leads to inconsistency, while under-customization frustrates teams. Strike a balance by providing sensible defaults and allowing overrides via a sx or style prop for edge cases.

"}]
qgen_v2:8576773c024c7fbe:
[{"question":"How would you structure a design system for a large-scale enterprise application, and what are the key considerations for each layer?","expectedPoints":["Identifies layers: design tokens, primitives, patterns, and product pages.","Explains purpose of each layer (e.g., tokens for consistency, primitives for reusability).","Discusses separation of concerns: tokens hold values, primitives are stateless, patterns compose logic.","Mentions tooling (e.g., Figma for design, Storybook for development) and versioning.","Addresses scalability: how to add new components without breaking existing ones."],"followUps":["How do you handle cross-cutting concerns like theming or accessibility across layers?","What trade-offs exist between using a design system vs. a component library like Material-UI?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine building with LEGO. Atoms are the smallest bricks (colors, fonts). Molecules are simple pieces like a wheel. Organisms are complex builds like a car. Templates are blueprints for a whole set, and Pages are the final playsets. A design system organizes these layers so you can reuse pieces easily and build new things faster without starting from scratch.","detailedExplanation":"

A design system is a collection of reusable components guided by clear standards. For a large-scale enterprise app, it's typically structured in layers to promote consistency and efficiency.

Layers of a Design System

  • Design Tokens: The foundational atoms—colors, spacing, typography, radii. Stored as variables (e.g., --color-primary: #0052cc). They ensure visual consistency across platforms.
  • Primitives / Base Components: Stateless UI elements like Button, Input, Text. They consume tokens but contain no business logic. Highly reusable.
  • Patterns / Composite Components: Complex components like Form, Modal, DataTable. They compose primitives and may include state management or validation logic.
  • Product Pages: The final pages built from patterns and primitives, tailored to specific features.

Key Considerations

  • Separation of Concerns: Tokens hold values, primitives handle styling, patterns manage logic. This makes updates safe and predictable.
  • Tooling: Use Storybook for component documentation, Figma for design tokens, and version control (e.g., semantic versioning) to manage changes.
  • Scalability: Define clear naming conventions and a governance process for adding new components. Avoid duplication by auditing existing components.
  • Accessibility: Enforce ARIA roles, keyboard navigation, and contrast ratios at the primitive layer so all composites inherit them.

Common Pitfall

Mixing business logic into primitives. For example, a Button should not know about API calls. Keep primitives pure; add logic only in patterns or pages. This prevents tight coupling and makes the system easier to maintain.

"}]
qgen_v2:98fa37d113fee496:
[{"question":"Explain the difference between semantic tokens and primitive tokens in a design token system, and why this separation is important for theming.","expectedPoints":["Primitive tokens are raw values (e.g., --blue-500: #3b82f6).","Semantic tokens describe purpose (e.g., --color-primary: var(--blue-500)).","Separation allows theme changes by reassigning semantic tokens without touching components.","Improves maintainability and scalability across multiple themes or brands."],"followUps":["How would you implement a dark mode using this token architecture?","What are the trade-offs of using CSS custom properties vs. a preprocessor for tokens?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a box of crayons. Primitive tokens are the actual crayon colors, like 'blue' or 'red'. Semantic tokens are like labels saying 'sky' or 'fire'. If you want to change the sky from blue to orange, you just relabel 'sky' to point to the orange crayon, instead of recoloring every picture. That's what semantic tokens do for websites.","detailedExplanation":"

Design tokens are the atoms of a design system — named entities that store visual design attributes such as colors, spacing, typography, etc. They are typically divided into two layers: primitive tokens and semantic tokens.

Primitive Tokens

These are the raw, foundational values. They represent the actual color hex codes, font sizes, or spacing units. For example:

  • --blue-500: #3b82f6
  • --red-500: #ef4444
  • --spacing-md: 16px

Primitives are often generated from a design palette and are not tied to any specific UI purpose.

Semantic Tokens

These tokens describe the intent or role of a design value within the UI. They reference primitive tokens. For example:

  • --color-primary: var(--blue-500)
  • --color-danger: var(--red-500)
  • --spacing-card-padding: var(--spacing-md)

Why the Separation Matters

The key benefit is theming flexibility. Components only ever use semantic tokens. To change a theme (e.g., from light to dark, or from brand A to brand B), you only need to reassign the semantic tokens to different primitives. For instance, in a dark theme, --color-primary might point to --blue-300 instead of --blue-500. No component code needs to change. This separation also enforces consistency across the UI and makes it easy to audit which design decisions are being used.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is to skip the semantic layer and let components reference primitives directly. This leads to hardcoded values scattered across the codebase, making theme changes a nightmare — you'd have to update every component individually. Always introduce a semantic abstraction to keep the system maintainable.

"}]

[{"question":"How do you decide what to test in a frontend application, and at which levels (unit, integration, e2e) do you focus your efforts?","expectedPoints":["Prioritize testing business logic and user flows over implementation details.","Use the testing trophy or pyramid: unit tests for logic, integration for components, e2e for critical paths.","Focus on high-risk areas: complex state management, async operations, third-party integrations.","Balance coverage with maintenance cost; avoid testing trivial or frequently changing UI.","Ensure tests give confidence to refactor and deploy without fear of regression."],"followUps":["How do you handle testing for components that rely on external APIs or browser APIs?","What would you do if your team has low test coverage but a tight deadline?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box. You don't need to check every single toy every day—just the ones that might break or are important. Testing is like that: you test the parts of your app that could cause big problems if they break, so you can change things later without fear.","detailedExplanation":"

Testing strategy in frontend development is about maximizing confidence while minimizing maintenance overhead. The goal is not 100% coverage but ensuring that the most critical parts of the application are reliable.

What to Test

Focus on business logic and user-facing behavior. Test the parts that are most likely to break or cause regressions: complex state management, async data flows, authentication, form validation, and integration with third-party services. Avoid testing trivial UI details (e.g., exact CSS values) or implementation internals that change frequently.

Testing Levels

The testing trophy (or pyramid) suggests:

  • Unit tests for pure functions, utilities, and isolated logic. Fast and cheap to run.
  • Integration tests for components interacting with state, APIs, or other components. This is where most bugs live.
  • End-to-end (e2e) tests for critical user journeys (e.g., login, checkout). Slow but high confidence.

Why It Matters in Production

A good testing strategy enables fearless refactoring and continuous deployment. Without it, teams become hesitant to change code, leading to technical debt. With it, you can ship features faster and with fewer regressions.

Common Pitfall

Over-testing implementation details (e.g., testing internal state or method calls) leads to brittle tests that break on every refactor, reducing trust in the test suite. Instead, test the observable behavior (what the user sees or does).

"}]
qgen_v2:95a090b738a17665:
[{"question":"How would you decide the distribution of tests across unit, integration, and E2E levels in a frontend project?","expectedPoints":["Understands the testing pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, fewest E2E tests.","Recognizes trade-offs: unit tests are fast/cheap but low confidence; E2E are slow/expensive but high confidence.","Mentions the 'trophy' variant (Kent C. Dodds) emphasizing integration tests as the sweet spot.","Applies to frontend: unit test pure logic/hooks, integration test component interactions, E2E test critical user flows.","Considers project context: team size, release frequency, risk tolerance."],"followUps":["When would you deviate from the pyramid and write more E2E tests?","How do you decide what to test at each level without overlap?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a LEGO castle. Unit tests check each brick is strong. Integration tests check if two bricks fit together well. E2E tests build the whole castle and see if it stands. You want many brick checks, some fit checks, and only a few full castle tests because they take a long time.","detailedExplanation":"

The testing pyramid is a strategy for distributing tests across different levels to balance speed, cost, and confidence. The classic pyramid has three layers: unit tests (many, fast, cheap), integration tests (fewer, slower, more expensive), and E2E tests (few, slow, expensive). The 'trophy' variant proposed by Kent C. Dodds shifts emphasis to integration tests as the 'sweet spot' because they test how components work together without the brittleness of E2E tests.

How it works in frontend

  • Unit tests: Test pure functions, custom hooks, or utility functions in isolation. They are fast and help catch logic errors early.
  • Integration tests: Test interactions between multiple components, such as a form with validation and submission. They provide high confidence for a moderate cost.
  • E2E tests: Simulate real user flows (e.g., login → search → checkout) using tools like Cypress or Playwright. They are slow and flaky but catch integration issues across the full stack.

Why it matters in production

Without a pyramid, teams often over-invest in E2E tests, leading to slow CI pipelines and brittle suites. The pyramid ensures you get fast feedback from unit tests while still covering critical paths with E2E. The trophy variant is especially useful for frontend because UI interactions are best tested at the integration level.

Common pitfall

Writing too many shallow unit tests that test implementation details (e.g., internal state changes) rather than behavior. These tests break on refactoring and provide low confidence. Instead, prefer integration tests that test the component from a user perspective.

"}]
qgen_v2:e802f1c44393099a:
[{"question":"Why does React Testing Library recommend querying elements by role/label/text instead of by class or internal state?","expectedPoints":["Tests should mimic user interactions, not implementation details.","Queries by role/label/text are more resilient to refactoring.","Avoids testing internal state or CSS class names that change frequently.","Helps catch real bugs that affect user experience.","Reduces test brittleness and maintenance overhead."],"followUps":["Can you give an example where querying by class would cause a test to break unnecessarily?","How would you test a button that changes text after a click using RTL best practices?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're testing a toy vending machine. Instead of checking if the internal gears turn (which you can't see), you check if the candy actually comes out when you press the button. React Testing Library says: test what the user sees and does, not the hidden machinery inside.","detailedExplanation":"

React Testing Library (RTL) advocates for testing components in a way that closely resembles how users interact with them. The core philosophy is: “The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they can give you.”

What It Is

RTL encourages querying elements by accessible roles, labels, or visible text rather than by implementation details like CSS classes, internal state, or DOM structure. For example, use getByRole('button', { name: /submit/i }) instead of document.querySelector('.btn-primary').

How It Works

RTL provides queries like getByRole, getByLabelText, getByText, and getByPlaceholderText. These queries rely on the accessibility tree, which browsers use to assist users with disabilities. By using these, your tests become more robust because they don't depend on internal implementation that might change during refactoring.

Why It Matters in Production

  • Resilience to refactoring: Changing CSS classes or internal state names won't break tests.
  • Better bug detection: Tests catch issues that affect actual user experience (e.g., missing labels, incorrect roles).
  • Accessibility by default: Encourages writing accessible components from the start.
  • Reduced flakiness: Avoids brittle selectors that break due to minor DOM changes.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is falling back to testing implementation details, such as checking component state directly (component.state()) or using data-testid excessively. While data-testid is a valid escape hatch, overusing it defeats the purpose of user-centric testing. Always prefer queries that reflect what the user perceives.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a monitoring and observability system for a large-scale single-page application to detect real user issues after deployment?","expectedPoints":["Distinguishes between synthetic monitoring and Real User Monitoring (RUM).","Covers key metrics: Core Web Vitals, error rates, API latency, and user session replays.","Describes a practical implementation: using Performance API, error boundaries, and beacon API for data collection.","Explains how to aggregate and visualize data (e.g., dashboards, alerts) and tie it to deployment pipelines.","Addresses privacy and performance overhead trade-offs."],"followUps":["How would you prioritize which metrics to monitor first in a startup vs. a mature product?","What would you do if your monitoring system itself causes performance degradation?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you built a lemonade stand. After you open, you need to know if customers are happy or if your stand is too slow. Monitoring is like having a friend secretly watch and tell you: 'Some cups are leaking!' or 'People are waiting too long.' This helps you fix problems before everyone leaves.","detailedExplanation":"

Monitoring & Observability is the practice of collecting, analyzing, and acting on data from your application in production to understand its health and user experience. For a senior frontend engineer, this goes beyond just checking if the server is up—it means instrumenting the client-side code to capture real user interactions.

Key Components

  • Real User Monitoring (RUM): Collects performance data from actual users via the Performance API (e.g., navigationTiming, paintTiming) and Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS).
  • Error Tracking: Use window.onerror and unhandledrejection to capture JavaScript errors, often sent to a service like Sentry.
  • User Session Replays: Tools like LogRocket record user interactions to replay and debug issues.
  • Data Transmission: Use the Beacon API (navigator.sendBeacon) to send data reliably even when the page is unloading, avoiding loss of critical metrics.

Why It Matters in Production

Without observability, you are blind to real-world issues like slow API calls on a specific mobile network, or a bug that only appears after a new deployment. It enables proactive detection and blameless post-mortems.

Common Pitfall

Over-instrumentation: collecting too many metrics can degrade performance and overwhelm the data pipeline. Always measure the overhead of your monitoring code (e.g., using performance.mark and performance.measure to profile the profiler).

"}]
qgen_v2:f0e096aea07a5ec3:
[{"question":"How would you set up monitoring for a production React app to catch runtime errors, measure real user performance, and track user behavior?","expectedPoints":["Mentions error tracking tools like Sentry or Rollbar for JS errors and promise rejections.","Explains RUM to measure Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) from real users, not lab tests.","Describes analytics/funnel tracking to identify drop-off points and connect performance to business metrics.","Discusses grouping errors with stack traces and breadcrumbs for debugging.","Highlights the importance of segmenting RUM data by device, network, and region."],"followUps":["How would you prioritize fixing a performance issue that affects only a small percentage of users?","What would you do if error tracking shows a spike but no clear stack trace?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're a detective watching a busy playground. You want to know if any kids fall (errors), how fast they can run across the field (performance), and which slides they use most (behavior). You set up cameras (tools) to catch falls, measure their speed from real kids (not just a test), and count how many use each slide. This helps you fix problems and make the playground better.","detailedExplanation":"

In production, monitoring is critical to ensure reliability, performance, and business success. The three pillars—error tracking, Real User Monitoring (RUM), and analytics/funnel—cover different aspects.

Error Tracking

Tools like Sentry or Rollbar capture JavaScript runtime errors, unhandled promise rejections, and API failures. They group similar errors, provide stack traces and breadcrumbs (user actions leading to the error), and alert teams. This helps developers reproduce and fix bugs quickly.

Real User Monitoring (RUM)

RUM measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) from actual users, unlike lab tests (e.g., Lighthouse). It segments data by device, network, and region to identify performance bottlenecks affecting real users. For example, slow LCP on mobile 3G might indicate a need for image optimization or code splitting.

Analytics & Funnel

Analytics tools track user behavior—page views, clicks, feature usage—and build funnels to see where users drop off (e.g., sign-up flow). Connecting performance metrics to business metrics (e.g., conversion rate) helps prioritize fixes that impact revenue.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is relying solely on lab tests or ignoring error grouping, leading to alert fatigue. Always combine RUM with error tracking and analytics to get a complete picture. For instance, a high bounce rate might be due to slow loading (RUM) or a broken button (error tracking).

"}]

[{"question":"How would you set up a performance budget for a large React application, and how do you reconcile differences between lab data (Lighthouse) and field data (CrUX) when the budget is violated?","expectedPoints":["Define budgets for resource sizes (scripts, images) and metrics (LCP, TTI).","Use tools like webpack-bundle-analyzer and Lighthouse CI to enforce budgets in CI/CD.","Explain that lab data is stable for debugging but field data reflects real user conditions and is used by Google for SEO.","Prioritize field data for decision-making but use lab data to identify root causes of regressions.","Handle budget violations by alerting, blocking builds, or triggering performance reviews."],"followUps":["What would you do if field data shows a metric is fine but lab data shows a violation?","How would you handle source maps in production to avoid exposing source code while still debugging errors?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a backpack for a trip. You set a weight limit so you don't bring too much stuff. A performance budget is like that weight limit for your website. Lab data is like testing your backpack at home on a scale, while field data is like actually carrying it on the trip with different weather and paths. Google uses the real trip data to decide if your site is fast enough for search results.","detailedExplanation":"

A performance budget is a set of limits on metrics like bundle size, number of requests, or Core Web Vitals (e.g., LCP, FID). It prevents scope creep by alerting or blocking builds that exceed thresholds. For a React app, you can use tools like webpack-bundle-analyzer to track bundle sizes and Lighthouse CI to measure lab metrics in CI/CD.

Lab vs Field Data

Lab data (Lighthouse) is collected in a controlled environment, making it great for debugging regressions. Field data (CrUX/RUM) comes from real users and varies by device, network, and location. Google uses field data for search ranking, so it's the ultimate truth. However, lab data helps you isolate issues because it's reproducible.

Reconciling Differences

When budgets are violated, first check if the violation is consistent across both data sources. If only lab data shows a problem, it might be a test environment issue (e.g., throttling settings). If only field data shows a problem, it could be a real user bottleneck (e.g., slow third-party scripts). Always prioritize field data for business decisions but use lab data to debug.

Source Maps in Production

Upload source maps privately to error monitoring services like Sentry, but never deploy them publicly. Public source maps expose your original code, creating security risks. Private source maps let you read minified stack traces without leaking source.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on lab data and ignoring field data. A site might score 100 on Lighthouse but perform poorly on slow 3G networks. Always validate budgets with real user metrics.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a deployment pipeline for a large-scale frontend application that supports feature flags and rollback?","expectedPoints":["Explain the role of bundlers (e.g., Webpack, Vite) in optimizing build output.","Describe how feature flags can be integrated (e.g., environment variables, runtime config).","Outline a rollback strategy (e.g., versioned deployments, canary releases).","Mention CI/CD integration and automated testing gates.","Address caching and CDN invalidation for fast rollback."],"followUps":["How would you handle feature flag evaluation at runtime without exposing logic?","What metrics would you monitor during a canary release?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you build a toy car. The bundler is like packing the car into a smaller box so it ships faster. Feature flags are like secret switches that turn on new parts only when you want. Rollback is like having a backup box ready if the new car breaks. This question asks how you'd set up the whole factory to pack, test, and safely deliver new toy cars.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your understanding of the entire frontend delivery pipeline, from code to production. A senior engineer must consider build optimization, safe rollout, and recovery.

Build & Bundling

The bundler (e.g., Webpack, Vite) transforms source code into optimized bundles. Key tasks: tree-shaking to remove dead code, code splitting to lazy-load chunks, and minification to reduce size. Use content hashing in filenames for long-term caching.

Feature Flags

Integrate feature flags via environment variables at build time or runtime configuration. For runtime flags, inject a JSON config file (e.g., /config.js) that the app fetches on load. This allows toggling features without redeploying. Ensure flags are evaluated securely on the server side if sensitive.

Deployment & Rollback

Use a CI/CD pipeline (e.g., GitHub Actions, Jenkins) with stages: lint, test, build, deploy. Deploy to a staging environment first, then use canary releases to gradually shift traffic. For rollback, keep previous builds in a CDN (e.g., S3 + CloudFront) and switch the index.html reference to the old bundle. Use feature flags as a safety net: if a flag causes errors, disable it instantly without redeploying.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to invalidate CDN cache after rollback. Always version your assets and use cache-busting hashes. Also, avoid mixing build-time and runtime flags without clear separation—this can lead to inconsistent behavior.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you implement code splitting in a React application to improve initial load time, and what are the trade-offs?","expectedPoints":["Uses React.lazy and Suspense for component-level splitting.","Dynamic import() syntax for route-based splitting.","Trade-off: extra network requests vs. smaller initial bundle.","Trade-off: loading delay on first visit to a split chunk.","Considers chunk naming and caching strategy."],"followUps":["How do you handle loading states and errors with lazy-loaded components?","What happens if two routes share a large dependency—how would you avoid duplicate code?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big toy box with all your toys. If you bring the whole box to a friend's house, it's heavy and slow. Code splitting is like packing only the toys you need for that day. When you want a different toy later, you go back and get it. This way, you start playing faster.","detailedExplanation":"

Code splitting is a technique that breaks your application's JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks that are loaded on demand. In React, the primary tool is React.lazy combined with Suspense. React.lazy takes a function that calls a dynamic import() and returns a Promise resolving to a module with a default export. The lazy component is then rendered inside a Suspense component, which shows a fallback UI (like a spinner) while the chunk loads.

How it works

When the application builds, tools like Webpack or Vite detect dynamic imports and create separate output files (chunks). At runtime, when React encounters a lazy component, it triggers the dynamic import, fetching the chunk from the server. Once loaded, React renders the component. This is especially effective for route-based splitting: each route gets its own chunk, so users only download code for the pages they visit.

Why it matters in production

Code splitting directly reduces the initial bundle size, leading to faster Time to Interactive and better Lighthouse scores. For large SPAs, it can cut initial load time by 50% or more. It also enables progressive enhancement—users get a working UI quickly while less critical features load in the background.

Common pitfalls

  • Over-splitting: Creating too many tiny chunks can cause excessive HTTP requests, hurting performance on slow networks. Aim for chunks around 20-50 KB.
  • Missing fallback: Not wrapping lazy components in Suspense will cause a runtime error. Always provide a fallback UI.
  • Duplicate dependencies: If two chunks share a large library, it may be duplicated. Use Webpack's SplitChunksPlugin to extract common dependencies into a shared chunk.
"}]

[{"question":"How would you deploy a new frontend feature to production while minimizing risk to users? Describe the steps from commit to production and the tools/strategies involved.","expectedPoints":["Mentions CI pipeline (lint, test, build) and preview deployment","Describes canary/blue-green deployment with gradual rollout","Explains feature flags to decouple deploy from release","Addresses handling of stale tabs (versioned assets, chunk load error recovery)","Includes rollback strategy (immediate flag toggle or revert)"],"followUps":["What would you do if a user on an old tab experiences a 'chunk load failed' error?","How do you decide the percentage for canary rollout?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a new slide for a playground slide. Instead of letting everyone use it at once, you first test it with just a few friends (canary). If they like it and it's safe, you let more kids use it gradually. You also have a secret switch (feature flag) that can turn the slide off instantly if someone gets hurt, without having to remove the whole slide. And if a kid is already on the old slide, you make sure they can finish safely before switching to the new one.","detailedExplanation":"

This question assesses your understanding of safe deployment strategies in a modern frontend CI/CD pipeline. The goal is to ship code frequently without breaking the user experience.

Pipeline Stages

  • Commit & PR: Developer pushes code, triggering CI.
  • CI (Lint, Test, Build): Automated checks ensure code quality and bundle integrity.
  • Preview Deployment: A temporary environment (e.g., Vercel preview) for manual QA or stakeholder review.
  • Canary/Blue-Green: Route a small percentage of traffic (e.g., 5%) to the new version. Monitor errors and performance. If issues arise, rollback by shifting traffic back to the old version.
  • Production: Gradual ramp-up to 100%. Keep rollback capability (e.g., previous build still deployed).

Feature Flags

Use feature flags (e.g., LaunchDarkly) to decouple deploy from release. Code can be deployed to production but hidden behind a flag. You can then enable the feature for 1% of users, then 50%, then 100%. If a bug is found, disable the flag instantly without a new deploy. This also enables A/B testing.

Handling Stale Tabs

Users with an open tab may have an older JavaScript bundle. When the app tries to load a new chunk (e.g., via lazy loading), it may fail because the chunk URL (with content hash) no longer exists. Mitigations:

  • Versioned assets with content hashes so old files remain available for a grace period.
  • Detect chunk load failure and reload the page to fetch the latest version.
  • Use service workers to cache critical assets.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on rollback without feature flags. Rollback requires a new deploy and may not revert database schema changes or API incompatibilities. Feature flags provide instant, granular control and are safer for gradual rollouts.

"}]

[{"question":"Why is Vite significantly faster than Webpack in development mode, and what trade-offs does it introduce compared to Webpack for production builds?","expectedPoints":["Vite uses native ES modules for dev, avoiding full bundling.","Vite leverages esbuild for pre-bundling dependencies, which is much faster than Webpack's JavaScript-based bundler.","Webpack bundles everything upfront, leading to slower cold starts.","Trade-off: Vite may have compatibility issues with some CommonJS modules or plugins that rely on Webpack-specific features.","For production, Vite uses Rollup for tree-shaking and optimization, which is more mature than esbuild for complex builds."],"followUps":["How would you handle a legacy codebase that uses Webpack-specific loaders when migrating to Vite?","Can you explain how Vite's HMR (Hot Module Replacement) works differently from Webpack's?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a Lego castle. But when you want to show the final castle to your friends, Vite might need to reorganize some pieces to make it look perfect, while Webpack already has it ready.","detailedExplanation":"

Vite achieves faster development speeds by leveraging native ES modules in the browser. During development, Vite serves source files directly without bundling, only pre-bundling dependencies using esbuild (a Go-based bundler) which is orders of magnitude faster than Webpack's JavaScript-based bundling. This eliminates the need to rebundle the entire application on every change, resulting in near-instant Hot Module Replacement (HMR).

How it works

In development, Vite acts as a dev server that transforms files on-demand. When a file changes, Vite only invalidates the module graph for that specific file and sends a WebSocket update to the browser. The browser then fetches the updated module via an HTTP request. This is fundamentally different from Webpack, which rebuilds the entire bundle in memory and patches the runtime.

Trade-offs in production

For production builds, Vite switches to Rollup for bundling because Rollup provides superior tree-shaking and code-splitting capabilities compared to esbuild. This introduces a slight inconsistency between dev and prod environments. Additionally, Vite's reliance on native ES modules can cause compatibility issues with CommonJS modules that don't have ESM equivalents, requiring manual configuration or plugins. Webpack, on the other hand, has a mature ecosystem of loaders and plugins that handle various module formats seamlessly.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is assuming Vite's production build will behave identically to development. Differences in module resolution or plugin behavior can lead to unexpected errors. Always test production builds thoroughly, especially when using non-standard module formats or custom plugins.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you compare Webpack, Vite, esbuild, and Rollup for a new production frontend project?","expectedPoints":["Webpack is best for complex apps with many custom loaders/plugins and Module Federation.","Vite offers fast dev experience using native ESM and esbuild, with Rollup for production builds.","esbuild is extremely fast (Go-based) but lacks advanced features for large-scale apps.","Rollup excels for library builds with superior tree-shaking and clean output formats.","Trade-offs: configuration complexity vs. speed vs. feature set."],"followUps":["When would you choose esbuild over Vite for a project?","What are the downsides of using Vite for a large enterprise app?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a big Lego castle. Webpack is like a huge toolbox with every tool you might need, but it takes time to set up. Vite is like a super-fast helper that builds your castle quickly while you work, then packs it neatly at the end. esbuild is a rocket-powered glue gun that works incredibly fast but only does simple jobs. Rollup is like a master sculptor who carves out exactly the pieces you need with no waste. You pick the right tool based on whether you're building a castle, a spaceship, or a tiny model.","detailedExplanation":"

When comparing Webpack, Vite, esbuild, and Rollup for a new production frontend project, the choice depends on the project's complexity, performance requirements, and team preferences.

Webpack

Webpack is the most mature and feature-rich bundler, with a vast ecosystem of loaders and plugins. It excels in complex applications that require custom build logic, code splitting, and Module Federation for micro-frontends. However, its configuration is verbose and slow for large projects, especially in development.

Vite

Vite leverages native ESM during development, using esbuild for pre-bundling dependencies, resulting in instant server start and fast HMR. For production, it uses Rollup for optimized builds. Vite is ideal for modern SPAs and frameworks like Vue, React, and Svelte, offering a great developer experience with minimal configuration.

esbuild

esbuild is an extremely fast bundler written in Go, often 10–100× faster than JavaScript-based tools. It's great for simple projects or as a transpiler/minifier, but lacks advanced features like code splitting, asset management, and plugin ecosystem, making it unsuitable for complex apps.

Rollup

Rollup is the go-to tool for building libraries due to its superior tree-shaking and ability to output clean ESM, CJS, and UMD formats. It produces smaller bundles but has a steeper learning curve and less mature plugin ecosystem than Webpack.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is choosing a tool based solely on speed without considering the project's needs. For example, using esbuild for a large enterprise app may lead to missing features and maintenance headaches. Conversely, using Webpack for a simple library can overcomplicate the setup.

In production, the decision often comes down to: Webpack for complex apps with custom requirements, Vite for modern web apps prioritizing dev speed, esbuild for fast builds in simple projects or as a sub-tool, and Rollup for publishing libraries.

"}]
qgen_v2:3b2bc5f10179df54:
[{"question":"Why is Vite's dev server significantly faster than Webpack's for large projects?","expectedPoints":["Vite uses native ES modules (ESM) in the browser during development.","It does not bundle the entire application upfront; instead, it serves modules on demand.","Only the modules requested by the browser are transformed (using esbuild for speed).","This eliminates the initial bundling step, making startup and HMR near-instant.","Webpack bundles everything before serving, causing slow startup for large projects."],"followUps":["How does Vite handle module transformation differently from Webpack in development?","What trade-off does Vite make by not bundling in development?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge Lego set. Webpack builds the entire model before you can play, which takes a long time. Vite lets you play with just the pieces you need right now, building only those as you go. So you start playing instantly, even if the whole set is huge.","detailedExplanation":"

Vite achieves fast development server startup by leveraging the browser's native support for ES modules (ESM). In development, Vite does not bundle the entire application. Instead, it serves modules on demand: when the browser requests a module, Vite transforms only that module using esbuild (a blazing-fast bundler written in Go) and sends it back. This eliminates the need to bundle all modules upfront, which is the bottleneck in tools like Webpack.

How it works

When you run vite dev, the server starts immediately because it only needs to set up a file watcher and a dev server. When the browser loads the app, it imports modules via <script type="module">. Vite intercepts these requests, performs necessary transformations (e.g., TypeScript, JSX, CSS), and serves the result. Only modules that are actually imported are processed. This approach also makes Hot Module Replacement (HMR) extremely fast, as only the changed module and its direct dependents need to be re-transformed.

Why it matters in production

In large projects, Webpack's dev server can take tens of seconds to start and HMR can be sluggish. Vite's on-demand strategy keeps the development experience snappy regardless of project size. However, for production builds, Vite uses Rollup to bundle and optimize the code, ensuring fast load times in the browser.

Common pitfall

A common misconception is that Vite is always faster than Webpack. While Vite excels in development, its production build speed depends on Rollup, which may be slower than esbuild for certain tasks. Also, Vite's ESM-based approach may not work well with legacy browsers that lack native ESM support, requiring additional polyfills or fallback builds.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you test a React component that makes an async API call and updates state based on the response? Walk through your approach, including mocking strategy and handling edge cases.","expectedPoints":["Uses async utilities like waitFor or findBy to handle asynchronous state updates","Mocks the API call at the network level (e.g., MSW) or module level (jest.mock) to avoid real requests","Tests both loading and error states, not just the happy path","Ensures test cleanup (e.g., aborting requests) to prevent flaky tests","Validates that the UI correctly reflects the data after the async operation"],"followUps":["What if the API call is in a useEffect with a cleanup function? How do you test that the cleanup runs correctly?","How would you test a race condition where two rapid API calls return out of order?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you order a toy online and the website shows a 'loading' spinner until it arrives. You want to test that the website works correctly: first it shows the spinner, then it shows the toy. But you don't want to actually order a real toy every time you test—that would be slow and expensive. So you pretend (mock) the delivery service to always send a toy quickly. You also test what happens if the toy never arrives (error) or if you cancel the order (cleanup).","detailedExplanation":"

What It Is

Testing async behavior in frontend components involves verifying that the UI correctly transitions through states like loading, success, and error when interacting with asynchronous operations (e.g., API calls).

How It Works

Use testing libraries like React Testing Library with waitFor or findBy queries to wait for elements to appear after async updates. Mock the API call at the network level using tools like MSW (Mock Service Worker) or at the module level with jest.mock. For example, mock fetch or a custom API module to return a resolved or rejected promise. Test each state: render the component, assert the loading indicator is present, wait for the mock to resolve, then assert the data is displayed. Also test error states by mocking a rejection.

Why It Matters in Production

Async tests catch real-world bugs like missing loading states, incorrect error handling, or race conditions. Without proper async testing, your app might show stale data or crash silently. Reliable async tests give confidence that the user experience remains smooth even when network conditions vary.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is not waiting for the async operation to complete before asserting. Using getBy instead of findBy or waitFor can cause tests to pass even when the component is broken because the assertion runs before the state updates. Always use async queries for elements that depend on async data.

"}]

[{"question":"You are testing a function that calls an external API and then logs the result to an analytics service. Which test doubles would you use for the API call and the analytics logger, and why?","expectedPoints":["Use a stub for the API call to return a fixed response.","Use a spy for the analytics logger to verify it was called with correct arguments.","Explain that stub controls the input, spy observes the side effect.","Avoid mocking the API call with expectations unless interaction verification is needed.","Prefer real logic for the function under test, only double at system boundaries."],"followUps":["What if the analytics logger also needs to return a value for the function to continue?","How would you decide between a stub and a mock for the API call?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're testing a robot that fetches a toy from a shelf and then tells a friend about it. You can replace the shelf with a pretend one that always gives the same toy (stub), and you can ask the friend to write down everything the robot says (spy). That way you check the robot's actions without using the real shelf or friend.","detailedExplanation":"

In testing, test doubles replace real dependencies to isolate the unit under test. The key distinction between stub, spy, and mock lies in their purpose and usage.

Stub

A stub provides predefined answers to calls made during the test. It is used when you need a dependency to return a specific value to drive the test scenario. For example, an API call that should return a user object. Stubs are state-verifying: you check the result of the function, not how it interacted with the stub.

Spy

A spy wraps a real object and records information about how it was called (e.g., number of calls, arguments). It is used to verify side effects like logging or analytics. Spies are behavior-verifying but they still execute the real implementation unless you stub specific methods.

Mock

A mock is a pre-programmed object with expectations about the calls it will receive. It fails the test if the actual calls don't match the expectations. Mocks are used for interaction testing between modules. They are stricter than spies because they enforce the exact call sequence.

Common Pitfall

Over-mocking leads to tests that only verify the mock setup, not real behavior. A common mistake is to mock everything, including simple data transformations, making tests brittle and uninformative. The rule of thumb: mock at system boundaries (network, I/O, time) and let the core logic run on real objects.

In the given scenario, the API call is a boundary (network), so a stub is appropriate to control its return value. The analytics logger is a side effect, so a spy is ideal to verify it was called correctly without replacing its real implementation.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you test a debounced function without waiting for real time?","expectedPoints":["Use fake timers (e.g., vi.useFakeTimers()) to control time.","Call the debounced function multiple times in quick succession.","Advance time by the debounce delay (e.g., vi.advanceTimersByTime(300)).","Assert that the function was called only once after the delay.","Restore real timers after the test to avoid side effects."],"followUps":["What if the debounce delay is 500ms but you advance only 400ms?","How would you test that the function is called with the latest arguments?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a doorbell that only rings if you press it and then wait 5 seconds without pressing again. If you press it twice quickly, it only rings once after the last press. To test this without actually waiting 5 seconds, you can pretend time is moving fast—like using a remote control to skip ahead 5 seconds instantly.","detailedExplanation":"

Testing asynchronous code like debounce requires controlling time to avoid real delays. Fake timers (e.g., vi.useFakeTimers() in Vitest) replace real timers with mock implementations that you can manually advance.

How it works

First, enable fake timers: vi.useFakeTimers(). Then call the debounced function multiple times. Use vi.advanceTimersByTime(300) to simulate the passage of 300ms. Since debounce only executes after the last call within the delay, the spy should have been called exactly once.

Why it matters in production

Real-time waits make tests slow and flaky. Fake timers ensure tests run in milliseconds, not seconds. This is critical for CI pipelines where speed matters. Also, it isolates the debounce logic from real time, making tests deterministic.

Common pitfall

Forgetting to restore real timers after the test with vi.useRealTimers(). If not restored, other tests may break because they rely on real timeouts. Always clean up in afterEach.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you implement contract testing between a frontend and a backend team to prevent API changes from breaking the frontend?","expectedPoints":["Describes contract testing as a pact between FE and BE defining expected request/response format.","Mentions tools like Pact or MSW for contract testing.","Explains that contract tests run in CI to catch mismatches before deployment.","Highlights that contract tests fail when BE changes a field without updating the contract.","Notes that contract testing complements integration tests by focusing on API shape, not behavior."],"followUps":["How would you handle versioning of contracts when both teams need to evolve the API?","What are the trade-offs of using consumer-driven contracts vs provider-driven contracts?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend agree to exchange LEGO pieces in a specific order. Contract testing is like writing down that agreement. If your friend suddenly changes the order, the test will tell you immediately, so you don't build a spaceship with the wrong pieces.","detailedExplanation":"

Contract testing is a technique to ensure that two independently developed services (e.g., frontend and backend) agree on the format of their communication. It prevents the classic scenario where the backend changes an API field and the frontend breaks in production without anyone noticing.

How It Works

The frontend (consumer) defines its expectations for a specific API call: the request shape, headers, and the expected response structure. This is called a consumer contract. The backend (provider) then verifies that it can satisfy that contract by running a provider verification test. Tools like Pact or MSW (Mock Service Worker) can be used to capture and verify these contracts.

Why It Matters in Production

In a microservices architecture, frontend and backend teams often work in parallel. Without contract testing, a backend change that renames a field or changes its type can go unnoticed until end-to-end tests or, worse, real users encounter errors. Contract tests run in CI pipelines, catching mismatches early and forcing teams to coordinate changes.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is treating contract tests as full integration tests. Contract tests only verify the shape of the data, not the business logic. For example, they check that a user.name field exists and is a string, but not that the name is correctly computed. Over-relying on contract tests can give a false sense of safety; they should be combined with other testing layers.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a component documentation system using Storybook for a large-scale design system, and what key considerations would you address?","expectedPoints":["Organize stories by component hierarchy and variants","Integrate with design tokens and theming","Ensure accessibility testing and visual regression","Automate documentation generation and deployment","Handle cross-team collaboration and versioning"],"followUps":["How would you manage component dependencies and shared contexts in Storybook?","What strategies would you use to keep documentation in sync with the actual codebase?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big box of LEGO bricks. Storybook is like a special display case where you can show each brick separately, how they look in different colors, and how they fit together. This helps everyone building with the bricks see what's available and test them without building the whole castle first.","detailedExplanation":"

Storybook is a tool for developing UI components in isolation, making it ideal for building a living documentation system for a design system. When designing such a system for a large-scale project, several key considerations arise.

Component Organization

Stories should be organized hierarchically, mirroring the component tree. Use naming conventions like Design System/Buttons/Primary to create a clear navigation structure. Each component should have stories for its variants (e.g., sizes, states, themes) and edge cases (loading, error, empty).

Design Tokens & Theming

Integrate design tokens (colors, typography, spacing) into Storybook so that components automatically reflect the current theme. Use addons like @storybook/addon-themes to allow switching between light/dark modes or brand themes. This ensures documentation stays consistent with the design system.

Testing & Quality

Leverage Storybook addons for accessibility testing (@storybook/addon-a11y) and visual regression (e.g., Chromatic). Each story becomes a test case, catching regressions early. Also include interaction tests using @storybook/test to verify user flows.

Automation & Deployment

Automate story generation using Component Story Format (CSF) and tools like Storybook Autodocs to generate documentation from code comments. Deploy the Storybook to a static hosting service (e.g., Netlify, Vercel) on every merge to main, ensuring the documentation is always up-to-date.

Collaboration & Versioning

For large teams, use Storybook Composition to combine multiple Storybooks from different micro-frontends into a single interface. Version your stories alongside the component library (e.g., using tags in Git) so that documentation matches the released version.

Common Pitfall: Treating Storybook as an afterthought rather than a core part of the development workflow. Without automation and team buy-in, documentation quickly becomes outdated. Integrate Storybook into the CI/CD pipeline and enforce that every component has stories before merging.

"}]
qgen_v2:c2bc9914c84f5cb4:
[{"question":"How does developing components in isolation (e.g., using Storybook) improve testing and collaboration compared to testing within the full application?","expectedPoints":["Isolation allows testing each component state (loading, error, empty) without complex app setup.","Stories serve as living documentation for designers and developers.","Visual regression testing catches UI breakages that unit tests miss.","Accessibility audits can be integrated directly into component stories.","Enables parallel development and faster feedback loops."],"followUps":["What are the trade-offs of using visual regression testing over traditional unit tests for UI components?","How would you handle component states that depend on asynchronous data or global state in isolation?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy car. Instead of testing it only when it's fully assembled on a track, you test each part separately—like the wheels, doors, and lights—to make sure they work perfectly. That way, if something breaks later, you know exactly which part is broken. Isolated component development is like that: you build and test each piece of your app's UI by itself, so you catch problems early and everyone on the team can see how each piece works.","detailedExplanation":"

Isolated component development (e.g., using Storybook) is a practice where UI components are built, tested, and documented independently of the full application. This approach fundamentally improves testing and collaboration in several ways.

How It Works

Each component is rendered in a sandboxed environment with mock data, allowing developers to cycle through all its states—loading, error, empty, and success—without needing a real backend or complex app state. Tools like Storybook generate a story for each state, which serves as both a visual test and documentation.

Why It Matters in Production

  • Faster debugging: When a UI bug appears, you can reproduce it in isolation without navigating the entire app.
  • Visual regression testing: Automated screenshot comparisons detect unintended visual changes (e.g., a button shifting 1px) that unit tests cannot catch.
  • Accessibility audits: Addons like @storybook/addon-a11y check contrast, ARIA labels, and focus order directly in each story, ensuring compliance early.
  • Collaboration: Designers and developers can review all component variants without running the app, reducing miscommunication.

Common Pitfall

Over-reliance on isolated testing can lead to integration blind spots. Components that work perfectly in isolation may fail when connected to real data or other components (e.g., state management, routing). Always complement isolated tests with integration or end-to-end tests.

"}]

[{"question":"What are the three main benefits of a Storybook story, and how do they support a design system?","expectedPoints":["Story serves as living documentation for other developers.","Story provides an isolated playground for development and testing.","Story acts as input for visual regression testing (e.g., Chromatic).","These benefits reduce duplication and improve maintainability in a design system.","Mentions the 'write once, use three times' philosophy."],"followUps":["How would you handle stories that depend on complex state or API calls?","Can you give an example of a visual regression test you've caught with Storybook?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you build a LEGO castle and take a photo of it. That photo shows others what you built (documentation), lets you play with the castle in different ways (playground), and helps you check if any bricks fall off later (visual test). Storybook stories are like those photos for your website components.","detailedExplanation":"

Storybook stories are a core concept that combines three roles into one: documentation, development playground, and visual regression test input. This 'write once, use three times' approach is especially valuable for teams maintaining a design system.

1. Living Documentation

Each story is a runnable example of a component in a specific state. Developers and designers can browse stories to understand how a component behaves, what props it accepts, and see real UI output. This replaces static documentation that quickly becomes outdated.

2. Isolated Playground

Stories allow developers to work on a component in isolation, without needing the full application. This speeds up development and debugging because you can test edge cases (like loading or error states) in a focused environment.

3. Visual Regression Testing

Tools like Chromatic or Percy capture screenshots of each story and compare them against baselines. Any unintended visual change is flagged, preventing UI bugs from reaching production. This is critical for design systems where consistency across many components is paramount.

Why It Matters in Production

In a design system, components are reused across multiple applications. A single story update can propagate documentation, development examples, and visual tests simultaneously. This reduces duplication and ensures that all three artifacts stay in sync. Without this approach, teams often end up with separate, inconsistent documentation and test suites.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is writing stories that are too complex or tightly coupled to application logic. Stories should be self-contained and use mock data or decorators to simulate dependencies. Overly complex stories become brittle and hard to maintain, defeating the purpose of the 'three-in-one' benefit.

"}]
