[{"question":"In a messaging app like WhatsApp, how would you design the message ordering system to ensure messages are displayed in the correct order across multiple devices, considering network delays and offline scenarios?","expectedPoints":["Use a globally unique, time-sortable ID like Snowflake to order messages by ID rather than timestamp.","Handle offline messages by storing them in a persistent queue and delivering them when the device reconnects.","Use server-side timestamps or logical clocks to resolve ordering conflicts.","Consider using a distributed database with ordered primary keys (e.g., Cassandra with clustering order)."],"followUps":["How would you handle message ordering in a group chat where messages from different users arrive out of order?","What are the trade-offs of using client-side timestamps vs server-side timestamps for ordering?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are sending sticky notes to each other. To know which note came first, you could number them in order. But if you both write numbers, they might get mixed up. So you ask a central person (the server) to stamp each note with a special number that always increases. That way, even if notes arrive late, you can sort them by the stamp number to see the correct order.","detailedExplanation":"

In a messaging app, ensuring messages are displayed in the correct order across multiple devices is critical for user experience. The naive approach of relying on client-side timestamps fails because clocks on different devices may be out of sync, and network delays can cause messages to arrive out of order.

Key Design Elements

  • Globally Unique, Time-Sortable IDs: Use a system like Snowflake ID (64-bit: timestamp + machine ID + sequence) to generate IDs that are both unique and sortable by time. This allows messages to be ordered by ID without needing a separate created_at field.
  • Server-Side Ordering: The server assigns the ID when it receives the message, ensuring a consistent global order. This avoids issues with client clock skew.
  • Persistent Queue for Offline Messages: When a recipient is offline, messages are stored in a queue (e.g., Kafka) and delivered in order when the device reconnects. The Snowflake ID ensures the correct sequence is maintained.
  • Database Schema: Use a distributed database like Cassandra with a primary key that includes a clustering column ordered by message ID. For example: PRIMARY KEY ((group_id), message_id) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (message_id DESC). This allows efficient retrieval of messages in order.

Handling Group Chats

In group chats, messages from different users may arrive at different servers. Using a distributed ID generator (like Snowflake) ensures that IDs are unique across all servers and still sortable by time. The server that receives the message assigns the ID, so ordering is based on server receipt time, which is consistent.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is relying on client-side timestamps for ordering. This can lead to incorrect order when clocks are out of sync or when messages are sent offline. Always use server-assigned, time-sortable IDs to guarantee correct ordering.

"}]
qgen_v2:4916fb00af540f94:
[{"question":"How would you design the real-time driver location tracking and matching system for a ride-sharing app like Uber? Focus on the geospatial indexing approach and state machine management.","expectedPoints":["Explain why brute-force O(N) scanning is impractical for millions of drivers.","Describe GeoHash and its boundary problem, then contrast with H3 hexagonal grid.","Propose using Redis GEOADD/GEOSEARCH for radius queries with pagination.","Define a trip state machine with transitions (requested → searching → accepted → arrived → in_trip → completed/cancelled).","Mention distributed locks (e.g., Redis SETNX) to prevent race conditions during state transitions."],"followUps":["How would you handle the boundary problem with GeoHash in production?","What happens if a driver's location update is delayed or lost?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're trying to find the closest ice cream truck to your friend in a huge city. If you check every single truck one by one, it takes too long. Instead, you can divide the city into a grid of hexagons (like a honeycomb) and only look at trucks in your friend's hexagon and the six around it. That's what Uber's H3 system does. Also, when a ride is happening, the app keeps track of what step it's in (like 'waiting for driver', 'driver arrived', 'on trip') so nothing gets confused.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a real-time geo-matching system at scale, a core challenge for ride-sharing platforms. The key components are geospatial indexing and a trip state machine.

Geospatial Indexing

A naive approach scans all drivers (O(N)) — infeasible for millions. GeoHash encodes lat/lng into a string prefix, enabling fast prefix-based lookups, but suffers from the boundary problem: two points very close but on opposite sides of a GeoHash cell have different prefixes, causing missed matches. H3 (Uber's open-source library) uses a hexagonal grid where each cell has 6 equidistant neighbors, eliminating corner issues. In production, you'd store driver locations in Redis using GEOADD and query with GEOSEARCH for radius-based matching, optionally using ASC COUNT 10 to get the nearest drivers efficiently.

Trip State Machine

Each ride progresses through states: requested → searching_driver → driver_accepted → driver_arrived → in_trip → completed (or cancelled). State transitions must be atomic to avoid race conditions (e.g., two drivers accepting the same trip). Use distributed locks (e.g., Redis SETNX) to ensure only one transition happens at a time. The state machine should be idempotent — retrying a transition yields the same result, making the system resilient to failures.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring the boundary problem with GeoHash leads to inconsistent matching near cell edges. Always test with real-world coordinates and consider using H3 or a hybrid approach. Also, failing to handle stale location updates (e.g., driver hasn't sent location for 30 seconds) can cause mismatches — implement a heartbeat timeout and remove stale drivers from the index.

"}]
qgen_v2:9fb6f34417dce09d:
[{"question":"You are designing a real-time collaborative document editor (like Google Docs). How would you handle conflict resolution when multiple users edit the same paragraph simultaneously?","expectedPoints":["Mentions Operational Transformation (OT) or CRDT as conflict resolution strategies.","Explains how changes are applied and merged without data loss.","Discusses trade-offs: OT requires centralized server, CRDT is peer-to-peer but more complex.","Considers performance implications for large documents and many users.","Addresses undo/redo and consistency guarantees (eventual consistency)."],"followUps":["How would you handle offline editing and sync when the user comes back online?","What are the pros and cons of using a centralized server vs. a peer-to-peer architecture for this system?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are coloring the same picture at the same time. If you both try to color the same spot, you need a rule to decide whose color stays. In a document editor, we use special rules called Operational Transformation or CRDT to make sure everyone's changes are combined correctly without losing any work.","detailedExplanation":"

Real-time collaborative editing is a challenging system design problem because multiple users can modify the same document simultaneously. The core issue is conflict resolution — ensuring that all edits are applied consistently across all clients without data loss.

Conflict Resolution Strategies

  • Operational Transformation (OT): Each edit is represented as an operation (e.g., insert 'a' at position 5). When concurrent operations conflict, the server transforms them so they can be applied in a consistent order. OT is used in Google Docs and requires a centralized server to coordinate transformations.
  • CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types): Data structures designed so that concurrent updates can be merged without conflicts. Each character has a unique ID, and edits are commutative. CRDTs work well in peer-to-peer systems but can have higher storage overhead.

How It Works

In a typical system, each client sends operations to a server. The server orders operations and broadcasts them. With OT, the server transforms operations to maintain consistency. With CRDT, each client merges incoming changes using a deterministic algorithm.

Why It Matters in Production

Users expect real-time collaboration with no lost edits. The chosen strategy affects latency, server load, and offline support. OT is proven at scale (Google Docs) but requires a central server. CRDT enables offline-first and peer-to-peer but is more complex to implement correctly.

Common Pitfall

Assuming that simple last-write-wins is sufficient. This can cause lost edits when users type simultaneously. Always use a proper conflict resolution algorithm to preserve all changes.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a cross-chain wallet SDK that handles token swaps and checkout in a single transaction, considering gas fees, slippage, and user experience?","expectedPoints":["Abstracts chain-specific logic behind a unified interface","Handles gas estimation and fee payment across chains","Implements slippage protection and fallback routes","Provides a simple checkout API that aggregates swap and transfer","Considers UX with loading states, error handling, and transaction status"],"followUps":["How would you handle a scenario where the user's wallet doesn't have enough gas on the destination chain?","What tradeoffs would you consider between using a bridge vs. a direct swap?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a magic wallet that can use money from different piggy banks (like Ethereum, Solana). You want to buy a toy that costs dollars, but you only have Ethereum. The wallet needs to swap your Ethereum for dollars and pay the toy store, all in one smooth step. You also need to make sure you don't lose money due to price changes or extra fees.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a cross-chain wallet SDK like FunKit requires abstracting the complexity of multiple blockchains into a simple developer API. The core challenge is enabling a user to pay with any asset on any chain while the merchant receives a stablecoin on a preferred chain.

Unified Interface

The SDK should expose a single checkout(amount, currency, recipientChain) method that internally handles chain detection, token approval, swap, and transfer. Each chain adapter implements a common interface for balance checks, gas estimation, and transaction building.

Gas and Fee Management

Gas fees vary by chain and can be paid in the native token. The SDK must estimate gas, check user balance, and potentially swap a small portion of the user's token for gas if needed. A common pitfall is assuming the user always has native gas tokens; the SDK should offer a 'gasless' mode where fees are deducted from the payment amount.

Slippage and Routing

Token swaps are subject to slippage. The SDK should allow configurable slippage tolerance and use a DEX aggregator (e.g., 1inch, ParaSwap) to find the best route. Fallback routes are essential if the primary route fails due to liquidity or price impact.

Transaction Lifecycle

The SDK must emit events for each step (approval, swap, transfer) and handle retries or reverts. A production concern is handling partial failures—e.g., swap succeeds but transfer fails—requiring a recovery mechanism or atomic execution via intents.

User Experience

Developers need clear callbacks for loading, success, and error states. The SDK should also provide a simulation mode to estimate costs before execution. A common pitfall is blocking the UI during transaction confirmation; instead, use optimistic updates and background polling.

"}]

[{"question":"Given that FunXYZ has only 20 engineers but processes $100M in monthly payments and plans to scale to 50 engineers, what are the key architectural and organizational challenges you anticipate, and how would you address them?","expectedPoints":["Identifies scaling challenges: maintaining reliability, handling increased transaction volume, and onboarding new engineers without degrading performance.","Discusses architectural strategies: microservices, event-driven design, or modular monolith to decouple payment processing from other features.","Addresses organizational challenges: knowledge transfer, code review processes, and maintaining engineering culture during rapid hiring.","Mentions monitoring and observability: need for robust logging, metrics, and alerting to ensure payment system reliability.","Considers trade-offs: speed of scaling vs. technical debt, and balancing feature velocity with system stability."],"followUps":["How would you prioritize between building new features and improving system reliability given the rapid growth?","What specific metrics would you track to ensure the payment system remains reliable as transaction volume increases?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a lemonade stand that suddenly gets super popular. You need to make more lemonade faster, but you also have to make sure every cup tastes perfect. If you hire more helpers, you need to teach them your secret recipe and make sure they don't mess up. That's like what FunXYZ faces: they need to handle more money transactions without errors while growing their team quickly.","detailedExplanation":"

FunXYZ is at a critical inflection point: processing $100M in monthly payments with only 20 engineers, and planning to scale to 50 engineers. This rapid growth introduces both architectural and organizational challenges that must be addressed to maintain reliability and velocity.

Architectural Challenges

With a small team handling high-value transactions, the system must be designed for fault tolerance and observability. Key considerations include:

  • Decoupling payment processing from other services using event-driven architecture or message queues (e.g., Kafka, RabbitMQ) to ensure that payment failures don't cascade.
  • Implementing idempotency keys to prevent duplicate charges, especially during retries.
  • Using circuit breakers and bulkheads to isolate failures in third-party integrations (e.g., payment gateways).
  • Investing in comprehensive monitoring (metrics, traces, logs) to quickly detect and diagnose issues in the payment pipeline.

Organizational Challenges

Scaling the team from 20 to 50 engineers requires deliberate processes to avoid chaos:

  • Onboarding and knowledge transfer: Create clear documentation, runbooks, and mentorship programs to ramp up new hires quickly.
  • Code review and quality gates: Establish automated testing (unit, integration, end-to-end) and mandatory code reviews to maintain code quality.
  • Engineering culture: Preserve a blameless post-mortem culture and encourage cross-team communication to avoid silos.

Why It Matters in Production

Payment systems are mission-critical; any downtime or data inconsistency can lead to financial loss and reputational damage. Balancing feature development with reliability is a constant trade-off. A common pitfall is underinvesting in observability early on, making it hard to debug issues when the system grows. Proactive investment in monitoring and automation is essential.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a reusable data-fetching hook in React that handles loading, error, and success states, and how would you test it?","expectedPoints":["Uses useReducer or multiple useState for state management","Handles race conditions with cleanup or AbortController","Returns { data, loading, error } and supports refetch","Tests with mock fetch, verifies state transitions","Considers caching or deduplication for production"],"followUps":["How would you modify the hook to support pagination or infinite scroll?","What if the component unmounts before the fetch completes?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a magic box that fetches toys from a shelf. You need to show 'loading' while waiting, 'error' if something goes wrong, or the toy itself. This question asks how you'd build that magic box in React so it works every time, even if you change your mind and walk away before the toy arrives.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a robust, reusable data-fetching hook—a core pattern in modern React apps. The interviewer wants to see if you can handle real-world complexities beyond a simple useEffect.

What It Is

A custom hook like useFetch that encapsulates the logic for fetching data from an API, managing the lifecycle of the request, and exposing state to the component.

How It Works

  • State management: Use useReducer or multiple useState calls to track data, loading, error. A reducer gives predictable state transitions (idle → loading → success/error).
  • Side effects: Use useEffect to trigger the fetch. Include a cleanup function (e.g., AbortController) to cancel the request if the component unmounts or dependencies change, preventing memory leaks and race conditions.
  • Reusability: Accept a URL or fetch function as a parameter. Return an object with data, loading, error, and a refetch method.
  • Testing: Mock fetch or use a library like msw. Test that the hook transitions through states correctly and that cleanup cancels pending requests.

Why It Matters in Production

Production apps need consistent error handling, loading indicators, and performance. A well-designed hook reduces boilerplate, prevents common bugs (like updating state on unmounted components), and makes the codebase easier to maintain.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring race conditions: if the URL changes quickly, an old response might overwrite new data. Always use a cleanup mechanism or a unique request ID to discard stale responses.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you prepare for a multi-stage frontend interview process that includes a recruiter screen, a hiring manager deep dive, a live coding assessment, and a final round with the founder?","expectedPoints":["Tailor preparation to each stage: research company, practice coding, prepare behavioral stories.","Demonstrate time management and flexibility for timezone constraints.","Show commitment by adjusting schedule to accommodate interview slots.","Understand the purpose of each stage and what interviewers look for.","Prepare to discuss technical background in depth with the hiring manager."],"followUps":["What specific technical topics would you review for the deep dive with the hiring manager?","How would you handle a rescheduling request if you had a conflict?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"behavioral","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big test at school that has four parts: a quick chat with the principal, a longer talk with your favorite teacher, a puzzle-solving session with classmates, and a final meeting with the school founder. This question assesses your ability to navigate a multi-stage interview process, which is common for senior roles. The key is to strategically prepare for each stage while demonstrating professionalism and commitment.

Stage-Specific Preparation

  • Recruiter Screen (30'): Focus on your fit, motivation, and salary expectations. Prepare a concise summary of your experience and why you're interested in the role.
  • Hiring Manager Deep Dive (45'): Expect in-depth technical questions. Review your past projects, architectural decisions, and trade-offs. Be ready to discuss system design, performance optimization, and team collaboration.
  • Live Coding Assessment: Practice coding under time pressure. Focus on clean code, problem-solving, and communication while coding. Use a platform like LeetCode or HackerRank.
  • Final Round with Founder: This is about cultural fit and vision alignment. Research the company's values and be ready to share how you embody them.

Timezone and Scheduling

The note about strict rescheduling and NY timezone slots (SGT 7am-10am or 8pm-12am) highlights the need for flexibility. Show willingness to adjust your schedule, even if it means staying up late. This demonstrates commitment and reliability.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is treating all stages the same. For example, being too casual in the recruiter screen or too technical in the founder chat. Tailor your approach: recruiter wants to know if you're a good fit, hiring manager wants technical depth, live coding tests practical skills, and founder assesses cultural alignment.

In production, this mirrors how senior engineers must adapt communication to different stakeholders (e.g., product managers, executives, junior developers). Mastering this skill is crucial for career growth.

"}]

How would you prevent unnecessary re-renders?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a scoreboard for a video game that shows players' scores changing in real time. That's what this question is about—building a live-updating dashboard that stays fast and reliable.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a real-time data visualization system in React, a common requirement for dashboards showing live metrics like compensation data. The key is balancing data freshness with performance.

Core Architecture

Use useState to hold the compensation data array, useEffect to set up a WebSocket connection (or polling with setInterval) that updates the state on new messages. For WebSocket, ensure cleanup in the effect's return to prevent memory leaks. For polling, use useRef to store the interval ID and clear it on unmount.

Performance Optimizations

  • Memoization: Wrap expensive components or calculations with React.memo and useMemo to avoid re-rendering when props haven't changed.
  • Virtualization: For large datasets (e.g., hundreds of employees), use a library like react-window to render only visible rows.
  • Batching Updates: If updates arrive frequently, batch them using a debounce or throttle mechanism to reduce render cycles.

State Management

Consider using useReducer for complex state logic (e.g., merging incoming updates with existing data). For global state across components, a lightweight solution like React Context or Zustand can avoid prop drilling.

Error Handling & Resilience

Implement reconnection logic for WebSocket failures with exponential backoff. Show a loading skeleton initially, and if data is stale, display a warning banner. Use ErrorBoundary to catch rendering errors gracefully.

Common Pitfall

Over-fetching or updating state too frequently can cause excessive re-renders. Always compare incoming data with current state (e.g., using a unique ID) before updating, and consider using useRef for values that don't trigger re-renders (like WebSocket instance).

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a scalable frontend architecture for a multi-product B2B platform that must support high traffic, frequent feature releases, and strict compliance requirements?","expectedPoints":["Proposes a monorepo or micro-frontend approach to share design system and utilities across products.","Emphasizes a robust design system (e.g., Ant Design, custom) for consistency and speed.","Discusses state management strategy (e.g., Redux, MobX) and data flow for complex business logic.","Addresses compliance (e.g., audit logging, data validation) and performance optimization (lazy loading, caching).","Mentions testing strategy (unit, integration, E2E) and CI/CD for frequent releases.","Considers team scalability and onboarding through clear module boundaries and documentation."],"followUps":["How would you handle versioning and backward compatibility when multiple products share components?","What trade-offs would you consider between a monorepo and micro-frontends for this scenario?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a big Lego castle with many different rooms (products). You need to make sure all rooms use the same kind of bricks (design system) so they look consistent. Also, you want to be able to add new rooms quickly without breaking the existing ones. That's what this question is about: planning the best way to organize your code so it's easy to work on, safe, and fast.","detailedExplanation":"

This question assesses your ability to architect a frontend system that can scale across multiple products while maintaining consistency, performance, and compliance—key challenges in B2B platforms like those at Zalo or Binance.

Core Architecture

A monorepo (e.g., using Nx or Turborepo) or micro-frontends (e.g., Module Federation) can share a common design system and utilities. A monorepo simplifies code sharing and versioning, while micro-frontends offer independent deployment. For B2B, a monorepo with clear package boundaries often strikes the right balance.

Design System

Invest in a custom design system (or extend Ant Design) with reusable components, tokens, and documentation. This ensures visual consistency across products and accelerates development. Use Storybook for component isolation and testing.

State Management & Data Flow

For complex business logic (e.g., multi-step forms, compliance checks), use a predictable state container like Redux Toolkit or MobX. Implement middleware for side effects (e.g., API calls, audit logging). Keep data normalized to avoid duplication.

Compliance & Performance

Compliance requires audit trails (log every user action), data validation (both client and server), and access control. Performance: lazy load routes, use code splitting, and employ caching (e.g., React Query) for API responses. Monitor with tools like Lighthouse.

Testing & CI/CD

Adopt a testing pyramid: unit tests for utilities, integration tests for components, and E2E tests (Cypress) for critical flows. CI/CD pipelines should run tests, lint, and build before deployment. Use feature flags to release gradually.

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering early. Start with a simple monorepo and a minimal design system; evolve as needs grow. Avoid premature micro-frontends—they add complexity in routing, state sharing, and deployment.

"}]

This question assesses your ability to design a production-grade SDK that meets the needs of a hypergrowth crypto payments company. The key is to demonstrate modularity, type safety, and observability.

Modular Architecture

Use a core module for shared logic (e.g., transaction lifecycle, error codes) and platform adapters for each e-commerce platform (Shopify, WooCommerce, custom). Each adapter implements a common interface, allowing the SDK to work across different checkout flows. This mirrors the micro-frontend approach you used at Binance.

TypeScript Generics

[{"question":"Explain how React reconciliation works and why the `key` prop is critical for list rendering performance.","expectedPoints":["Reconciliation is the diffing algorithm that compares virtual DOM trees to minimize real DOM updates.","Keys help identify which items changed, added, or removed, enabling efficient reordering instead of re-rendering all items.","Using index as key can cause bugs with stateful components or incorrect DOM updates when list order changes.","Stable, unique keys (e.g., item IDs) allow React to preserve component state and avoid unnecessary unmounts/remounts.","Without keys, React defaults to index-based comparison, leading to potential performance issues and UI glitches."],"followUps":["What happens if you omit the `key` prop entirely in a list?","Can you give an example where using index as key leads to a bug?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a row of toy blocks, and you want to change the order. If each block has a name (like 'key'), you can just swap them. Without names, you might have to rebuild the whole row. React uses keys to know which parts to move and which to keep, making updates faster and avoiding mistakes.","detailedExplanation":"

Reconciliation is React's algorithm for updating the DOM efficiently. When state or props change, React creates a new virtual DOM tree and compares it with the previous one using a diffing algorithm. Instead of rebuilding the entire DOM, React computes the minimal set of changes needed.

Role of the key Prop

In lists, React uses keys to match children across renders. A stable, unique key (like a database ID) tells React which items have changed, been added, or removed. This allows React to reorder existing DOM nodes rather than unmounting and remounting them, preserving component state and improving performance.

Why Index as Key is Problematic

Using the array index as a key is an anti-pattern. If the list order changes (e.g., sorting, insertion, deletion), the index no longer corresponds to the same item. React may reuse the wrong DOM node, leading to state bugs (e.g., input values persisting on wrong rows) or incorrect animations. It can also cause unnecessary re-renders.

Production Importance

In large lists or dynamic UIs, proper key usage prevents performance bottlenecks and subtle UI bugs. It is a common interview topic because misuse is a frequent source of issues in real-world React applications.

Common Pitfall

Using key={index} when the list can be reordered or filtered. Always prefer a unique identifier from your data, or generate a stable ID if none exists.

"}]
qgen_v2:5d14a31db30deb7a:
[{"question":"Explain how React's reconciliation algorithm decides whether to update, unmount, or remount a component when the virtual DOM changes.","expectedPoints":["Compares element type at same position; if type changes, unmount and remount.","If type same, checks props; if props unchanged, skip update; if changed, update props and preserve state.","Defining a component inside another creates new function reference each render, causing remount.","Key prop helps track elements in lists; stable keys prevent unnecessary remounts.","State is lost on type change but preserved on prop change."],"followUps":["What happens if you use array index as key in a list?","How would you debug a component that resets its state unexpectedly?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box with different toys. If you replace a toy car with a toy plane, you throw away the car and get a new plane. But if you keep the same car and just change its color, you keep the car and paint it. React does the same with components: if the type changes, it destroys the old one and creates a new one; if the type stays the same, it just updates the props.","detailedExplanation":"

Reconciliation is React's algorithm for efficiently updating the DOM when state or props change. It compares the new virtual DOM tree with the previous one, starting from the root and working down.

Decision Process

  • Element type changed? If the type (e.g., <div> to <span>, or <Foo> to <Bar>) differs at the same position, React unmounts the old component (destroying its state and effects) and mounts a new one.
  • Same type? React then checks props. If props are unchanged (via shallow comparison), it skips the update for that subtree. If props changed, it updates the existing instance, preserving state and re-running effects as needed.

Common Pitfall: Inline Component Definitions

Defining a child component inside a parent function creates a new function reference on every render. React sees a different 'type' each time, causing unmount + remount on every render, resetting state. Example:

function Parent() { const Child = () => <div>...</div>; return <Child />; }

This is a killer trap for senior engineers to spot.

The key Prop

In lists, key helps React match elements across renders. Use stable, unique IDs (not array index) to avoid unnecessary remounts when items are reordered or filtered.

Why It Matters in Production

Understanding reconciliation prevents performance issues (unnecessary remounts) and state bugs (unexpected resets). It's essential for optimizing large component trees and debugging mysterious behavior.

"}]
qgen_v2:61687007fe508c5c:
[{"question":"You are building a multi-step checkout form with fields for shipping address, payment method, and order summary. The state includes interdependent fields (e.g., shipping method affects total cost). Would you use useState or useReducer? Explain your reasoning.","expectedPoints":["Recognizes that interdependent fields and complex logic favor useReducer.","Mentions that useReducer centralizes state transitions for easier testing/debugging.","Acknowledges that useReducer handles multiple action types (e.g., UPDATE_SHIPPING, APPLY_COUPON) cleanly.","Considers trade-off: useReducer adds boilerplate vs useState simplicity.","Optionally suggests pairing useReducer with Context for global state."],"followUps":["How would you test the reducer function in isolation?","What if the form only had two independent fields (e.g., name and email)? Would your answer change?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box with many different toys that can change each other. If you have just one toy (like a ball), it's easy to remember its state. But if you have many toys that interact (like a train set where adding a car changes the track), you need a rulebook (reducer) to keep everything organized. useState is like remembering one toy; useReducer is like having a rulebook for many toys.","detailedExplanation":"

When building a multi-step checkout form with interdependent fields (e.g., shipping method affects total cost, coupon code changes discount), the decision between useState and useReducer hinges on complexity and maintainability.

What it is

useState is a React hook for managing simple, independent state values. useReducer is an alternative that accepts a reducer function and an initial state, dispatching actions to update state in a predictable way.

How it works

With useReducer, you define a reducer function that takes the current state and an action, and returns the next state. Actions are dispatched like { type: 'UPDATE_SHIPPING', payload: 'express' }. This centralizes all state transitions, making them explicit and testable.

Why it matters in production

In a checkout form, state updates often depend on previous state (e.g., toggling a coupon might recalculate totals). Using useReducer avoids stale state bugs that can occur with useState when multiple updates are batched. It also simplifies debugging because each state change is logged as an action. Additionally, the reducer can be unit tested independently of React components, ensuring reliability.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is over-engineering: using useReducer for trivial state (e.g., a single boolean toggle) adds unnecessary boilerplate. Stick to useState for simple, independent values. For interdependent fields with multiple actions, useReducer is the right choice.

In summary, for a checkout form with interdependent fields and multiple action types, useReducer provides a scalable, testable, and maintainable solution. Pairing it with React Context can also avoid prop drilling without adding external libraries.

"}]
qgen_v2:fbbff23a1a737d7c:
[{"question":"When would you choose useMemo over useCallback, and vice versa? Give a concrete example where using the wrong one could cause a bug or performance issue.","expectedPoints":["useMemo returns a memoized value; useCallback returns a memoized function.","useMemo for expensive computations; useCallback for stable function references passed to memoized children.","Wrong choice example: using useCallback for a computed value (returns function, not value) or using useMemo for a function (returns result, not reference).","Mention that useCallback is essentially useMemo(() => fn, deps) for functions.","Profile before optimizing; over-memoization can hurt performance."],"followUps":["How would you debug a situation where a child wrapped in React.memo is still re-rendering?","What happens if you omit the dependency array in useMemo or useCallback?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that does a slow calculation, like counting all the stars in a picture. useMemo remembers the answer so you don't have to count again unless the picture changes. useCallback is like remembering which button to press to start the counting—it gives you the same button every time so your friend (a child component) doesn't get confused and redo everything.","detailedExplanation":"

useMemo and useCallback are React hooks that help optimize performance by avoiding unnecessary work during re-renders. They both rely on memoization, but they serve different purposes.

What they do

useMemo memoizes the result of a computation. It takes a function and a dependency array, and only re-executes the function when dependencies change. The returned value is the computed result. Use it for expensive calculations or derived state that shouldn't be recalculated on every render.

useCallback memoizes a function reference. It takes a function and a dependency array, and returns the same function instance across renders unless dependencies change. Use it when passing callbacks to child components wrapped in React.memo or using useEffect that depend on reference equality.

How they work

Internally, useCallback(fn, deps) is equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps). The difference is semantic: useMemo is for values, useCallback is for functions. Using the wrong one can lead to bugs. For example, using useMemo to memoize a function would return the function's return value, not the function itself, breaking the intended behavior.

Why it matters in production

In large component trees, unnecessary re-renders can cause jank. Memoization helps reduce work, but it's not free. Each hook adds memory overhead and dependency comparison cost. Overusing them can actually degrade performance. The rule of thumb: profile first. Only add memoization when you measure a real bottleneck, such as a slow render or a child re-rendering due to new prop references.

Common pitfall

A frequent mistake is wrapping every function or value in useMemo/useCallback without profiling. This can make code harder to read and may not improve performance. Another pitfall is incorrect dependency arrays—missing dependencies can cause stale closures, while including unnecessary ones defeats memoization.

"}]

[{"question":"You have a table with 10,000 rows that needs to support sorting and filtering. Walk me through your optimization strategy, in order, and explain why you would choose each step.","expectedPoints":["Start with virtualization (e.g., react-window) to render only visible rows","Add pagination or infinite scroll with IntersectionObserver for UX","Use React.memo with stable refs to prevent unnecessary re-renders","Apply useMemo for sort/filter computations to avoid recomputation","Use stable keys (unique IDs) and CSS containment for browser optimization","Consider useDeferredValue or Web Workers for heavy operations"],"followUps":["How would you measure the performance improvement at each step?","What trade-offs would you consider between virtualization and pagination?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge list of toys, and you can only see a few at a time. Virtualization is like only drawing the toys you can see, not all 10,000. Pagination is like turning pages in a book. React.memo is like remembering which toys haven't changed so you don't redraw them. useMemo is like doing math only when needed. Stable keys are like giving each toy a unique name so the computer doesn't get confused. CSS containment is like telling the browser to only worry about the toy you're looking at. useDeferredValue is like waiting a tiny bit to update the list so typing stays smooth. Web Workers are like asking a friend to do the heavy math in another room.","detailedExplanation":"

When dealing with a large dataset (10,000 rows), the goal is to minimize DOM work, JavaScript execution, and layout recalculations. The optimization steps should be applied in order, measuring after each to ensure real improvement.

1. Virtualization

Use libraries like react-window or TanStack Virtual to render only the rows currently visible in the viewport. This is the biggest win because it reduces the number of DOM nodes from 10,000 to maybe 20-30. The browser only paints what's visible, drastically reducing layout and paint time.

2. Pagination / Infinite Scroll

Combine virtualization with pagination (e.g., 50-100 rows per page) or infinite scroll using IntersectionObserver. This improves UX by loading data in chunks and reduces initial load time. However, virtualization alone may still cause performance issues if the entire dataset is kept in memory.

3. React.memo + Stable Refs

Wrap row components with React.memo to prevent re-renders when props haven't changed. Ensure that callback props (e.g., onClick) are stable using useCallback or useRef. Without this, every parent re-render would cause all rows to re-render, even if they are virtualized.

4. useMemo for Sort/Filter

Use useMemo to memoize the sorted/filtered array so that it's only recomputed when dependencies (e.g., sort key, filter text) change. This avoids expensive array operations on every parent re-render.

5. Stable Keys

Always use unique, stable IDs as keys (e.g., database IDs). Never use array index, because index-based keys cause incorrect state reuse and unnecessary re-renders when the list order changes (e.g., after sorting).

6. CSS Containment

Apply contain: layout style paint on each row to tell the browser that changes inside a row don't affect outside elements. This reduces the browser's layout and paint work per row.

7. Defer Updates (React 18+)

Use useDeferredValue or startTransition to mark the list update as non-urgent. This keeps the input responsive while the list updates in the background.

8. Web Worker

As a last resort, offload heavy sort/filter computations to a Web Worker to avoid blocking the main thread. This is only needed if the dataset is extremely large or the operations are complex.

Common pitfall: Applying all optimizations upfront without measuring. Always profile with browser DevTools (Performance tab) to identify the actual bottleneck. Often, virtualization alone solves 90% of the problem.

"}]

[{"question":"You need to fetch user data from three independent APIs, but you only care about the first successful response. Which promise combinator would you use, and why?","expectedPoints":["Identifies Promise.any as the correct combinator.","Explains that Promise.any resolves with the first fulfilled promise.","Mentions that if all reject, it returns an AggregateError.","Distinguishes from Promise.race which settles on first rejection.","Provides a real-world use case like fallback CDN or redundant services."],"followUps":["How would you handle the case where all three APIs fail?","What is the difference between Promise.any and Promise.race in terms of rejection behavior?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have three different ice cream shops, and you want the first one that can give you a cone. Promise.any is like asking all three at once and taking the first one that says 'yes'. If all say 'no', you get a sad message. Promise.race would take the first answer even if it's 'no', which is not what you want.","detailedExplanation":"

Promise.any is the correct choice when you need the first successful (fulfilled) promise from a set of independent operations. It resolves with the value of the first promise that fulfills, ignoring any rejections until all promises have rejected, at which point it rejects with an AggregateError containing all rejection reasons.

How it works

Given an iterable of promises, Promise.any returns a single promise that resolves as soon as one of the input promises fulfills. If no promise fulfills (i.e., all reject), the returned promise rejects with an AggregateError.

Why it matters in production

This combinator is ideal for fallback patterns where you have multiple redundant services (e.g., CDNs, API endpoints) and you want the fastest successful response. It improves resilience and latency by not waiting for a slow or failing service.

Common pitfall

Developers often confuse Promise.any with Promise.race. Promise.race settles on the first promise that settles (either fulfills or rejects), so if the first promise rejects, Promise.race rejects immediately. Promise.any only rejects if all promises reject, making it safer for fallback scenarios.

"}]

[{"question":"Implement a custom React hook `useExponentialBackoff` that retries an async function up to `maxRetries` times with exponential backoff and jitter. The hook should expose `execute`, `loading`, `error`, and `retryCount`.","expectedPoints":["Implements exponential backoff formula: delay = baseDelay * 2^attempt + random jitter","Handles max retries and stops after exhaustion","Exposes loading, error, retryCount states and an execute function","Avoids retrying on non-retryable errors (e.g., 4xx) or idempotency issues","Uses useRef or useCallback to avoid stale closures"],"followUps":["How would you add a cancellation mechanism to stop retries on unmount?","What if the async function is not idempotent? How would you prevent duplicate side effects?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're trying to call a friend but the line is busy. Instead of giving up, you wait a little longer each time before calling again, and you add a tiny random extra wait so you and your other friends don't all call at the exact same moment. This hook does that for computer requests.","detailedExplanation":"

The exponential backoff retry pattern is a strategy for handling transient failures in network requests. Instead of immediately retrying after a failure, the client waits an exponentially increasing amount of time before each subsequent attempt. Adding jitter (randomness) prevents the thundering herd problem, where many clients retry simultaneously after a server recovers, overwhelming it again.

Implementation Details

The hook should manage state for loading, error, and retryCount. The core logic uses the formula: delay = baseDelay * 2^attempt + random(0, jitterMax). The execute function is an async function that wraps the original async operation, catches errors, and schedules retries using setTimeout with the computed delay. It must track the current attempt count and stop after maxRetries.

Why It Matters in Production

In payment SDKs or any critical system, network failures are inevitable. Exponential backoff with jitter ensures that retries are spaced out, reducing server load and increasing the chance of success. Without jitter, all clients would retry at the same time, causing a spike. The pattern also respects idempotency: retrying a non-idempotent operation (e.g., charging a credit card) could cause duplicate charges. Therefore, the hook should allow the caller to specify which errors are retryable (e.g., only 5xx or network errors, not 4xx).

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is forgetting to clean up timers when the component unmounts. If the user navigates away, a retry scheduled via setTimeout could still fire and update state on an unmounted component, causing memory leaks or errors. Always use a cleanup function in useEffect or a ref to cancel pending retries.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain how event delegation works and why it's preferred over attaching individual event listeners to each child element.","expectedPoints":["Attaches a single listener to a parent container instead of N listeners to each child.","Uses event bubbling to capture events from child elements.","Checks e.target or e.target.closest to identify the actual child that triggered the event.","Reduces memory usage and avoids rebinding for dynamically added elements.","Common pitfall: forgetting to stop propagation when needed, or not using closest() for nested elements."],"followUps":["How would you handle a scenario where you need to stop event delegation for a specific child?","What are the performance implications of event delegation in a large list with thousands of items?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big box of toys and you want to know when a child picks any toy. Instead of putting a sensor on each toy, you put one sensor on the box. When a toy is picked, the box sensor tells you which toy it was. That's event delegation: one listener on the parent catches events from all children.","detailedExplanation":"

Event delegation is a pattern where a single event listener is attached to a parent element to manage events for all its children, leveraging event bubbling. When an event occurs on a child, it bubbles up to the parent, where the listener checks the event.target to determine which child triggered it.

How It Works

Instead of adding a listener to each child (O(N) listeners), you add one listener to the container (O(1)). Inside the handler, you use e.target.closest('.child-selector') to find the actual child element. This automatically handles dynamically added children because the parent listener remains attached.

Why It Matters in Production

  • Memory efficiency: Fewer listeners reduce memory usage, especially in large lists or tables.
  • Dynamic content: Newly added elements are automatically covered without rebinding.
  • Single source of truth: Easier maintenance and debugging.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to use closest() can lead to issues when the target is a nested element (e.g., a span inside a button). Also, if you call e.stopPropagation() inside a child handler, delegation may break. Always ensure the parent listener is not accidentally removed or blocked.

"}]

In a React dApp, handling wallet connection and transaction lifecycle is crucial for a smooth user experience. The process begins with detecting the provider (e.g., window.ethereum injected by MetaMask). You should check for its existence and then request account access using provider.request({ method: 'eth_requestAccounts' }). Once connected, you can create a provider (for reading) and a signer (for writing) using libraries like ethers.js or wagmi.

Transaction Lifecycle

For write operations, you prepare a transaction object (to, data, value) and send it via the signer. The user must confirm in their wallet. The transaction goes through states: pending (waiting for confirmation), confirmed (mined), or failed (reverted or out of gas). Your UI should reflect these states, showing loading indicators and the transaction hash. After confirmation, refetch relevant data to avoid stale state, as blockchain state changes asynchronously.

Common Pitfalls

  • Network Mismatch: Ensure the user's wallet is on the correct network. Use provider.getNetwork() and prompt the user to switch if needed.
  • Error Handling: Transactions can fail due to insufficient gas or contract reverts. Catch errors and display user-friendly messages.
  • UX for Loading: Transaction confirmations can take seconds to minutes. Show a loading spinner and the transaction hash so users can track progress.

Production Considerations

In production, use modern hooks like wagmi's useAccount, useSendTransaction, and useWaitForTransaction to manage state declaratively. Always handle edge cases like wallet disconnection or multiple accounts. By following these patterns, you build a robust dApp that users trust.

"}]

[{"question":"What are the key differences between an EOA and a smart contract wallet, and why would a dApp choose one over the other?","expectedPoints":["EOA controlled by private key; smart wallet by contract logic.","Smart wallets support batch transactions, social recovery, and gas abstraction.","EOAs are simpler but limited; smart wallets offer better UX at higher complexity.","Choice depends on tradeoff between security, user experience, and development cost."],"followUps":["How would you implement gas abstraction in a smart wallet?","What are the security risks of using a smart contract wallet compared to an EOA?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a piggy bank (EOA) that you can only open with one key. If you lose the key, your money is gone. A smart wallet is like a bank account with rules: you can have multiple guardians to help you if you forget your password, you can pay fees with different currencies, and you can do several things in one go, like buying and approving at the same time.","detailedExplanation":"

The core distinction between an Externally Owned Account (EOA) and a Smart Contract Wallet lies in control and flexibility. An EOA is controlled solely by a private key—like MetaMask or a hardware wallet. It can only sign and send transactions, must pay gas in ETH, and recovery is limited to seed phrases. Batch transactions are impossible.

A Smart Contract Wallet is governed by contract code, enabling custom logic such as multisig, session keys, and spending limits. It supports gas abstraction (paymaster can sponsor fees in any token), social recovery (guardians can restore access), and batch transactions (e.g., approve + swap in one call). Examples include Safe, Argent, and Fun.xyz.

Why It Matters in Production

For dApps targeting mainstream adoption, smart wallets dramatically improve user experience. Users can pay gas in USDC, recover accounts without seed phrases, and execute complex operations in a single click. However, they introduce higher development complexity, potential smart contract bugs, and reliance on off-chain infrastructure (e.g., relayers).

Common Pitfall

Assuming smart wallets are always better. For simple use cases (e.g., a one-time transfer), an EOA is cheaper and more secure. Over-engineering with a smart wallet can lead to unnecessary gas costs and attack surface.

"}]

[{"question":"How does EIP-4337 enable gas payment in ERC-20 tokens without changing the Ethereum protocol?","expectedPoints":["UserOperation is a pseudo-transaction sent to an alt-mempool.","Bundler aggregates UserOps into a single on-chain transaction.","Paymaster contract pays gas on behalf of the user, can be reimbursed in ERC-20.","EntryPoint contract verifies and executes UserOps, coordinating with Paymaster.","No protocol change needed; all logic is in smart contracts."],"followUps":["What role does the Bundler play in this architecture?","How does the Paymaster ensure it gets reimbursed?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to buy a toy but only have a gift card, not cash. Normally, the store only takes cash. But a friend (Bundler) collects your gift card and other kids' gift cards, then pays cash for all of you together. Another friend (Paymaster) lends you cash if you promise to pay back with your gift card later. This way, you can buy the toy without needing cash yourself.","detailedExplanation":"

EIP-4337 introduces account abstraction by allowing users to send UserOperations (pseudo-transactions) to an alt-mempool, separate from the regular Ethereum mempool. These UserOps are not real transactions until a Bundler (off-chain node) aggregates multiple UserOps into a single on-chain transaction and submits it to the EntryPoint contract.

How Gas Payment in ERC-20 Works

When a user has no ETH for gas, they can specify a Paymaster contract in their UserOp. The Paymaster is an optional contract that pays the gas fee on behalf of the user. The EntryPoint contract verifies the UserOp's signature, then calls the Paymaster to validate and pay for the gas. After execution, the Paymaster can be reimbursed in ERC-20 tokens (e.g., USDC) via a pre-approved transfer or other mechanisms.

Why It Matters in Production

This architecture enables seamless UX for dApps like Fun.xyz: users can pay gas in stablecoins, batch multiple actions (e.g., approve + swap) in one transaction, use session keys for gaming, and recover wallets socially. All without modifying the Ethereum protocol itself.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is assuming the Paymaster can arbitrarily sponsor gas without risk. In practice, the Paymaster must implement proper validation to prevent reentrancy attacks or spam (e.g., a user draining the Paymaster's ETH). The Paymaster should verify the UserOp's sender and call data, and use a paymasterData field for off-chain agreements.

"}]

This question tests your ability to design a frontend abstraction that simplifies complex cross-chain workflows while addressing real-world risks like bridge hacks, latency, and gas management.

Core Design

The frontend should expose a single FunKit SDK method that takes the user's source asset and desired destination asset, then internally orchestrates bridging, swapping, and payment. The SDK should abstract away chain-specific details and provide a unified promise-based interface.

Key Considerations

  • Bridge Risk: Use a whitelist of audited bridges or a decentralized bridge aggregator. Show a risk disclaimer and allow users to choose between speed and security.
  • Latency: Implement optimistic UI with progress steps (e.g., 'Bridging...', 'Swapping...', 'Confirming...'). Use polling or WebSocket to update status without blocking the UI.

[{"question":"Why does a failed Ethereum transaction still cost gas, and how would you handle this in a frontend SDK to avoid burning user funds?","expectedPoints":["EVM performs computation before revert, consuming gas.","Validators must be compensated for work done.","State is rolled back but gas is not refunded.","Use estimateGas and eth_call simulation before signing.","Catch reverts early to prevent user from paying for failures."],"followUps":["How would you simulate a transaction using ethers.js or web3.js?","What if estimateGas itself fails? How do you handle that gracefully?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you order a pizza but change your mind after the chef starts making it. You still have to pay for the ingredients and work already done, even though you don't get the pizza. Similarly, a failed transaction uses computer work on the blockchain, so you still pay for that work.","detailedExplanation":"

Why a failed transaction still costs gas: The Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) executes the transaction's code until it hits a REVERT opcode or runs out of gas. During execution, computational resources (CPU, memory, storage) are consumed. Validators must be compensated for this work, regardless of the transaction's success. The state is rolled back, but the gas spent is not refunded because the work was already done.

Gas Fee Components (EIP-1559)

  • Gas Limit: Maximum gas units the user authorizes (safety ceiling).
  • Max Fee Per Gas: Upper bound user is willing to pay per gas unit.
  • Base Fee: Set by network demand; burned (removed from supply).
  • Priority Fee (Tip): Paid to validator as incentive to include the transaction.
  • Gas Used: Actual computation cost (≤ Gas Limit).

SDK Implication

To avoid burning user funds on failed transactions, always simulate the transaction before asking the user to sign. Use estimateGas to get an approximate gas limit, and eth_call (or provider.call()) to simulate execution off-chain. If the simulation reverts, catch the error and inform the user without sending a real transaction.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on estimateGas without simulating the call. estimateGas may succeed even if the transaction would revert due to state changes (e.g., a token approval that later fails). Always combine estimateGas with a dry-run eth_call to catch reverts early.

"}]

Designing a React SDK for embedding a chat widget requires balancing performance, customization, and developer experience. The goal is to provide a drop-in component that works seamlessly across different host environments.

Key Design Decisions

  • Bundle Size & Isolation: Use a single, minified bundle with React and ReactDOM as externals to avoid duplication. For CSS isolation, use Shadow DOM or an iframe to prevent style leaks. Shadow DOM is lighter but may have accessibility limitations; iframes offer full isolation but add overhead.
  • API Surface: Expose a React component (e.g., <ChatWidget apiKey='...' />) with props for theming, position, and callbacks. Provide a standalone script for non-React sites that mounts the widget into a container div.
  • Lifecycle Management: Use useEffect cleanup to destroy the widget on unmount, preventing memory leaks. Lazy-load the widget's core logic (e.g., via dynamic import()) to avoid blocking page load.
  • Versioning & Compatibility: Follow semver and provide a migration guide. Use peer dependencies for React to avoid version conflicts. For host sites with a different React version, consider using React's context isolation or a wrapper that uses its own React instance.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is not handling multiple instances. If a host site mounts two widgets, they must share state (e.g., WebSocket connection) without conflicts. Use a singleton pattern for the core service and unique IDs for each instance.

In production, this design ensures the widget is performant (small bundle, lazy loading), non-intrusive (no CSS conflicts), and easy to integrate (simple API, good docs).

"}]

When embedding a checkout widget that must be PCI-compliant, support React apps, and simple static sites, a hybrid embedding strategy (similar to Stripe's pattern) is the industry standard. Key Strategies

  • Iframe for PCI compliance: Payment input fields must be PCI-compliant. An iframe provides a sandboxed environment that isolates the payment form from the parent page, preventing CSS conflicts and script access.   • NPM package for React apps: For modern web apps using bundlers (Webpack, Vite), an NPM package offers type safety (TypeScript), version control, and tree-shaking to reduce bundle size. It requires a build setup but integrates seamlessly with component lifecycles.
  • Script tag for simple sites: For static HTML sites or quick integrations, a script tag is easiest. It auto-updates via CDN but pollutes the global namespace and lacks type checking.

In production, you might offer multiple integration paths: a script tag for simple sites, an NPM package for React/Vue/Angular, and an iframe for payment flows. This is the Stripe pattern. A common pitfall is assuming one size fits all; instead, provide clear documentation for each method and handle graceful degradation when the SDK fails to load (e.g., show a fallback message).

How would you ensure status consistency across client disconnections?","expectedPoints":["Uses WebSocket with polling fallback for real-time updates","Handles reorgs by tracking confirmation count and allowing status reversal","Separates source and destination chain tracking for cross-chain transactions","Implements resubscription and event replay on client reconnect"],"followUps":["How would you determine the confirmation count threshold per chain?","What mechanism would you use to replay missed events after reconnection?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're tracking a package delivery across two different shipping companies. This question tests your ability to design a distributed system that provides real-time transaction status updates across multiple blockchains, handling common pitfalls like reorgs and network interruptions.

Core Architecture

The system uses a WebSocket connection from the client to an API Gateway, which authenticates the user and subscribes them to a channel. The gateway communicates with a Status Aggregator (pub/sub) that receives events from chain-specific Chain Watchers. Each watcher connects to RPC nodes (e.g., Alchemy, Infura) via WebSocket and polls as a fallback. Transaction data is stored in a TX Database with fields like txHash, chainId, status, and confirmations.

Handling Reorgs

Blockchain reorganizations can cause a transaction's status to revert (e.g., from confirmed back to pending). To handle this, the system must track confirmation count (e.g., 12 blocks for Ethereum, 15 for BSC) and allow status to go backward. The UI should show a 'pending' state with a warning if a reorg occurs, and only mark as final after enough confirmations.

Cross-Chain Transactions

For cross-chain operations, the source chain's completion does not guarantee the destination chain's status. The system must track both sides independently, with separate entries in the database. The aggregator correlates them via a shared identifier (e.g., a unique transaction ID).

Client Reconnection

When a client disconnects, the server must resubscribe the user to their channels and replay missed events. This can be done by storing a sequence number or timestamp per subscription and sending all events after that point. The client should also implement a polling fallback to catch any gaps.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is assuming a transaction is final after a single block confirmation. This ignores reorgs and can lead to incorrect UI states. Always use chain-specific confirmation thresholds and allow status reversals.

"}]

[{"question":"Design a multi-layer cache strategy for a token price widget that 1000 users open simultaneously. How would you coordinate client memory, browser cache, CDN, and server cache to minimize latency while ensuring price freshness?","expectedPoints":["Explains each layer's role: client memory (Zustand, TTL 30s), browser cache (Cache-Control: max-age=15, stale-while-revalidate), CDN (stale-while-revalidate, geographic), server cache (Redis, TTL 5-15s), origin (aggregator).","Describes client-side deduplication using a Map to share in-flight requests across components.","Addresses stale-while-revalidate pattern to serve stale data while fetching fresh data in background.","Mentions the 'slippage trap' and need to refresh price right before swap submission.","Considers trade-offs between latency and freshness, e.g., TTL values and cache invalidation."],"followUps":["How would you handle cache invalidation when a user manually refreshes the price?","What happens if the CDN cache is stale and the origin is slow? How do you ensure the user sees a recent price?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a lemonade stand and many customers want to know the price. Instead of asking the supplier every time, you write the price on a board (client memory), keep a copy in your notebook (browser cache), have a friend at the corner (CDN) who also knows the price, and only call the supplier (origin) when needed. This way, customers get the price fast, but you still check the supplier occasionally to make sure the price is correct.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a multi-layer cache strategy for a high-traffic real-time data widget. The goal is to minimize latency while maintaining price freshness, especially when 1000 users open the widget simultaneously.

Layer Breakdown

  • Client Memory (Zustand): Store the price in a global state with a TTL of 30 seconds. This provides ~0ms access for components on the same page. Use a Map to deduplicate in-flight requests: if two components request the same price, they share the same Promise.
  • Browser Cache (HTTP): Set Cache-Control: max-age=15, stale-while-revalidate. This allows the browser to serve a cached response for 15 seconds, and then serve stale data while revalidating in the background (5ms latency).
  • CDN Edge (Cloudflare): Use stale-while-revalidate at the edge with geographic distribution. Latency ~20-50ms. The CDN can serve stale data and fetch fresh data from the origin asynchronously.
  • Server Cache (Redis): A region-aware cache with TTL 5-15 seconds. Latency ~5-10ms. This reduces load on the origin aggregator.
  • Origin (Aggregator): Fetches from multiple sources (Chainlink, CoinGecko) with latency 100-500ms. This is the source of truth but slow.

Coordination & Pitfalls

Use client-side deduplication to avoid redundant network calls. Implement a Map that maps request keys to Promises; when a new request comes, check if a Promise exists, if so return it, otherwise create a new one and store it.

A common pitfall is the slippage trap: if a user sees a stale price and submits a swap, the actual price may have moved, causing financial loss. Always refresh the price right before swap submission, bypassing caches if necessary.

In production, you must balance freshness vs. latency. Use shorter TTLs for volatile assets, and longer for stable ones. Monitor cache hit rates and adjust TTLs dynamically.

"}]

[{"question":"Design an error handling architecture for a payment SDK that categorizes errors and provides appropriate UX responses.","expectedPoints":["Categorizes errors into distinct types (user, network, wallet, chain, SDK bug, provider).","Defines a structured error class with machine-readable code, developer message, user message, cause, and retryable flag.","Specifies UX response per category (inline form error, auto-retry, toast, simulation, generic message, status page).","Considers retry logic with backoff and fallback mechanisms.","Includes logging/monitoring (e.g., Sentry) for SDK bugs."],"followUps":["How would you handle retry logic for network errors to avoid overwhelming the user?","What additional information would you include in the error object for debugging?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a vending machine that takes money and gives snacks. Sometimes you put in the wrong coins (user error), sometimes the machine loses internet (network error), sometimes you change your mind (wallet error). You need to tell the person what went wrong in a way they understand, and maybe try again automatically if it's a temporary problem. That's what this error handling system does for a payment app.","detailedExplanation":"

This question asks you to design an error handling architecture for a payment SDK, which is critical for production reliability and user experience. The goal is to categorize errors so that each type gets an appropriate response, both for the developer (debugging) and the end user (UX).

Error Categories

The architecture defines six categories: User Error (4xx, e.g., invalid input), Network Error (connection lost), Wallet Error (user rejects sign), Chain Error (transaction revert), SDK Bug (misconfiguration), and Provider Error (backend down). Each has a distinct UX response: inline form error with actionable fix, auto-retry with backoff, toast with guidance, simulate before sign, generic message with Sentry logging, and status page link with retry.

Error Class Structure

The FunPayError class extends Error and includes fields: code (machine-readable enum), message (developer-facing), userMessage (end-user friendly), cause (original error), and retryable (boolean). This structure allows consistent error handling across the SDK.

Why It Matters in Production

In a payment SDK, errors must be handled gracefully to avoid losing transactions or confusing users. Categorizing errors helps decide whether to retry automatically (network), show a clear message (user error), or escalate to developers (SDK bug). The retryable flag enables safe retry logic with exponential backoff, preventing server overload.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is treating all errors the same, e.g., showing a generic 'Something went wrong' for every failure. This frustrates users and hides actionable information. Another pitfall is not including the original error (cause) in the error object, making debugging harder. Always preserve the root cause for logging.

"}]

[{"question":"Tell me about a time you had to make a significant technical decision on a frontend project. How did you approach it and what was the outcome?","expectedPoints":["Uses STAR structure clearly","Demonstrates ownership and accountability","Shows hypergrowth mindset (e.g., scaling, trade-offs)","Highlights attention to detail and intrinsic motivation","Result is measurable or impactful"],"followUps":["What alternatives did you consider and why did you choose that one?","How did you ensure the team was aligned with your decision?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"behavioral","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friends are building a giant Lego castle. You have to decide whether to build a strong foundation first or add cool towers right away. You explain your choice by telling a story about a time you made a big decision, what you did, and how it turned out. That's what this question is about.","detailedExplanation":"

This behavioral question is designed to assess how you handle real-world technical challenges in a frontend role. Interviewers use the STAR framework (Situation, Task, Action, Result) to evaluate your problem-solving process and soft skills.

What It Is

You are asked to describe a specific instance where you made a significant technical decision—like choosing a state management library, refactoring a component, or adopting a new build tool. The goal is to see if you take ownership, consider trade-offs, and drive results.

How to Structure Your Answer

  • Situation: Set the context. What project were you working on? What was the team size? What constraints existed (time, resources, legacy code)?
  • Task: What was your responsibility? Were you the lead, or did you influence the decision?
  • Action: Describe the steps you took. Did you research alternatives? Run a spike? Consult teammates? How did you weigh pros and cons? Emphasize your attention to detail and intrinsic motivation to improve the system.
  • Result: Quantify the outcome. Did it improve performance, reduce bugs, speed up development? Use metrics like load time reduction, developer satisfaction, or fewer production incidents.

Why It Matters in Production

Senior engineers are expected to make decisions that affect the entire product. This question reveals whether you can balance hypergrowth needs (e.g., scalability, maintainability) with practical constraints. A strong answer shows you don't just follow trends but think critically about long-term impact.

Common Pitfall

Don't describe a decision that was obvious or had no trade-offs. Interviewers want to see you wrestle with ambiguity. Also, avoid taking sole credit—acknowledge team collaboration to show you're a team player.

"}]
qgen_v2:84f5934627128345:
[{"question":"Walk me through a recent project where you had to make a significant technical decision. Use the STAR framework, focusing on the action you took.","expectedPoints":["Situation: Brief context (team size, business problem) in under 30 seconds","Task: Clearly states their specific role and ownership","Action: Deep technical details, trade-offs considered, alternatives ruled out","Result: Quantified metrics or business impact, lessons learned"],"followUps":["What was the biggest trade-off you had to make, and why did you choose that path?","If you could go back, would you change anything about your approach?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"behavioral","childExplanation":"Imagine you're telling a story about building a LEGO castle. You need to quickly say who you built it with and what the problem was (like the tower kept falling). Then spend most of the time explaining exactly how you fixed it—what pieces you chose and why. Finally, show how it turned out—did it stand tall? That's the STAR way.","detailedExplanation":"

The STAR framework (Situation, Task, Action, Result) is a structured method for answering behavioral interview questions. It ensures your answer is concise, focused, and impactful.

What it is

STAR breaks down a story into four parts: Situation (context), Task (your role), Action (what you did), and Result (outcome). For senior roles, the Action section should dominate (60-70% of your time) because it demonstrates your technical depth and decision-making.

How it works

  • Situation (10-15%): Set the scene briefly—team size, business problem, user pain. Keep it under 30 seconds.
  • Task (5-10%): Clarify your specific ownership. What were you personally responsible for? 15-20 seconds.
  • Action (60-70%): Dive into the details. What technical decisions did you make? What trade-offs did you consider? What alternatives did you rule out? This is where you prove seniority by showing depth.
  • Result (10-15%): Share metrics, numbers, business impact, and lessons learned. Keep it under 30 seconds.

Why it matters in production

Interviewers want to see how you think and solve problems. A well-structured STAR answer shows you can communicate clearly, prioritize effectively, and deliver results. It also prevents you from rambling or focusing too much on context.

Common pitfall

Spending 70% of your time on Situation (describing the team or product) and only 20% on Action. This tells the interviewer nothing about you. Always invert the ratio: let your actions speak louder than the setup.

"}]
