[{"question":"Tell me about a time you identified and fixed a subtle edge case or race condition that others missed.","expectedPoints":["Describes a specific bug with clear technical details (e.g., race condition, async timing).","Explains how they discovered it (e.g., code review, testing, production monitoring).","Shows ownership: took initiative to fix and verify the solution.","Demonstrates attention to detail and impact (e.g., prevented data loss, improved reliability).","Reflects on what they learned or how they changed their testing approach."],"followUps":["How did you ensure the fix didn't introduce new issues?","What would you do differently to catch this earlier in the development cycle?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"behavioral","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a LEGO tower and you notice a tiny crack in one brick that could make the whole tower fall later. You fix it before anyone else sees it. That's what this question is about—finding small, hidden problems that could cause big trouble later.","detailedExplanation":"

This behavioral question targets attention to detail and ownership—two traits explicitly valued by Fun.xyz. The interviewer wants to see if you can identify subtle issues that others overlook, especially in complex, asynchronous systems.

What They're Looking For

A strong answer includes a real-world example where you caught a race condition, edge case, or precision bug that could have caused data corruption, UI glitches, or performance degradation. Describe the context (e.g., a feature with concurrent API calls), how you discovered the bug (e.g., during code review, stress testing, or production monitoring), and the steps you took to fix it.

How to Structure Your Answer

  • Situation: Briefly set the scene—what feature, team, and technology stack.
  • Task: What was the expected behavior? Why was the bug hard to find?
  • Action: Detail your debugging process: how you reproduced the issue, root cause analysis, and the fix (e.g., adding locks, debouncing, or reordering async calls).
  • Result: Quantify impact—e.g., reduced error rate from X% to Y%, prevented data loss, or improved user experience.
  • Reflection: What testing practices or code review improvements did you suggest to prevent similar bugs?

Common Pitfall

Don't pick a trivial bug like a typo in CSS. The bug should be non-obvious and require deep understanding of the system's concurrency or state management. Also, avoid blaming others—focus on your proactive role.

"}]

This behavioral question assesses your ability to navigate technical conflict professionally. Senior engineers are expected to advocate for their ideas while respecting others' expertise.

What It Is

Interviewers want a real example where you disagreed with a senior colleague on a technical decision. The focus is on your process: how you presented your case, listened to feedback, and reached a resolution.

How to Structure Your Answer

Use the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result). For example, from your 'Disagreement with Senior' story: describe the project, the specific disagreement (e.g., architecture choice), the data you gathered (e.g., performance benchmarks), the discussion, and the outcome (e.g., adopted your approach or committed to theirs).

Why It Matters in Production

In real-world teams, disagreements are inevitable. A senior engineer must balance conviction with collaboration. The ability to 'disagree and commit'—fully supporting the team's decision after a debate—is critical for team velocity and morale.

Common Pitfall

Avoid making the senior engineer look unreasonable or focusing on 'winning.' Instead, emphasize mutual respect and what you learned. Interviewers want to see you can handle conflict without damaging relationships.

What was your specific contribution, and what measurable results did you achieve?","expectedPoints":["Uses 'I' not 'we' to describe personal contribution","Provides specific numbers (e.g., LCP from 3.2s to 1.4s)","Mentions trade-offs considered (e.g., bundle size vs. speed)","Ends with a clear measurable outcome","Connects the experience to the target company's product"],"followUps":["What trade-offs did you consider when making that change?","How would you approach a similar performance issue at Fun.xyz?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"behavioral","childExplanation":"Imagine you helped make a video game load faster. You also mention that making it faster meant using more memory, but it was worth it because players didn't wait as long. That's a good answer because it's honest and shows you made a real difference.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to communicate a performance improvement story effectively, avoiding common behavioral answer anti-patterns. The interviewer wants to see that you can take ownership, quantify results, discuss trade-offs, and connect your experience to their company.

What to Include

  • Use 'I' not 'we': Clearly state your personal contribution. For example, 'I identified that the main thread was blocked by image decoding and implemented lazy loading.'
  • Provide specific numbers: Instead of 'improved performance,' say 'LCP dropped from 3.2s to 1.4s, a 56% improvement.'
  • Discuss trade-offs: Every decision has downsides. Mention them, e.g., 'Lazy loading reduced initial load time but required more complex state management for scroll position.'
  • Close with measurable outcome: End with the business impact, like 'This led to a 12% increase in user retention.'
  • Tie to the target company: Conclude with why this experience makes you excited about Fun.xyz, e.g., 'I see similar challenges in Fun.xyz's feed, and I'm eager to apply these techniques.'

Common Pitfall

Avoid giving a generic, one-pager answer. Keep your story under 90 seconds and invite follow-up. Also, never badmouth a previous employer; frame challenges positively.

"}]

[{"question":"Design a universal checkout SDK that can be embedded across multiple platforms (web, mobile web, native apps) with a single codebase. How would you handle state isolation between multiple instances on the same page, quotation refresh, and error taxonomy?","expectedPoints":["Proposes a frozen first response pattern to prevent duplicate submissions","Describes tri-state UI flag (idle, loading, error) for each checkout step","Explains ref-based double-tap lock to avoid double clicks","Covers state isolation via closures or iframe/Shadow DOM per instance","Defines error taxonomy (network, validation, timeout) with retry strategies"],"followUps":["How would you handle quotation refresh when the user changes quantity or coupon?","What platform-specific variants would you consider for iOS vs Android vs web?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a universal checkout machine that works in any store (web, app, etc.). You need to make sure that if two people use it at the same time on the same page, they don't mix up their orders. Also, you need to prevent someone from accidentally pressing the 'pay' button twice. And if something goes wrong, you need to know exactly what kind of error it is so you can fix it properly.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a production-grade checkout SDK that is platform-agnostic, resilient, and user-friendly. The Universal Checkout module at Binance faced exactly these challenges, and the same patterns apply to Fun.xyz's FunKit SDK.

Frozen First Response Pattern

To prevent duplicate order submissions, the SDK should freeze the first successful response from the backend and ignore any subsequent responses. This is achieved by storing the response in a ref or closure variable and checking it before processing any new response.

Tri-State UI Flag

Each checkout step (e.g., address, payment, review) should have a tri-state flag: idle, loading, error. This allows the UI to show appropriate feedback (spinner, error message) and prevents the user from proceeding until the current step is resolved.

Ref-Based Double-Tap Lock

A ref-based lock (e.g., useRef(false)) is set to true when a submission starts and reset to false after the response. Any click that occurs while the lock is active is ignored. This is more reliable than a simple boolean state because it avoids stale closures.

State Isolation

When multiple checkout instances exist on the same page (e.g., A/B testing or multiple products), each instance must have its own isolated state. This can be done using closures (each instance gets its own copy of state variables) or by rendering each instance inside an iframe or Shadow DOM to prevent CSS and JavaScript conflicts.

Error Taxonomy

Define a clear error taxonomy:

  • Network errors (timeout, 5xx) → retry with exponential backoff
  • Validation errors (4xx) → show inline field errors
  • Business logic errors (e.g., insufficient balance) → show user-friendly message with action
Each error type should have a unique code and a retry strategy.

Quotation Refresh

When the user changes quantity, coupon, or shipping method, the SDK must refresh the quotation without resetting the entire checkout flow. This can be done by debouncing the change event and calling a dedicated refreshQuotation API, then updating only the price-related state.

Platform Variants

For web, use React components with responsive design. For mobile web, optimize for touch events and smaller screens. For native apps, provide a bridge (e.g., React Native module) that exposes the same API but renders native UI components. The core logic (state management, API calls) should be shared via a headless SDK.

Common Pitfall: Not handling the case where the user navigates away and comes back. The SDK should persist the checkout state (e.g., in sessionStorage or a global store) and restore it on mount.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design an interactive architecture diagram component that visualizes a multi-layered system, allowing users to expand/collapse layers and color-code them by category?","expectedPoints":["Component decomposition: separate diagram container, layer, expand/collapse controls, and legend.","State management: track which layers are expanded, possibly using a Set or object.","Color coding: map layer categories to CSS classes or inline styles.","Performance: avoid re-rendering all layers on expand/collapse; use memoization or keys.","Accessibility: ensure keyboard navigation and ARIA attributes for expandable regions."],"followUps":["How would you handle a large number of layers (e.g., 100) to maintain smooth interactions?","What if the diagram needs to be interactive beyond expand/collapse, like dragging layers?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big toy box with many compartments. You want to show someone what's inside each compartment without opening all at once. So you make a picture where each compartment can be clicked to open or close. You also color the compartments by what kind of toys are inside (like blue for cars, green for balls). This is like building a special picture that people can click to explore.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a complex, interactive UI component that visualizes hierarchical architecture layers. The key is to break down the problem into manageable sub-components and state management.

Component Decomposition

You should propose a component tree like:

  • ArchitectureDiagram – top-level container managing state and rendering layers.
  • Layer – individual layer with expand/collapse behavior and color coding.
  • ExpandCollapseAll – buttons to expand/collapse all layers.
  • Legend – explains color meanings.

State Management

Use a Set or object to track which layers are expanded. For example, expandedLayers: Set. The Layer component receives an isExpanded prop and a toggle handler. Avoid storing expanded state in each layer to keep a single source of truth.

Color Coding

Define a mapping from category (e.g., 'structure', 'state') to CSS class or inline style. Pass the category as a prop to Layer and apply the corresponding color via className or style.

Performance Considerations

For many layers, use React.memo on Layer to prevent re-renders when other layers toggle. Use stable keys (layer IDs). For very large lists, consider virtualization (e.g., react-window) but ensure expand/collapse works with virtualized rows.

Accessibility

Each expandable layer should have role=\"button\", aria-expanded, and aria-controls pointing to the content region. Support keyboard interaction (Enter/Space to toggle).

Common Pitfall

Storing expanded state in each Layer component using local state. This makes it hard to implement \"Expand All\" / \"Collapse All\" because you'd need to ref each layer. Always lift state up to the parent.

"}]

[{"question":"Why should `isNewCrossCurrencyUI` never be included in a `useEffect` dependency array?","expectedPoints":["It is a tri-state value (null|false|true).","Including it in deps can cause infinite loops or unnecessary re-renders.","State transitions are controlled externally, not via effect triggers.","It should only be set by explicit logic, not as a reactive dependency."],"followUps":["What pattern would you use instead to react to changes in this state?","How does this relate to the ARS bypass scenario?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a light switch that can be off, on, or broken. If you tell your robot to do something every time the switch changes, but the switch is broken and flickers randomly, the robot will go crazy. That's why we don't put a tricky switch like that in the list of things that trigger the robot.","detailedExplanation":"

isNewCrossCurrencyUI is a tri-state variable that can be null, false, or true. It represents a specific UI mode that should not be treated as a reactive dependency in useEffect because its transitions are controlled by explicit business logic, not by side effects.

Why it's problematic in useEffect deps

  • Infinite loops: If the effect modifies the state that caused it to run, you get a loop. Since isNewCrossCurrencyUI can change due to user actions or external events, including it in deps may cause the effect to fire again, leading to re-renders and potential loops.
  • Unnecessary re-renders: The value might change frequently (e.g., from null to false), triggering effects that don't need to run. This hurts performance.
  • State machine violation: The rule states that once isNewCrossCurrencyUI becomes false, it should never go back to true. If an effect depends on it and tries to reset it, you break that invariant.

Production impact

In a real app, this could cause unexpected UI flickers, infinite re-renders, or incorrect state transitions. For example, the ARS bypass scenario explicitly skips this state machine, so having an effect that reacts to it would interfere.

Common pitfall

Developers often add every state variable to useEffect deps without thinking about whether the effect should actually react to that variable. The fix is to only include deps that are truly needed for the effect's logic, and to use useRef or callbacks to avoid unnecessary dependencies.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain how React's reconciliation algorithm works when comparing two trees of elements, and describe the role of keys in optimizing this process.","expectedPoints":["Reconciliation uses a heuristic O(n) algorithm based on assumptions: different element types produce different trees, and keys hint at element identity.","When comparing, React first compares root elements; if types differ, it tears down the old tree and builds a new one.","If types match, React updates the existing component instance and recurses on children, using keys to match children across renders.","Keys should be stable, unique, and predictable; using index as key can cause performance issues or bugs with list reordering.","The algorithm avoids full tree diffing (O(n^3)) by making these assumptions, enabling efficient updates."],"followUps":["What happens if you use the index as a key when the list order changes?","How does React handle reconciliation for components with the same type but different props?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have two sets of building blocks. React is like a smart robot that wants to update the first set to look like the second set with as few moves as possible. Instead of checking every single block, it assumes that if the first block in the first set is a different type (like a red square vs a blue circle), it will just replace the whole thing. For blocks of the same type, it updates them in place. Keys are like name tags on blocks that help the robot know which block is which, even if they are moved around. Without name tags, the robot might get confused and rebuild everything.","detailedExplanation":"

Reconciliation is React's algorithm for updating the DOM efficiently when state or props change. It compares the new virtual DOM tree with the previous one and computes the minimal set of changes.

How It Works

React's reconciliation is based on two key assumptions: (1) two elements of different types will produce different trees, and (2) developers can hint at which child elements may be stable across renders using a key prop. When comparing two trees, React first compares the root elements. If the root element types are different (e.g., <div> vs <span>), React tears down the old tree and builds a new one from scratch. If the types are the same, React updates the existing component instance and then recursively reconciles the children.

Role of Keys

For lists of children, React uses keys to match children from the old tree to the new tree. Keys should be stable, unique, and predictable (e.g., a database ID). Using the array index as a key can lead to performance issues or bugs when the list order changes, because React may reuse component instances incorrectly, causing state mismatches.

Why It Matters in Production

Efficient reconciliation is critical for performance in large applications. Without it, React would need to do a full tree diff (O(n^3) complexity), which is impractical. The heuristic O(n) algorithm makes React fast enough for most use cases.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is using key={index} in dynamic lists. This can cause issues like incorrect state preservation or unnecessary re-renders when items are added, removed, or reordered. Always use a unique identifier from your data when possible.

"}]
qgen_v2:6ef1d4302880dc0c:
[{"question":"How would you design a frontend SDK that runs inside a customer's app, ensuring isolation, communication, and lifecycle management, similar to a micro-frontend architecture?","expectedPoints":["Proposes JS sandbox isolation (e.g., using iframe or Proxy-based sandbox) to prevent global conflicts.","Describes an event bus or custom events for communication between host and SDK.","Discusses bundle strategy: single-bundle vs. dynamic loading, and tradeoffs.","Explains lifecycle hooks (mount, unmount, update) for clean integration.","Mentions production considerations: error boundaries, versioning, and performance impact."],"followUps":["How would you handle shared dependencies like React or lodash between host and SDK?","What if the customer app uses a different framework (e.g., Vue or Angular)?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you build a special toy that other kids can put inside their own toy box. You need to make sure your toy doesn't break their toys, can talk to them, and can be taken out easily. That's what an SDK does inside a customer's app.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a frontend SDK that runs inside a customer's app requires careful consideration of isolation, communication, bundle strategy, and lifecycle management. This is analogous to micro-frontend architectures like Qiankun, where a shell host manages multiple micro-apps.

Isolation

To prevent conflicts with the host app, the SDK must run in a sandboxed environment. Common approaches include:

  • Iframe: Full isolation but limited communication and styling issues.
  • Proxy-based sandbox: Using JavaScript Proxy to intercept global variable access and restore them on unmount. Qiankun uses this to isolate window mutations.
  • Shadow DOM: For CSS isolation, but not for JS.

Communication

An event bus or custom events (e.g., window.dispatchEvent) allows the host and SDK to exchange data without tight coupling. For example, the SDK can emit events when user actions occur, and the host can listen and respond.

Bundle Strategy

Two main strategies exist:

  • Single-bundle pseudo-MFE: The SDK is a single JS bundle loaded via <script>. Simple but may cause version conflicts if the host uses the same libraries.
  • Dynamic loading: Use loadMicroApp (like Qiankun) to fetch and mount the SDK on demand. Better isolation but more complex.

Lifecycle Management

The SDK should expose lifecycle hooks: bootstrap, mount, unmount, and update. The host calls these to control the SDK's presence. This ensures proper cleanup (e.g., removing event listeners, restoring globals) to avoid memory leaks.

Production Considerations

  • Error boundaries: Wrap the SDK in a React error boundary to prevent crashes from affecting the host.
  • Versioning: Use semver and provide a changelog. Consider a version check on load.
  • Performance: Lazy load non-critical parts, minimize bundle size, and avoid blocking the main thread.

Common Pitfall: Failing to properly clean up global modifications on unmount, leading to state pollution in the host app. Always restore the original window properties and remove added DOM elements.

"}]

[{"question":"How does Qiankun achieve JavaScript sandbox isolation for each micro-frontend, and what are the trade-offs of using a Proxy-based sandbox versus an iframe-based approach?","expectedPoints":["Explains that Qiankun uses a Proxy to intercept global variable access and create a separate window context per app.","Mentions that the sandbox prevents global pollution by restoring the original window after unmount.","Compares with iframes: iframes provide stronger isolation but add communication overhead and styling challenges.","Notes that Proxy-based sandbox may have performance overhead and cannot fully isolate all side effects (e.g., timers, network).","Acknowledges that Qiankun's sandbox is sufficient for most UI-level isolation but not for security-critical scenarios."],"followUps":["What happens if a micro-frontend modifies a global object like Array.prototype? How does Qiankun handle that?","Can you describe a scenario where the Proxy-based sandbox might fail to isolate properly?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a shared room with your sibling. You each have your own toy box, but sometimes you accidentally grab each other's toys. Qiankun is like a magic barrier that gives each of you your own invisible copy of the room, so when you reach for a toy, you only see your own. This way, you don't mess up each other's stuff. But it's not perfect—if you throw a toy across the room, it might still land in your sibling's area.","detailedExplanation":"

Qiankun is a micro-frontend framework that enables multiple independent frontend applications (e.g., React, Vue) to coexist within a single host page. A core feature is its JavaScript sandbox, which isolates each micro-frontend's global scope to prevent interference.

How It Works

Qiankun uses a Proxy-based sandbox. When a micro-frontend mounts, it creates a fake window object using a JavaScript Proxy. All global variable reads/writes from the micro-frontend are intercepted by the Proxy and redirected to this fake window. The original window is saved and restored when the app unmounts. This ensures that global variables, setTimeout overrides, or prototype modifications do not leak across apps.

Comparison with iframes

An iframe provides a fully isolated browsing context (separate document, global scope, and even a separate event loop). However, iframes introduce challenges: they are harder to style consistently, communication requires postMessage, and they add memory overhead. Qiankun's Proxy sandbox is lighter and allows seamless DOM integration (e.g., modals can overlay the host page). The trade-off is that Proxy-based isolation is not as strong—certain side effects like window.open, timers, or network requests can still leak if not explicitly patched.

Why It Matters in Production

In a large enterprise app with multiple teams, global pollution is a common source of bugs. The sandbox enables safe coexistence of legacy and modern frameworks without rewriting. However, developers must be aware of its limitations: for example, if a micro-frontend uses a library that relies on window properties not covered by the sandbox, it may break.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is assuming the sandbox provides security isolation. It does not—it only prevents accidental global collisions. Malicious code can still escape the Proxy. For security-critical apps, iframes or Web Workers are recommended.

"}]
qgen_v2:926ca8a8d486c02a:
[{"question":"How would you design an SDK widget that must be embedded in a host application while ensuring full isolation from the host's global scope, CSS, and JavaScript?","expectedPoints":["Use Shadow DOM for CSS encapsulation and DOM isolation.","Use iframe for complete JavaScript and DOM isolation, with postMessage for communication.","Avoid leaking global variables by wrapping SDK code in an IIFE or module pattern.","Handle lifecycle (init, mount, unmount) with proper cleanup to prevent memory leaks.","Consider CSP and CORS when loading SDK from a CDN.","Provide configuration via explicit API (e.g., function call) rather than relying on global scope."],"followUps":["What are the trade-offs between using Shadow DOM vs iframe for isolation?","How would you handle communication between the SDK and the host application?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a special toy that can be placed inside another toy's room. You need to make sure your toy doesn't mess up the other toy's stuff (like its colors or buttons). You can either put your toy in a clear box (Shadow DOM) that only affects itself, or put it in a separate room (iframe) with a walkie-talkie (postMessage) to talk to the outside. Both ways keep things clean.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing an SDK widget for embedding in a host application requires careful isolation to prevent conflicts with the host's global scope, CSS, and JavaScript. The two primary approaches are Shadow DOM and iframes, each with distinct trade-offs.

Shadow DOM

Shadow DOM provides CSS scoping and DOM encapsulation by attaching a shadow root to an element. Styles and DOM queries inside the shadow tree do not leak out, and external styles do not penetrate in (except for inherited properties). However, JavaScript execution still shares the same global scope, so the SDK must avoid polluting globals (e.g., by wrapping code in an IIFE or using ES modules). Communication with the host is direct via function calls or events.

Iframe

An iframe offers complete isolation of JavaScript, DOM, and CSS because it runs in a separate browsing context. Communication must happen via postMessage, which adds complexity and latency. The iframe can be sandboxed for extra security. This approach is heavier but safer for untrusted code.

Lifecycle Management

The SDK should expose a clear lifecycle: init(config), mount(container), unmount(). During unmount, all event listeners, timers, and DOM nodes must be cleaned up to prevent memory leaks. This is critical in single-page applications where the SDK may be mounted/unmounted repeatedly.

Configuration and Communication

Configuration (API key, theme, callbacks) should be passed explicitly via the init method, not via global variables. For iframe-based SDKs, use postMessage with a well-defined protocol (e.g., { type: 'event', payload: {...} }). For Shadow DOM, expose an event API like sdk.on('success', cb).

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is relying on global scope for configuration or callbacks, which breaks when multiple SDK instances exist or when the host uses strict CSP. Always encapsulate state within the SDK instance.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you implement a micro-frontend architecture using React, and what are the key challenges you would address?","expectedPoints":["Use Module Federation or iframes for independent deployment","Handle shared dependencies and version conflicts","Ensure consistent styling and communication between micro-frontends","Manage routing and state across boundaries","Address performance and bundle size concerns"],"followUps":["How would you share state between micro-frontends without tight coupling?","What strategies would you use to avoid CSS conflicts?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big Lego castle, but different teams build different parts (like the tower, the wall, and the gate). Micro-frontends let each team work on their own part independently, but they need to make sure the parts fit together nicely. You have to plan how they communicate and share pieces without breaking each other's work.","detailedExplanation":"

Micro-frontend architecture extends the microservices concept to the frontend, allowing multiple teams to develop, deploy, and maintain independent parts of a web application. In React, this can be achieved using tools like Module Federation (Webpack 5) or iframe embedding.

Key Implementation Steps

  • Module Federation: Configure each micro-frontend as a remote module that can be loaded dynamically by a host application. This enables independent deployment and versioning.
  • Shared Dependencies: Use shared configuration in Module Federation to avoid duplicate React instances and manage version conflicts.
  • Communication: Use custom events, a shared event bus, or a lightweight pub/sub system for cross-application communication.
  • Routing: Implement a shell application that handles top-level routing and delegates to micro-frontends based on the URL path.
  • Styling: Use CSS-in-JS, CSS Modules, or scoped styles to prevent style leakage.

Why It Matters in Production

Large organizations benefit from independent deployability, team autonomy, and technology diversity. However, it introduces complexity in performance (multiple bundles), testing (integration), and governance (consistent UX).

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering communication between micro-frontends, leading to tight coupling. Instead, prefer loose coupling via events or a shared API layer, and avoid sharing state directly.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a frontend agent that automatically detects and fixes UI bugs in a React application using user interactions, backend logs, and tracking data?","expectedPoints":["Describes a system that collects and correlates data from multiple sources (user clicks, console errors, API failures, analytics events).","Explains how the agent identifies bug patterns (e.g., component crashes, slow renders, broken flows) using heuristics or ML.","Details a feedback loop where the agent suggests or auto-applies fixes (e.g., code patches, config changes, rollbacks).","Mentions safety mechanisms like canary deployments, rollback, or human-in-the-loop approval.","Addresses integration with existing tools (e.g., Sentry, Datadog, React DevTools, feature flags)."],"followUps":["How would you ensure the agent doesn't introduce new bugs while fixing existing ones?","What metrics would you use to measure the agent's effectiveness?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a robot that watches people play a video game. When a player gets stuck because of a glitch, the robot looks at what the player did, what the game's computer logs say, and what the game's sensors recorded. Then the robot figures out how to fix the glitch automatically, like patching a hole in the game's code. This is like having a smart helper that keeps the game running smoothly without waiting for a human to fix it.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a self-healing frontend system that leverages multiple data sources to proactively identify and resolve bugs in a React application. The core idea is to create an AI agent that continuously monitors user interactions, backend logs, and tracking data to detect anomalies and automatically apply fixes.

How It Works

The agent collects data from three main sources:

  • User interactions: Clicks, form submissions, navigation paths (via tools like react-tracked or custom analytics).
  • Backend logs: API errors, latency spikes, 4xx/5xx responses (from services like Sentry or Datadog).
  • Tracking data: Conversion funnels, feature usage, error rates (from Amplitude or Mixpanel).

The agent correlates these signals to pinpoint bug patterns. For example, if many users click a button but get no response, and backend logs show a 500 error, the agent identifies a broken API call. It then suggests or auto-applies a fix, such as rolling back a recent deployment, toggling a feature flag, or patching the React component (e.g., adding error boundaries or retry logic).

Why It Matters in Production

In a fintech context like KYB (Know Your Business), bugs directly impact conversion rates and compliance. A manual debugging process is slow and costly. An agent reduces mean time to resolution (MTTR) from hours to minutes, improving user experience and trust. It also aligns with a get-things-done culture by automating toil and freeing engineers for higher-value work.

Common Pitfall

A major risk is automated fix propagation—the agent might apply a patch that fixes one bug but breaks another. To mitigate, always include a human-in-the-loop for critical changes, use canary deployments, and maintain a rollback mechanism. Additionally, ensure the agent's decision logic is transparent and auditable, especially in regulated environments.

"}]

[{"question":"When would you choose a workflow over an agent for a frontend feature that involves multiple LLM calls?","expectedPoints":["Workflow is for predictable, predefined steps; agent for open-ended tasks.","Workflow has lower complexity and predictable cost.","Agent adds overhead of decision-making loops and tool use.","Start simple; only add agent complexity when necessary.","Example: data extraction pipeline vs. customer support chatbot."],"followUps":["How would you handle error recovery in a workflow vs. an agent?","What metrics would you use to decide it's time to upgrade from workflow to agent?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're baking cookies. A workflow is like following a recipe step by step—you know exactly what to do. An agent is like a chef who decides what to cook based on what's in the fridge—it figures things out as it goes. You'd use a workflow when the steps are clear, and an agent when you need to adapt.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your understanding of the trade-offs between workflows and agents in LLM-powered frontend features, as outlined in Anthropic's framework.

What is a Workflow?

A workflow is a predefined sequence of LLM calls and deterministic steps. For example, a frontend feature that extracts data from a document, validates it with one LLM call, then formats it with another. The control flow is fixed, making it predictable in both behavior and cost. You know exactly how many LLM calls will be made.

What is an Agent?

An agent uses an LLM to decide the next action, often in a loop, until a goal is achieved. It can call tools, fetch data, or make decisions dynamically. This is suitable for open-ended tasks like a customer support chatbot that may need to look up information, ask clarifying questions, or escalate. Complexity and cost are variable because the number of steps is unknown upfront.

When to Choose Workflow over Agent

  • Predictable steps: If the process is well-understood (e.g., classify → extract → summarize), a workflow is simpler and cheaper.
  • Cost control: Workflows have a fixed number of LLM calls, making budgeting easier.
  • Reliability: Less room for unexpected behavior; easier to test and debug.
  • Anthropic's advice: “Start with the simplest solution. Only add agent complexity when needed.”

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering: Using an agent for a task that could be done with a simple workflow. This adds unnecessary latency, cost, and complexity. Always start with the simplest pattern and only escalate when the requirements demand dynamic decision-making.

"}]
qgen_v2:2a233a85fe48a277:
[{"question":"How would you implement a ReAct agent loop in a React frontend that calls an LLM and uses tools like getLogs? Walk through the state management, loop control, and error handling.","expectedPoints":["State management: store history of thought, action, observation in a state array.","Loop control: use async/await with a while loop that checks done() and maxIterations.","Tool execution: implement a function registry that maps action names to async functions.","Error handling: catch errors in tool calls and append as observations, handle LLM failures.","UI integration: update React state after each step to show progress (e.g., streaming)."],"followUps":["How would you handle a tool that returns a large amount of data?","What if the LLM returns an invalid action name?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a robot that can think and act. It keeps a diary of what it thinks, what it does, and what it sees. You need to make the robot write in its diary, then decide what to do next, and keep going until it finishes its job. In React, you manage that diary as state and run the loop step by step.","detailedExplanation":"

The ReAct pattern (Reasoning + Acting) is a loop where an LLM alternates between reasoning (Thought) and taking actions (Action) using tools, then observing the results (Observation). In a React frontend, implementing this requires careful state management and async control.

State Management

Store the history of each step as an array of objects: { thought, action, observation }. Use React's useState to hold this array. After each step, append the new entry to trigger re-renders.

Loop Control

Use an async function with a while loop. The loop condition checks !done() && iteration < maxIterations. Inside, call the LLM to get a thought and action, execute the tool, observe the result, and update state. Use await for LLM calls and tool executions to avoid blocking the UI.

Tool Execution

Maintain a registry object mapping action names to async functions (e.g., { getLogs: async (traceId) => {...} }). When the LLM returns an action, look it up and call it with the provided arguments. If the action is invalid, append an error observation.

Error Handling

Wrap tool calls in try-catch. On failure, append an observation with the error message. Also handle LLM API errors (e.g., network failure) by breaking the loop and showing an error state.

UI Integration

Update React state after each step to display the history progressively. Use useEffect to start the loop on mount or user action. Consider using useReducer for complex state transitions.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to handle the case where the LLM returns an infinite loop (e.g., repeating the same thought). Implement a maximum iteration limit and a diversity check to break out.

"}]

[{"question":"In a Binance-like trading dashboard, which agent architecture would you choose for handling user queries that require both market data retrieval and trade execution, and why?","expectedPoints":["Identifies Orchestrator-Workers as the best fit for parallel specialist tasks.","Explains that the orchestrator dispatches tasks to workers (e.g., market data, trade execution).","Mentions scalability and fault tolerance benefits.","Contrasts with simpler architectures like Single Agent (limited) or Router (less flexible).","Acknowledges trade-offs like increased complexity."],"followUps":["How would you handle a worker failure in this architecture?","What if the orchestrator becomes a bottleneck?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're running a restaurant. A single waiter (Single Agent) takes orders and cooks—too slow. An orchestrator (head waiter) assigns tasks: one chef cooks, another preps drinks (Orchestrator-Workers). This is best for a busy trading app because specialists work in parallel, just like chefs making different dishes at the same time.","detailedExplanation":"

The Orchestrator-Workers pattern is ideal for a Binance-like trading dashboard because it enables parallel execution of specialized tasks. The orchestrator acts as a central coordinator, receiving user queries and dispatching them to dedicated worker agents—for example, one worker for market data retrieval, another for trade execution, and a third for risk assessment.

How It Works

The orchestrator parses the user's intent (e.g., 'Buy 1 BTC if price drops below $60k') and breaks it into sub-tasks. Workers operate independently, often in parallel, and return results to the orchestrator, which aggregates them into a final response. This mirrors a microservices architecture where each worker is a stateless service.

Why It Matters in Production

  • Scalability: Workers can be scaled horizontally based on load (e.g., more market data workers during high volatility).
  • Fault Tolerance: If a worker fails, the orchestrator can retry or fallback, isolating failures.
  • Specialization: Each worker can be optimized for its domain (e.g., using a low-latency API for trades).

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is making the orchestrator too smart—embedding business logic instead of just routing. This creates a monolithic orchestrator that becomes a single point of failure and a bottleneck. Keep the orchestrator thin; let workers own the logic.

Compared to other patterns: Single Agent lacks parallelism; Router + Specialists is faster but less flexible for dynamic tasks; Hierarchical adds unnecessary depth; Swarm is overkill for production systems. Orchestrator-Workers strikes the right balance for complex, real-time financial applications.

"}]
qgen_v2:13fac1b9dd485a83:
[{"question":"How would you design an orchestrator-worker system for automated bug triage, and what are the key trade-offs between a centralized orchestrator and a decentralized worker mesh?","expectedPoints":["Describes orchestrator as a central coordinator that dispatches tasks and aggregates results.","Explains worker specialization (e.g., session replay, log analysis) and how they operate independently.","Discusses trade-offs: single point of failure vs. coordination complexity, scalability, and fault tolerance.","Mention of human-in-the-loop for validation and feedback loop for improvement.","Considers error handling, retries, and idempotency in worker design."],"followUps":["How would you handle a worker that fails or times out?","What if two workers produce conflicting hypotheses?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a team of detectives solving a mystery. The orchestrator is the lead detective who decides which specialist to call (like a fingerprint expert or a witness interviewer) and in what order. Each specialist works on their own piece, then reports back. The lead puts it all together and presents the solution to the boss (human reviewer). This system helps find bugs faster by dividing the work, but if the lead gets sick, the whole case stops—that's a trade-off.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a multi-agent system for automated bug triage, a common pattern in modern DevOps and AI-assisted debugging pipelines. The orchestrator-worker architecture is a classic master-slave pattern where a central coordinator (the orchestrator) manages a set of specialized workers.

Core Design

The orchestrator (e.g., a Claude/GPT-4 agent) receives a bug report and decides which workers to invoke and in what sequence. Workers are independent services, each with a single responsibility: Session Replay parses user interactions, Log Investigator queries Datadog/Splunk, Code Locator uses source maps and embeddings, Hypothesis Generator ranks root causes, and Fix Suggester produces a patch. The orchestrator aggregates results and presents them to a human reviewer.

Key Trade-offs

  • Centralized vs. Decentralized: A centralized orchestrator simplifies coordination and state management but creates a single point of failure and bottleneck. A decentralized mesh (e.g., using message queues or event-driven workers) improves fault tolerance and scalability but increases complexity in task ordering and conflict resolution.
  • Worker Granularity: Fine-grained workers (one per task) allow parallel execution and easier debugging but increase overhead. Coarse-grained workers reduce network calls but may become too complex.
  • Error Handling: Workers can fail due to timeouts or data issues. The orchestrator should implement retries with exponential backoff, circuit breakers, and fallback strategies (e.g., skip a worker if it fails repeatedly).
  • Human-in-the-Loop: The final review by a senior engineer ensures quality and provides feedback to improve the system (e.g., via reinforcement learning or updating worker prompts).

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is tight coupling between the orchestrator and workers, such as sharing internal data structures or relying on synchronous calls. This makes the system brittle and hard to scale. Instead, use asynchronous messaging (e.g., Kafka, SQS) and define clear input/output contracts (e.g., JSON schemas) for each worker.

"}]

[{"question":"Design an end-to-end bug detection pipeline for a frontend application that uses session replay and observability data. How would you structure the pipeline to minimize false positives and ensure fast triage?","expectedPoints":["Pipeline stages: trigger, triage, reproduce, hypothesize, investigate, verify, fix, review.","Use of session replay and logs for reproduction and hypothesis generation.","Observability for the pipeline itself: log tool calls, track time-to-hypothesis, false positive rate, cost.","Integration with version control and testing to verify fixes.","Human-in-the-loop for final approval and feedback loop to improve future triage."],"followUps":["How would you prioritize which bugs to investigate first?","What metrics would you use to measure the pipeline's effectiveness?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a robot that helps find and fix problems in a video game. When a player reports a bug, the robot watches a recording of the game (session replay) to see what happened. It then guesses what might be wrong, checks the game's code and logs, runs tests to confirm, and suggests a fix. A human checks the fix before it's applied. The robot also keeps track of how long it takes and how often it's wrong, so it can get better over time.","detailedExplanation":"

An end-to-end bug detection pipeline for frontend applications leverages observability and session replay to automate the process from bug report to fix. The pipeline consists of several stages:

Trigger

Bugs are detected via Sentry alerts, conversion drops, or user reports. These triggers initiate the pipeline.

Triage

Each bug is categorized as UI, logic, network, or data issue. This helps route to the right investigation path.

Reproduce

Using session replay tools (e.g., LogRocket, FullStory), the pipeline extracts exact user steps to reproduce the bug.

Hypothesize

The system generates 3-5 ranked root causes based on code analysis, logs, and past bug patterns.

Investigate

For each hypothesis, the pipeline queries relevant code and logs to gather evidence.

Verify

Automated tests are run to confirm the hypothesis. The system compares the bug with historical bugs to avoid duplicates.

Fix

A patch and test are generated automatically, along with a draft pull request.

Review

A human approves the fix, and feedback is stored to improve future triage.

Critical: The pipeline itself must be observable. Log every tool call, track time-to-hypothesis, false positive rate, and cost per investigation. This ensures continuous improvement and trust in the system.

Common pitfall: Over-reliance on automation without human oversight can lead to high false positive rates. Always include a human-in-the-loop for final approval and feedback.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a memory layer for a React-based AI assistant that persists user preferences across sessions and retrieves relevant past interactions?","expectedPoints":["Differentiates short-term (context window) from long-term (vector DB, structured store) memory.","Proposes using embeddings for semantic search over past conversations.","Suggests structured storage for key facts (e.g., user preferences in Postgres).","Considers episodic memory for bug fixes or past solutions.","Mentions injection into system prompt or context window for retrieval.","Addresses performance and scalability concerns (e.g., indexing, caching)."],"followUps":["How would you handle memory conflicts when the same fact is updated?","What tradeoffs exist between using a vector DB vs. a structured store for this use case?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a robot helper that remembers what you like and what you've done before. Short-term memory is like what you just said, long-term memory is like a diary where it writes down important things. When you ask it something, it quickly checks its diary to give you a better answer.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to architect a memory system for an AI assistant, which is crucial for maintaining context and personalization across sessions. A senior frontend engineer must understand how to integrate such systems with a React frontend, even if the heavy lifting is on the backend.

Key Components

  • Short-term memory: The current context window (e.g., 200K tokens for Claude) holds the ongoing conversation and tool outputs. This is ephemeral and lost after the session.
  • Long-term memory: Persisted via a vector database (e.g., Pinecone) for semantic search over past conversations, a structured store (e.g., Postgres) for key facts like user preferences, and episodic memory for past solutions (e.g., bug fixes).
  • Procedural memory: Workflows the agent has learned, which can be stored as templates or routines.

How It Works

When a user interacts, the assistant retrieves relevant memories by embedding the current query and searching the vector DB. Key facts are fetched from the structured store and injected into the system prompt. Episodic memories are matched by similarity to the current problem. All retrieved data is placed into the context window to inform the response.

Why It Matters in Production

Without memory, each session starts from scratch, leading to repetitive questions and poor personalization. A well-designed memory layer reduces friction, improves user satisfaction, and enables the assistant to learn from past interactions. However, it introduces complexity in data synchronization, privacy, and storage costs.

Common Pitfall

Overloading the context window with too many memories can cause the model to lose focus or exceed token limits. Use retrieval augmentation to fetch only the most relevant memories, and consider compression or summarization for long histories.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain how React's reconciliation algorithm works and why the `key` prop is critical for list rendering.","expectedPoints":["Describes virtual DOM diffing process","Explains that keys help identify which items changed, added, or removed","Mentions that using index as key can cause bugs with dynamic lists","Notes that stable, unique keys improve performance and prevent state mismatches","Connects to real-world issues like incorrect component state or unnecessary re-renders"],"followUps":["What happens if you omit the key prop entirely?","Can you give an example where using index as key leads to a bug?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a list of toys on a shelf. If you want to know which toy is new or moved, you need a name tag on each toy. React uses 'keys' like those name tags to track items in a list. Without keys, React might get confused and mess up the order or lose a toy's special features.","detailedExplanation":"

Reconciliation is React's algorithm for updating the DOM efficiently. When a component's state or props change, React creates a new virtual DOM tree and diffs it against the previous one. The diffing algorithm works by comparing elements of the same type and position, and it uses the key prop to match children across renders.

How It Works

React compares the root elements first. If the type changes (e.g., <div> to <span>), it tears down the old subtree and builds a new one. For lists, React iterates over both the old and new children simultaneously. Without keys, it uses index-based matching, which can lead to incorrect updates when items are reordered, added, or removed.

Why Keys Matter

Keys provide a stable identity for each element. This allows React to recognize which items have changed, been added, or removed. Using unique and stable keys (like an ID from your data) ensures that the component state is preserved correctly and that only the necessary DOM updates occur. In production, this prevents bugs like input fields retaining values from other rows or animations breaking.

Common Pitfall

Using the array index as a key is a frequent mistake. While it works for static lists, it causes issues when the list is dynamic (e.g., items are reordered, inserted, or deleted). For example, if you have a list of items with input fields and you remove an item, the indices shift, causing React to reuse the wrong component instances and leading to stale state or incorrect UI.

"}]

[{"question":"How would you implement a flashcard flip animation in React that toggles between front and back content on click, ensuring accessibility and performance?","expectedPoints":["Uses state to track flipped status and CSS transforms for 3D flip effect.","Handles click events with proper ARIA attributes (role, aria-pressed).","Optimizes re-renders with React.memo or useMemo if needed.","Ensures keyboard accessibility (Enter/Space to flip).","Avoids layout thrashing by using transform instead of width/height changes."],"followUps":["How would you add a smooth entrance animation when the card first appears?","What if you had a deck of 100 cards – how would you manage performance?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine a flashcard that flips over like a pancake. You click it, and it turns to show the answer. We use a special CSS trick to make it look 3D, and we make sure people using keyboards or screen readers can also flip it. We also keep it fast so it doesn't slow down even with many cards.","detailedExplanation":"

To implement a flashcard flip animation in React, you need to combine state management with CSS 3D transforms for the visual flip effect. The core idea is to toggle a boolean state (e.g., isFlipped) on click, then apply a rotateY(180deg) transform to the card container. The front and back faces are positioned absolutely with backface-visibility: hidden so only one side is visible at a time.

Implementation Steps

  • Create a Flashcard component with a flipped state using useState.
  • Attach an onClick handler to toggle the state. For keyboard accessibility, also listen for onKeyDown (Enter/Space).
  • Add ARIA attributes: role=\"button\", aria-pressed={flipped}, and tabIndex={0} to make it focusable.
  • Use CSS: .card { perspective: 1000px; } .card-inner { transition: transform 0.6s; transform-style: preserve-3d; } .card-inner.flipped { transform: rotateY(180deg); } .card-front, .card-back { backface-visibility: hidden; position: absolute; } .card-back { transform: rotateY(180deg); }

Why It Matters in Production

This pattern is common in interactive UI components like carousels, modals, or onboarding tutorials. Using CSS transforms instead of JavaScript-driven animations ensures smooth 60fps performance because transforms are composited on the GPU. Accessibility is not optional – without proper ARIA and keyboard support, users with disabilities cannot interact with the component.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to set transform-style: preserve-3d on the parent element. Without it, the 3D effect breaks and the back face may appear mirrored or not at all. Also, avoid animating width or height as it triggers layout recalculations; always prefer transform and opacity for animations.

"}]
qgen_v2:6aca44ffba70e4e1:
[{"question":"How would you design a React SDK for a smart wallet that abstracts account abstraction (EIP-4337) from developers? Focus on the API surface and integration patterns.","expectedPoints":["Exposes a simple hook like useSmartWallet that handles user operations and bundler interactions","Provides configuration for entry point, paymaster, and gas estimation","Supports both EOA and smart contract wallet flows transparently","Includes error handling and loading states for async operations","Follows composable patterns (e.g., provider pattern, context) for flexibility"],"followUps":["How would you handle multiple bundler providers or fallback logic?","What tradeoffs would you consider between a higher-order component vs. a hook-based API?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a special wallet that can do smart things like pay for gas in tokens. You want to give other developers a simple tool (like a magic button) so they don't have to understand all the complicated parts. Your job is to design that tool so it's easy to use and works well.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a React SDK for a smart wallet with account abstraction (EIP-4337) requires careful thought about the developer experience and underlying complexity. The goal is to abstract away the intricacies of user operations, bundlers, and paymasters while providing a flexible and robust API.

Core API Surface

The primary interface should be a hook like useSmartWallet that returns wallet state, methods to send transactions, and connection status. This hook internally manages the lifecycle of user operations: constructing them, signing with the user's EOA, sending to a bundler, and tracking the transaction hash. Developers should only need to call sendUserOperation with a target and calldata.

Configuration and Providers

Use a Provider pattern (e.g., SmartWalletProvider) to inject global config: entry point address, bundler URL, paymaster options, and gas estimation strategy. This follows the same pattern as ethers.providers or web3-react, making it familiar. For paymasters, offer a paymaster prop that can be a URL or a custom function, allowing developers to choose between sponsored or token-based gas.

Handling Different Wallet Types

The SDK should transparently support both EOAs and smart contract wallets. For EOAs, it can automatically deploy a smart wallet if needed (using a factory). The hook should expose a isDeployed flag and a deploy method. This is critical for production where users may not have a smart wallet yet.

Error Handling and Loading States

Every async operation should return loading, error, and data states. Use React Suspense or manual state management to handle pending user operations. Provide clear error messages for common failures like insufficient funds, bundler rejection, or invalid signature.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is exposing too much internal detail, like raw user operation objects or bundler RPC calls. This defeats the purpose of abstraction. Instead, keep the API high-level and only expose advanced options via an optional overrides parameter for power users.

Integration Patterns

Follow Kent C. Dodds' patterns for hooks: keep them focused, use useCallback for stable references, and avoid prop drilling with context. The SDK should also export TypeScript types for all configuration and return values, leveraging generics for type-safe calldata encoding.

"}]

[{"question":"Explain how React's Reconciliation, Virtual DOM, and Fiber work together to improve performance.","expectedPoints":["Virtual DOM is a lightweight copy of the real DOM used to track state changes.","Reconciliation is the diffing process that compares the new Virtual DOM with the previous one.","Fiber is a data structure that enables incremental rendering and prioritization of updates.","The process reduces costly real DOM manipulations by batching updates and applying minimal changes.","Fiber allows interruption of work to handle high-priority updates, improving responsiveness."],"followUps":["How does Fiber enable concurrent mode in React?","What is the role of the 'key' prop in reconciliation?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big Lego castle (the real DOM). Every time you want to change a brick, it takes a lot of effort. React keeps a blueprint (Virtual DOM) of the castle. When you want to change something, React compares the new blueprint with the old one (reconciliation) and figures out exactly which bricks to change. Fiber is like a smart assistant that can pause and resume the work if something more urgent comes up, so the castle stays smooth and fast.","detailedExplanation":"

React's Reconciliation, Virtual DOM, and Fiber are core concepts that work together to optimize UI updates. The Virtual DOM is an in-memory representation of the real DOM. When a component's state changes, React creates a new Virtual DOM tree and compares it with the previous one using a diffing algorithm (reconciliation). This process identifies the minimal set of changes needed to update the real DOM, avoiding expensive full re-renders.

How Fiber Enhances Reconciliation

Fiber is a reimplementation of the reconciliation engine introduced in React 16. Each fiber node represents a unit of work and stores information about the component, its state, and its relationships (child, sibling, return). Fiber enables incremental rendering: the reconciliation work can be split into chunks and spread over multiple frames. It also supports priority-based scheduling, allowing high-priority updates (e.g., user input) to interrupt lower-priority work (e.g., data fetching).

Why It Matters in Production

Without these optimizations, frequent state changes would cause janky user experiences. By batching updates and using a diffing strategy, React ensures smooth 60fps rendering. Fiber's ability to pause and resume work is critical for concurrent mode, where React can prepare updates in the background without blocking the main thread.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is not using key props correctly in lists. Without stable keys, React may unnecessarily re-render or misidentify elements, leading to performance degradation or bugs. Always use unique, stable keys for list items to help the diffing algorithm work efficiently.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[State Change] --> B[New Virtual DOM] B --> C[Diff with Old Virtual DOM] C --> D[Fiber Tree] D --> E[Schedule Updates] E --> F[Batch & Patch Real DOM] F --> G[Updated UI]"}]

[{"question":"When does a React component re-render?","expectedPoints":["Props change","State change","Context change","Parent re-render"],"followUps":["How can you prevent unnecessary re-renders?","What is the role of memoization in React?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that lights up when you press a button (state change), or when someone gives it a new battery (props change), or when the room gets darker (context change), or when the table it sits on shakes (parent re-render). React components are like that toy—they re-render when something changes that they care about.","detailedExplanation":"

React components re-render when any of the following occur: props change, state change, context value changes, or the parent component re-renders. This is the core mechanism that keeps the UI in sync with data.

How it works

React uses a virtual DOM to efficiently update the real DOM. When a component re-renders, React compares the new virtual DOM tree with the previous one (a process called reconciliation) and applies only the necessary changes to the actual DOM. However, re-rendering itself (calling the component function) can be expensive if the component tree is large or if it contains heavy computations.

Why it matters in production

Unnecessary re-renders can lead to performance degradation, especially in complex applications. Understanding the triggers helps developers optimize by using techniques like React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, or PureComponent to skip re-renders when inputs haven't changed.

Common pitfall

A frequent mistake is assuming that React.memo or PureComponent prevents all re-renders. They only perform a shallow comparison of props/state. If you pass inline objects or functions as props, they will be new references on every render, causing the child to re-render despite memoization. Always use useCallback for functions and useMemo for objects to maintain stable references.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Props Change] --> B[Component Re-render] C[State Change] --> B D[Context Change] --> B E[Parent Re-render] --> B B --> F[Virtual DOM Diff] F --> G[Update Real DOM]"}]

[{"question":"Explain the key differences between Server Components (RSC) and Client Components in React, and describe when you would choose one over the other in a production application.","expectedPoints":["Server Components render on the server, reducing client-side JavaScript and improving initial load time.","Client Components run in the browser, enabling interactivity and state management.","Server Components cannot use hooks or browser APIs; they are ideal for static or data-fetching parts.","Client Components are necessary for event handlers, effects, and dynamic UI updates.","Trade-offs: Server Components improve performance and security but limit interactivity; Client Components offer full interactivity at the cost of larger bundles."],"followUps":["How would you decide which parts of a page should be Server Components vs Client Components?","Can a Server Component contain a Client Component? What are the implications?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that can be built either at the factory (server) or at home (client). Factory-built toys arrive ready to play, so you can play faster, but you can't change them. Home-built toys take longer to set up, but you can customize them however you want. Server Components are like factory-built toys—they load fast but aren't interactive. Client Components are like home-built toys—they take more time to load but can be interactive.","detailedExplanation":"

Server Components (RSC) and Client Components are two distinct component types in React, each designed for different rendering environments. Server Components execute on the server, generating static HTML that is sent to the client. They never ship JavaScript to the browser, which reduces bundle size and improves initial load time. Client Components, on the other hand, run entirely in the browser, allowing them to use React hooks, state, effects, and browser APIs.

Key Differences

  • Rendering Location: Server Components render on the server; Client Components render on the client.
  • Interactivity: Server Components cannot handle user interactions (e.g., onClick) or use hooks like useState or useEffect. Client Components are required for any interactive behavior.
  • Data Fetching: Server Components can directly access databases or backend services without exposing credentials to the client, enhancing security.
  • Bundle Size: Server Components contribute zero JavaScript to the client bundle, while Client Components add to it.

When to Use Each

Use Server Components for static content, data fetching, and parts of the UI that don't need interactivity—such as product listings, blog posts, or SEO-critical sections. They improve performance and security. Use Client Components for interactive elements like forms, buttons, modals, or any component that relies on state or effects.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is trying to use hooks or event handlers inside a Server Component. This will cause a build error. To include interactivity, you must wrap the interactive part in a Client Component (marked with 'use client') and import it into the Server Component. However, be mindful that Client Components cannot be directly passed server-only data (like database connections) as props; they must receive serializable data.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[React App] --> B{Component Type?} B -->|Server Component| C[Render on Server] B -->|Client Component| D[Render on Client] C --> E[Static HTML sent to client] C --> F[No JS bundle for this component] D --> G[Full JS bundle sent] D --> H[Can use hooks & events] E --> I[Fast initial load] G --> J[Interactive UI]"}]

[{"question":"Explain what a closure is in JavaScript and provide a practical example.","expectedPoints":["Closure is a function that retains access to its lexical scope even when executed outside that scope.","It can access and modify variables from its outer function after the outer function has returned.","Each closure has its own separate environment; multiple closures from the same outer function do not share state.","Closures are commonly used for data encapsulation, factory functions, and maintaining state in callbacks.","The example should demonstrate a function returning another function that uses a variable from the outer scope."],"followUps":["How would you use a closure to create a private variable?","What is a common pitfall when using closures in loops?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine a backpack that you can carry around. Inside the backpack, you have a notebook that remembers things you wrote earlier, even if you are far away from home. A closure is like that backpack: it's a function that carries its own memory of variables from where it was created, so it can use them later even in a different place.","detailedExplanation":"

A closure is a fundamental concept in JavaScript where a function retains access to its lexical scope (the variables that were in scope at the time of its creation) even after the outer function has finished executing. This allows the inner function to remember and interact with variables from its outer environment.

How it works

When a function is defined inside another function, the inner function forms a closure over the outer function's variables. The JavaScript engine keeps a reference to the outer function's scope chain, so the variables are not garbage-collected as long as the closure exists. In the provided example, tạo_hàm_lấy_điểm_tích returns a function that closes over the variable điểm_tích. Each call to the returned function increments and returns that variable, demonstrating that the closure maintains its own state.

Why it matters in production

Closures are widely used for:

  • Data encapsulation – creating private variables that cannot be accessed from outside.
  • Factory functions – generating functions with preset behavior (like the example).
  • Event handlers and callbacks – preserving state across asynchronous operations.
  • Module pattern – building modules with private and public members.

Common pitfall

A frequent mistake occurs when closures are created inside loops using var. Since var has function scope, all closures share the same variable, leading to unexpected results. The fix is to use let (block scope) or an IIFE to capture the correct value per iteration.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Outer Function] -->|creates| B[Closure] B -->|remembers| C[Lexical Scope] C --> D[Variables] B -->|can be called| E[Later Execution] E -->|accesses| D subgraph Example F[outer: tạo_hàm_lấy_điểm_tích] --> G[inner: function()] G --> H[điểm_tích variable] end"}]

[{"question":"Explain the differences between arrow functions and regular functions in JavaScript, focusing on the 'this' keyword.","expectedPoints":["Arrow functions use => syntax and have implicit return for single expressions.","Arrow functions do not have their own 'this'; they inherit from the enclosing lexical scope.","Regular functions have their own 'this' determined by how they are called (dynamic binding).","Arrow functions cannot be used as constructors or with 'new' keyword.","Arrow functions do not have 'arguments' object; use rest parameters instead."],"followUps":["How would you fix a common bug where 'this' is undefined inside a regular function callback?","Can you give an example where using an arrow function for an object method causes unexpected behavior?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a remote control that can talk to different toys. A regular function is like a remote that always points to the toy you're holding it towards. An arrow function is like a remote that is stuck pointing to the same toy it was given when it was made, no matter where you point it. That's why arrow functions don't change what 'this' means.","detailedExplanation":"

Arrow functions and regular functions differ primarily in how they handle the this keyword, syntax, and their capabilities.

Syntax

Arrow functions use a concise syntax: const fn = (params) => expression. If the body is a single expression, it is implicitly returned. Regular functions use the function keyword and require explicit return.

The this Keyword

This is the most critical difference. Regular functions have their own this value, which is determined by how the function is called (dynamic binding). For example, if called as a method, this refers to the object; if called standalone, this refers to the global object (or undefined in strict mode).

Arrow functions do not have their own this. Instead, they capture the this value from the surrounding lexical context (the enclosing function or global scope). This makes them ideal for callbacks or closures where you want to preserve the outer this.

Other Differences

  • Constructors: Arrow functions cannot be used with new because they lack the internal [[Construct]] method.
  • arguments object: Arrow functions do not have their own arguments object; you must use rest parameters (...args).
  • Methods: Using arrow functions as object methods can lead to bugs because this will not refer to the object but to the outer scope.

Production Pitfall

A common mistake is using arrow functions for event handlers or object methods expecting dynamic this. For example, in a React class component, using an arrow function for a method ensures this refers to the class instance, but in a plain object, it may refer to the global object.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart TD A[Regular Function] --> B[Own 'this'?] B -->|Yes| C[Determined by call site] C --> D[Method: obj.fn() -> this=obj] C --> E[Standalone: fn() -> this=global/undefined] A2[Arrow Function] --> B2[Own 'this'?] B2 -->|No| C2[Inherits from outer scope] C2 --> D2[Lexical 'this' fixed at definition]"}]

[{"question":"Explain how the prototype chain works in JavaScript, including how ES6 classes utilize it for inheritance.","expectedPoints":["Describes prototype chain as a mechanism for property lookup through parent objects.","Explains that ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over prototype-based inheritance.","Shows how methods are added to the class's prototype object.","Demonstrates instance creation linking to prototype via __proto__.","Mentions that the chain ends at Object.prototype with null prototype."],"followUps":["What happens if you modify a method on a prototype after instances are created?","How does the 'super' keyword work in the context of the prototype chain?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine a family tree where kids can use their parents' tools. In JavaScript, objects can borrow methods from their 'parent' objects. If an object doesn't have a method, it asks its parent, and if not there, asks the grandparent, and so on. ES6 classes are just a fancy way to set up this family tree.","detailedExplanation":"

The prototype chain is JavaScript's core inheritance mechanism. Every object has an internal link to another object called its prototype. When a property or method is accessed on an object, JavaScript first checks the object itself. If not found, it follows the prototype link to the next object, continuing until the property is found or the chain ends at Object.prototype, whose prototype is null.

How ES6 Classes Use the Prototype Chain

ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over the existing prototype-based inheritance. When you declare a class, JavaScript creates a prototype object for that class. Methods defined inside the class are added to this prototype. For example:

class Animal { eat() { ... } }

This is equivalent to:

function Animal() {} Animal.prototype.eat = function() { ... };

When you instantiate a class with new, the new object's internal [[Prototype]] (accessible via __proto__ or Object.getPrototypeOf) is set to the class's .prototype object. This creates the chain: instance → Class.prototype → Object.prototype → null.

Inheritance with extends

The extends keyword sets up prototype chain between classes. For class Dog extends Animal, Dog.prototype's prototype becomes Animal.prototype. So a dog instance can access both Dog.prototype methods (like bark) and Animal.prototype methods (like eat) via the chain.

Why It Matters in Production

Understanding the prototype chain is crucial for debugging unexpected property access, optimizing memory (shared methods on prototype vs per-instance), and avoiding common pitfalls like overwriting prototype methods accidentally. It also helps when working with legacy code or libraries that manipulate prototypes directly.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is assuming that class methods are copied to each instance. In reality, they live on the prototype, so modifying a prototype method after instantiation affects all existing instances. For example:

class A { foo() { return 1; } } const a = new A(); A.prototype.foo = () => 2; console.log(a.foo()); // 2

This can lead to unexpected behavior if not anticipated.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[dog instance] --> B[Dog.prototype] B --> C[Animal.prototype] C --> D[Object.prototype] D --> E[null] subgraph Chain A B C D E end"}]
