[{"question":"Explain how Promises work in JavaScript and how they differ from callbacks.","expectedPoints":["Defines Promise as an object for handling async operations","Explains that Promise has states: pending, fulfilled, rejected","Compares Promise chaining vs callback nesting (callback hell)","Mentions .then() and .catch() for handling results and errors","Describes how async/await simplifies Promise usage"],"followUps":["How would you handle multiple independent async operations with Promises?","What happens if you forget to return a Promise in a .then() chain?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you order a pizza. A Promise is like the receipt they give you. You can do other things while waiting, and when the pizza is ready, you get it (fulfilled) or they tell you it's burnt (rejected). Callbacks are like giving them your phone number to call you when it's ready, but if you have many orders, it gets messy. Promises make it easier to handle multiple orders.","detailedExplanation":"

A Promise is an object representing the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation. It has three states: pending, fulfilled, and rejected. Promises provide a cleaner alternative to callbacks by allowing chaining with .then() and error handling with .catch().

How it works

When you create a Promise, you pass an executor function with resolve and reject parameters. Calling resolve transitions the Promise to fulfilled, and reject to rejected. The .then() method takes two optional callbacks: one for fulfillment and one for rejection. .catch() is syntactic sugar for .then(null, rejection).

Why it matters in production

Promises avoid callback hell (deeply nested callbacks) and make async code more readable. They enable error propagation through the chain, so you can handle errors in one place. With async/await, Promises become even more synchronous-looking, improving maintainability.

Common pitfall

Forgetting to return a Promise inside a .then() callback. This breaks the chain, causing the next .then() to run immediately with undefined instead of waiting for the async operation. Always ensure you return the Promise you want to chain.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Async Operation] --> B[Promise] B --> C{Pending} C -->|Success| D[Fulfilled] C -->|Failure| E[Rejected] D --> F[.then()] E --> G[.catch()] F --> H[Next Promise]"}]

[{"question":"Explain the difference between the == and === operators in JavaScript.","expectedPoints":["== performs type coercion before comparison","=== compares both value and type without coercion","Examples like 5 == '5' vs 5 === '5'","Common pitfalls with falsy values (0 == false)","Recommendation to use === for predictable results"],"followUps":["Can you give an example where == might lead to unexpected behavior?","How would you check if a variable is null or undefined safely?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have two boxes: one with the number 5 and another with the word '5'. The == operator is like a friend who says 'they are the same' because both have the idea of 5, even though one is a number and one is a word. The === operator is a strict friend who says 'they are different' because one is a number and one is a word. In JavaScript, using == can sometimes trick you, so it's safer to use ===.","detailedExplanation":"

The == (loose equality) and === (strict equality) operators are used to compare values in JavaScript. The key difference is that == performs type coercion before comparing, while === compares both value and type without coercion.

How they work

With ==, JavaScript converts the operands to the same type before comparison. For example, 5 == '5' returns true because the string '5' is converted to the number 5. With ===, no conversion happens, so 5 === '5' returns false because the types differ (number vs string).

Why it matters in production

Using == can lead to subtle bugs, especially with falsy values like 0, '', null, undefined, and false. For instance, 0 == false is true because both are falsy, but 0 === false is false. This can cause unexpected behavior in conditionals. Most style guides (e.g., Airbnb, Google) recommend using === exclusively to avoid these pitfalls.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is using == to check for null or undefined. While null == undefined is true, it's better to use explicit checks like value === null || value === undefined or the ?? operator for clarity.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[== Operator] --> B[Type Coercion] B --> C[Compare Values] C --> D[5 == '5' -> true] E[=== Operator] --> F[No Coercion] F --> G[Compare Type & Value] G --> H[5 === '5' -> false] D --> I[Unexpected Results?] H --> J[Predictable]"}]

[{"question":"What are Core Web Vitals and how do you optimize them?","expectedPoints":["Defines LCP, CLS, TBT with target thresholds","Explains optimization for LCP (images, CDN, code)","Explains optimization for CLS (fixed sizes, avoid JS layout shifts)","Explains optimization for TBT (efficient JS, lazy loading)","Mentions real-world impact on user experience and SEO"],"followUps":["How would you debug a poor CLS score in production?","What trade-offs exist between optimizing LCP and TBT?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy house. LCP is how fast the main door appears, CLS is if the walls suddenly move after you place furniture, and TBT is when you can't move because glue is drying. We want the door fast, walls steady, and no sticky delays.","detailedExplanation":"

Core Web Vitals are a set of real-world metrics that measure user experience on the web. They consist of three key indicators:

Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)

LCP measures the time it takes for the largest content element (image, video, or text block) to become visible. The target is under 2.5 seconds. To optimize, use optimized images (compression, next-gen formats), reduce HTTP requests, leverage a CDN, and minimize render-blocking resources.

Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)

CLS quantifies unexpected layout shifts during page load. A good score is below 0.1. Optimize by setting explicit dimensions on images and embeds, using font-display swap, and avoiding inserting content above existing elements after load.

Total Blocking Time (TBT)

TBT measures the total time the main thread is blocked by long tasks, preventing user interaction. Target is under 100 ms. Optimize by breaking up long JavaScript tasks, using code splitting, lazy loading non-critical resources, and deferring third-party scripts.

In production, poor Core Web Vitals can lead to lower search rankings and higher bounce rates. A common pitfall is focusing on only one metric; for example, aggressively optimizing LCP by lazy-loading images might increase CLS if dimensions aren't reserved. Always test with tools like Lighthouse and monitor real-user data via the Chrome User Experience Report.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Core Web Vitals] --> B[LCP] A --> C[CLS] A --> D[TBT] B --> E[<2.5s] C --> F[<0.1] D --> G[<100ms] E --> H[Optimize images, CDN] F --> I[Fixed sizes, no shifts] G --> J[Efficient JS, lazy load]"}]
qgen_v3:a9ae6fa1c9ebaf3e:
[{"question":"Explain how virtualization (windowing) works in the context of frontend performance optimization, and describe a scenario where you would use it to improve rendering performance.","expectedPoints":["Defines virtualization as rendering only visible items in a scrollable list.","Explains how it reduces DOM nodes and memory usage.","Describes a scenario like a chat app with thousands of messages.","Mentions libraries like react-window or react-virtualized.","Discusses trade-offs: complexity, scroll jump, and accessibility."],"followUps":["How would you handle variable item heights in a virtualized list?","What are the performance implications of virtualization on initial render?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a long list of toys, but you can only see a few at a time. Instead of building all the toys, you only build the ones you can see. When you scroll, you swap them out. This saves space and makes things faster.","detailedExplanation":"

Virtualization (windowing) is a technique used in frontend development to render only the visible portion of a large list or grid, dramatically reducing the number of DOM nodes and improving performance. Instead of rendering all items (e.g., thousands of rows), the component calculates which items are within the viewport (plus a small overscan buffer) and renders only those. As the user scrolls, the component recycles DOM nodes and updates their content.

How It Works

Typically, a virtualized list uses a container with a fixed height and overflow: hidden. It creates a spacer element to simulate the total scrollable height. The visible items are positioned absolutely based on their index. Libraries like react-window or react-virtualized handle the math: they track scroll position, compute visible range, and render only those items. When the user scrolls, the component recalculates and swaps out items.

Why It Matters in Production

Rendering thousands of DOM nodes causes jank, high memory usage, and slow interactions. Virtualization is essential for data-heavy UIs like chat logs, infinite feeds, or large tables. It keeps the DOM lean, reduces layout thrashing, and ensures smooth scrolling even on low-end devices.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is not handling variable item heights correctly. If items have dynamic heights, the component cannot accurately calculate the total scroll height or item positions, leading to scroll jumps or blank spaces. Solutions include measuring items after render or using a fixed height with content clipping.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Scrollable Container] --> B[Calculate Viewport] B --> C[Visible Items Only] C --> D[Render DOM Nodes] D --> E[Recycle Nodes on Scroll] E --> B subgraph Virtualization B C D E end"}]
qgen_v3:46197f34c37f3a15:
[{"question":"Explain the difference between debounce and throttle, and provide a use case for each.","expectedPoints":["Debounce delays execution until after a pause in events; throttle limits execution rate.","Debounce is used for input autocomplete; throttle for scroll or resize events.","Both improve performance by reducing function call frequency.","Debounce resets timer on each event; throttle ensures at most one call per interval."],"followUps":["How would you implement a debounce function that also supports immediate execution?","Can you think of a scenario where using throttle instead of debounce would be better?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're playing a game where you have to press a button to shoot. Debounce is like waiting until you stop pressing the button for a moment before shooting once. Throttle is like shooting only once every second, no matter how fast you press. Both help prevent too many actions from happening too quickly.","detailedExplanation":"

Debounce and throttle are two techniques used to control how often a function is executed in response to frequent events, such as typing, scrolling, or resizing. They are essential for optimizing performance in frontend applications.

Debounce

Debounce ensures that a function is called only after a specified period of inactivity. Each time the event fires, the timer resets. If the event fires again before the timer expires, the previous timer is cleared and a new one starts. The function executes only when the events stop for the given wait time. Common use cases include search input autocomplete (waiting for the user to stop typing before making an API call) and form validation.

Throttle

Throttle ensures that a function is called at most once in a specified interval. No matter how many times the event fires, the function will only execute once per interval. This is useful for scroll event handlers (e.g., infinite scroll) and resize event handlers (e.g., recalculating layout).

Key Differences

  • Debounce groups a burst of events into a single call after a pause.
  • Throttle guarantees a regular flow of calls, preventing too many executions.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is using debounce for scroll events, which can cause the handler to never fire if the user keeps scrolling continuously. Throttle is more appropriate for continuous events.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Event] --> B{Debounce?} B -->|Yes| C[Reset Timer] C --> D[Wait for pause] D --> E[Execute once] B -->|No| F{Throttle?} F -->|Yes| G[Check interval] G -->|Time elapsed| H[Execute] G -->|Not elapsed| I[Skip] H --> J[Reset interval]"}]
qgen_v3:398421dd32206ead:
[{"question":"Explain code splitting and lazy loading strategies for optimizing web application performance.","expectedPoints":["Code splitting divides code into smaller chunks loaded on demand.","Lazy loading defers loading of resources until they are needed.","Both reduce initial bundle size and improve load time.","Common tools: Webpack, Rollup, ES modules, dynamic imports.","Trade-offs: increased complexity, potential for delayed interactions."],"followUps":["How would you implement code splitting in a React application?","What are the downsides of overusing lazy loading?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge toy box. Instead of carrying it all at once, you split it into smaller bags (code splitting) and only open a bag when you need a specific toy (lazy loading). This makes it faster to start playing.","detailedExplanation":"

Code splitting and lazy loading are performance optimization techniques that reduce the initial load time of a web application by deferring the loading of non-critical code.

What They Are

Code splitting is the practice of breaking the application's bundle into smaller chunks that can be loaded on demand. Lazy loading is the technique of deferring the loading of a chunk until it is actually needed (e.g., when a user navigates to a route or interacts with a component).

How They Work

Modern bundlers like Webpack and Rollup support dynamic imports (import()) to create split points. For example, in a React app, you can use React.lazy() with Suspense to lazy-load a component. The browser fetches the chunk only when the component is rendered.

Why They Matter in Production

Large JavaScript bundles can significantly delay the Time to Interactive (TTI). By splitting code, you serve only the essential code initially, improving perceived performance and reducing bandwidth usage. This is critical for mobile users or slow networks.

Common Pitfall

Over-splitting can lead to too many small HTTP requests, causing network overhead. Also, lazy loading critical above-the-fold content can hurt First Contentful Paint (FCP). A good strategy is to split by routes or rarely used features.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[App Bundle] -->|Code Splitting| B[Chunk 1: Core] A --> C[Chunk 2: Feature A] A --> D[Chunk 3: Feature B] B -->|Loads immediately| E[Initial Render] C -->|Lazy load on route| F[Feature A Loaded] D -->|Lazy load on interaction| G[Feature B Loaded]"}]
qgen_v3:bfcf322b814bf081:
[{"question":"How does normalizing state reduce complexity and improve performance in frontend applications, and how would you combine it with optimistic updates?","expectedPoints":["Explains normalization: storing each entity type in a flat dictionary keyed by ID, avoiding duplication.","Describes benefits: easier updates, no cascading changes, faster lookups, and simpler reducer logic.","Defines optimistic update: updating UI immediately before server confirmation, then reverting on failure.","Shows integration: normalized state allows targeted optimistic updates without affecting other entities.","Mentions trade-offs: need for rollback logic, potential stale data, and increased complexity in error handling."],"followUps":["How would you handle rollback of an optimistic update in a normalized state store?","What tools or libraries would you use to implement this pattern in a React/Redux app?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big box of LEGO pieces. If you keep all pieces mixed together, finding one brick is hard. Normalizing means sorting each color into its own small box, so you can quickly grab the brick you need. Optimistic update is like building with a piece you think you have, even before checking if it's really there—you assume it works, and if not, you fix it later.","detailedExplanation":"

Normalizing state is a pattern where data is stored in a flat, denormalized structure—typically as a dictionary of entities keyed by ID, with separate collections for each entity type (e.g., users, posts). This avoids duplication and makes updates predictable: changing a single entity only requires updating one location in the store, preventing cascading re-renders and simplifying reducer logic.

How It Works

Instead of nested objects like { posts: [{ id: 1, author: { name: 'Alice' } }] }, you store { posts: { 1: { id: 1, authorId: 1 } }, users: { 1: { name: 'Alice' } } }. Selectors then join data on the fly. This makes optimistic updates more manageable: when a user performs an action (e.g., liking a post), you immediately update the likes count in the normalized post entity, assuming success. If the server fails, you revert just that one field.

Why It Matters in Production

In large-scale apps, normalized state reduces bugs from stale or inconsistent data. Combined with optimistic updates, it delivers a snappy user experience—UI updates instantly, and rollback is localized. However, it requires careful handling of rollback logic (e.g., saving previous state snapshots) and managing pending states to avoid showing incorrect data.

Common Pitfall

Over-normalization can lead to excessive joins and complex selectors, hurting performance. Always balance normalization with the actual query patterns of your UI.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Action] --> B[Optimistic Update] B --> C[Normalized Store] C --> D[Update Entity by ID] D --> E[UI Renders Immediately] E --> F{Server Response} F -- Success --> G[Confirm Update] F -- Failure --> H[Rollback Entity] H --> C"}]
qgen_v3:88c40abbda9af692:
[{"question":"Compare and contrast Polling, Long-polling, WebSocket, and SSE for real-time data updates. When would you choose each?","expectedPoints":["Explains Polling: periodic requests, simple but wasteful.","Explains Long-polling: holds connection until data available, reduces unnecessary requests.","Explains WebSocket: full-duplex, low latency, requires server support.","Explains SSE: server push, unidirectional, simpler than WebSocket.","Provides trade-offs based on performance, resource usage, and browser support."],"followUps":["How would you implement a fallback mechanism for browsers that don't support WebSocket?","What are the scalability concerns with WebSocket compared to SSE?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to know when your mom bakes cookies. Polling is like asking every 5 minutes. Long-polling is asking once and waiting until she says 'done'. WebSocket is like a phone call where you both talk anytime. SSE is like a radio where mom announces when cookies are ready, but you can't reply. Each has pros and cons depending on how often you need updates and how much effort you want to spend.","detailedExplanation":"

Real-time data updates are crucial for modern web applications like chat, live notifications, or dashboards. Four common techniques exist: Polling, Long-polling, WebSocket, and Server-Sent Events (SSE).

Polling

Client sends HTTP requests at fixed intervals (e.g., every 5 seconds). Simple to implement but inefficient: many requests return no new data, wasting bandwidth and server resources. Suitable for low-frequency updates where latency isn't critical.

Long-polling

Client sends a request; server holds it open until new data is available, then responds. Client immediately sends a new request. Reduces empty responses but still uses HTTP overhead and can cause connection buildup. Better than polling for moderate update frequency.

WebSocket

Establishes a persistent, full-duplex connection over a single TCP socket. Low latency, real-time bidirectional communication. Requires server-side support (e.g., ws library) and browser API. Ideal for chat, gaming, collaborative editing. Scalability can be challenging due to stateful connections.

Server-Sent Events (SSE)

Server pushes events to client over HTTP. Unidirectional (server to client), simpler than WebSocket, works over standard HTTP/2. Built-in reconnection and event IDs. Not supported in all browsers (e.g., IE). Good for notifications, stock tickers, or any one-way data stream.

Choosing the Right Method

  • Performance: WebSocket or SSE for frequent updates.
  • Resource usage: Long-polling or SSE to reduce server load.
  • Browser support: WebSocket widely supported; SSE lacks IE support.
  • Bidirectionality: WebSocket if client needs to send data frequently.

Common Pitfall: Using Polling for high-frequency updates can overwhelm both client and server. Always consider the update frequency and network conditions.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client] -->|Polling: request every 5s| B[Server] A -->|Long-polling: hold request| C[Server] C -->|respond when data| A A <-->|WebSocket: persistent full-duplex| D[Server] A -->|SSE: server push| E[Server] E -->|event stream| A"}]
qgen_v3:c63f059427684668:
[{"question":"Explain the Critical Rendering Path (CRP) and its steps.","expectedPoints":["CRP converts HTML/CSS/JS to pixels on screen.","Steps: DOM construction, CSSOM construction, render tree, layout, paint, composite.","Optimization techniques: lazy loading, code splitting, caching.","CRP runs in the event loop via the rendering engine."],"followUps":["How does JavaScript execution block CRP?","What is the difference between layout and paint?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a LEGO castle. First, you read the instructions (HTML parsing) to know what pieces you have. Then you figure out where each piece goes (layout). Next, you color each piece (paint). Finally, you put all the colored pieces together to see the whole castle (composite). The Critical Rendering Path is like this process for a webpage.","detailedExplanation":"

The Critical Rendering Path (CRP) is the sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into pixels on the screen. It is crucial for understanding page load performance.

Steps of CRP

  • DOM Construction: The browser parses HTML and builds the Document Object Model (DOM) tree.
  • CSSOM Construction: CSS is parsed to create the CSS Object Model (CSSOM) tree.
  • Render Tree: The DOM and CSSOM are combined to form the render tree, which contains only visible elements.
  • Layout: The browser calculates the position and size of each element in the viewport.
  • Paint: The browser fills in pixels for each element (e.g., colors, images).
  • Composite: Layers are combined to produce the final image.

Why It Matters

Optimizing CRP reduces time to first paint and improves user experience. Techniques like lazy loading (deferring non-critical resources), code splitting (loading only needed JS), and caching (reusing resources) minimize blocking and reflows.

Common Pitfall

Blocking the CRP with synchronous JavaScript or large CSS files delays rendering. Always use async or defer for scripts and inline critical CSS.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[HTML] --> B[DOM Tree] C[CSS] --> D[CSSOM Tree] B --> E[Render Tree] D --> E E --> F[Layout] F --> G[Paint] G --> H[Composite] H --> I[Draw]"}]
qgen_v3:99e1d19a15ecf4a4:
[{"question":"What are the differences between cookies, localStorage, and IndexedDB for client-side storage?","expectedPoints":["Cookies are sent with every HTTP request, limited to ~4KB, and can be set to expire.","localStorage provides up to ~5-10MB, persists until cleared, and is not sent to server automatically.","IndexedDB is an asynchronous NoSQL database for large structured data, supports indexing and transactions.","Use cases: cookies for session/auth, localStorage for simple key-value data, IndexedDB for complex offline apps."],"followUps":["When would you choose IndexedDB over localStorage?","What security considerations apply to each storage mechanism?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have three different boxes to keep your toys. Cookies are like tiny sticky notes that you attach to your backpack and show to everyone you meet. localStorage is like a big drawer in your room that stays there until you clean it out. IndexedDB is like a huge filing cabinet where you can organize lots of papers and find them quickly. Each is good for different things.","detailedExplanation":"

Client-side storage mechanisms allow web applications to persist data on the user's device. The three main options are cookies, localStorage, and IndexedDB, each with distinct characteristics.

Cookies

Cookies are small text files (max ~4KB) that are automatically sent with every HTTP request to the same domain. They can be set with an expiration date and support HttpOnly and Secure flags. Common uses include session management, user preferences, and tracking. However, they increase request size and are limited in capacity.

localStorage

localStorage is a key-value store with a typical limit of 5-10MB per origin. Data persists until explicitly deleted and is not sent to the server automatically. It is synchronous and only stores strings. Ideal for caching UI state or simple data that doesn't need server interaction.

IndexedDB

IndexedDB is an asynchronous, NoSQL-like database that can store large amounts of structured data (hundreds of MBs). It supports indexes, transactions, and object stores. It is more complex but suitable for offline-first applications, file storage, or complex querying.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is using localStorage for sensitive data, as it is accessible via JavaScript and vulnerable to XSS. Always use HttpOnly cookies for authentication tokens and consider encryption for sensitive data in IndexedDB.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client Storage] --> B[Cookies] A --> C[localStorage] A --> D[IndexedDB] B --> E[~4KB, sent with requests] C --> F[~5-10MB, key-value] D --> G[Large, async, NoSQL]"}]
qgen_v3:3415fe953e537af1:
[{"question":"Explain how CORS works and why it is necessary for web security.","expectedPoints":["CORS is a browser security mechanism that controls cross-origin HTTP requests.","It prevents malicious sites from reading sensitive data from another origin.","Preflight requests (OPTIONS) are sent for non-simple requests to check server permissions.","Server must include Access-Control-Allow-Origin header to allow cross-origin access.","CORS mitigates attacks like XSS and CSRF by enforcing same-origin policy exceptions."],"followUps":["What is the difference between simple and preflight requests?","How would you handle CORS errors in a production API?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you live in a gated community (your website) and want to visit a friend in another community (another website). CORS is like a security guard at the gate who checks if your friend's community allows visitors from your community. If yes, you can enter; if not, you're blocked. This prevents strangers from sneaking in.","detailedExplanation":"

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security mechanism that controls how web pages from one origin can request resources from a different origin. It is essential for preventing malicious websites from accessing sensitive data from another site without permission.

How It Works

When a web application makes a cross-origin request, the browser adds an Origin header. For simple requests (e.g., GET with standard headers), the browser sends the request directly and checks the response for Access-Control-Allow-Origin. For non-simple requests (e.g., PUT, DELETE, or custom headers), the browser first sends a preflight request using the HTTP OPTIONS method to ask the server if the actual request is allowed. The server must respond with appropriate CORS headers like Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, and Access-Control-Allow-Headers.

Why It Matters in Production

Without CORS, any website could make requests to your API and read the response, leading to data theft or CSRF attacks. Proper CORS configuration ensures that only trusted origins can interact with your backend, protecting user data.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * in production, which allows any origin. This is acceptable for public APIs but dangerous for authenticated endpoints. Always restrict to specific origins and avoid using wildcards with credentials.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Browser] -->|Request with Origin| B[Server] B -->|Response with ACAO header| A subgraph Preflight C[OPTIONS request] --> D[Server checks permissions] D -->|Allowed| E[Actual request sent] end A -->|Non-simple request| C"}]
qgen_v3:464a077444425613:
[{"question":"Explain the difference between XSS and CSRF attacks and how to prevent them.","expectedPoints":["XSS injects malicious scripts into web pages; CSRF tricks users into performing unwanted actions.","XSS exploits trust in user input; CSRF exploits trust in authenticated sessions.","Prevention for XSS includes output encoding, input validation, and Content Security Policy (CSP).","Prevention for CSRF includes anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies, and double-submit cookies.","Both require defense-in-depth; CSP helps mitigate XSS, while SameSite cookies help mitigate CSRF."],"followUps":["How would you implement a CSRF token in a React application?","Can CSP prevent all XSS attacks? Why or why not?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a diary that only you can write in. XSS is like someone sneaking a note into your diary that makes you do things you didn't want to do. CSRF is like someone tricking you into writing something in your diary without you realizing it. To protect your diary, you check every note (XSS prevention) and use a secret code for each page (CSRF prevention).","detailedExplanation":"

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) and CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) are two distinct web security attacks that exploit different trust relationships.

XSS

XSS occurs when an attacker injects malicious scripts (e.g., JavaScript) into a web application, which then executes in the victim's browser. This exploits the trust a user has in a legitimate website. The injected script can steal cookies, redirect users, or deface pages. There are three main types: Stored XSS (malicious script saved on the server), Reflected XSS (script reflected off a server via a request), and DOM-based XSS (vulnerability in client-side code).

CSRF

CSRF tricks an authenticated user into performing unintended actions on a web application where they are logged in. It exploits the trust a website has in the user's browser (via cookies). For example, an attacker can craft a malicious link that, when clicked by a logged-in user, changes their email or transfers funds.

Prevention

  • XSS Prevention: Use output encoding (e.g., HTML entity encoding), input validation (whitelist allowed characters), and Content Security Policy (CSP) to restrict script sources. Avoid using innerHTML with untrusted data.
  • CSRF Prevention: Use anti-CSRF tokens (unique, unpredictable tokens sent with each request), SameSite cookies (set to Strict or Lax), and double-submit cookies (send cookie value in a header). Also, verify the Origin or Referer header.

Common Pitfall

Developers often confuse XSS and CSRF. A common mistake is relying solely on input validation for XSS prevention, which is insufficient; output encoding is critical. For CSRF, using GET requests for state-changing operations is a major vulnerability.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Browser] -->|Legitimate Request| B[Web App] C[Attacker] -->|XSS: Injects Script| D[Victim Browser] D -->|Executes Malicious Code| B C -->|CSRF: Forged Request| E[Victim Browser] E -->|Sends Authenticated Request| B subgraph Prevention F[Output Encoding] G[CSP] H[CSRF Token] I[SameSite Cookie] end B --> F B --> G B --> H B --> I"}]
qgen_v3:d8d20b1e9f5016b7:
[{"question":"Explain the testing pyramid and the purpose of each layer: unit, integration, and end-to-end tests.","expectedPoints":["Describes the three layers: unit, integration, e2e.","Explains unit tests focus on individual functions/classes.","Explains integration tests verify combined units work together.","Explains e2e tests simulate real user scenarios from start to finish.","Mentions the pyramid shape implies more unit tests, fewer e2e tests."],"followUps":["Why is it recommended to have more unit tests than e2e tests?","How would you decide what to test at each layer in a React application?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine building a LEGO castle. Unit tests check each brick is perfect. Integration tests check if two bricks click together correctly. End-to-end tests check the whole castle stands and a LEGO knight can walk through the gate. The pyramid means you test many bricks, fewer connections, and just a few full castles.","detailedExplanation":"

The testing pyramid is a concept that describes the ideal distribution of automated tests across three layers: unit, integration, and end-to-end (e2e). It emphasizes having a large number of fast, isolated unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and a small number of slow, comprehensive e2e tests at the top.

Unit Tests

These test the smallest pieces of code—individual functions, methods, or classes—in isolation. They are fast, reliable, and help catch bugs early. For example, testing a formatDate function with various inputs.

Integration Tests

These verify that multiple units work together correctly. They test interactions between components, such as a React component calling an API and updating state. They are slower than unit tests but catch interface mismatches.

End-to-End Tests

These simulate real user scenarios from start to finish, often using tools like Cypress or Playwright. They test the entire system—UI, backend, database—but are brittle and slow. Use them sparingly for critical user flows.

In production, the pyramid guides test strategy: invest heavily in unit tests for fast feedback, use integration tests for key interactions, and limit e2e tests to core journeys. A common pitfall is over-relying on e2e tests, leading to slow, flaky suites that discourage developers from running them.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart TD subgraph Testing Pyramid A[Unit Tests] --> B[Integration Tests] B --> C[E2E Tests] end A --> D[Fast & Isolated] B --> E[Check Interactions] C --> F[Simulate User Flows]"}]
qgen_v3:d0cce5023f7a40ac:
[{"question":"Why should we prefer behavior-driven testing (BDD) over implementation-focused unit testing?","expectedPoints":["BDD aligns tests with user requirements and business value","BDD reduces coupling to implementation details, making refactoring safer","BDD improves maintainability by avoiding brittle tests","BDD reduces test duplication by focusing on outcomes","BDD increases confidence in deployment by validating real behavior"],"followUps":["Can you give an example where a unit test would break but the behavior remains correct?","When would you still choose implementation-focused unit tests over BDD?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're testing a vending machine. A behavior test checks if you get a soda when you put in money and press a button. An implementation test checks if the internal gears turn exactly 3 times. The behavior test is better because it focuses on what the user cares about, and it still works even if the machine's insides are redesigned.","detailedExplanation":"

Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) focuses on testing the observable behavior of an application from the user's perspective, rather than testing internal implementation details. This approach uses natural language scenarios (Given-When-Then) that are understandable by both technical and non-technical stakeholders.

Key Benefits

  • Alignment with requirements: BDD tests directly validate that the application meets business needs, reducing the risk of building the wrong thing.
  • Reduced coupling: Tests are less dependent on code structure, so refactoring or changing internal logic doesn't break tests as long as the behavior remains the same.
  • Improved maintainability: BDD tests are more stable and require fewer updates when implementation changes, lowering maintenance cost.
  • Less duplication: BDD encourages testing outcomes rather than every internal function, avoiding overlapping tests.
  • Higher confidence: Passing BDD tests gives stronger assurance that the system works correctly in real scenarios.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is to write BDD tests that are too granular or that still test implementation details (e.g., checking internal state). This defeats the purpose and creates brittle tests. BDD tests should focus on external behavior and side effects visible to the user.

While BDD is powerful, unit tests still have their place for testing complex algorithms, edge cases, or critical business logic where isolating a small unit is necessary. The key is to use the right tool for the right level of abstraction.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Action] --> B[BDD Test] B --> C[Verify Outcome] A --> D[Unit Test] D --> E[Check Internal State] subgraph BDD B C end subgraph Unit D E end"}]
qgen_v3:eae35f353fc11c49:
[{"question":"Explain the differences between mock, stub, and spy in testing, and describe scenarios where each is most appropriate.","expectedPoints":["Mock: pre-programmed expectations, used to verify behavior (interaction testing).","Stub: provides canned answers, used to control indirect inputs.","Spy: wraps real object, records calls, allows partial mocking.","Mock vs Stub: mocks assert on interactions, stubs only return values.","Spy use case: when you need real behavior but want to verify calls."],"followUps":["How would you decide between using a mock and a stub for a given test?","Can you give an example where a spy is more appropriate than a mock?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're testing a robot that delivers packages. A mock is like a fake person who always gives the same answer, so you can check if the robot asks the right questions. A stub is like a fake mailbox that always has a package inside, so you can test what the robot does when it finds one. A spy is like a camera that watches the real person and records what they do, so you can check later if they did the right thing.","detailedExplanation":"

Mock, stub, and spy are test doubles used to isolate the system under test (SUT) from its dependencies. They serve different purposes and are chosen based on the testing goal.

Mock

A mock is an object that pre-programs expectations about which methods will be called, with what arguments, and how many times. It is used for behavior verification — you assert that the SUT interacted with the mock correctly. Mocks are ideal when you want to ensure that certain side effects (e.g., sending an email, logging) occur as expected.

Stub

A stub provides predefined responses to method calls, but does not verify interactions. It is used for state verification — you check the SUT's state after execution. Stubs are useful when you need to control indirect inputs, such as returning a specific value from a database call.

Spy

A spy wraps a real object and records method calls, arguments, and return values. It allows partial mocking: you can use the real implementation for most methods but override some. Spies are helpful when you want to verify that a method was called (or not called) while still using the real object's behavior.

When to Use Each

  • Mock: When you need to verify interactions (e.g., “Did the service call the repository?”).
  • Stub: When you need to provide controlled responses (e.g., “Return a user object when getUser is called”).
  • Spy: When you need real behavior but want to track calls (e.g., “Log the number of times sendEmail is invoked”).

Common Pitfall

Overusing mocks can lead to brittle tests that break when implementation details change. Prefer stubs for state verification and reserve mocks for critical interactions. Spies should be used sparingly as they can hide coupling issues.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR SUT[System Under Test] -->|calls| Double[Test Double] subgraph Test Doubles M[Mock] S[Stub] Sp[Spy] end M -->|pre-programmed expectations| Behavior[Behavior Verification] S -->|canned responses| State[State Verification] Sp -->|wraps real object| Record[Record Calls] Behavior -->|assert interactions| Pass[Test Pass/Fail] State -->|assert state| Pass Record -->|verify calls| Pass"}]
qgen_v3:157851cea9c8e8e8:
[{"question":"Walk me through how you would design the frontend architecture for a real-time collaborative document editor like Google Docs, using the RADIO framework.","expectedPoints":["Identifies key requirements: real-time sync, conflict resolution, offline support, accessibility.","Proposes architecture: component tree, state management (e.g., CRDT or OT), data flow.","Discusses UI/UX design: toolbar, cursor presence, version history.","Covers implementation choices: WebSockets, service workers, rendering strategy.","Addresses optimization: debouncing, virtual scrolling, lazy loading, performance monitoring."],"followUps":["How would you handle conflict resolution when two users edit the same paragraph simultaneously?","What metrics would you use to measure perceived performance in this application?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and a friend are drawing on the same digital whiteboard from different computers. You need to design how the drawing app works so that both of you see changes instantly and don't mess up each other's work. This question asks you to think through all the parts of that app, from the buttons on screen to how data travels across the internet.","detailedExplanation":"

The RADIO framework provides a structured approach to frontend system design. Let's apply it to a real-time collaborative document editor.

Requirements (R)

Key requirements include real-time synchronization with low latency, conflict resolution (e.g., Operational Transform or CRDT), offline support via service workers, accessibility (ARIA labels, keyboard navigation), and scalability to many concurrent users. Non-functional requirements: perceived performance (instant feedback), reliability (no data loss), and maintainability (modular code).

Architecture (A)

Propose a component tree: App → Toolbar, Editor, Sidebar (comments, version history). Use a state management library (e.g., Redux or Zustand) with a WebSocket layer for real-time updates. Data flow: user input → local state → optimistic update → send to server → receive acknowledgment → reconcile. For conflict resolution, use CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) to merge changes without a central server.

Design (D)

UI/UX design: toolbar with formatting options, cursor presence showing other users' selections, version history slider. Ensure responsive design for mobile and desktop.

Implementation (I)

Choose React for component-based UI, WebSocket for bidirectional communication, Service Worker for offline caching, and IndexedDB for local storage. Use virtual scrolling for long documents and lazy loading for images.

Optimization (O)

Optimize perceived performance: debounce local saves, show optimistic UI, use requestAnimationFrame for smooth rendering. Monitor with Lighthouse and Web Vitals. Common pitfall: ignoring memory leaks from WebSocket listeners; always clean up on unmount.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR R[Requirements] --> A[Architecture] A --> D[Design] D --> I[Implementation] I --> O[Optimization] subgraph RADIO R A D I O end subgraph Example A1[Component Tree] A2[State Mgmt] A3[WebSocket] A --> A1 A --> A2 A --> A3 end"}]
qgen_v3:0121a8fe8cd53d79:
[{"question":"What is the core difference between frontend and backend system design?","expectedPoints":["Frontend focuses on user experience, client architecture, rendering, and perceived performance.","Backend focuses on throughput, database, server scaling, and data processing.","Both require trade-off thinking and scaling considerations.","Frontend design involves component organization, state management, network optimization."],"followUps":["Can you give an example of a trade-off in frontend system design?","How does perceived performance differ from actual performance?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a lemonade stand. Frontend design is like how you arrange the cups, signs, and payment so customers have a smooth experience. Backend design is like how you manage your lemon supply and make sure you don't run out. Both are important but focus on different things.","detailedExplanation":"

Frontend system design and backend system design serve different purposes in software architecture. The core difference lies in their primary focus areas.

Frontend System Design

Frontend design centers on the client side: how the application is structured in the browser, how components are organized, how state is managed, and how rendering affects perceived performance. Key concerns include:

  • Component architecture: Reusable, maintainable UI components.
  • State management: Client-side data flow (e.g., Redux, Context API).
  • Rendering optimization: Virtual DOM, lazy loading, code splitting.
  • Network efficiency: Caching, CDN, API request batching.
  • User experience: Smooth interactions, fast load times, accessibility.

Backend System Design

Backend design focuses on the server side: handling requests, processing data, and scaling infrastructure. Key concerns include:

  • Throughput and latency: Number of requests per second, response times.
  • Database design: Schema, indexing, replication, sharding.
  • Server scaling: Horizontal vs vertical scaling, load balancing.
  • Data consistency: ACID vs BASE, distributed transactions.
  • Security and authentication: Authorization, encryption.

Why It Matters in Production

Understanding the distinction helps teams allocate resources effectively. A frontend-heavy application (e.g., a real-time dashboard) needs optimized rendering and state management, while a backend-heavy system (e.g., a payment processor) needs robust data handling and high throughput. Both require trade-off thinking: for example, caching improves performance but may serve stale data.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming frontend design doesn't need to consider scale. While backend scales servers, frontend must scale code complexity and user interactions. For instance, a poorly designed component tree can cause re-renders that degrade performance even on a fast server.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Frontend Design] --> B[User Experience] A --> C[Client Architecture] A --> D[Perceived Performance] E[Backend Design] --> F[Throughput] E --> G[Database] E --> H[Server Scaling] subgraph Trade-offs I[Caching vs Freshness] J[Bundle Size vs Features] end"}]

[{"question":"How would you categorize and prioritize requirements for a social media feed feature in a frontend system design interview?","expectedPoints":["Distinguishes functional vs non-functional requirements","Identifies constraints like device, network, browser support","Prioritizes based on user impact and technical feasibility","Provides concrete examples for each category"],"followUps":["How would you handle conflicting requirements between performance and feature richness?","What trade-offs would you consider for offline support?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy box. Functional requirements are what the box does (holds toys, has a lid). Non-functional requirements are how well it works (strong enough, easy to open). Constraints are rules like 'must fit under the bed' or 'must be made of wood'. In a frontend interview, you need to think about all these for a feature like a news feed.","detailedExplanation":"

In frontend system design, categorizing requirements is crucial for building a scalable and user-friendly product. The main categories are functional, non-functional, and constraints.

Functional Requirements

These define what the system does. For a social media feed, examples include: users can create posts, like, comment, scroll infinitely, and upload images. They are the core features that directly deliver user value.

Non-Functional Requirements

These describe how the system performs. Key aspects include: performance (load time < 2s), smoothness (60fps scrolling), offline support, accessibility (a11y), and SEO. They often determine user satisfaction and retention.

Constraints

These are boundaries like target devices (mobile-first vs desktop), supported browsers (Chrome, Safari), network conditions (3G/4G), multi-language support, and framework choice (React, Vue). Constraints limit design options but ensure feasibility.

Prioritization

Use a MoSCoW method (Must-have, Should-have, Could-have, Won't-have) or impact-effort matrix. For example, infinite scroll is a must-have for engagement, while offline mode might be a should-have. Always align with business goals and user needs.

Common Pitfall

Mixing functional and non-functional requirements. For instance, saying 'the feed must load fast' is non-functional, not functional. Keep categories distinct to avoid confusion during implementation.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Requirements] --> B[Functional] A --> C[Non-Functional] A --> D[Constraints] B --> E[Post, Like, Comment] C --> F[Performance, Offline] D --> G[Device, Browser, Network]"}]

[{"question":"How would you optimize the performance of an application with many concurrent requests?","expectedPoints":["Use debouncing or throttling to limit request frequency.","Implement request deduplication or caching.","Consider Web Workers for offloading heavy tasks.","Use connection pooling or keep-alive for network requests.","Monitor and optimize critical rendering path (e.g., lazy loading)."],"followUps":["How would you handle race conditions when requests complete out of order?","What tools would you use to measure performance impact?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're at a busy ice cream shop with many customers. To serve everyone quickly, you might group orders, prepare popular flavors in advance, and have multiple workers. Similarly, in an app, we can group requests, cache results, and use background workers to handle many tasks at once without slowing down.","detailedExplanation":"

Optimizing an application with many concurrent requests is crucial for maintaining responsiveness and scalability. The key is to reduce the load on the main thread and network.

Key Strategies

  • Debouncing and Throttling: Limit how often a function can fire. Debouncing waits for a pause, throttling ensures a maximum rate. Useful for search inputs or scroll events.
  • Request Deduplication: If multiple components request the same data simultaneously, combine them into one network call and share the result. Libraries like React Query or SWR do this automatically.
  • Caching: Store responses in memory (e.g., Map) or using service workers. Avoid redundant fetches for unchanged data.
  • Web Workers: Offload CPU-intensive tasks (parsing, encryption) to a separate thread so the UI stays responsive.
  • Connection Management: Use HTTP/2 multiplexing, keep-alive, and limit the number of simultaneous connections (e.g., browser limit of 6 per domain).

Why It Matters in Production

Poor handling leads to thundering herd problems, increased latency, and potential server overload. Users experience janky interfaces and timeouts.

Common Pitfall

Over-optimizing prematurely. Not all requests need debouncing or caching. Measure first (e.g., using Chrome DevTools Performance tab) to identify actual bottlenecks.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Concurrent Requests] --> B{Queue?} B -->|Yes| C[Debounce/Throttle] B -->|No| D[Parallel Requests] C --> E[Single Request] D --> F[Cache/Dedup] F --> G[Response] E --> G"}]

[{"question":"How would you architect a React application to ensure separation of concerns and maintainability as it scales?","expectedPoints":["Divides app into independent modules (e.g., components, services, state management).","Uses component composition with clear props/state boundaries.","Applies lazy loading and code splitting for performance.","Separates UI from business logic (e.g., custom hooks, container/presentational pattern).","Considers folder structure (feature-based or type-based)."],"followUps":["How do you decide when to create a new component vs. extending an existing one?","What tradeoffs do you consider when choosing between lifting state up and using context?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a giant LEGO castle. Instead of making one huge block, you build separate towers, walls, and gates that can be moved or changed without breaking the whole castle. In React, we split the app into small, independent pieces (components) that each do one job, so it's easier to fix or add new features later.","detailedExplanation":"

When architecting a React application for scale, the goal is to achieve separation of concerns — each part of the codebase has a single, well-defined responsibility. This makes the app easier to understand, test, and maintain.

Key Principles

  • Component decomposition: Break the UI into small, reusable components. Each component should ideally do one thing (Single Responsibility Principle).
  • Clear data flow: Use props to pass data down and callbacks to communicate up. Avoid deeply nested prop drilling by using context or state management libraries only when necessary.
  • Separation of UI and logic: Extract business logic into custom hooks or separate service modules. Keep components focused on rendering.
  • Folder structure: Organize by feature (e.g., features/auth, features/dashboard) rather than by type (components, containers, etc.) to improve cohesion.
  • Performance optimization: Use React.lazy and Suspense for code splitting, and React.memo or useMemo to avoid unnecessary re-renders.

How It Works in Production

In a real-world app, you might have a feature-based architecture where each feature folder contains its own components, hooks, styles, and tests. For example, a UserProfile feature would include UserProfile.js, useUserProfile.js, UserProfile.css, and UserProfile.test.js. This makes it easy to locate and modify code related to a specific feature without affecting others.

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering the architecture too early. Avoid creating too many abstractions (e.g., premature custom hooks, complex state management) before the app's needs are clear. Start simple, then refactor as patterns emerge.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[App] --> B[Feature Modules] B --> C[Components] B --> D[Hooks] B --> E[Services] C --> F[UI Rendering] D --> G[Business Logic] E --> H[API Calls]"}]

[{"question":"How would you test the data flow in a layered SPA architecture to ensure each layer is independently verifiable?","expectedPoints":["Identify layers: Network, Store, View Model, View.","Use mocks/stubs for dependencies between layers.","Test data flow from server to view and event flow from view to server.","Verify each layer's responsibility in isolation.","Ensure integration tests cover the boundaries between layers."],"followUps":["How would you test the View Model layer without rendering UI components?","What strategies do you use to mock the Network layer in unit tests?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine a restaurant kitchen where different chefs handle different tasks: one gets ingredients (Network layer), one prepares dishes (Store), one plates them (View Model), and one serves (View). To test each chef's work, you'd check the ingredients separately, the recipe steps, the plating, and the serving. Similarly, in a web app, we test each layer independently by pretending (mocking) the other layers are working correctly.","detailedExplanation":"

In a layered SPA architecture, testing each layer independently is crucial for maintainability and reliability. The typical layers are Network (data fetching), Store (state management), View Model (business logic), and View (UI components).

How to Test Each Layer

  • Network Layer: Test API client functions with mocked HTTP responses. Verify caching, retry logic, and token handling. Use tools like nock or msw to simulate server responses.
  • Store Layer: Test state updates and selectors in isolation. For example, with Redux, test reducers and selectors without connecting to the UI. Ensure derived data is computed correctly.
  • View Model Layer: Test hooks or container components by mocking the store and network. For instance, test a custom hook that fetches data and transforms it. Verify that it returns the correct state and handles loading/error states.
  • View Layer: Test presentational components by passing props directly. Use snapshot tests or behavior tests (e.g., with React Testing Library) to ensure correct rendering and event handling.

Why It Matters in Production

Independent testing allows teams to refactor or replace layers without breaking other parts. For example, migrating from Redux to Zustand only requires updating the Store layer tests, while View Model and View tests remain unchanged. This reduces regression risk and speeds up development.

Common Pitfall

Testing too many layers together (e.g., rendering a full component that triggers API calls) leads to flaky tests and slow feedback. Instead, isolate each layer and use mocks for dependencies. However, also include a few integration tests that verify the boundaries between layers (e.g., Network → Store) to catch real-world issues.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR Server-->|REST/GraphQL|Network Network-->|normalized data|Store Store-->|derived state|ViewModel ViewModel-->|props|View View-->|events|ViewModel ViewModel-->|actions|Store Store-->|queries|Network subgraph Test Boundaries Network Store ViewModel View end"}]
qgen_v3:92d742f7567812f5:
[{"question":"Explain the Container vs Presentational pattern in React and describe a scenario where you would choose to use it.","expectedPoints":["Defines Container as responsible for state, data fetching, and side effects.","Defines Presentational as purely UI, receiving props, no business logic.","Mentions benefits: separation of concerns, reusability, easier testing.","Provides a concrete example (e.g., UserProfile).","Explains when to use: when UI needs to be reused with different data sources."],"followUps":["How would you test a Presentational component in isolation?","What are the downsides of this pattern in modern React with hooks?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy car that can drive itself (smart) and a toy car that you push with your hand (dumb). The smart car knows how to move and where to go, while the dumb car just looks nice and moves only when you push it. In React, a Container component is like the smart car—it handles data and logic. A Presentational component is like the dumb car—it just displays what it's given. This makes it easy to reuse the display part in different places.","detailedExplanation":"

The Container vs Presentational pattern is a design approach in React that separates components into two categories based on their responsibilities.

What it is

A Container (or smart) component manages state, data fetching, and side effects. It contains the business logic and passes data down to presentational components via props. A Presentational (or dumb) component is only concerned with rendering the UI. It receives props and displays them, with no internal state or side effects related to data.

How it works

In practice, a Container component uses hooks like useState and useEffect to fetch data and manage state. It then renders a Presentational component, passing the data as props. The Presentational component simply renders the UI based on those props. For example, a UserProfileContainer fetches user data and passes it to UserProfile, which displays the name and email.

Why it matters in production

  • Separation of concerns: Logic and UI are decoupled, making code easier to understand and maintain.
  • Reusability: Presentational components can be reused across different parts of the app or even in different projects (e.g., in a design system or Storybook).
  • Testability: Presentational components are pure functions of props, making them trivial to unit test. Container components can be tested by mocking data sources.

Common pitfall

Overusing this pattern can lead to prop drilling and unnecessary complexity, especially with the advent of React Hooks. In modern React, it's often acceptable to have components that manage both state and rendering, especially for smaller components. The pattern is most beneficial when you have complex data logic or need to reuse UI across multiple data sources.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Container] -->|props| B[Presentational] A -->|state & logic| C[Data Source] B -->|renders UI| D[DOM] subgraph Smart A C end subgraph Dumb B end"}]

[{"question":"What are the main benefits and trade-offs of using micro-frontends in a large-scale application?","expectedPoints":["Benefits: independent deployment, scalability, performance optimization, security isolation","Trade-offs: duplicate bundles, integration complexity, UX inconsistency","Suitable for large projects with multiple independent teams","Each team can own a separate frontend module, possibly using different frameworks"],"followUps":["How would you handle shared dependencies across micro-frontends to avoid bundle duplication?","What strategies can ensure UX consistency across independently deployed micro-frontends?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a giant Lego castle. Instead of one person building it, you split it into smaller parts like the tower, the wall, and the gate. Each part is built by a different team. This makes it easier to work on, but sometimes parts might use the same Lego pieces (duplicate bundles) and the castle might look a bit mismatched (UX inconsistency).","detailedExplanation":"

Micro-frontends are an architectural pattern where a large frontend application is decomposed into smaller, independent applications, each owned by a separate team. This approach is particularly beneficial for large-scale projects with multiple teams working concurrently.

Benefits

  • Independent deployment: Each micro-frontend can be developed, tested, and deployed independently, reducing coordination overhead.
  • Scalability: Teams can scale their own parts without affecting others, and new features can be integrated more easily.
  • Performance optimization: By splitting the app, initial load time can be reduced as only necessary micro-frontends are loaded.
  • Security isolation: Each micro-frontend can have its own security boundaries, limiting the blast radius of vulnerabilities.

Trade-offs

  • Duplicate bundles: Shared libraries (e.g., React) may be loaded multiple times, increasing overall bundle size.
  • Integration complexity: Communication between micro-frontends, routing, and state management become more complex.
  • UX inconsistency: Different teams may produce inconsistent UI/UX unless a shared design system is enforced.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is underestimating the overhead of integration. Teams often assume micro-frontends are a silver bullet, but they require careful planning for shared dependencies, cross-team communication, and consistent user experience. Without a strong shared design system and tooling, the application can become fragmented and harder to maintain.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Monolith App] --> B[Micro-frontend 1] A --> C[Micro-frontend 2] A --> D[Micro-frontend 3] subgraph Benefits E[Independent Deploy] F[Scalability] G[Performance] end subgraph Trade-offs H[Duplicate Bundles] I[Integration Complexity] J[UX Inconsistency] end B --> E C --> F D --> G B --> H C --> I D --> J"}]
qgen_v3:96aea7debaff89d4:
[{"question":"Compare CSR, SSR, SSG, and ISR in terms of when and where HTML is generated, and how each affects SEO, time-to-interactive, and server cost.","expectedPoints":["CSR: HTML generated on client via JS; poor SEO, slow TTI, low server cost.","SSR: HTML generated on server per request; good SEO, faster FCP but slower TTI, higher server cost.","SSG: HTML generated at build time; excellent SEO, fast TTI, minimal server cost.","ISR: HTML generated at build time but revalidated on demand; good SEO, fast TTI, moderate server cost.","Trade-offs: CSR for dynamic apps, SSR for SEO-critical dynamic content, SSG for static content, ISR for large sites with frequent updates."],"followUps":["How would you decide between SSR and SSG for a blog site?","What are the caching implications of ISR compared to pure SSG?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy factory. CSR builds the toy at your home using a kit (slow to start, but you can change it easily). SSR builds the toy at the factory and ships it ready (fast to see, but the factory works hard). SSG builds all toys before you order (super fast, but can't change easily). ISR builds toys ahead but can update them later without rebuilding everything.","detailedExplanation":"

Client-Side Rendering (CSR) generates HTML entirely in the browser using JavaScript. The server sends a minimal HTML shell and the client fetches data and renders the page. This leads to poor SEO because crawlers may not execute JS, and slow time-to-interactive (TTI) because the user sees a blank page until JS loads. Server cost is low because the server only serves static files.

Server-Side Rendering (SSR) generates HTML on the server for each request. The server fetches data, renders the page, and sends fully populated HTML. This improves SEO and First Contentful Paint (FCP), but TTI may still be delayed because the client must hydrate the page. Server cost is higher due to per-request rendering.

Static Site Generation (SSG) generates HTML at build time. The result is static files served via CDN. This yields excellent SEO and fast TTI because no server processing is needed. Server cost is minimal. However, content updates require a full rebuild.

Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR) combines SSG with on-demand revalidation. Pages are generated at build time but can be updated after a configurable time or via webhook. This provides good SEO and fast TTI while allowing content updates without full rebuilds. Server cost is moderate due to revalidation requests.

Common Pitfall

Choosing SSR for a mostly static site increases server load unnecessarily. Conversely, using SSG for highly dynamic content (e.g., user dashboards) leads to stale data. Always match the rendering pattern to the content's update frequency and SEO requirements.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Request] --> B{Pattern?} B -->|CSR| C[Client renders HTML] B -->|SSR| D[Server renders HTML] B -->|SSG| E[Pre-built static HTML] B -->|ISR| F[Static + revalidate] C --> G[Poor SEO, slow TTI] D --> H[Good SEO, faster FCP] E --> I[Excellent SEO, fast TTI] F --> J[Good SEO, fast TTI]"}]
qgen_v3:168ca853df5465e5:
[{"question":"Compare SSR, SSG, and ISR in terms of hydration cost and use cases. When would you choose one over the others?","expectedPoints":["SSR hydrates on each request, higher server load but fresh content.","SSG pre-renders at build time, zero hydration cost at request, best for static content.","ISR combines SSG with revalidation, reducing rebuild cost while keeping content fresh.","Choose SSR for dynamic user-specific content, SSG for marketing pages, ISR for blogs with periodic updates."],"followUps":["How does streaming SSR affect Time to Interactive (TTI)?","What are the tradeoffs of using Islands Architecture vs. SSR?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy house. SSR builds the whole house fresh each time someone visits. SSG builds it once and shows the same house to everyone. ISR builds it once but updates parts when needed. You choose based on how often the house needs to change.","detailedExplanation":"

Hydration is the process of attaching JavaScript event handlers to server-rendered HTML, making a static page interactive. Different rendering strategies affect when and how hydration happens, impacting performance.

SSR (Server-Side Rendering)

In SSR, the server generates HTML per request, including data fetching. The client receives fully rendered HTML but must still hydrate it. This means hydration happens on every page load, which can delay interactivity (TTI). SSR is ideal for pages with dynamic, user-specific content (e.g., dashboards) where SEO and fresh data are critical.

SSG (Static Site Generation)

SSG pre-renders pages at build time. The HTML is static, and hydration occurs only once when the page loads. There is no server-side work per request, leading to fast load times. Best for content that doesn't change often, like marketing pages or documentation.

ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration)

ISR combines SSG with on-demand revalidation. Pages are pre-rendered statically but can be updated after a set interval or via a webhook. This reduces the need for full rebuilds while keeping content fresh. Suitable for blogs or e-commerce product pages that update periodically.

Common Pitfall: Overusing SSR for static content increases server costs and slows TTI. Always evaluate content freshness needs and user interaction patterns.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Request] --> B{Strategy?} B -->|SSR| C[Server renders HTML] C --> D[Client hydrates] B -->|SSG| E[Pre-built HTML] E --> F[Client hydrates] B -->|ISR| G[Stale HTML] G --> H[Revalidate?] H -->|Yes| I[Server re-renders] I --> J[Update cache] H -->|No| K[Serve stale]"}]
qgen_v3:4d639b04d1fc158e:
[{"question":"Explain the concept of hydration in the context of server-side rendered React applications. How does it differ from client-side rendering?","expectedPoints":["Hydration attaches JS event handlers to static server-rendered HTML.","It makes the page interactive without re-rendering the entire DOM.","Client-side rendering builds DOM from scratch in the browser.","Hydration improves perceived performance and SEO.","Mismatches between server and client HTML can cause errors."],"followUps":["What happens if the server-rendered HTML doesn't match the client's virtual DOM?","How does hydration relate to the concept of 'time to interactive'?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you get a coloring book page that's already colored in (the server sends finished HTML). Hydration is like adding stickers and moving parts to that page so you can play with it. Without hydration, the page is just a picture you can't touch.","detailedExplanation":"

Hydration is the process in server-side rendered (SSR) React applications where the static HTML sent from the server is 'activated' by attaching JavaScript event handlers and state to the corresponding DOM nodes on the client. This allows the page to become interactive without re-rendering the entire DOM tree.

How It Works

When a user requests a page, the server generates the complete HTML markup for the initial view and sends it to the browser. The browser displays this HTML immediately, giving the user a fast first paint. Then, React's JavaScript bundle loads and hydrates the existing DOM by attaching event listeners and initializing component state. React reconciles the virtual DOM with the real DOM, but instead of creating new DOM nodes, it reuses the existing ones.

Why It Matters in Production

Hydration is crucial for SEO and perceived performance. Search engines can crawl the fully rendered HTML, and users see content quickly. However, hydration can be expensive if the JavaScript bundle is large, delaying Time to Interactive (TTI). Techniques like partial hydration (e.g., Islands Architecture) hydrate only interactive components, reducing JS payload.

Common Pitfall

A frequent issue is hydration mismatch — when the server-rendered HTML differs from what React expects on the client (e.g., due to dynamic data like timestamps or user-specific content). This can cause React to discard the server HTML and re-render from scratch, negating SSR benefits. Always ensure server and client render identical output for the initial render.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Request] --> B[Server renders HTML] B --> C[Browser displays static HTML] C --> D[JS loads & hydrates] D --> E[Interactive page] subgraph SSR B end subgraph Hydration D end"}]
