[{"question":"Explain how you would improve a website's Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) score and why it matters for user experience.","expectedPoints":["LCP measures loading performance of the largest visible element.","Optimize images (compress, use modern formats, lazy load).","Minimize render-blocking resources (CSS/JS).","Use CDN and server-side optimizations to reduce TTFB.","LCP directly impacts perceived load speed and SEO ranking."],"followUps":["How would you debug a slow LCP caused by a hero image?","What trade-offs exist between image quality and LCP?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're waiting for a big picture to appear on a website. LCP is like how long it takes for that picture to show up. To make it faster, you can make the picture smaller, use a faster delivery truck (CDN), or make sure other stuff doesn't block it. Google cares because users get impatient if the main thing doesn't load quickly.","detailedExplanation":"

Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) is a Core Web Vital that measures the time it takes for the largest visible content element (e.g., an image, video, or text block) to become fully rendered within the viewport. It is a key indicator of perceived loading speed.

How to Improve LCP

  • Optimize images: Compress images, use next-gen formats like WebP, and implement lazy loading for below-the-fold images.
  • Minimize render-blocking resources: Inline critical CSS, defer non-critical JavaScript, and use async or defer attributes.
  • Reduce server response time (TTFB): Use a CDN, optimize backend logic, and leverage caching.
  • Preload key resources: Use <link rel=preload> for hero images or fonts.

Why It Matters

Google uses LCP as a ranking factor. A poor LCP (over 2.5 seconds) leads to higher bounce rates and lower user satisfaction. In production, monitoring LCP with tools like Lighthouse or Web Vitals library helps catch regressions.

Common Pitfall

Over-optimizing images by excessive compression can degrade visual quality. Always balance file size with acceptable quality, especially for hero images.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Request] --> B[Server Response TTFB] B --> C[HTML Parsing] C --> D[Render-Blocking Resources] D --> E[Largest Element Load] E --> F[LCP Measured] subgraph Optimizations G[CDN] --> B H[Image Optim] --> E I[Minify CSS/JS] --> D end"}]
qgen_v3:b2af9b0484730053:
[{"question":"Explain how you would diagnose and fix a poor Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) score in a production React application.","expectedPoints":["Identify LCP element (e.g., hero image, heading) using browser DevTools or Lighthouse.","Optimize image delivery: use modern formats (WebP/AVIF), lazy loading, preload LCP image.","Reduce server response time (TTFB) via CDN, caching, or server-side rendering.","Eliminate render-blocking resources (CSS/JS) by deferring non-critical scripts.","Measure improvement with performance budgets and continuous monitoring."],"followUps":["What if the LCP element is a text block? How would you optimize that?","How would you handle LCP in a single-page application with client-side routing?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy car. The LCP is like the time it takes for the car's biggest part (like the body) to appear. If it's slow, you can make the part smaller or start building it earlier. For a website, we make big images load faster or show text sooner so the page feels quick.","detailedExplanation":"

Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures the time it takes for the largest visible content element (image, video, or text block) to render within the viewport. A poor LCP (>2.5s) frustrates users and hurts SEO. In a React app, common culprits include large hero images, slow API responses, and render-blocking scripts.

Diagnosis

Use Chrome DevTools Performance panel or Lighthouse to identify the LCP element. Check the Network tab for its load time and the Timings tab for TTFB (Time to First Byte).

Fixes

  • Optimize images: Compress, use WebP/AVIF, set explicit width/height to prevent layout shifts, and preload the LCP image via <link rel="preload">.
  • Reduce TTFB: Use a CDN, enable server-side caching, or implement server-side rendering (SSR) for initial content.
  • Eliminate render-blocking resources: Defer non-critical CSS/JS, inline critical CSS, and use async or defer for scripts.
  • Code splitting: Lazy-load non-critical React components to reduce bundle size.

Common Pitfall

Over-optimizing images by removing all quality can degrade user experience. Always balance compression with visual fidelity.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Request] --> B[Server Response TTFB] B --> C[Load HTML] C --> D[Parse & Render] D --> E[Identify LCP Element] E --> F[Optimize Image/Text] F --> G[Measure LCP < 2.5s]"}]
qgen_v3:745dd050aa918c5d:
[{"question":"How would you optimize a React component that re-renders too often due to a parent component's state changes, specifically when the child component receives an expensive computation result as a prop?","expectedPoints":["Use React.memo to prevent unnecessary re-renders if props haven't changed.","Use useMemo to memoize the expensive computation result.","Use useCallback to memoize callback functions passed as props.","Explain that memoization has a cost and should only be applied when profiling shows a performance issue."],"followUps":["What could go wrong if you overuse useMemo or useCallback?","How would you decide whether to use React.memo or useMemo?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that does a slow calculation every time you touch it. If your parent shakes the table (re-renders), the toy recalculates even if nothing changed. We can put a sticker on the toy that says 'only recalculate if the numbers change' – that's like useMemo. We can also wrap the toy in a magic box that stops it from shaking unless its own parts change – that's React.memo.","detailedExplanation":"

The question addresses a common performance bottleneck in React: unnecessary re-renders of child components caused by parent state updates. The goal is to prevent the child from re-rendering when its props haven't changed, especially when it performs an expensive computation.

Key Optimization Techniques

  • React.memo: A higher-order component that memoizes the rendered output. It performs a shallow comparison of props and skips re-rendering if they are the same. This is ideal for leaf components that receive stable props.
  • useMemo: Memoizes the result of an expensive computation. It recalculates only when its dependencies change. This prevents re-running the computation on every render.
  • useCallback: Memoizes a function reference. Useful when passing callbacks to child components to avoid breaking the memoization of React.memo.

Why It Matters in Production

In large applications, unnecessary re-renders can cause jank, high CPU usage, and poor user experience. Optimizing with these hooks reduces the rendering workload, especially in components with heavy computations or large lists. However, premature optimization can add complexity and overhead, so profiling with React DevTools is essential.

Common Pitfall

Overusing useMemo and useCallback without profiling can actually hurt performance because the memoization itself has a cost (memory and comparison). Only apply them when you have identified a performance bottleneck via profiling.

[{"question":"How does React.lazy work with Suspense to implement code splitting, and what are the trade-offs compared to using a library like loadable-components?","expectedPoints":["React.lazy uses dynamic import() to split code at the component level.","Suspense shows a fallback UI while the lazy component loads.","Trade-offs: React.lazy is built-in but doesn't support SSR; loadable-components supports SSR and more features.","Consider bundle size impact, network latency, and user experience during loading."],"followUps":["How would you handle error boundaries with lazy-loaded components?","What strategies can you use to preload lazy components before they are needed?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big toy box, but you only bring out the toys you need one at a time. This makes starting to play much faster because you don't have to unpack the whole box at once.","detailedExplanation":"

React.lazy is a built-in function that enables code splitting at the component level. It works by accepting a function that calls a dynamic import(), which returns a Promise resolving to a module with a default export containing a React component. The lazy component is then rendered inside a Suspense component, which shows a fallback (e.g., a spinner) while the chunk is being loaded.

How It Works

When the app renders, React.lazy triggers the dynamic import only when the component is first needed. The imported chunk is cached after loading, so subsequent renders are instant. Suspense catches the pending state and displays the fallback until the chunk is ready.

Why It Matters in Production

Code splitting reduces the initial bundle size, improving First Contentful Paint (FCP) and Largest Contentful Paint (LCP). It is especially useful for large applications with many routes or heavy components (e.g., charts, editors). However, it introduces a loading delay for the first visit to a lazy-loaded route, which must be handled gracefully with a meaningful fallback.

Trade-offs vs. loadable-components

  • React.lazy is simpler and built-in, but does not support Server-Side Rendering (SSR) out of the box. It also lacks advanced features like preloading or timeout handling.
  • loadable-components is a third-party library that supports SSR, provides more control over loading states, and offers utilities like loadable.lib for splitting non-component code.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to wrap lazy components in Suspense will cause a runtime error. Also, using React.lazy with named exports requires a re-export as default, which can be confusing.

[{"question":"How does list virtualization improve performance when rendering a large list of 10,000 items?","expectedPoints":["Only renders items within the viewport plus a small buffer","Reuses DOM nodes as the user scrolls","Reduces memory usage and improves scroll smoothness","Common libraries: react-window, react-virtuoso, @tanstack/virtual"],"followUps":["What happens when the user scrolls quickly? How does virtualization handle that?","How would you decide whether to use virtualization or pagination?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge list of 10,000 toys, but you can only see 10 at a time through a small window. Instead of building all 10,000 toys, you only build the ones you can see right now. As you move the window, you build new toys and throw away the ones you can't see anymore. This makes the list fast and doesn't use too much memory.","detailedExplanation":"

List virtualization (also called windowing) is a technique to render only the items that are currently visible in the viewport, plus a small overscan buffer. This drastically reduces the number of DOM nodes and improves performance for large lists.

How it works

Instead of rendering all 10,000 items, virtualization calculates which items are visible based on scroll position and renders only those. As the user scrolls, old items are removed from the DOM and new ones are added. The total height of the list is maintained via a placeholder element to keep the scrollbar correct.

Why it matters in production

Rendering thousands of DOM nodes simultaneously causes high memory usage, slow initial load, and janky scrolling. Virtualization keeps the DOM small (e.g., 10–15 nodes), leading to smooth scrolling and lower memory footprint. It's essential for infinite scroll, chat logs, and large data tables.

Common pitfall

Forgetting to set a fixed or estimated item height. Most virtualization libraries require a known or estimated item size to calculate positions. If items have dynamic heights, you need to measure them or use a library that supports variable sizes, otherwise scrolling will be jumpy.

[{"question":"How would you optimize a React component that re-renders too often due to a closure capturing a stale value?","expectedPoints":["Identifies stale closure as the cause","Suggests useCallback with correct dependencies","Mentions useRef for mutable values","Explains how memoization prevents unnecessary re-renders","Notes that stale closures can lead to bugs and performance issues"],"followUps":["What if the closure depends on a value that changes frequently?","How does useCallback differ from useMemo in this context?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that remembers a number. If you change the number but the toy still uses the old one, it's like a stale closure. In React, we use special tools like useCallback to make sure the toy always uses the latest number, so the app runs smoothly.","detailedExplanation":"

A stale closure occurs when a function captures a variable that later changes, but the function still references the old value. In React, this often happens with event handlers or effects that depend on props or state. The function is created once and doesn't update when dependencies change, leading to incorrect behavior and unnecessary re-renders.

How it works

When a component re-renders, new function instances are created. If you pass a function as a prop without memoization, child components may re-render even if the function's logic hasn't changed. Using useCallback with the correct dependency array ensures the function reference stays stable unless dependencies change. For values that shouldn't trigger re-renders, useRef provides a mutable container that persists across renders.

Why it matters in production

Stale closures can cause subtle bugs (e.g., outdated state in callbacks) and degrade performance by causing unnecessary re-renders of large component trees. In high-frequency interactions like scrolling or typing, this can lead to jank and poor user experience.

Common pitfall

Omitting dependencies from the array (e.g., []) when the closure uses a variable that changes. This creates a stale closure that never updates. Always include all variables the closure depends on, or use useRef for values that should not trigger re-creation.

[{"question":"How would you handle race conditions when making multiple API requests in a React application?","expectedPoints":["Use AbortController to cancel stale requests","Use unique request identifiers or timestamps to ignore outdated responses","Leverage libraries like React Query that handle deduplication and stale data","Consider optimistic updates with rollback on failure"],"followUps":["What if the user rapidly types in a search box?","How would you test for race conditions?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're ordering pizza online and you click 'order' twice by accident. The restaurant might get two orders and charge you twice. In web apps, when you send multiple requests quickly, the responses can arrive in the wrong order. We use tricks like canceling old requests or ignoring late responses to make sure only the latest one counts.","detailedExplanation":"

Race conditions in API requests occur when multiple asynchronous operations compete, and the order of responses doesn't match the order of requests. This is common in search autocomplete, pagination, or any scenario where user actions trigger rapid successive API calls.

How to handle race conditions

  • AbortController: Use the browser's AbortController to cancel previous in-flight requests when a new request is made. For example, in a search input, abort the previous request on each keystroke.
  • Request identifiers: Assign a unique ID (e.g., timestamp) to each request and ignore responses whose ID doesn't match the latest request ID.
  • Library support: Use libraries like React Query or SWR that automatically deduplicate requests and handle stale data. They provide built-in mechanisms to cancel or ignore outdated responses.
  • Optimistic updates: Update the UI immediately and revert if the response indicates failure. This requires careful state management to avoid inconsistent UI.

Why it matters in production

Race conditions can lead to displaying incorrect data, double submissions, or inconsistent UI state. For example, in a paginated list, if the user clicks page 2 then quickly page 3, the response for page 2 might arrive after page 3, causing the list to show page 2's data. This degrades user experience and can cause data integrity issues.

Common pitfall

Relying solely on setTimeout or debouncing without canceling requests. Debouncing reduces the number of requests but doesn't prevent race conditions if the server responds out of order. Always combine debouncing with request cancellation.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Action] --> B[Request 1] A --> C[Request 2] B --> D[Server] C --> D D --> E[Response 1] D --> F[Response 2] E --> G[Process Response 1] F --> H[Process Response 2] G --> I[Update UI] H --> I subgraph Race Condition B C E F end"}]

[{"question":"In a frontend-heavy application with complex data relationships, how would you decide between REST and GraphQL? Walk through the trade-offs.","expectedPoints":["Recognizes REST's simplicity and HTTP caching benefits","Identifies GraphQL's flexibility in avoiding over/under-fetching","Discusses caching complexity and versioning differences","Considers team expertise and application complexity","Mentions real-world scenarios like mobile vs web"],"followUps":["How would you handle caching in a GraphQL client?","What if your backend team is more familiar with REST?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you're ordering a pizza. REST is like a menu with fixed combos—you might get extra toppings you don't want or miss some you need. GraphQL is like building your own pizza: you pick exactly what goes on it, so you get only what you want. But building your own pizza takes more effort to order and might slow down the kitchen if too many custom orders come in.","detailedExplanation":"

When deciding between REST and GraphQL for a frontend-heavy application, you must evaluate several trade-offs that impact development speed, performance, and maintainability.

Data Fetching Flexibility

REST exposes multiple fixed endpoints, each returning a predefined set of fields. This often leads to over-fetching (getting unnecessary data) or under-fetching (needing multiple requests to gather all required data). GraphQL, on the other hand, allows the client to specify exactly which fields it needs in a single request, minimizing payload size and reducing network round trips.

Caching

REST benefits from HTTP caching at the network level (e.g., CDN, browser cache) because each URL uniquely identifies a resource. GraphQL typically uses a single endpoint, making HTTP caching ineffective. Instead, you need client-side caching solutions like Apollo Client or Relay that normalize and cache data by type and ID, which adds complexity.

Versioning

REST often requires versioning via URL paths (e.g., /v1/users) or headers, leading to multiple API versions. GraphQL handles evolution by deprecating fields in the schema while keeping backward compatibility, allowing smoother transitions without breaking clients.

Production Considerations

For simple APIs with stable data requirements, REST is easier to implement and cache. For complex UIs with deeply nested data (e.g., a dashboard showing user profiles, posts, and comments), GraphQL reduces the number of requests and payload size. However, GraphQL requires more upfront schema design and can shift complexity to the server, potentially causing performance issues like N+1 queries if not optimized with batching (e.g., DataLoader).

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is adopting GraphQL for every project without considering team expertise. If the team is unfamiliar with GraphQL's caching and query optimization, it can lead to slower development and poor performance. Start with REST for simple CRUD apps and consider GraphQL when you see clear over-fetching or multiple round trips in your UI.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client] -->|REST| B[GET /users] A -->|REST| C[GET /users/1/posts] A -->|GraphQL| D[POST /graphql] D --> E["{users {name posts {title}}}"] B --> F[Over-fetch: all fields] C --> G[Under-fetch: multiple calls] E --> H[Exact data: name & title]"}]

[{"question":"You are building a social media feed that updates frequently. Which pagination strategy would you choose and why?","expectedPoints":["Recognizes that offset-based pagination can cause duplicate or missed items when new data is inserted.","Proposes cursor-based pagination as the solution for dynamic datasets.","Explains that cursor-based pagination uses a unique identifier to maintain a stable position.","Mentions that infinite scroll is a common UI pattern for feeds, often paired with cursor-based pagination.","Acknowledges tradeoff: cursor-based pagination does not support arbitrary page jumps."],"followUps":["How would you implement cursor-based pagination in a React component with infinite scroll?","What are the downsides of using offset-based pagination for a real-time feed?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you are reading a story that keeps getting new pages added at the beginning. If you use page numbers (like page 2), you might miss some pages or read the same page twice. Instead, you can use a bookmark that marks exactly where you left off, so even if new pages are added, you always continue from the right spot. That's what cursor-based pagination does.","detailedExplanation":"

Pagination is a technique to split a large dataset into smaller chunks (pages) to improve performance and user experience. There are three common strategies: offset-based, cursor-based, and infinite scroll (which often uses cursor-based underneath).

Offset-Based Pagination

Uses ?page=2&limit=20. It is simple and allows jumping to any page. However, when new items are inserted (e.g., new posts in a feed), the offset becomes unstable: items shift, causing duplicates or missed items. This is a major drawback for dynamic data.

Cursor-Based Pagination

Uses a unique cursor (e.g., ?after=cursor123) to mark the position of the last item. It is stable even when new items are added, because the cursor points to a specific record. This makes it ideal for real-time feeds and infinite scroll. The tradeoff is that you cannot jump to an arbitrary page; you can only fetch the next or previous set.

Infinite Scroll

A UI pattern where more data is loaded automatically when the user scrolls near the bottom. It is often implemented with IntersectionObserver and paired with cursor-based pagination for stability.

Production Considerations

For a social media feed, cursor-based pagination is the best choice because it avoids data inconsistency. Offset-based pagination can cause a poor user experience when new posts appear. A common pitfall is forgetting to handle loading states and race conditions when fetching pages asynchronously.

[{"question":"How would you handle race conditions in an autocomplete search component when the user types quickly?","expectedPoints":["Identifies race condition: later response overwrites earlier correct result","Proposes AbortController to cancel in-flight requests","Mentions debounce to reduce request frequency","Suggests checking request freshness before updating state"],"followUps":["How would you implement a check to ensure only the latest response is used?","What are the trade-offs of using debounce vs. AbortController alone?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're asking a friend for directions, but you keep changing your mind. If you ask for 'rea' then 'react', your friend might answer the first question after you've already moved on. To fix this, you can interrupt them when you change your mind (like AbortController) and wait a tiny bit before asking to make sure you're done thinking (like debounce).","detailedExplanation":"

Race conditions in autocomplete occur when the user types quickly and multiple network requests are sent. A later response may arrive before an earlier one, causing the UI to display stale or incorrect results. This is a classic stale closure problem.

How it works

The solution involves three techniques:

  • AbortController: Before sending a new request, abort any pending request using controller.abort(). This ensures only the latest request completes.
  • Debounce: Delay the request by ~300ms after the user stops typing. This reduces the number of requests and minimizes race conditions.
  • Freshness check: After the response arrives, verify that the query that triggered it is still the current input value. If not, discard the response.

Why it matters in production

Without these measures, users see flickering or incorrect suggestions, leading to poor UX. In high-traffic apps, unnecessary requests also waste bandwidth and server resources.

Common pitfall

Relying solely on debounce without cancellation can still cause race conditions if the debounce time is too short. Conversely, using only AbortController without debounce may still fire many requests. Combining both is best practice.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User types 'rea'] --> B[Send request A] A --> C[User types 'react'] C --> D[Abort request A] D --> E[Send request B] E --> F[Response B arrives] F --> G[Check freshness: query is 'react'] G --> H[Update UI with B] B -.-> I[Response A arrives late] I --> J[Discard A]"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a resilient autocomplete component that handles network failures and race conditions?","expectedPoints":["Uses debounce to limit request frequency","Cancels stale requests with AbortController","Implements retry with exponential backoff for failures","Handles timeout to prevent hanging requests","Provides graceful degradation (e.g., cached results or error message)"],"followUps":["How would you ensure idempotency if the autocomplete triggers a POST request?","What strategy would you use if the user types faster than the debounce delay?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're typing a search term and the app suggests words. If the internet is slow, you don't want old suggestions to pop up after you've typed more. So the app waits a tiny bit after you stop typing (debounce), cancels old requests (like hanging up a phone call), and if a request fails, it tries again but waits longer each time (like waiting longer before knocking again).","detailedExplanation":"

Resilience in frontend applications ensures the UI remains responsive and correct despite network failures or race conditions. For an autocomplete component, the key challenges are race conditions (out-of-order responses) and transient failures (timeouts, server errors).

Core Strategies

  • Debounce: Use a debounce delay (e.g., 300ms) to reduce the number of requests. Only send a request after the user stops typing for that duration.
  • AbortController: Cancel the previous in-flight request when a new keystroke triggers a new request. This prevents stale responses from overwriting newer results.
  • Timeout: Set a timeout (e.g., 5 seconds) on each request using AbortSignal.timeout() or a custom wrapper. If the request takes too long, abort it and show a fallback.
  • Retry with Exponential Backoff: On failure (network error, timeout), retry the request with increasing delays (1s, 2s, 4s) up to a maximum number of retries. Use jitter to avoid thundering herd.
  • Idempotency: For POST-based autocomplete, include an idempotency key (e.g., a hash of the query) so that retries do not create duplicate entries on the server.
  • Graceful Degradation: If all retries fail, show cached suggestions (if available) or a user-friendly message like “No suggestions available” instead of a blank screen or spinner.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring race conditions: Without canceling stale requests, a slow response for an earlier query can overwrite the results of a later query, causing confusion. Always use AbortController to cancel outdated requests.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User types] --> B[Debounce 300ms] B --> C[Abort previous request] C --> D[Send new request with timeout] D --> E{Success?} E -- Yes --> F[Display suggestions] E -- No --> G[Retry with exponential backoff] G --> H{Max retries?} H -- No --> D H -- Yes --> I[Show cached/fallback]"}]

[{"question":"How would you decide between using Redux, Context API, or local component state for managing state in a large React application?","expectedPoints":["Categorizes state types (server, UI, shared, etc.)","Explains trade-offs: Context re-renders vs Redux selectors","Recommends local state for isolated UI state","Advises against over-engineering with global store","Mentions performance considerations"],"followUps":["How would you handle frequent state updates that affect many components?","What are the signs that your current state management approach is failing?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box. Some toys you play with alone (local state), some you share with a friend (Context), and some you need to keep track of for the whole playground (Redux). You wouldn't put every single toy in the big shared box because it would be messy and slow. So you decide based on who needs the toy.","detailedExplanation":"

State management is a critical architectural decision in frontend applications. The key is to classify state and choose the right tool for each category, rather than forcing everything into a single global store.

Types of State

  • Local UI state: form inputs, toggle visibility, dropdown open/close. Best kept in useState or useReducer within the component.
  • Shared state: user preferences, theme, language. Context API is suitable, but beware of unnecessary re-renders. Use memoization or split contexts.
  • Server state: data fetched from APIs. Tools like React Query or Redux Toolkit Query handle caching, loading, and synchronization better than raw Redux.
  • Complex global state: multi-step wizards, real-time collaboration. Redux with middleware (thunks, sagas) provides predictable updates and devtools.

Trade-offs

Context API is simple but causes re-renders of all consumers when any value changes. Redux offers fine-grained subscriptions via useSelector, but adds boilerplate. Local state is the most performant but cannot be shared.

Common Pitfall

Putting all state into a single Redux store leads to unnecessary complexity and performance issues. Instead, co-locate state as close as possible to where it's used, and lift it only when truly needed by multiple components.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[State Type] --> B[Local UI] A --> C[Shared] A --> D[Server] A --> E[Complex Global] B --> F[useState/useReducer] C --> G[Context API] D --> H[React Query / RTK Query] E --> I[Redux + Middleware]"}]

[{"question":"How would you decide where to place state in a React application? Walk through your decision process using a decision tree.","expectedPoints":["Classifies state as server vs client origin","For client state, checks if it's used in multiple places","Recommends appropriate tools: useState for local, Context/Zustand for global, React Query for server","Considers URL state for shareable/filter state","Mentions trade-offs like performance, complexity, and reusability"],"followUps":["When would you choose Context over Zustand or Redux?","How do you handle loading and error states for server state?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box. This helps you not lose toys and keeps the room tidy.","detailedExplanation":"

When designing a React application, deciding where to place state is crucial for maintainability, performance, and scalability. A common approach is to use a decision tree that starts by identifying the origin of the state: server or client.

Server State

If the state comes from a server (e.g., list of posts, user profile, products), it is considered server state. This type of state is often asynchronous and needs caching, synchronization, and error handling. Recommended tools include React Query, SWR, or RTK Query. These libraries handle fetching, caching, and updating server data, reducing boilerplate and improving user experience.

Client State

If the state originates from the client or UI interactions, further classify it:

  • Local State: Used only in one component or a small subtree. Examples: form input values, toggle states, hover effects. Use useState or useReducer.
  • Global UI State: Shared across many components. Examples: theme, authentication status, modal visibility, shopping cart. Use Context API, Zustand, or Redux.
  • URL State: State that should be persisted in the URL for sharing or browser navigation. Examples: filter selections, active tab, search query. Store in URL parameters using react-router or Next.js navigation.

Why It Matters in Production

Proper state placement prevents prop drilling, reduces unnecessary re-renders, and makes the codebase easier to reason about. For instance, putting server state in a global store like Redux often leads to stale data and extra complexity; instead, dedicated server-state libraries handle caching and background updates efficiently.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is overusing global state for everything. Developers sometimes put all state in Redux or Context, causing performance issues and making components harder to test. Always start with local state and lift it only when needed.

[{"question":"How would you manage server state in a React application, and why might you choose a library like React Query over storing it in Redux?","expectedPoints":["Distinguishes server state from client state","Explains async nature, staleness, caching, refetching needs","Compares Redux (manual cache logic) vs React Query (built-in)","Mentions deduplication, background refetch, stale-while-revalidate","Acknowledges trade-offs: bundle size, learning curve, simplicity"],"followUps":["What if you need to share server state across many components?","How would you handle optimistic updates with React Query?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box (app) with toys (data) from the store (server). Some toys are from the store and can get old or change. You could write down everything yourself (Redux), but it's easier to use a helper (React Query) that automatically checks if toys are still good and gets new ones when needed.","detailedExplanation":"

Server state refers to data that lives on a remote server and needs to be fetched, cached, and synchronized with the client. Unlike client state (e.g., form inputs), server state is asynchronous, can become stale, and often requires refetching and deduplication of requests.

Why not Redux?

Many developers historically stored server data in Redux, but this forces you to manually implement caching, loading/error states, and refetch logic. Redux is excellent for global UI state (theme, auth) but not optimized for server state patterns like stale-while-revalidate or background refetch.

Benefits of React Query / SWR

Libraries like React Query provide:

  • Automatic caching and cache invalidation
  • Deduplication of concurrent requests
  • Background refetching to keep data fresh
  • Built-in loading, error, and success states
  • Optimistic updates and pagination support

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is treating server state as client state and manually syncing it with Redux, leading to complex, error-prone code. Instead, use React Query for server state and keep Redux (or Context) for truly global client state.

[{"question":"Explain the unidirectional data flow in Redux and how it helps with debugging.","expectedPoints":["Describes the cycle: View dispatches Action, Reducer creates new State, Store updates View.","Mentions that state changes only happen through actions and reducers.","Explains that unidirectional flow makes state changes predictable and traceable.","Highlights that debugging is easier because you can log actions and state transitions.","Contrasts with two-way binding or mutable state patterns."],"followUps":["How would you debug a state change that produces an incorrect value?","What are the trade-offs of using Redux vs. a simpler state management solution?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine a library where you can only get a book by filling out a form (action) and the librarian (reducer) updates the catalog (store). You always know who took which book because every change goes through the same process. That makes it easy to find mistakes.","detailedExplanation":"

Unidirectional data flow is a core principle of Redux where data moves in a single direction through the application. The cycle is: View → Action → Reducer → Store → View (re-render).

How it works

The View dispatches an Action (a plain object with a type and optional payload). The Reducer is a pure function that takes the current state and the action, and returns a new state object. The Store then updates and notifies subscribed Views, which re-render with the new state.

Why it matters in production

This pattern makes state changes predictable and traceable. Every state mutation is centralized in reducers, so you can log actions, inspect state diffs, and even implement time-travel debugging. It simplifies debugging because you always know exactly what action caused a state change and what the previous state was.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is mutating state directly inside a reducer instead of returning a new object. This breaks the unidirectional flow and makes debugging impossible because the store cannot detect changes. Always use immutable updates (e.g., spread operator or Object.assign).

[{"question":"Design the props API for a reusable Modal component. What props would you include and why?","expectedPoints":["Includes essential props like isOpen, onClose, children.","Considers accessibility (aria attributes, focus trap).","Handles edge cases (click outside, escape key).","Provides customization (size, title, footer).","Avoids unnecessary complexity (no redundant props)."],"followUps":["How would you handle multiple modals on the same page?","What if the modal needs to be draggable or resizable?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a popup box that can show messages or forms. You need to decide what knobs and buttons the popup should have so other developers can use it easily. For example, you need a way to open it, close it, and put content inside. You also need to make sure it works well with keyboard and screen readers, like pressing Escape to close it.","detailedExplanation":"

When designing a Modal component's props API, the goal is to create an interface that is intuitive, flexible, and hard to misuse. A well-designed API anticipates common use cases while avoiding unnecessary complexity.

Essential Props

  • isOpen (boolean): Controls visibility. The parent manages state to keep the modal controlled.
  • onClose (function): Callback when the modal requests to close (e.g., clicking overlay, pressing Escape).
  • children (ReactNode): Content to render inside the modal body.

Accessibility & Behavior

  • ariaLabel (string): Provides an accessible name for screen readers.
  • closeOnOverlayClick (boolean, default true): Allows closing by clicking outside.
  • closeOnEscape (boolean, default true): Enables keyboard dismissal.
  • focusTrap (boolean, default true): Keeps focus within the modal for keyboard users.

Customization

  • size ('sm' | 'md' | 'lg'): Preset widths for consistency.
  • title (string): Optional header text.
  • footer (ReactNode): Optional footer area for actions.

Why It Matters in Production

A good props API reduces bugs and improves developer experience. For example, forcing onClose prevents unclosable modals. Accessibility props ensure compliance with WCAG standards. Avoiding props like isLoading keeps the component focused on its single responsibility.

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering: adding too many props (e.g., animationDuration, zIndex) makes the component hard to maintain. Instead, use CSS custom properties or a theme context for advanced customization.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Parent Component] -->|isOpen, onClose| B[Modal] B -->|children| C[Content] B -->|ariaLabel| D[Accessibility] B -->|size| E[Style] B -->|closeOnOverlayClick| F[Behavior] F -->|true| G[Click Outside Closes]"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a reusable dropdown component API that supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes while following the principle of composition over configuration?","expectedPoints":["Uses composition: separate Dropdown, Option, Trigger components.","Supports controlled mode via value + onChange props.","Supports uncontrolled mode via defaultValue prop.","Provides sensible defaults (e.g., open state, selection).","Includes built-in accessibility (ARIA roles, keyboard navigation)."],"followUps":["How would you handle a scenario where the dropdown needs to fetch options asynchronously?","What tradeoffs exist between using a single large component vs. multiple composable parts?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box with many small building blocks. Instead of one big block that does everything, you can combine small blocks to build different things. Similarly, a dropdown component should be made of small pieces like a list and a button, so you can change how it looks or works without breaking it. You can also let it either control itself or let a parent control it, like a remote control for a toy car.","detailedExplanation":"

When designing a reusable dropdown component API, the key is to follow composition over configuration. Instead of a monolithic component with 30 props, break it down into smaller composable parts: Dropdown, DropdownTrigger, DropdownMenu, DropdownItem. This allows developers to customize layout and behavior without fighting a rigid API.

Controlled vs Uncontrolled

Support both modes: controlled via value and onChange props (parent manages state), and uncontrolled via defaultValue (component manages its own state). This gives flexibility: use controlled when you need to sync with form state, uncontrolled for simple use cases.

Sensible Defaults

Provide defaults like open={false}, placement='bottom-start', and a default onSelect that updates internal state. This ensures the component works out-of-the-box with minimal props.

Accessibility

Build in ARIA roles (combobox, listbox, option), keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Enter, Escape), and focus management. This is non-negotiable for production components.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is exposing too many internal implementation details (e.g., isOpen state as a prop) which breaks encapsulation. Instead, use render props or slots for custom rendering while keeping state internal.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Dropdown] --> B[Trigger] A --> C[Menu] C --> D[Item1] C --> E[Item2] A --> F[Controlled?] F -- Yes --> G[value+onChange] F -- No --> H[defaultValue]"}]

[{"question":"Design a compound Tabs component that allows users to customize the layout (e.g., placing the tab list at the bottom or left side) while keeping the state management internal. How would you implement it?","expectedPoints":["Uses React Context to share active tab state between Tabs, List, Trigger, Panel.","Provides a flexible API where children can be rearranged without breaking functionality.","Handles accessibility (keyboard navigation, ARIA roles) for tab interactions.","Avoids prop drilling by keeping state internal via context.","Allows custom styling or layout via className or render props."],"followUps":["How would you handle the case where the user wants to render the tab panels in a different order than the triggers?","What if the user wants to use a custom component as a trigger instead of a button?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a remote control with buttons for different channels. The compound pattern lets you move the buttons anywhere on the remote, and they still work because they secretly talk to each other through a hidden wire (Context). So you can design the remote however you like, and the buttons will still change the channel correctly.","detailedExplanation":"

The Compound Components pattern in React allows you to build a set of components that work together implicitly, sharing state via React Context rather than through explicit props. This is ideal for complex UI widgets like Tabs, where you want to give the consumer full control over the layout and markup while keeping the internal logic (which tab is active) hidden.

How it works

The parent Tabs component holds the state (e.g., activeTab) and provides it via a Context. Child components like Tabs.List, Tabs.Trigger, and Tabs.Panel consume that context. When a Trigger is clicked, it calls a function from context to update the active tab. The Panel checks the context to decide whether to render itself. This way, the consumer can reorder or restructure the children arbitrarily without breaking functionality.

Why it matters in production

This pattern solves the prop drilling problem and keeps the API clean. Instead of passing dozens of props down, you let the consumer compose the UI declaratively. It also improves accessibility: you can enforce ARIA roles and keyboard navigation inside the compound components, ensuring a consistent accessible experience regardless of layout.

Common pitfall

Over-engineering: not every component needs to be compound. If the layout is fixed and simple, a single component with props is easier. Also, be careful with context value stability — if you create a new object on every render, all consumers re-render unnecessarily. Use useMemo or split context into multiple providers.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR Tabs -- provides context --> Context Context -- consumed by --> List Context -- consumed by --> Trigger Context -- consumed by --> Panel Trigger -- updates activeTab --> Context Panel -- reads activeTab --> Context"}]

[{"question":"How would you implement a caching strategy for a user's profile data that updates infrequently but must reflect changes within 5 minutes?","expectedPoints":["Uses Cache API or service worker for network-first with cache fallback","Sets a cache expiration (e.g., 5 minutes) using Cache-Control or manual timestamp","Invalidates cache on explicit user action (e.g., profile update) or via stale-while-revalidate","Handles offline gracefully by serving cached data","Avoids stale closure by ensuring cache check is inside the fetch handler"],"followUps":["How would you invalidate the cache if the user updates their profile?","What are the trade-offs of using Cache API vs. localStorage for this scenario?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box where you keep your favorite toys. Sometimes you want to play with a toy that you haven't used in a while, but you want to make sure it's still the same toy. So you put a note on the box saying 'check this toy again after 5 minutes' to make sure it hasn't changed. That's like caching: you keep a copy of data to use quickly, but you also set a timer to refresh it so you don't use old information.","detailedExplanation":"

Caching is a technique to store copies of data temporarily so that future requests can be served faster. In frontend development, we often cache API responses to reduce network latency and improve user experience, especially on slow or unreliable connections.

How It Works

For a user profile that updates infrequently, a network-first with cache fallback strategy is appropriate. The service worker intercepts fetch requests, tries to get the latest data from the network, and if successful, updates the cache. If the network fails, it serves the cached response. To ensure freshness within 5 minutes, you can set a max-age header on the server or manually check a timestamp stored alongside the cached response.

Why It Matters in Production

Proper caching reduces server load, speeds up page loads, and enables offline functionality. However, cache invalidation is notoriously hard. A common pitfall is serving stale data because the cache was not invalidated after a user action (e.g., profile update). To solve this, you can use stale-while-revalidate or explicitly delete the cache entry when the user modifies their profile.

Common Pitfall

Using localStorage for caching API responses can lead to stale closure issues if the cache is not checked properly. Also, localStorage is synchronous and blocks the main thread, making it unsuitable for large or frequent caching. The Cache API is designed for HTTP caching and works asynchronously, making it a better choice for service workers.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Request] --> B{Network Available?} B -->|Yes| C[Fetch from Network] C --> D[Update Cache] D --> E[Return Response] B -->|No| F[Check Cache] F --> G{Cache Fresh?} G -->|Yes| H[Return Cached] G -->|No| I[Return Stale + Revalidate]"}]
qgen_v3:4f30bd69665c3538:
[{"question":"Describe the client-side caching layers a request passes through, from fastest to most persistent, and explain how each layer affects data freshness.","expectedPoints":["Lists layers: App/Memory, Storage (localStorage/IDB), Service Worker, HTTP cache (CDN/browser), Server.","Explains that layers closer to the app are faster but more volatile.","Discusses trade-off between speed and freshness (stale data risk).","Mentions invalidation strategies (e.g., cache headers, SW update)."],"followUps":["How would you invalidate a stale cache in the Service Worker layer?","What are the pros and cons of using localStorage vs IndexedDB for caching?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy you want to play with. You can keep it in your pocket (fastest but small), in your backpack (slower but bigger), or in the garage (slowest but biggest). The closer to you, the quicker you get it, but it might be an old toy. Caching works the same way: the fastest cache is in the app's memory, but it might have old data. You need to decide when to throw away old toys to get new ones.","detailedExplanation":"

Client-side caching is a multi-layered system that stores copies of resources closer to the user to reduce latency and server load. The layers, from fastest to most persistent, are:

  • App/Memory Cache (e.g., React Query cache): In-memory, extremely fast, but cleared on page refresh. Best for transient data.
  • Storage (localStorage, IndexedDB): Persistent across sessions, but slower than memory. Used for larger datasets or offline support.
  • Service Worker Cache (Cache API): Programmable cache that intercepts network requests. Enables offline-first strategies but requires careful invalidation.
  • HTTP Cache (Browser & CDN): Controlled by Cache-Control, Expires, ETag headers. The browser caches responses automatically; CDNs cache at the edge.
  • Server: The origin, always the source of truth but slowest.

The key trade-off is speed vs. freshness: layers closer to the app are faster but more likely to serve stale data. A common pitfall is stale data—cached responses that are no longer accurate. To mitigate this, use cache invalidation strategies like setting short max-age, using ETag for conditional requests, or implementing a Service Worker that checks for updates. In production, a well-designed caching strategy can dramatically improve performance, but poor invalidation leads to bugs and user frustration.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[App/Memory] --> B[Storage] B --> C[Service Worker] C --> D[HTTP Cache] D --> E[Server] subgraph Speed A B C D E end A -- fastest --> B -- faster --> C -- fast --> D -- slow --> E -- slowest"}]

[{"question":"You need to store a user's authentication token client-side. Compare localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies for this purpose, considering security and persistence. Which would you choose and why?","expectedPoints":["localStorage persists across sessions but is accessible via JS (XSS vulnerable).","sessionStorage is per-tab and cleared on close, still JS-accessible.","Cookies with HttpOnly and Secure flags are not accessible via JS, reducing XSS risk.","Cookies are automatically sent with requests, suitable for server-side auth.","Recommend cookies for sensitive tokens; localStorage for non-sensitive data."],"followUps":["How would you handle token refresh with cookies?","What if you need to store a large offline dataset? Which storage would you use?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a secret key to your clubhouse. If you put it in a box that anyone can open (like localStorage), a bad person could steal it. But if you put it in a special box that only the clubhouse door can open (like a cookie with HttpOnly), it's safer. For big things like a game you want to play offline, you'd use a big storage room (IndexedDB).","detailedExplanation":"

When storing authentication tokens client-side, the primary concern is security against XSS attacks. Here's a comparison:

localStorage

Data persists across sessions and is accessible via JavaScript. This makes it vulnerable to XSS: if an attacker injects script, they can read the token. Use for non-sensitive data like theme preferences.

sessionStorage

Similar to localStorage but cleared when the tab closes. Still JS-accessible, so same XSS risk. Suitable for temporary per-session data.

Cookies

Can be set with HttpOnly (prevents JS access), Secure (only sent over HTTPS), and SameSite (CSRF protection). This makes them the safest option for tokens. However, cookies are sent with every request, which can impact performance if large. Size limit is ~4KB.

Recommendation

For authentication tokens, use cookies with HttpOnly and Secure flags. For non-sensitive data that needs persistence, localStorage is acceptable. For large offline data, use IndexedDB.

Common Pitfall

Storing tokens in localStorage without considering XSS. Always assume your app might have an XSS vulnerability and choose storage accordingly.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Auth Token] --> B{Storage Choice} B --> C[localStorage] B --> D[sessionStorage] B --> E[Cookie] C --> F[Persists, JS-accessible] D --> G[Per-tab, JS-accessible] E --> H[HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite] F --> I[Vulnerable to XSS] G --> I H --> J[Safe from XSS]"}]
qgen_v3:84974984fbb10df5:
[{"question":"Explain the stale-while-revalidate caching strategy and how it differs from a simple TTL-based approach. When would you choose one over the other?","expectedPoints":["Stale-while-revalidate returns cached (stale) data immediately, then fetches fresh data in background.","TTL-based cache expires data at a fixed time; requests block until fresh data is fetched.","Stale-while-revalidate improves perceived performance and resilience to network failures.","TTL is simpler but can cause latency spikes on cache miss; stale-while-revalidate trades eventual consistency for speed.","Choose stale-while-revalidate for user-facing data where freshness is not critical; TTL for data that must be strictly up-to-date."],"followUps":["How would you implement stale-while-revalidate in a React app using a library like SWR or React Query?","What are the trade-offs of using stale-while-revalidate for real-time data like stock prices?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a favorite toy that you play with every day. Sometimes, you want to play with it right away, but it's a little dirty. With stale-while-revalidate, you can play with the toy immediately (even if it's a bit dirty), while your parent cleans a new one in the background. When the new one is ready, you switch to it. This way, you never have to wait. TTL is like saying 'I will only play with the toy for exactly 10 minutes, then I must wait for a new one.'","detailedExplanation":"

Stale-while-revalidate is a caching strategy that prioritizes responsiveness over strict freshness. When a request is made, the cache returns the stale (previously cached) data immediately, while simultaneously fetching the latest data from the server in the background. Once the fresh data arrives, the cache is updated and the UI re-renders. This is the core mechanism behind libraries like SWR and React Query.

How It Works

  • On first request, fetch from network and cache the response.
  • On subsequent requests, return cached data instantly (even if stale) and trigger a background re-fetch.
  • When the background fetch completes, update the cache and optionally notify the UI.

Comparison with TTL

A TTL-based cache sets a fixed expiration time. Once the TTL expires, the next request must wait for a fresh fetch, causing a latency spike. Stale-while-revalidate avoids this by serving stale data during the revalidation window, providing a smoother user experience.

Why It Matters in Production

In production, network latency and server load are unpredictable. Stale-while-revalidate ensures that users see content immediately, even on slow connections, while keeping data reasonably fresh. It's ideal for news feeds, dashboards, or any non-critical data where a few seconds of staleness is acceptable.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is using stale-while-revalidate for real-time data (e.g., stock prices, chat messages) where staleness can lead to incorrect decisions. In such cases, a TTL with short expiration or WebSocket push is more appropriate.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Request] --> B{Cache Hit?} B -->|Yes| C[Return Stale Data] C --> D[Background Fetch] D --> E[Update Cache] B -->|No| F[Fetch from Server] F --> G[Cache & Return] E --> H[Notify UI]"}]
qgen_v3:881822af34f25418:
[{"question":"Compare WebSockets, SSE, and Long Polling for real-time updates. When would you choose one over the other?","expectedPoints":["WebSockets: full-duplex, low latency, suitable for bidirectional communication like chat.","SSE: unidirectional from server to client, simpler, auto-reconnect, good for notifications or stock tickers.","Long Polling: emulates push with repeated requests, higher latency, fallback for older browsers.","Trade-offs: latency, complexity, browser support, and data direction requirements.","Production considerations: connection limits, scalability, and fallback strategies."],"followUps":["How would you implement a 'user is typing' indicator using these techniques?","What are the main challenges when scaling WebSocket connections across multiple servers?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to know when your friend sends you a message. Long Polling is like calling them every few seconds to ask 'Any new message?' – it works but wastes time. SSE is like your friend calling you whenever they have news – only one-way. WebSocket is like a phone call where both of you can talk at the same time – perfect for chatting.","detailedExplanation":"

Real-time updates are essential for modern web apps like chat, notifications, and live dashboards. Three common techniques are WebSockets, Server-Sent Events (SSE), and Long Polling. Each has distinct trade-offs in latency, complexity, and data direction.

WebSockets

WebSockets provide a full-duplex communication channel over a single TCP connection. Both client and server can send messages anytime with low latency. This is ideal for bidirectional apps like chat, collaborative editing, or gaming. However, it requires a dedicated server setup and can be complex to scale horizontally due to persistent connections.

Server-Sent Events (SSE)

SSE is unidirectional – the server pushes events to the client over HTTP. It is simpler than WebSockets, with built-in auto-reconnect and event IDs. SSE is perfect for one-way updates like stock tickers, news feeds, or notifications. It works over standard HTTP/2, but does not support sending data from client to server.

Long Polling

Long Polling is a fallback technique where the client sends a request and the server holds it open until new data is available. It simulates push but introduces higher latency and overhead due to repeated HTTP requests. It is still used for browser compatibility but is less efficient than SSE or WebSockets.

Production Considerations

  • Latency: WebSocket < SSE < Long Polling (lowest to highest).
  • Complexity: WebSocket requires managing connections and reconnection logic; SSE is simpler; Long Polling is easiest to implement but hardest to scale.
  • Scalability: WebSocket servers need sticky sessions or a pub/sub layer; SSE can leverage HTTP/2 multiplexing; Long Polling can cause high server load.
  • Browser Support: WebSocket and SSE are widely supported in modern browsers; Long Polling works everywhere.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is using WebSockets for simple one-way updates, adding unnecessary complexity. Conversely, using Long Polling for a chat app leads to poor user experience due to high latency. Always match the technique to the data flow direction and latency requirements.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client] -->|HTTP Request| B[Long Polling] B -->|Hold & Response| A A -->|EventSource| C[SSE] C -->|Push Events| A A -->|WebSocket| D[WebSocket] D -->|Full-Duplex| A subgraph Server B C D end"}]

[{"question":"How would you implement an optimistic UI update for a 'like' button, and what are the key considerations for rollback?","expectedPoints":["Immediately update UI state before server confirmation","Send async request to server in parallel","Rollback UI to previous state on server error or timeout","Handle race conditions and multiple rapid clicks","Provide user feedback during pending state (e.g., disable button)"],"followUps":["How would you handle the case where the server responds with a different final state than the optimistic update?","What if the user navigates away before the server responds?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're playing a game where you press a button to score a point. Instead of waiting for the referee to confirm, the scoreboard lights up immediately. If the referee says 'no point', the score goes back. That's optimistic UI—it feels fast because you see the result right away, even before the server says it's okay.","detailedExplanation":"

Optimistic UI is a pattern where the client immediately reflects a user action in the UI before receiving confirmation from the server. This creates a snappy, real-time feel even under network latency.

How it works

When a user clicks 'like':

  • The UI instantly updates (e.g., heart icon fills, count increments).
  • An asynchronous request is sent to the server.
  • If the server responds with success, the optimistic state becomes the real state.
  • If the server responds with an error or times out, the UI rolls back to the previous state and optionally shows an error message.

Key considerations

  • Idempotency: Ensure the server can handle duplicate requests (e.g., if the user clicks multiple times).
  • Race conditions: If the user performs multiple actions quickly, the UI must track the correct order and final state.
  • Rollback logic: Store the previous state (e.g., in a variable or state management) to revert cleanly.
  • User feedback: Disable the button or show a spinner during the pending request to prevent confusion.

Common pitfall

Not handling optimistic updates for non-idempotent actions (e.g., incrementing a counter). If the server fails and the client rolls back, but the server actually processed the request, the count becomes inconsistent. Use optimistic with conflict resolution or rely on server-driven state for critical data.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User clicks like] --> B[Update UI instantly] B --> C[Send request to server] C --> D{Server response?} D -- Success --> E[Keep UI as is] D -- Error/Timeout --> F[Rollback UI to previous state] F --> G[Show error notification]"}]
qgen_v3:78cb2e39e2f19851:
[{"question":"How would you design a frontend application to support both accessibility (a11y) and internationalization (i18n) from the start?","expectedPoints":["Use semantic HTML and ARIA attributes for screen readers","Support keyboard navigation and focus management","Separate UI strings into locale files and use i18n libraries","Handle locale-specific formats (dates, numbers, currencies) via CLDR/ICU","Test with real assistive technologies and multiple locales"],"followUps":["How would you handle right-to-left (RTL) languages in your design?","What are the trade-offs of using a runtime i18n library vs. build-time translation extraction?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a playground that every kid can enjoy, whether they use a wheelchair, speak a different language, or prefer to play with their eyes closed. Accessibility means adding ramps and braille signs so everyone can play. Internationalization means having signs in many languages and showing the time in each kid's local time. You need to plan both from the start so no one is left out.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a frontend application that is both accessible and internationalized requires a holistic approach from the very beginning. These two concerns often intersect—for example, a screen reader must announce translated text correctly, and date pickers must be navigable via keyboard while respecting locale formats.

Accessibility (a11y) Foundations

Start with semantic HTML (e.g., <nav>, <button>, <h1>) to provide native accessibility. Use ARIA attributes (e.g., aria-label, role) only when semantics are insufficient. Ensure keyboard navigation with logical tab order and visible focus indicators. Implement skip links and manage focus for dynamic content (e.g., modals). Test with real screen readers (NVDA, VoiceOver) and keyboard-only usage.

Internationalization (i18n) Foundations

Separate all user-facing strings into locale files (e.g., JSON). Use an i18n library like react-intl or i18next to interpolate variables and handle plurals. For dates, numbers, and currencies, rely on the Intl API (e.g., Intl.DateTimeFormat) or CLDR data. Support RTL layouts by using logical CSS properties (margin-inline-start) and flipping UI components.

Integration and Production Considerations

Accessibility and i18n must be tested together. For example, a translated string might be longer and break a layout, or an ARIA label might not be translated. Use automated tools (axe-core, Lighthouse) and manual testing with multiple locales. A common pitfall is forgetting to update ARIA labels when the language changes—always bind them to translated strings.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Start Design] --> B[Semantic HTML] A --> C[Locale Files] B --> D[ARIA Attributes] B --> E[Keyboard Nav] C --> F[Intl API] C --> G[RTL Support] D & E & F & G --> H[Test with Screen Readers & Locales]"}]
qgen_v3:979322272d320816:
[{"question":"How would you ensure a dynamic content update (e.g., a live search results list) is announced to screen readers without causing a stale closure issue in React?","expectedPoints":["Uses aria-live region (e.g., aria-live="polite") to announce dynamic content","Avoids stale closures by using useCallback or useRef for event handlers","Ensures the live region is present in the DOM before updates","Considers using aria-atomic for complete region updates","Tests with actual screen reader or automated tools like axe"],"followUps":["What if the search results update very frequently? How would you throttle announcements?","How would you handle focus management after the results update?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a magic box that tells a blind person what's new on the screen. If you update the box too fast or with old information, it gets confusing. You need to make sure the box always has the latest news and doesn't get stuck with old messages.","detailedExplanation":"

When dynamic content updates (e.g., live search results), screen readers need to be notified. The standard approach is to use an ARIA live region (aria-live="polite" or "assertive"). This tells assistive technologies to watch for changes and announce them.

How it works

Wrap the dynamic area in a container with aria-live="polite". When the content inside changes (e.g., via React state update), the screen reader will announce the new text. For complex updates, aria-atomic="true" ensures the entire region is treated as one unit.

Stale closures in React

A common pitfall is using a callback that captures an old state value (stale closure). For example, if you have a useEffect that sets up an event listener referencing a state variable, the listener might use an outdated value. Use useCallback with proper dependencies or useRef to hold the latest value.

Why it matters in production

Without proper live regions, screen reader users miss critical updates, making the app unusable. Stale closures cause bugs like announcing old results or failing to update. Testing with tools like axe or Lighthouse catches these issues.

Common pitfall

Forgetting to add the live region before the dynamic content appears. The container must exist in the DOM initially (even if empty) so the screen reader registers it. Also, avoid using aria-live on elements that are removed/re-added, as the announcement may not trigger.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User types] --> B[Search API call] B --> C[Update state] C --> D[React re-render] D --> E[aria-live region] E --> F[Screen reader announces]"}]
qgen_v3:170c109f02451f2f:
[{"question":"How would you implement i18n in a React application to support multiple languages and locales, including RTL languages?","expectedPoints":["Use a library like react-intl or i18next for string externalization and ICU MessageFormat for plurals/gender.","Leverage native Intl API for locale-aware formatting of dates, numbers, and currencies.","Support RTL layouts using logical CSS properties (e.g., margin-inline-start) instead of physical properties.","Lazy-load language bundles to reduce initial bundle size.","Avoid hardcoding strings; use translation keys and manage translations in separate files."],"followUps":["How would you handle dynamic content like user-generated text that needs translation?","What are the performance implications of loading multiple locale files?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that talks. You want it to speak English, Vietnamese, and Arabic. You don't want to build a new toy for each language. Instead, you store all the words in a box and pick the right ones when the toy starts. Also, for Arabic, the toy reads from right to left, so you need to flip the direction. This is what i18n does for apps.","detailedExplanation":"

Internationalization (i18n) is the process of designing an application to support multiple languages and cultural conventions without requiring code changes. In a React app, a robust i18n implementation involves several key practices.

String Externalization

Never hardcode text. Use a library like react-intl or i18next to define translation keys. For example, intl.formatMessage({ id: 'welcome' }). This allows translators to work on separate JSON files without touching code.

Pluralization and Gender

Use ICU MessageFormat to handle plurals and gender correctly. For instance, {count, plural, one {# item} other {# items}}. This ensures grammatical correctness across languages.

Locale-Aware Formatting

Leverage the native Intl API for dates, numbers, and currencies. Example: new Intl.NumberFormat('vi-VN', { style: 'currency', currency: 'VND' }).format(1500000) outputs 1.500.000 ₫. This avoids third-party dependencies and is well-optimized.

RTL Support

For right-to-left languages like Arabic or Hebrew, use logical CSS properties (e.g., margin-inline-start instead of margin-left). This automatically flips the layout based on the dir attribute on the <html> tag.

Lazy Loading

Only load the locale data for the current language to reduce bundle size. Use dynamic imports: import('intl/locale-data/jsonp/vi.js'). This improves initial load time.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting that translated strings can be longer or shorter than the original, causing layout breaks. Always design flexible UIs that can accommodate varying text lengths.

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) is one of the most common frontend security vulnerabilities. It occurs when an attacker injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. In a React application, the framework automatically escapes values in JSX, which prevents many XSS attacks. However, there are still attack vectors that developers must handle.

Key Protection Strategies

  • Default Escaping: React escapes all string content in JSX, so {userInput} is safe. But this only applies to text content, not attributes like href or src.
  • Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML: This prop bypasses React's escaping. If you must use it, sanitize the HTML first with a library like DOMPurify.
  • Sanitize User Input: For any user-generated content that will be rendered as HTML, always sanitize it on the client side before inserting into the DOM.
  • Content Security Policy (CSP): Set HTTP headers like Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self' to restrict which scripts can execute. This provides a strong second line of defense.
  • Validate and Encode: Ensure all user input is validated for expected formats and encoded when used in URLs or attributes.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on React's default escaping is not enough. For example, if you set href to user input without validation, an attacker could use javascript:alert(1) to execute code. Always validate URLs and use URL constructor or a library to parse and sanitize.

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) is a security vulnerability where an attacker injects malicious scripts into a web application, which then executes in the victim's browser. In React, the primary defense is that JSX escapes all values by default, preventing script injection. However, developers can bypass this using dangerouslySetInnerHTML, which should be avoided or used with sanitization libraries like DOMPurify.

How it works

Attackers often inject scripts via input fields, URL parameters, or stored data. When the app renders this unsanitized data, the browser executes the script. React's default behavior escapes HTML entities, turning <script> into plain text.

Why it matters in production

XSS can lead to session hijacking, data theft, or defacement. Even with React's built-in protection, developers must:

  • Never use dangerouslySetInnerHTML with untrusted data.
  • Sanitize HTML with libraries like DOMPurify when rendering rich content.
  • Set a Content Security Policy (CSP) to restrict script sources.
  • Validate and sanitize input on the server side as well.

Common pitfall

Relying solely on client-side sanitization. Attackers can bypass client-side checks by sending requests directly to the server. Always sanitize server-side and use CSP as a defense-in-depth measure.

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is a vulnerability where attackers inject malicious scripts into web pages. In React, the primary defense is automatic escaping of values rendered in JSX. For example, {userInput} is safe because React escapes HTML entities. However, when you need to render raw HTML, you must use dangerouslySetInnerHTML, which bypasses React's escaping and directly inserts HTML into the DOM.

How to handle user-submitted HTML safely

  • Sanitize the HTML string using a library like DOMPurify before passing it to dangerouslySetInnerHTML. DOMPurify removes malicious tags and attributes while preserving safe ones.
  • Set Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to restrict script sources. For example, script-src 'self' prevents inline scripts from executing.
  • Use HttpOnly cookies to prevent JavaScript from accessing sensitive cookies, mitigating cookie theft even if XSS occurs.

Why it matters in production

XSS can lead to data theft, session hijacking, and defacement. A single unescaped user input can compromise all users. React's default escaping covers most cases, but developers must remain vigilant when using dangerouslySetInnerHTML or when rendering HTML from third-party sources.

Common pitfall

Relying solely on client-side sanitization. Attackers can bypass client-side checks by sending requests directly to the server. Always validate and sanitize input on the server as well. Client-side sanitization is only for UX and defense-in-depth.
