[{"question":"How would you test a React component that makes an async API call on mount, and how do you ensure the test is reliable (not flaky)?","expectedPoints":["Uses a mock for the API call (e.g., jest.spyOn or MSW) to avoid real network requests.","Tests loading, success, and error states with async utilities like waitFor or findBy.","Avoids implementation details (e.g., not testing setState directly) and focuses on rendered output.","Handles cleanup and prevents test interdependence (e.g., resetting mocks in afterEach)."],"followUps":["What if the API call is inside a useEffect with dependencies? How do you test different dependency values?","How would you test a component that uses a third-party hook like useQuery from React Query?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're testing a toy that fetches a new toy from a box when you press a button. Instead of actually opening the real box (which might be slow or broken), you pretend to open a fake box that always gives the right toy quickly. That way, you can check if the toy shows up correctly without waiting or worrying about the real box failing.","detailedExplanation":"

Testing async components is a common source of flaky tests. The key is to mock external dependencies (like API calls) so tests are fast, deterministic, and isolated. For a React component that fetches data on mount, you can use jest.spyOn to mock fetch or axios, or better, use Mock Service Worker (MSW) to intercept network requests at the service worker level.

How to test

  • Render the component with @testing-library/react.
  • Use waitFor or findBy to wait for async updates (e.g., data to appear).
  • Test three states: loading (spinner or placeholder), success (data rendered), and error (error message).
  • Mock the API to resolve or reject as needed.

Why it matters in production

Flaky tests erode trust in the test suite. By mocking async calls, you eliminate network latency and server variability. This ensures tests pass consistently, catching real regressions rather than random failures.

Common pitfall

Testing implementation details like setState calls or internal state values. Instead, test what the user sees (DOM output). Also, forgetting to clean up mocks between tests can cause cross-test pollution.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Component Mounts] --> B[useEffect fires] B --> C[Async API call] C --> D{Mock?} D -- Yes --> E[Fake response] D -- No --> F[Real network] E --> G[Update state] G --> H[Render UI] H --> I[Assert with waitFor]"}]

[{"question":"When would you choose a spy over a mock for verifying that a callback was invoked with the correct arguments?","expectedPoints":["Spy wraps a real function and records calls; mock replaces the function entirely.","Use spy when you want to preserve the real implementation while verifying interactions.","Use mock when you need to define expectations and fail the test if they are not met.","Spy is lighter and less intrusive; mock can lead to brittle tests if overused.","Prefer spy for callbacks/analytics where real behavior matters; mock for external dependencies."],"followUps":["How would you test that an analytics event was sent exactly once with the correct payload?","What are the risks of using mocks for internal implementation details?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy robot that can walk and talk. A spy is like watching the robot carefully to see how many times it says 'hello' and what words it uses, but the robot still works normally. A mock is like replacing the robot's voice box with a fake one that only says 'hello' when you expect it to, and if it says something else, the test fails. You use a spy when you want to check the real robot's actions, and a mock when you want to control exactly what happens.","detailedExplanation":"

Spy and mock are both test doubles used to verify interactions, but they serve different purposes. A spy wraps the real implementation and records information about how it was called (e.g., number of invocations, arguments). It does not replace the function's behavior unless you explicitly stub it. In contrast, a mock is a completely fake object that comes with built-in expectations about how it should be called. If those expectations are not met, the test fails automatically.

When to use each

Use a spy when you want to verify that a callback or side effect (like an analytics event) was triggered correctly, but you still want the real logic to execute. For example, if you have a function that calls an analytics library, you can spy on the analytics method to ensure it was called with the right parameters without disabling the actual analytics behavior. This makes the test less brittle because you are not overriding the implementation.

Use a mock when you are testing a module that depends on an external service (e.g., an API client). You want to define exactly how that dependency should be called and fail the test if the interaction deviates. Mocks are powerful but can lead to over-mocking, where tests only verify that the code called the mock correctly, not that the system works end-to-end.

Common pitfall

Developers often use mocks for everything, including internal functions that are not at the system boundary. This makes tests fragile and tightly coupled to implementation details. A good rule of thumb is to mock at the boundary (network, time, storage) and use spies or real implementations for internal logic.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Test] --> B{Verify interaction?} B -->|Yes| C[Spy or Mock?] C -->|Real behavior needed| D[Spy] C -->|Expectations only| E[Mock] D --> F[Record calls] E --> G[Fail if wrong] F --> H[Assert manually] G --> I[Auto-fail]"}]

[{"question":"How would you test a debounced function without waiting real time?","expectedPoints":["Use fake timers (vi.useFakeTimers) to control time","Call debounced function multiple times in quick succession","Advance time by the debounce delay (vi.advanceTimersByTime)","Assert the function was called only once","Restore real timers after test"],"followUps":["What happens if you advance time by less than the delay?","How would you test that the debounced function receives the latest arguments?"],"difficulty":"mid","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a doorbell that only rings if you haven't pressed it again within 5 seconds. Testing this normally would require waiting 5 seconds each time. But with fake timers, you can pretend time moves instantly, so you can test the doorbell behavior in a split second.","detailedExplanation":"

Testing asynchronous code like debounce requires controlling time to avoid slow, flaky tests. The key is to use fake timers (e.g., vi.useFakeTimers() in Vitest or jest.useFakeTimers() in Jest). This replaces setTimeout, setInterval, and Date.now with mock implementations that you control.

How it works

First, enable fake timers. Then call the debounced function multiple times in quick succession. Use vi.advanceTimersByTime(300) to simulate the passage of 300ms. Since the debounce resets its timer on each call, only the last call's timer should fire, resulting in a single invocation. Finally, assert the spy was called once.

Why it matters in production

Real timers would make tests slow (e.g., waiting 300ms per test) and unreliable (time-dependent). Fake timers give deterministic, fast tests. This pattern is essential for testing search inputs, auto-save, or resize handlers.

Common pitfall

Forgetting to restore real timers after the test (vi.useRealTimers()). If not restored, other tests may break because they rely on real time. Also, ensure you advance time by exactly the debounce delay; advancing less will leave pending timers and the assertion will fail.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Call debounced fn] --> B[Timer starts 300ms] B --> C[Call again before 300ms] C --> D[Reset timer] D --> E[Advance time 300ms] E --> F[Timer fires] F --> G[Fn called once]"}]

[{"question":"How would you implement a contract test between a frontend and a backend that are developed independently?","expectedPoints":["Describes a contract (e.g., OpenAPI, Pact) that defines expected request/response shapes.","Frontend uses a mock provider (e.g., Pact consumer test) to verify its expectations.","Backend runs a provider test to confirm it satisfies the contract.","CI pipeline runs both sides; failure blocks deployment.","Catches breaking changes before production."],"followUps":["What happens if the backend changes a field name but the frontend hasn't updated yet?","How do you handle optional fields in contract tests?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are building two halves of a toy. You agree on a drawing (the contract) that shows exactly how your pieces fit together. If your friend changes their piece without telling you, the toy won't fit. A contract test checks that both halves still match the drawing, so you catch problems early.","detailedExplanation":"

Contract testing is a technique to verify that two independently developed services (e.g., frontend and backend) can communicate correctly. It uses a shared contract — a formal specification of API requests and responses, often defined with tools like Pact or OpenAPI.

How it works

The frontend (consumer) writes a consumer test that defines its expectations: “I expect a GET /users to return a JSON array with objects containing id and name.” This test runs against a mock provider generated from the contract. The backend (provider) runs a provider test that verifies its actual API matches the same contract. Both tests are executed in CI.

Why it matters in production

Without contract tests, a backend change (e.g., renaming username to name) can silently break the frontend. Contract tests catch such mismatches before deployment, reducing integration bugs and enabling parallel development.

Common pitfall

Treating contract tests as full integration tests. They should only verify the shape of data, not business logic. Over-specifying (e.g., exact values) makes tests brittle. Use matchers (like Pact’s like()) to allow flexible values while enforcing structure.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR subgraph Consumer[Frontend] A[Write expectations] --> B[Consumer test] end subgraph Provider[Backend] C[Provider test] --> D[Verify contract] end B --> E{Contract} E --> C E --> F[CI pipeline] F --> G[Pass/Fail]"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a component documentation system using Storybook for a large-scale design system?","expectedPoints":["Organize stories by component hierarchy and variants","Integrate with design tokens and theming","Automate visual regression testing with Chromatic","Enable collaboration between developers and designers","Ensure accessibility checks in stories"],"followUps":["How do you handle component dependencies and mock data in stories?","What strategies do you use to keep stories in sync with the live design system?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big box of LEGO bricks. Storybook is like a special display case where you can see each brick clearly, try different colors, and make sure they all fit together before building a big castle. It helps everyone on the team see and test the bricks without having to build the whole castle first.","detailedExplanation":"

Storybook is an open-source tool for developing UI components in isolation. It acts as a living documentation system where each component is showcased with its different states and variations. For a large-scale design system, Storybook becomes the single source of truth for the component library.

How to Design the System

  • Organize stories by component hierarchy: Mirror the component folder structure (e.g., atoms/buttons, molecules/cards) and include stories for every variant (primary, secondary, disabled, loading, etc.).
  • Integrate design tokens: Use addons like @storybook/addon-toolbars to switch themes (light/dark) or apply custom tokens, ensuring components adapt correctly.
  • Automate visual regression testing: Use Chromatic (by Storybook) to capture screenshots of each story on every commit. Any visual change triggers a review, preventing regressions.
  • Enable collaboration: Share Storybook via a hosted URL (e.g., Chromatic or Netlify) so designers and product managers can review components without needing to run the app.
  • Accessibility checks: Include the @storybook/addon-a11y to automatically flag contrast issues, missing labels, etc.

Why It Matters in Production

Without a system like Storybook, components are tested only within the full application, making it hard to isolate bugs or verify edge cases. Storybook accelerates development by allowing parallel work on UI and logic, and it serves as a living style guide that stays up-to-date with code changes.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is not mocking data and dependencies properly. Components often rely on context, API calls, or Redux stores. Without proper mocking in stories, the isolated view may break or misrepresent the component. Always use addon-interactions or decorators to provide mock contexts.

Isolated component development (e.g., using Storybook) means building and testing each UI component independently, outside the full application context. This approach is a cornerstone of modern frontend architecture because it addresses several real-world pain points.

What It Is

Each component is developed in a sandbox where you can render it with any combination of props, simulating all its states (loading, empty, error, edge cases) without needing a backend, authentication, or complex data setup. Tools like Storybook provide a catalog of these component states, called stories.

How It Works

You write a story for each state. For example, a UserCard component might have stories for default, loading, error, and empty. These stories are executable code that renders the component with specific props. The tool provides an interactive UI to view, tweak, and test each story.

Why It Matters in Production

  • Faster development: Developers can build and iterate on a component without booting up the entire app or mocking complex data flows.
  • Living documentation: Stories serve as a single source of truth for how a component looks and behaves, accessible to designers, QA, and other developers.
  • Visual regression testing: Automated screenshot comparisons catch unintended UI changes (e.g., a button shifting 2px) that unit tests miss.
  • Accessibility audits: Built-in addons can check contrast ratios, ARIA attributes, and keyboard navigation per component, making a11y fixes easier.

Common Pitfall

Over-isolating components that are tightly coupled to global state (e.g., Redux, context) can lead to stories that don't reflect real usage. Mitigate by using decorators to wrap stories with mock providers, or by refactoring to rely on props instead of global state.

[{"question":"What are the three roles a single Storybook story serves, and why does this make it valuable for a design system?","expectedPoints":["Serves as living documentation","Serves as an isolated development playground","Serves as input for visual regression testing","Reduces duplication and maintenance effort","Enables collaboration across the team"],"followUps":["How would you structure stories to maximize these benefits?","What happens if a story is not kept in sync with the actual component?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy car that you want to show your friends, let them play with it, and also check if it gets scratched over time. One story does all three jobs, so you don't have to build separate things for each.","detailedExplanation":"

A Storybook story is a single exported function that renders a component in a specific state. It serves three distinct roles simultaneously:

1. Living Documentation

Each story is a runnable example that shows exactly how a component looks and behaves with given props. Team members and stakeholders can browse stories to understand the component's API and visual variants without reading code.

2. Isolated Development Playground

Developers can interact with each story in isolation, tweaking args via Storybook's controls panel. This speeds up development and debugging because you don't need to navigate the full app.

3. Visual Regression Test Input

Tools like Chromatic or Percy capture screenshots of each story and compare them against baselines. Any unintended visual change triggers a failure, catching regressions early.

This “write once, use three ways” approach reduces duplication and maintenance overhead. For a design system, it ensures that documentation, development, and testing stay in sync. A common pitfall is treating stories only as documentation and neglecting to update them when the component changes, which breaks the testing and playground value. To avoid this, integrate story generation into your component development workflow and enforce visual reviews in CI.

[{"question":"How would you design a micro-frontend architecture using Module Federation to allow two independently deployed apps to share a common header component at runtime?","expectedPoints":["Explain Module Federation as a runtime module loading mechanism","Describe configuration of exposes and remotes in webpack","Discuss versioning and shared dependency management","Address fallback or error handling for remote module failures","Consider performance implications like chunk splitting and caching"],"followUps":["How would you handle version conflicts if the shared header component updates independently?","What happens if the remote app is down when the host tries to load the header?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine two different websites that want to use the same top bar. Module Federation is like a magic box that lets one website borrow the top bar from the other website while you're using it, without having to copy the code. It's like borrowing a toy from a friend's house instead of buying your own.","detailedExplanation":"

Module Federation is a webpack feature that enables micro-frontend architectures by allowing multiple independently built and deployed applications to share code at runtime. It works by exposing specific modules from one app (the remote) and consuming them in another app (the host) via a dynamic loading mechanism.

How It Works

Each app configures its webpack with the ModuleFederationPlugin. The remote app declares which modules it exposes (e.g., exposes: { './Header': './src/Header' }). The host app lists the remote as a dependency (remotes: { app1: 'app1@http://cdn.com/remoteEntry.js' }). At runtime, the host fetches the remote's entry file and loads the shared module asynchronously.

Why It Matters in Production

This pattern allows teams to develop and deploy independently, reducing coordination overhead. It enables gradual migration, A/B testing, and canary releases. However, it introduces complexity in dependency management — shared libraries like React must be configured as singletons to avoid duplicate instances. Version mismatches can cause runtime errors.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is not handling the asynchronous nature of remote module loading. If the remote is unavailable, the host app may break. Always implement error boundaries and fallback UI for remote components. Also, ensure proper caching strategies for remote entry files to balance freshness and performance.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR subgraph Host App A[Host Entry] B[Header Component] end subgraph Remote App C[Remote Entry] D[Exposed Header] end A -->|loads| C C -->|provides| D D -->|renders in| B"}]

[{"question":"In a micro-frontend architecture using Module Federation, how does the Host app load a Remote module at runtime, and what are the key considerations for ensuring reliability?","expectedPoints":["Host uses dynamic import or Module Federation runtime API to fetch remote entry.","Remote exposes modules via configured exposes in webpack config.","Shared dependencies to avoid duplication and version conflicts.","Error handling for network failures or version mismatches.","Versioning and deployment strategies to maintain compatibility."],"followUps":["How would you handle a scenario where a remote module fails to load?","What strategies can you use to share state between host and remote?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big toy set with different parts like a castle, a car, and a tree. The Host is like you asking for the parts at playtime, and the Remote is each friend sending their part. You need to make sure the parts fit together and that if a friend is late, you can still play with the parts you already have.","detailedExplanation":"

Micro-frontend architecture with Module Federation allows a Host application to load and integrate Remote modules at runtime. The Host acts as a shell that provides a common layout, routing, and orchestration, while each Remote (e.g., Cart, Search, Profile) is developed and deployed independently by separate teams.

How it works

The Host uses dynamic imports or the Module Federation runtime API to fetch the remote entry point (e.g., remoteEntry.js) from a remote server. The Remote exposes specific modules via its webpack configuration using the exposes property. At runtime, the Host loads the remote entry, which then asynchronously loads the required modules. Shared dependencies (like React or utility libraries) are configured in the shared property to avoid duplication and ensure version compatibility.

Key considerations for reliability

  • Error handling: Implement fallback UI or retry logic when a remote module fails to load due to network issues or server errors.
  • Versioning: Use semantic versioning and ensure backward compatibility. Consider using version mismatch detection to prevent runtime errors.
  • Deployment strategy: Coordinate deployments to avoid breaking changes. Use canary releases or feature flags to test new versions gradually.
  • Shared dependencies: Carefully manage shared libraries to prevent multiple instances or incompatible versions.

Common pitfall

A frequent mistake is not handling asynchronous loading properly, leading to blank screens or broken UI when a remote module is slow or fails. Always provide loading states and error boundaries.

[{"question":"Why must React be declared as a shared singleton in Module Federation?","expectedPoints":["Prevents multiple React instances from loading","Multiple React instances break hooks and context","Singleton ensures a single React instance is shared","Configured via shared: { react: { singleton: true } }"],"followUps":["What happens if two remotes load different React versions?","How would you debug a hooks error caused by duplicate React?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend each bring your own identical toy box to play together. If you both open your own box, you'll have two sets of toys that don't mix well. Module Federation is like agreeing to share one toy box so everything works smoothly. React must be a shared singleton to avoid having two separate copies that break how components talk to each other.","detailedExplanation":"

Module Federation allows multiple independent JavaScript applications (micro-frontends) to be composed at runtime. A critical requirement is that certain libraries, especially React, must be shared as a singleton across all federated modules.

Why Singleton?

React relies on a single internal state tree for hooks and context. If two different versions of React are loaded, each will have its own internal state, causing hooks to fail (e.g., useState returning undefined) and context to break (values not shared). This leads to cryptic errors like “Invalid hook call” or “Cannot read property 'current' of null”.

How to Configure

In the Webpack config of the host or remote, you declare React as a shared singleton:

shared: { react: { singleton: true, requiredVersion: '^17.0.0' } }

This ensures Webpack's runtime only loads one instance of React, even if multiple remotes request it.

Production Importance

In production, failing to set singleton: true can cause intermittent bugs that are hard to reproduce. It also increases bundle size due to duplicate libraries. Proper sharing reduces payload and prevents runtime conflicts.

Common Pitfall

Developers often forget to set singleton: true or mismatch version ranges, causing the fallback to load multiple copies. Always test with browser DevTools to verify only one React instance is present.

[{"question":"How would you design a distributed tracing system for a frontend request that passes through an API gateway and multiple microservices, and what alert strategy would you implement to avoid alert fatigue?","expectedPoints":["Proposes a trace ID propagated from frontend through all services (e.g., via headers like x-request-id).","Describes collection of span data (start time, duration, service name) and aggregation in a centralized store (e.g., Jaeger, Zipkin).","Suggests alerting on high-latency percentiles (e.g., p99) or error rates, with dynamic thresholds and grouping to reduce noise.","Mention use of service-level objectives (SLOs) and burn rate alerts to prioritize critical issues."],"followUps":["How would you ensure trace context is preserved across asynchronous calls (e.g., message queues)?","What metrics would you use to distinguish between a transient spike and a persistent degradation?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're trying to find out why a package delivery is slow. You put a tracking number on the package, and every time it moves through a different station (like sorting, loading, driving), you record the time. Later, you can see which station took too long. Alerts are like a phone call to the manager only when a station is consistently slow, not just one time, so they don't get annoyed by false alarms.","detailedExplanation":"

Distributed tracing is essential for understanding the performance of a request that flows through multiple services. The core idea is to propagate a unique trace ID from the frontend (e.g., via HTTP headers like x-request-id or traceparent) through the API gateway and every downstream microservice. Each service creates spans that record the start time, duration, and metadata (service name, operation). These spans are sent to a centralized tracing backend (e.g., Jaeger, Zipkin, or Datadog APM) which reconstructs the entire trace tree.

How it works

When a frontend request arrives, the API gateway generates a trace ID. This ID is passed via headers to each service. Each service creates a child span for its own work (e.g., database query, external API call). Spans are collected asynchronously and later correlated by the trace ID. The frontend can also include the trace ID in logs or error responses for debugging.

Alert strategy to avoid fatigue

Instead of alerting on every slow request, use statistical aggregation (e.g., p99 latency over 5-minute windows) and burn rate alerts based on SLOs. For example, if the p99 latency exceeds 500ms for 10 minutes, trigger an alert. Group alerts by service and error type to reduce noise. Use dynamic thresholds that adapt to historical patterns, and implement alert deduplication and silencing for known issues.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is sampling too aggressively, which can miss rare but critical slow requests. Use adaptive sampling (e.g., keep all traces for high-latency requests) or head-based sampling with a minimum rate.

[{"question":"How does the frontend participate in distributed tracing, and why is context propagation important?","expectedPoints":["Frontend generates a trace-id and passes it via headers (e.g., traceparent).","Trace consists of multiple spans (e.g., page load, API call, DB query).","Context propagation links frontend and backend logs for end-to-end debugging.","Helps identify bottlenecks (e.g., slow DB query) without guesswork."],"followUps":["What happens if the frontend fails to propagate the trace-id?","How would you implement trace propagation in a single-page application?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're tracking a package. The frontend is like the person who puts a tracking number on the package. That number (trace-id) is passed to every person who handles the package (backend services). If the package is slow, you can see exactly where it got stuck—like a database query taking too long—without guessing.","detailedExplanation":"

Distributed tracing is a technique used to track requests as they flow through multiple services in a microservices architecture. A trace represents the entire journey of a request, and it is composed of multiple spans, each representing a unit of work (e.g., a function call, an API request, a database query). Spans can be nested or sequential.

Frontend's Role

The frontend initiates the trace by generating a unique trace-id and passing it along with every API call via HTTP headers. The standard header is traceparent (W3C Trace Context). This allows the backend services to continue the trace by creating child spans that reference the same trace-id.

Why Context Propagation Matters

Without context propagation, logs from different services are isolated, making it nearly impossible to correlate a user's slow experience with a specific backend bottleneck. With distributed tracing, you can visualize a waterfall diagram showing the duration of each span. For example, if a database query takes 560ms out of a total 1200ms page load, you immediately know where to optimize.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is failing to propagate the trace context across asynchronous boundaries (e.g., WebSocket connections, background jobs). This breaks the trace and loses visibility. Always ensure that any outgoing request from the frontend includes the trace headers, and that backend services forward them correctly.

qgen_v3:d6a44987705c81ce:
[{"question":"How would you design an observability system for a frontend application that monitors user interactions, performance, and errors?","expectedPoints":["Covers logs, metrics, and traces as the three pillars","Describes concrete tools (e.g., Sentry, Datadog, OpenTelemetry)","Explains how to correlate data across pillars","Addresses sampling and privacy concerns","Mentions dashboard and alerting setup"],"followUps":["How would you handle high-volume event logging without degrading performance?","What metrics would you prioritize for a real-time collaborative app?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're a detective trying to solve a mystery in a big city. Logs are like witness statements—they tell you exactly what happened at a specific time. Metrics are like traffic cameras—they show you patterns, like which roads are busy. Traces are like following a single car's journey through the city—they help you find where it got stuck. Together, they help you understand and fix problems in your app.","detailedExplanation":"

Observability is the ability to understand the internal state of a system by examining its outputs. For frontend applications, this means collecting and analyzing data about user interactions, performance, and errors. The three pillars are logs, metrics, and traces.

Logs

Logs are discrete events with timestamps. They are excellent for debugging specific user sessions or errors. For example, a log might record 'User clicked button X at 12:34:56'. In production, logs are often structured (JSON) and sent to a centralized service like Sentry or LogRocket.

Metrics

Metrics are aggregated numerical data over time, such as latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99), error rates, or request counts. They are ideal for dashboards and alerting. Tools like Datadog or Prometheus can collect metrics from the browser via the Performance API or custom instrumentation.

Traces

Traces follow a single request as it travels through different services (e.g., frontend -> API -> database). In a frontend context, a trace might show the time spent in JavaScript execution, network requests, and rendering. Distributed tracing helps identify bottlenecks. OpenTelemetry is a common standard for instrumenting traces.

Production Considerations

In production, you must balance detail with performance. Sampling (e.g., logging only 1% of events) reduces overhead. Privacy is critical—never log sensitive user data. Correlating logs, metrics, and traces via a common request ID enables powerful debugging. A common pitfall is over-instrumentation, which can degrade performance and increase costs.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Action] --> B[Frontend App] B --> C[Logs] B --> D[Metrics] B --> E[Traces] C --> F[Centralized Logging] D --> G[Dashboard & Alert] E --> H[Distributed Tracing] F --> I[Debug Session] G --> J[Monitor Health] H --> K[Find Bottlenecks]"}]

[{"question":"Why should you alert on p95/p99 latency instead of average latency?","expectedPoints":["Average latency hides tail latency for a small percentage of users.","p95/p99 reflect the worst-case user experience.","Tail latency often affects power users with more data.","Alerting on average can miss real incidents.","p95/p99 thresholds are more actionable for debugging."],"followUps":["How would you set an initial p99 latency threshold for a new service?","What are the trade-offs of using p99 vs p95 for alerting?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're timing how long it takes kids to finish a race. The average time might look good, but if one kid takes forever, that kid is having a terrible time. p95/p99 are like looking at the slowest kids to make sure no one is left suffering.","detailedExplanation":"

Alerting on average latency is a common pitfall because averages can mask severe performance issues for a subset of users. For example, if 99% of requests complete in 200ms but 1% take 5 seconds, the average might still be ~250ms — appearing healthy while 1% of users (often your most data-heavy or important users) experience a terrible slowdown.

Why p95/p99 Matter

Percentiles like p95 (95th percentile) and p99 (99th percentile) explicitly capture the tail latency — the worst experiences. Alerting on these ensures you detect degradation that affects real users, not just the aggregate. In production, tail latency often correlates with complex queries, large payloads, or resource contention.

How to Use Them

  • Set actionable thresholds on p95/p99 (e.g., p99 latency > 2s for 5 minutes).
  • Combine with error rate alerts to avoid false positives from isolated spikes.
  • Use dynamic baselines to adapt to traffic patterns.

Common Pitfall

Alerting on p99 alone can be too noisy if the service has occasional long-tail requests due to garbage collection or network jitter. Pair p99 with a minimum request count or use p95 for a more stable signal.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Average Latency] -->|Hides| B[Tail Latency] B --> C[Affects 1% Users] C --> D[Power Users Suffer] A --> E[Looks Healthy] E --> F[Miss Real Incidents] G[p95/p99] -->|Reveals| B G --> H[Actionable Alerts]"}]

[{"question":"How would you optimize images to improve LCP on a content-heavy page?","expectedPoints":["Use modern formats like WebP or AVIF","Implement lazy loading for below-fold images","Serve responsive images via srcset and sizes","Use a CDN with image transformation capabilities","Consider preloading critical images"],"followUps":["How do you decide which images to preload?","What tradeoffs exist between WebP and AVIF?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big toy box. If you put all toys on top, it's hard to find the one you want quickly. Similarly, images on a webpage can slow it down. We can make images smaller (like using a smaller toy box), load only the ones you see first (like taking out toys one by one), and use special formats that keep quality but take less space.","detailedExplanation":"

Images often account for over 50% of a page's weight, making them the most impactful resource to optimize for LCP (Largest Contentful Paint). A senior frontend engineer should approach this systematically.

Key Strategies

  • Modern Formats: Use WebP or AVIF which offer 25-35% better compression than JPEG/PNG without quality loss. Serve them via <picture> with fallbacks.
  • Responsive Images: Use srcset and sizes attributes to serve the exact resolution needed for the viewport, avoiding oversized downloads.
  • Lazy Loading: Apply loading="lazy" to below-the-fold images so they load only when near the viewport. However, critical above-fold images must load eagerly.
  • CDN with Transformations: Use a service like Cloudinary or Imgix to dynamically resize, compress, and convert images on the fly based on device and network conditions.
  • Preloading: For the hero image (likely LCP candidate), add <link rel="preload" as="image" href="..."> to prioritize its download.

Production Considerations

In real-world apps, you must balance quality and performance. A common pitfall is over-optimizing by using aggressive compression that introduces visual artifacts. Always test with tools like Lighthouse and use fetchpriority="high" for the LCP element.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Image Request] --> B{Device & Viewport} B --> C[CDN] C --> D[Resize & Convert] D --> E[WebP/AVIF] E --> F[Lazy Load?] F -->|Yes| G[Load when near] F -->|No| H[Preload if critical] G --> I[Render] H --> I"}]
qgen_v3:5f1294f78dbacd0f:
[{"question":"How would you choose between AVIF, WebP, SVG, and JPEG/PNG for images in a production web app?","expectedPoints":["AVIF offers best compression but limited browser support; use as primary with fallback.","WebP is widely supported, ~30% smaller than JPEG/PNG; good fallback for AVIF.","SVG is ideal for vector graphics (icons, logos) due to scalability and CSS styling.","JPEG/PNG as last resort; PNG for transparency, JPEG for photos without transparency.","Use element with multiple sources for progressive enhancement."],"followUps":["How would you implement fallback logic in HTML/CSS?","What performance metrics would you track to validate your choice?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're packing a lunchbox. AVIF is like a vacuum-sealed bag that makes food tiny but not all lunchboxes can open it. WebP is a smaller ziplock bag that most lunchboxes can open. SVG is like a magic sticker that never gets blurry. JPEG/PNG are the regular containers everyone can use. You pack the best one first, but keep a backup so no one is left hungry.","detailedExplanation":"

Choosing the right image format is a critical performance and UX decision. The goal is to deliver the smallest possible file while maintaining visual quality and ensuring broad browser support.

Format Characteristics

  • AVIF: Offers the best compression (often 50% smaller than JPEG) with modern features like HDR and transparency. However, support is still growing (Chrome, Firefox, Safari 16.4+). Use as the primary format with fallbacks.
  • WebP: Widely supported (~97% of browsers), ~30% smaller than JPEG/PNG. Good fallback for AVIF. Supports lossy, lossless, and transparency.
  • SVG: Vector format, resolution-independent, infinitely scalable. Perfect for icons, logos, and illustrations. Can be styled with CSS and animated. File size is tiny for simple shapes.
  • JPEG/PNG: Universal fallback. JPEG for photos (no transparency), PNG for images needing transparency (e.g., logos with transparent backgrounds).

Implementation Strategy

Use the <picture> element to serve multiple formats: <picture><source srcset='image.avif' type='image/avif'><source srcset='image.webp' type='image/webp'><img src='image.jpg' alt='...'></picture>. The browser picks the first supported format. For SVGs, use <img src='icon.svg'> or inline SVG for styling.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on AVIF without fallbacks can break images in older browsers. Always provide WebP and JPEG/PNG fallbacks. Also, avoid using JPEG for images with text or sharp edges due to compression artifacts.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Image Request] --> B{Format Support?} B -->|AVIF| C[AVIF (Best compression)] B -->|WebP| D[WebP (~30% smaller)] B -->|SVG| E[SVG (Vector, scalable)] B -->|JPEG/PNG| F[JPEG/PNG (Fallback)] C --> G[Deliver] D --> G E --> G F --> G"}]
qgen_v3:80d36c16031a08d3:
[{"question":"How would you implement responsive images with lazy loading for a hero banner that is the LCP element?","expectedPoints":["Use with multiple elements for modern formats (AVIF, WebP).","Set explicit width and height (or aspect-ratio) to prevent CLS.","Do NOT use loading='lazy' on LCP images; use fetchpriority='high' and preload.","Provide a fallback with src, width, height, and alt.","Use decoding='async' for non-critical images, but not for LCP."],"followUps":["How would you preload the hero image to improve LCP?","What happens if you omit width and height on an image?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big picture on a website. You want the browser to pick the best version (like a smaller file) and only load it when needed. But if that picture is the first thing people see, you must load it immediately, not lazily. Also, you must tell the browser how big the picture is so the page doesn't jump around while loading.","detailedExplanation":"

Responsive images and lazy loading are key techniques to optimize web performance. The <picture> element allows the browser to choose the best image source based on format support and viewport size. For example, you can provide AVIF and WebP sources with a JPEG fallback. This reduces file size and improves load time.

Why explicit dimensions matter

Always set width and height (or use aspect-ratio) on the <img> element. This reserves space in the layout before the image loads, preventing layout shifts (CLS). Without dimensions, the browser doesn't know the image's aspect ratio, causing content to jump when the image finally loads — a common CLS culprit.

Lazy loading and LCP

loading='lazy' defers image loading until it's close to the viewport. This is great for below-the-fold images but harmful for LCP images (the largest contentful paint). Lazy-loading the hero image delays its load, worsening LCP. Instead, use loading='eager' (default) and fetchpriority='high' to prioritize it. You can also <link rel='preload'> the hero image for early discovery.

Common pitfall

A frequent mistake is applying loading='lazy' to all images, including the hero. This degrades LCP and user experience. Always evaluate which images are above the fold and treat them differently.

[{"question":"How would you design a service worker to implement an offline-first strategy for a news app?","expectedPoints":["Register service worker and handle install/activate events","Cache static assets during install and dynamic content via fetch event","Implement cache-first or network-first strategy based on resource type","Handle stale-while-revalidate for news articles","Graceful fallback when offline (e.g., show cached index page)"],"followUps":["How do you update the service worker and notify the user of new content?","What caching strategies would you use for images vs. API responses?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine a waiter who remembers your favorite orders even when the restaurant's kitchen is closed. A service worker is like that waiter—it stores parts of a website so you can still see them when you're offline. It helps the app load faster next time and can send you notifications even when the app isn't open.","detailedExplanation":"

A service worker is a script that runs in the background, separate from the web page, acting as a network proxy. It enables features like offline support, background sync, and push notifications. For an offline-first news app, the service worker should be registered and then handle the install, activate, and fetch events.

Installation and Activation

During the install event, pre-cache static assets (HTML, CSS, JS, logos) using the Cache API. After installation, the activate event is used to clean up old caches and take control of all clients.

Fetch Event Strategies

In the fetch event, implement different caching strategies:

  • Cache-first for static assets: serve from cache, falling back to network.
  • Network-first for dynamic content like news articles: try network first, fall back to cache if offline.
  • Stale-while-revalidate for frequently updated content: serve cached version immediately, then update cache from network in the background.

Why It Matters in Production

An offline-first approach improves user experience by providing instant loading and resilience to network failures. It reduces server load and bandwidth usage. However, a common pitfall is cache bloat—caching too much data without eviction policies, leading to storage quota issues. Always set cache size limits and version caches to allow updates.

[{"question":"How does a Service Worker intercept network requests and decide whether to serve from cache or fetch from the network?","expectedPoints":["Service Worker acts as a programmable proxy between the app and the network.","It intercepts fetch events using the 'fetch' event listener.","It can respond with cached resources from Cache Storage or forward to the network.","The decision logic (cache-first, network-first, stale-while-revalidate) is implemented in the fetch handler.","Service Worker only works on HTTPS or localhost for security reasons."],"followUps":["What happens if the Service Worker is not installed yet?","How would you implement a cache-first strategy in the fetch event?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a helper (Service Worker) that stands between you and the internet. When you ask for a toy (a webpage), the helper checks if you already have it in your toy box (cache). If yes, they give it to you instantly. If not, they go to the store (network) to get it. This makes things faster and works even if the store is closed (offline).","detailedExplanation":"

A Service Worker is a script that runs in the background, separate from the web page, and acts as a programmable proxy between the application and the network. It enables features like offline support, background sync, and push notifications.

How It Works

When a page makes a network request (e.g., fetch()), the browser fires a fetch event in the Service Worker. The Service Worker can intercept this event using self.addEventListener('fetch', handler). Inside the handler, the developer decides how to respond:

  • Cache-first: Check the Cache Storage first; if found, return the cached response. Otherwise, fetch from the network and optionally cache the response for future.
  • Network-first: Try the network first; if it fails (offline), fall back to the cache.
  • Stale-while-revalidate: Serve from cache immediately, then fetch from network in the background to update the cache for next time.

Why It Matters in Production

Service Workers are critical for Progressive Web Apps (PWAs). They reduce load times, save bandwidth, and provide a reliable experience even with flaky connections. However, they require careful handling of cache invalidation and versioning to avoid serving stale content.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is not handling the Service Worker lifecycle. For example, if the Service Worker is updated but the old one is still controlling pages, users may see inconsistent behavior. Developers must use self.skipWaiting() and clients.claim() to activate the new worker immediately.

[{"question":"Which caching strategy would you choose for a news feed that must always show fresh content but still work offline?","expectedPoints":["Network First strategy prioritizes network request","Falls back to cache when offline or network fails","Cache First would serve stale content","Stale-While-Revalidate may show outdated data briefly"],"followUps":["How would you implement this strategy in a Service Worker?","What happens if the network request fails but there is no cache?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a favorite comic book that gets a new page every day. If you always check the new page first (network first), you get the latest story. But if the internet is down, you can still read yesterday's page (cache). That way you always see the newest when possible, but never see a blank page.","detailedExplanation":"

Network First is the caching strategy where the Service Worker attempts to fetch the resource from the network first. Only if the network request fails (e.g., offline, timeout, server error) does it fall back to the cached response. This ensures the user always gets the most up-to-date content when connectivity is available, while still providing a fallback when offline.

How it works

In the Service Worker's fetch event, you first try fetch(event.request). If it succeeds, you update the cache with the new response and return it. If it fails, you look up the cache and return the cached response. The code typically uses event.respondWith with a promise that chains a network fetch and a cache fallback.

Why it matters in production

For dynamic content like news feeds, social media timelines, or stock prices, freshness is critical. Network First provides the best user experience: fast updates when online, and graceful degradation when offline. It avoids showing stale data that could mislead users.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is not handling the case where both network and cache fail. If the network fails and there is no cached response, the request will reject. Always provide a fallback response (e.g., a generic offline page) or catch the error gracefully. Also, be careful with cache invalidation: if you always update the cache on successful network fetch, the cache can grow large; implement a cache size limit or versioning.

[{"question":"How would you design a frontend architecture to allow hundreds of developers to work in parallel without merge conflicts or blocking each other?","expectedPoints":["Suggests micro-frontends or module federation to decouple teams.","Discusses clear ownership boundaries and independent deployment.","Mentions shared design system and component library for consistency.","Addresses versioning, dependency management, and integration testing.","Considers communication patterns between teams (e.g., events, APIs)."],"followUps":["How would you handle shared state or cross-team features?","What are the trade-offs of micro-frontends vs. monorepo?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine a big school project where many kids work on different parts of a poster. If everyone draws on the same paper, they might bump elbows and mess up each other's work. So you give each kid their own small paper, and later you glue them together. That way, everyone can work without fighting over space.","detailedExplanation":"

When a frontend codebase grows to hundreds of developers, the main challenge shifts from individual coding skills to organizational scalability. The goal is to enable many teams to work in parallel without stepping on each other's toes.

Key Architectural Approaches

  • Micro-frontends: Split the application into independent, deployable units owned by separate teams. Each micro-frontend can be built with its own tech stack and released independently, reducing coordination overhead.
  • Module Federation: A Webpack 5 feature that allows loading remote modules at runtime. Teams can share components or libraries dynamically without version lock-in.
  • Monorepo with strict ownership: Use a monorepo (e.g., Nx, Turborepo) with clear boundaries via CODEOWNERS and dependency graphs. This enforces that teams only modify their own packages.

How It Works in Practice

Teams define contracts (APIs, events, shared types) between their micro-frontends. A shared design system ensures visual consistency. Integration testing is done via contract tests or end-to-end tests on the composed app. Deployment pipelines are per-team, so a bug in one team's code doesn't block others.

Why It Matters in Production

Without such architecture, a large team suffers from merge conflicts, slow CI pipelines, and blocked releases. Micro-frontends or well-structured monorepos allow independent velocity and fault isolation.

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering: Splitting into too many micro-frontends can lead to duplicated code and performance overhead from loading many bundles. Always start with a monorepo and only split when the team size truly demands it.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Team A] --> B[Micro-frontend A] C[Team B] --> D[Micro-frontend B] E[Team C] --> F[Micro-frontend C] B --> G[Shell App] D --> G F --> G G --> H[User]"}]

[{"question":"Your team is growing and considering moving from a polyrepo to a monorepo. What are the key trade-offs you would evaluate, and how would you mitigate the main risks?","expectedPoints":["Identifies code sharing and atomic changes as key benefits of monorepo.","Acknowledges CI/CD complexity and need for tools like Nx or Turborepo.","Discusses team boundaries and the importance of CODEOWNERS or ownership policies.","Mentions potential build performance issues and incremental build strategies.","Considers migration cost and developer onboarding impact.","Proposes concrete mitigation for each risk (e.g., tooling, ownership, build caching)."],"followUps":["How would you handle a situation where two teams have conflicting dependencies in a monorepo?","What metrics would you use to decide if the migration is successful?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friends each have your own toy box (polyrepo). Sometimes you want to share a toy, but you have to walk to each other's house. A monorepo is like one big toy box where everyone can grab toys easily, but you need a smart system to make sure nobody takes the same toy at the same time and that you only clean up the toys you played with.","detailedExplanation":"

When evaluating a move from polyrepo to monorepo, the core trade-offs revolve around code sharing, change coordination, CI/CD complexity, and team autonomy.

Benefits of Monorepo

  • Easier code sharing: All code lives in one repository, so sharing libraries is trivial and updates are atomic across the entire codebase.
  • Atomic changes: A single pull request can update both a library and all its consumers, reducing versioning headaches and ensuring consistency.
  • Unified tooling: Linting, formatting, and testing can be standardized across all projects.

Risks and Mitigations

  • Build performance: Without smart tooling, building the entire repo can be slow. Mitigate by using Nx, Turborepo, or Bazel which support incremental builds and caching.
  • CI/CD complexity: A monorepo requires a CI system that can detect which projects changed and only run relevant tests/deployments. Use affected project detection (e.g., nx affected:test).
  • Team boundaries: Without clear ownership, teams may step on each other's toes. Implement CODEOWNERS files and branch protection rules to enforce review by the right team.
  • Dependency conflicts: Shared dependencies must be compatible. Use a single version policy or tools like Yarn Workspaces with resolutions to manage versions.
  • Migration cost: Moving from polyrepo to monorepo is a significant effort. Start with a gradual migration: move one service or library at a time, and keep the old repos until the migration is stable.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is to assume a monorepo automatically solves all coordination problems. Without proper tooling and ownership policies, it can lead to build times exploding and teams stepping on each other. Always invest in the right tooling and governance from day one.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Polyrepo] -->|Growing team| B{Monorepo?} B --> C[Code sharing easy] B --> D[Atomic changes] B --> E[CI complexity] B --> F[Team boundaries] C --> G[Nx/Turborepo] D --> H[Single PR] E --> I[Affected detection] F --> J[CODEOWNERS]"}]

[{"question":"What is the primary reason to adopt micro-frontends, and what are the main trade-offs?","expectedPoints":["Primary reason: enable independent development and deployment across multiple teams.","Trade-offs include increased complexity in shared dependencies, UI consistency, and performance.","Should only be considered when teams are blocked by each other's deployment cycles.","Prefer a modular monolith with clear boundaries as a simpler starting point.","Micro-frontends solve organizational problems, not technical ones."],"followUps":["How would you decide between a modular monolith and micro-frontends for a new project?","What strategies can mitigate the performance impact of micro-frontends?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friends are building a giant Lego castle. If you all work on the same pile of bricks, you might bump into each other. Micro-frontends let each friend build their own tower separately, so they don't get in each other's way. But then you have to make sure the towers fit together nicely, which can be tricky and sometimes makes the castle slower to build.","detailedExplanation":"

Micro-frontends are an architectural pattern where a frontend application is decomposed into smaller, independent applications that can be developed, tested, and deployed by separate teams. The primary motivation is organizational: enabling multiple teams to work and ship independently without blocking each other. This is especially valuable in large organizations where coordination overhead becomes a bottleneck.

When to Use

Micro-frontends are not a performance optimization or a way to reduce bundle size. In fact, they often increase complexity and can degrade performance due to loading multiple runtimes, duplicated dependencies, and communication overhead. They should only be considered when there is clear evidence that teams are blocked by shared deployment cycles or codebase conflicts. A modular monolith with well-defined module boundaries is usually a better starting point.

Trade-offs

  • Shared dependencies: Managing common libraries (e.g., React, UI components) across micro-frontends is challenging and can lead to version conflicts or duplication.
  • UI consistency: Ensuring a uniform look and feel requires a shared design system and strict governance.
  • Performance: Each micro-frontend may load its own framework instance, increasing initial load time and memory usage.
  • Operational complexity: Requires sophisticated orchestration (e.g., module federation, iframes) and deployment pipelines.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is adopting micro-frontends prematurely because they sound modern or scalable. This adds unnecessary complexity without solving a real organizational problem. Always start simple and only split when the pain of coordination outweighs the cost of splitting.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Monolith] -->|Team grows| B[Modular Monolith] B -->|Teams blocked| C[Micro-frontends] C --> D[Independent Deploy] C --> E[Shared Dependencies] C --> F[Performance Overhead] D --> G[Team Autonomy] E --> H[Version Conflicts] F --> I[Slower Load]"}]

[{"question":"Design a real-time chat application. How would you clarify requirements before starting the design?","expectedPoints":["Identify functional requirements: 1-1 and group messaging, message history, delivery/read receipts, typing indicator, online status, image sharing.","Identify non-functional requirements: low latency (<100ms perceived), offline resilience, smooth scrolling with thousands of messages, no message loss.","Prioritize requirements: distinguish core vs. nice-to-have features.","Clarify scale: number of users, messages per day, group size limits.","Define constraints: supported platforms, data retention policies, security needs."],"followUps":["How would you prioritize these requirements if you had to ship a minimal viable product?","What trade-offs would you consider between latency and reliability?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a walkie-talkie app for you and your friends. Before you start, you need to decide what it should do: send messages to one friend or a group, show if a message was delivered or read, and let you send pictures. You also want it to be fast, work even when the internet is slow, and not lose any messages. This is like making a list of all the features and how well they need to work before you build it.","detailedExplanation":"

In a frontend system design interview, clarifying requirements is the first and most critical step. For a real-time chat application, you must distinguish between functional and non-functional requirements.

Functional Requirements

  • 1-1 and group messaging: Users can send and receive text messages to individuals or groups.
  • Message history: Past messages are stored and retrievable.
  • Delivery/read receipts: Show if a message was delivered (single check) or read (double check).
  • Typing indicator: Show when another user is typing.
  • Online status: Display whether a user is online or offline.
  • Image sharing: Users can send images.

Non-Functional Requirements

  • Low latency: Perceived delay under 100ms for message delivery.
  • Offline resilience: App should work gracefully with intermittent connectivity (e.g., queue messages locally).
  • Smooth scrolling: Efficient rendering of thousands of messages (virtualization).
  • No message loss: Guarantee delivery via retries and acknowledgments.

Why It Matters in Production

Without clear requirements, you risk building a system that doesn't meet user expectations. For example, ignoring offline support leads to lost messages and poor user experience. Clarifying scale (e.g., 10K vs 10M users) drastically changes architecture choices like polling vs WebSockets.

Common Pitfall

Jumping into technical details (e.g., WebSocket vs SSE) before agreeing on requirements. Always start with what before how.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Clarify Requirements] --> B[Functional] A --> C[Non-Functional] B --> D[1-1 & Group Chat] B --> E[History & Receipts] B --> F[Typing & Online] B --> G[Image Sharing] C --> H[Low Latency] C --> I[Offline Resilience] C --> J[Smooth Scrolling] C --> K[No Message Loss]"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a chat client architecture that supports offline message queuing and real-time synchronization?","expectedPoints":["Separates UI, state, WebSocket, queue, and persistence layers","Uses IndexedDB for offline message storage","Implements local queue for pending messages with retry logic","Handles reconnection and message ordering after reconnect"],"followUps":["How would you handle message deduplication after reconnect?","What happens if the user sends a message while offline and then goes online?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a chat app like WhatsApp. When you send a message, it first goes into a 'waiting room' (local queue) and gets saved in a notebook (IndexedDB). If you're offline, the message stays in the waiting room until you're back online, then it gets sent. The app also has a special phone line (WebSocket) for instant messages, and a big board (state store) that keeps all messages organized by ID so everything stays in order.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a robust chat client architecture that works reliably under poor network conditions. A senior engineer must consider offline resilience, real-time updates, and data consistency.

Core Layers

  • UI Layer: Renders the chat view with virtualized message list and composer. It should be decoupled from data logic.
  • State Store: Normalizes messages by ID (e.g., using Redux or Zustand). This ensures a single source of truth and simplifies updates.
  • WebSocket Layer: Manages persistent connection, automatic reconnection with exponential backoff, and message framing.
  • Local Queue: Holds outgoing messages that haven't been acknowledged by the server. It persists to IndexedDB so messages survive page reloads.
  • IndexedDB: Stores full message history for offline access. Queries are indexed by conversation ID and timestamp.

How It Works

When the user sends a message, it's immediately added to the UI optimistically, saved to IndexedDB, and pushed to the local queue. The WebSocket layer attempts to send it. On success, the server acknowledges and the message is removed from the queue. On failure (offline), the message stays queued. Upon reconnect, the queue is flushed in order. The state store merges incoming server messages with local ones, deduplicating by a client-generated temporary ID.

Why It Matters in Production

Users expect chat apps to work seamlessly even with spotty connectivity. A naive implementation that blocks on send or loses messages on reload leads to poor UX and data loss. This architecture ensures at-least-once delivery and offline access.

Common Pitfall

Failing to handle message ordering after reconnect. If the server assigns final IDs, the client must map temporary IDs to server IDs and reorder messages correctly. Also, avoid sending duplicate messages by tracking sent status.

[{"question":"Why is normalized state preferred over nested state for managing chat messages in a Redux store?","expectedPoints":["Avoids data duplication and inconsistency","Enables O(1) lookup by ID","Simplifies updates (e.g., message status change)","Easier to handle relationships (e.g., messages per conversation)","Improves performance with large datasets"],"followUps":["How would you update the status of a single message in both normalized and nested structures?","What trade-offs exist when using normalized state?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big box of toys. If you keep all toys in one pile, finding a specific toy is hard and you might have duplicates. Normalized state is like having a list of all toys with a number, and then a separate list showing which toys belong to which shelf. This way, you can find any toy instantly and never have two copies of the same toy.","detailedExplanation":"

Normalized state is a pattern for organizing data in a Redux store to avoid duplication and enable efficient lookups. Instead of nesting objects (e.g., conversations containing arrays of message objects), you store entities in flat dictionaries keyed by ID.

How it works

In the example, messages.byId holds all message objects keyed by their ID. messages.byConversation maps conversation IDs to arrays of message IDs. This separation means a single message object exists in one place. To update a message's status, you only modify messages.byId[m1].status, and any reference to that message (e.g., in a conversation list) automatically reflects the change because it uses the ID.

Why it matters in production

In a chat app with thousands of messages, nested state leads to deep copies and complex reducers. Normalized state simplifies updates: changing a message status is a single Object.assign or spread operation. It also prevents data inconsistency—if a message appears in multiple conversations, you don't have to update every copy.

Common pitfall

Developers often forget to normalize relationships like byConversation. Without it, you lose the ability to quickly fetch all message IDs for a conversation. Always maintain lookup tables for every relationship.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR subgraph Normalized A[messages.byId] --> B[m1: {text, status}] C[messages.byConversation] --> D[c1: [m1, m2]] end subgraph Nested E[conversations] --> F[c1: {messages: [{...}]}] end B -.->|ID reference| D"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a real-time chat feature to handle offline messages and prevent duplicates?","expectedPoints":["Uses client-generated idempotency keys for deduplication","Implements local queue with IndexedDB for offline messages","Employs WebSocket with reconnection and exponential backoff","Syncs missed messages using timestamp or cursor after reconnect"],"followUps":["How do you handle message ordering when the user is scrolling through history?","What tradeoffs exist between WebSocket and SSE for this use case?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're sending a letter to a friend. You write a unique number on it so if you have to send it again, your friend knows it's the same letter and doesn't read it twice. If you lose internet, you keep the letter in a drawer (IndexedDB) and send it when you're back online. Also, you keep the phone line open (WebSocket) so you can talk instantly.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a robust real-time chat system that handles network failures and ensures message delivery without duplicates. The core challenge is maintaining exactly-once delivery semantics in an unreliable network.

Client-Generated Idempotency Keys

Each message is assigned a client-generated unique ID (e.g., UUID). When the client sends a message, it includes this ID. If the server receives the same ID again (due to retry), it ignores the duplicate. This prevents double-posting when the client retries after a timeout or reconnect.

Offline Queue & Local Persistence

Messages composed while offline are stored in a local queue backed by IndexedDB. When connectivity returns, the queue is flushed, sending each message with its original idempotency key. The server deduplicates based on the key.

WebSocket Reconnection & Sync

Use WebSocket for bidirectional communication. Implement exponential backoff for reconnection attempts. After reconnecting, the client sends the last known timestamp or cursor to request missed messages. The server responds with all messages after that point.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is relying solely on server-side timestamps for ordering. Network delays can cause out-of-order arrival. Instead, use server-assigned sequence numbers or Lamport clocks to order messages consistently, and buffer incoming messages until they can be placed in the correct order.

[{"question":"Design a typeahead/autocomplete component that handles API calls, debounce, caching, and keyboard accessibility. Walk through your approach.","expectedPoints":["Debounce input to limit API calls","Handle race conditions with request cancellation or timestamps","Implement client-side caching to reduce network requests","Support keyboard navigation (arrow keys, enter, escape) and ARIA attributes"],"followUps":["How would you handle a slow network where a later response arrives before an earlier one?","What caching strategy would you use and how would you invalidate the cache?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're typing a search and every letter tries to call a friend for suggestions. If you type fast, you'd annoy your friend with too many calls. So you wait a tiny bit after you stop typing before calling. Also, if you ask twice, you might get the second answer before the first, so you ignore old answers. You also remember past answers so you don't ask again for the same thing. And you let people use arrow keys to pick suggestions without a mouse.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a performant and accessible autocomplete component, a common UI pattern. The key challenges are debouncing input, race condition handling, caching, and keyboard accessibility.

Debouncing

Use a debounce function (e.g., 300ms) to delay the API call until the user stops typing. This prevents excessive network requests. Implement it with setTimeout and clearTimeout in a custom hook or utility.

Race Conditions

When multiple requests are in flight, a later response may arrive before an earlier one, causing stale data to overwrite fresh data. Solutions include: request cancellation (using AbortController in fetch or cancel() in axios) or timestamp comparison (discard responses older than the latest request).

Caching

Cache results by query string to avoid redundant API calls. Use a simple object or Map with an expiration time (e.g., 5 minutes). Invalidate cache when the user clears input or after a timeout. Consider memory limits.

Keyboard Accessibility

Implement aria-combobox, aria-activedescendant, and keyboard handlers for ArrowDown, ArrowUp, Enter (select), and Escape (close). Manage focus and highlight the active option visually.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to handle race conditions leads to flickering or incorrect suggestions. Always cancel or ignore outdated requests.

This design uses a timer to wait until you stop typing, then sends one request, and cancels any old requests so you only see the latest result.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a robust autocomplete/search component that handles real-world network issues. The key points are debouncing, request cancellation, and race condition prevention.

Debouncing

Use a debounce timer (e.g., 300ms) to avoid firing an API call on every keystroke. This reduces server load and improves performance. Implement it with setTimeout and clear it on each new input.

Request Cancellation with AbortController

When a new keystroke triggers a new API call, cancel the previous in-flight request using AbortController. This prevents the old response from overwriting the new one (race condition). In the fetch call, pass the signal from the controller. On cancellation, catch the AbortError and ignore it.

Race Condition Handling

Even with debounce, if network responses arrive out of order, a stale response can update the UI. By aborting the previous request, you ensure only the latest request's response is processed. Additionally, you can use a request ID or timestamp to ignore outdated responses.

State Management

Manage states like loading, results, error, and noResults. Show a loading indicator while fetching. Handle errors gracefully (e.g., network failure).

Accessibility

Ensure the dropdown is keyboard navigable, uses ARIA roles (combobox, listbox, option), and announces results to screen readers.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to clean up the debounce timer and AbortController on component unmount, leading to memory leaks and state updates on unmounted components. Always cancel in the cleanup function of useEffect.

[{"question":"In an autocomplete component, how do you handle race conditions where an earlier search request completes after a later one?","expectedPoints":["Identifies race condition: earlier response overwrites later response","Uses AbortController to cancel previous request on new input","Alternative: ignore stale responses by tracking latest query ID","Combines with debounce to reduce request frequency","Considers caching to avoid redundant requests"],"followUps":["What happens if you don't handle the race condition?","How would you implement a cache for autocomplete results?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're searching for a toy. You type 'car', then quickly change to 'truck'. The first search for 'car' might come back after 'truck' results, showing you car results instead of truck. To fix this, we cancel the first search when you start typing 'truck' so only the latest search matters.","detailedExplanation":"

Race conditions in autocomplete occur when the response from an earlier query arrives after the response from a later query, causing the UI to display outdated results. This is a common problem in asynchronous environments where network requests complete out of order.

How It Works

The typical solution is to cancel the previous request before initiating a new one. The AbortController API provides a standard way to abort fetch requests. In a React useEffect, you create a new AbortController for each query and call controller.abort() in the cleanup function. This ensures that when the query changes (or the component unmounts), any in-flight request is cancelled, and its .catch handler ignores the AbortError.

Why It Matters in Production

Without handling race conditions, users experience stale or incorrect results, leading to confusion and poor UX. In production autocomplete components, this is a critical detail that interviewers look for. Additionally, combining cancellation with debouncing (e.g., 300ms delay) reduces the number of requests, and caching (e.g., a Map of query to results) avoids redundant network calls for repeated queries.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is to only use debounce without cancellation. Debounce delays the request but does not prevent an earlier request from overwriting a later one if the earlier request takes longer. Always pair debounce with request cancellation or ignore stale responses by comparing a request ID.

Infinite-scroll image galleries are common in social media and stock photo sites. The core challenge is rendering a large number of images (10,000+) without performance degradation. The solution involves several key techniques.

Virtual Scrolling (Windowing)

Only render images that are within the viewport plus a small buffer. Use a library like react-window or implement a custom virtual list. This keeps the DOM small, ensuring 60fps scrolling and bounded memory usage.

Lazy Loading & Progressive Enhancement

Use IntersectionObserver to detect when an image enters the viewport. Load a tiny, blurred placeholder first (e.g., 20px wide), then replace with the full-resolution image. Memory Management

Unbind event listeners when components unmount. Recycle DOM nodes by reusing a fixed pool of image elements. Limit the cache of loaded images (e.g., keep only 2-3 screens worth). Use requestAnimationFrame for smooth animations and debounce scroll handlers.

Lightbox & Zoom

Implement a modal overlay for the lightbox. Use CSS transforms (scale) for zoom to leverage GPU acceleration. For left/right navigation, preload adjacent images. Ensure keyboard accessibility (arrow keys, Escape).

Common Pitfall

Not handling rapid scrolling: if the user scrolls quickly, many images may start loading simultaneously, causing network congestion. Use a priority queue to load images closest to the viewport first, and cancel requests for images that have scrolled out of view (using AbortController).
