Virtualization is a technique to render only the items currently visible in the viewport, dramatically reducing DOM size and memory usage. For a photo gallery with 10,000+ images, rendering all nodes would cause jank and high memory consumption. Instead, we maintain a fixed number of DOM nodes (e.g., viewport height / average row height + buffer) and recycle them as the user scrolls.

Handling Variable Aspect Ratios

Images often have different aspect ratios, which complicates grid layout. A common approach is to have the server return width and height for each image. The client can then calculate the displayed size using CSS aspect-ratio property or by setting explicit dimensions. For a justified layout (like Google Photos), you need to precompute row heights based on total aspect ratios and available width.

Lazy Loading & Placeholders

To avoid layout shift, use LQIP (Low-Quality Image Placeholder) or a solid color matching the image's average color. The placeholder occupies the exact space until the full image loads. Use loading="lazy" attribute or Intersection Observer to trigger loading only when the image approaches the viewport.

DOM Node Recycling

Instead of creating new DOM nodes for each new image, reuse nodes that scrolled out of view. This reduces garbage collection pressure and improves performance. Libraries like react-window or react-virtuoso implement this pattern.

Scroll Position Management

When prepending images (e.g., infinite scroll upwards), maintain scroll position by storing the offset of the first visible item and adjusting after new items are inserted. Use scrollTop and getBoundingClientRect to calculate the new position.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to account for image load time when calculating row heights. If images load after layout, they can cause reflow and jumpy scrolling. Always reserve space using placeholders with known dimensions.

[{"question":"How would you design a frontend data table that supports millions of rows and hundreds of columns with inline editing, sorting, and filtering?","expectedPoints":["Proposes 2D virtualization (both rows and columns)","Discusses server-side sorting/filtering for performance","Includes sticky headers and first column for usability","Handles inline editing with optimistic updates and debouncing","Considers memory management and re-render optimization"],"followUps":["How would you handle cell-level undo/redo for inline edits?","What tradeoffs exist between client-side and server-side filtering?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a giant spreadsheet with millions of rows and hundreds of columns. Designing an infinite data table for millions of rows and hundreds of columns requires a combination of virtualization, server-side processing, and optimized rendering.

Core Architecture

Use 2D virtualization (e.g., react-virtualized or react-window with custom grid) to render only the visible rows and columns. The table container has a fixed viewport, and as the user scrolls, the virtualizer calculates which cells to mount/unmount. This keeps DOM nodes minimal.

Data Fetching

Implement server-side sorting and filtering. The client sends sort/filter parameters to the API, which returns only the requested page of data. This avoids loading millions of rows into memory. Use debouncing for filter inputs to reduce API calls.

Sticky Elements

Use CSS position: sticky for the header row and first column. For 2D virtualization, you may need to synchronize scroll positions between the sticky elements and the main grid. Libraries like react-sticky can help.

Inline Editing

When a user edits a cell, apply optimistic updates to the local state immediately, then send a PATCH request to the server. Use a debounced save to avoid excessive requests. Handle conflicts by reverting the cell if the server rejects the update.

Performance Considerations

Use React.memo or shouldComponentUpdate to prevent re-rendering of unchanged cells. For large datasets, consider immutable data structures to enable fast equality checks. Avoid inline functions in render props.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is using client-side sorting/filtering on the entire dataset. This will freeze the browser for millions of rows. Always delegate heavy operations to the server.

This question tests your ability to design a high-performance data grid, a common requirement in enterprise applications like dashboards or spreadsheets. The core challenge is rendering a massive dataset (1M+ rows) without freezing the UI.

Virtualization

The key technique is windowed rendering (virtualization). Only the rows and columns visible in the viewport are rendered in the DOM. Libraries like react-window or react-virtualized can help, but you should understand the underlying mechanism: calculate the scroll position, determine which rows/columns are visible, and render only those. This reduces DOM nodes from millions to a few hundred.

Data Management

Store the data in a flat array. For sorting and filtering, avoid mutating the original array; instead, compute a sorted/filtered list of indices or a new array. Use memoization to avoid re-sorting on every render. For filtering, consider a trie or indexed map for fast lookups.

Sticky Header and First Column

Use CSS position: sticky for the header and first column. However, with virtualization, you may need to render the header and first column in a separate fixed container that scrolls synchronously with the main grid. This requires careful coordination of scroll events.

Performance Optimizations

Debounce sort/filter operations (e.g., 200ms delay). Use requestAnimationFrame for scroll handling to avoid jank. For column resizing, update only the affected column widths without re-rendering all rows. Use React.memo and useCallback to prevent unnecessary re-renders of row components.

Editing and Selection

Manage editing state per cell (e.g., which cell is in edit mode). For multi-row selection, maintain a Set of selected row IDs. Batch updates to avoid excessive state changes.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is to re-render the entire grid on every scroll or sort event. Always measure and optimize using browser DevTools (Performance tab). Also, avoid storing derived data (like sorted rows) in state; compute it with useMemo.

The row and column labels stay fixed so you always know where you are.","detailedExplanation":"

Two-way virtualization is a technique used to render large grids (e.g., spreadsheets, data tables) efficiently by only creating DOM elements for the rows and columns currently visible in the viewport. This is crucial for performance when dealing with millions of cells, as rendering all of them would cause severe lag and memory issues.

How It Works

The grid tracks the scroll position in both horizontal and vertical directions. Based on the scroll offset and the known dimensions of rows and columns (or estimated if dynamic), it calculates which rows and columns fall within the visible area. Only those cells are rendered. As the user scrolls, the grid re-calculates and updates the DOM, often reusing existing elements (DOM recycling) to minimize creation and destruction.

Sticky Headers and Columns

Sticky headers and columns remain fixed while the rest of the grid scrolls. This can be achieved using CSS position: sticky on the header row and first column, or by rendering them in separate overlays that are positioned absolutely and updated on scroll. The challenge is to keep them aligned with the virtualized content.

Why It Matters in Production

In applications like Google Sheets, trading platforms, or admin dashboards, users expect to scroll through thousands of rows without jank. Two-way virtualization makes this possible by keeping the DOM size small (e.g., 50 rows × 10 columns instead of 1,000,000 cells).

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is not accounting for variable row heights or column widths. If dimensions are not known in advance, you must measure them after rendering and update the scroll calculations accordingly, which can introduce complexity and performance overhead.

That's what server-side sorting does — it keeps the heavy work on the server and only sends you the small chunk you need.","detailedExplanation":"

For large datasets (e.g., 1 million rows), performing sorting and filtering on the client side is impractical because it would block the main thread, causing the UI to freeze. Instead, the server should handle these operations using database indexes, which are optimized for such tasks. The client then requests only a small window of data via pagination.

How It Works

The client sends a request like GET /rows?sort=price:desc&filter=active&offset=4000&limit=100 to fetch rows 4000–4099 sorted by price descending. For data that changes frequently, cursor-based pagination is preferred: GET /rows?after=cursor_abc&limit=100. This avoids issues with offset-based pagination when rows are inserted or deleted.

Why It Matters in Production

  • Performance: Server-side sorting leverages database indexes, making it fast even for millions of rows.
  • User Experience: The UI remains responsive because only a small subset of data is transferred and rendered.
  • Scalability: The server can handle many concurrent users without overwhelming the client.

Common Pitfall

Variable row heights complicate virtualization. If each row has a different height (e.g., due to text wrapping), the virtualized list must measure and cache actual heights or use estimation with adjustment. Fixed row heights simplify the implementation significantly.

[{"question":"How does Adaptive Bitrate Streaming (ABR) handle a sudden drop in network bandwidth during playback?","expectedPoints":["Video is divided into chunks at multiple quality levels","Player selects next chunk at a lower bitrate to avoid buffering","Playback continues seamlessly without interruption","Prioritizes continuity over maximum quality","Uses MSE to feed chunks into the video element"],"followUps":["What trade-offs does ABR make between quality and startup time?","How would you implement a simple ABR algorithm that reacts to bandwidth changes?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're watching a cartoon that comes in small pieces. If your internet gets slow, instead of stopping the cartoon, the player picks a smaller, lower-quality piece that downloads faster, so the cartoon keeps playing without freezing.","detailedExplanation":"

Adaptive Bitrate Streaming (ABR) is a technique used by modern video players (e.g., YouTube, Netflix) to deliver smooth playback over varying network conditions. The core idea is to split the video into small chunks (typically 2–10 seconds long), each encoded at multiple bitrates (quality levels). A manifest file (e.g., M3U8 for HLS, MPD for DASH) lists all available chunks and their qualities.

How It Works

When the network bandwidth drops, the ABR algorithm detects the slowdown (e.g., via download speed measurements or buffer occupancy). Instead of requesting the next chunk at the current high quality, it selects a lower bitrate chunk that can be downloaded faster. The player uses the Media Source Extensions (MSE) API to feed these chunks into the <video> element, enabling seamless transitions without reloading the entire video.

Why It Matters in Production

ABR is critical for user experience: rebuffering (video freezing) is far more harmful to engagement than temporary quality drops. A common production strategy is to start at a low bitrate to minimize startup time, then gradually increase quality as bandwidth is measured. This balances the trade-off between fast playback and high quality.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is reacting too aggressively to a single slow chunk, causing frequent quality oscillations (the “yo-yo” effect). Robust ABR algorithms use smoothing (e.g., averaging bandwidth over several chunks) and buffer-based logic (e.g., if the buffer is large, stay at high quality; if it shrinks, drop quality).

[{"question":"Design a collaborative text editor like Google Docs. How would you handle real-time synchronization and conflict resolution?","expectedPoints":["Identify functional requirements: concurrent editing, cursor awareness, real-time sync, offline support, version history.","Identify non-functional requirements: no data loss, no overwrite conflicts, low latency, eventual convergence.","Propose CRDT or OT for conflict resolution; explain trade-offs.","Discuss architecture: WebSocket for real-time, operational transformation or CRDT-based backend.","Address offline editing with local state and sync on reconnect."],"followUps":["How would you scale the system to millions of concurrent users?","What happens if a user's changes conflict with another's after reconnecting from offline?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are drawing on the same whiteboard at the same time. You both have markers, and you can see each other's drawings instantly. If you both try to draw in the same spot, the system needs to decide which mark stays. This is like a smart whiteboard that uses special rules to combine everyone's changes without losing anything, even if someone draws while offline and then comes back online.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a collaborative text editor like Google Docs requires handling real-time synchronization and conflict resolution among multiple users. The core challenge is ensuring that all users see the same final document state despite concurrent edits.

Functional Requirements

  • Multiple users can edit simultaneously.
  • Each user sees others' cursors and selections.
  • Changes propagate in real-time.
  • Offline editing with later sync.
  • Version history for rollback.

Non-Functional Requirements

  • No data loss: Every keystroke is preserved.
  • No overwrite conflicts: Edits are merged, not overwritten.
  • Low latency: Changes appear within milliseconds.
  • Convergence: All users eventually see the same document.

Conflict Resolution Strategies

Two main approaches: Operational Transformation (OT) and CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types). OT transforms operations against concurrent operations to maintain consistency. CRDTs use data structures that automatically merge without conflict. OT is used by Google Docs; CRDTs are simpler for peer-to-peer systems.

Architecture

Use WebSockets for real-time communication. Each client sends operations to a server that applies them and broadcasts to others. For offline support, clients store local operations and replay them on reconnect. Version history can be stored as a sequence of operations.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring network partitions and reconnection storms. When many users reconnect after offline, the server must handle a flood of operations without overwhelming the system. Use batching and throttling.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User1] -->|WebSocket| B[Server] C[User2] -->|WebSocket| B B -->|Broadcast| A B -->|Broadcast| C subgraph Conflict Resolution D[OT/CRDT] end B --> D D --> B"}]

If one colors a part and the other colors over it, the first kid's work might disappear. To fix this, we use special rules so that both kids' colors show up correctly, like merging their drawings together. This is what OT and CRDT do for text editing.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a real-time collaborative text editor requires solving the concurrent editing problem: when two users edit the same document simultaneously, their changes must be merged without losing data or causing inconsistencies. The two main approaches are Operational Transformation (OT) and Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types (CRDT).

Operational Transformation (OT)

OT works by representing each edit as an operation (e.g., insert 'X' at position 5). When operations from different users conflict, OT transforms one operation against the other so that they can be applied in any order and still produce the same final document. For example, if User A inserts at position 5 and User B deletes at position 3, the insert position may need to be adjusted after the delete. OT requires a central server or a well-defined ordering of operations.

CRDT

CRDTs are data structures designed so that operations can be applied in any order and still converge. For text, a common CRDT is the Logoot or RGA (Replicated Growable Array). Each character is assigned a unique identifier (e.g., a tuple of site ID and local counter). Merging is done by sorting these identifiers, ensuring all replicas eventually have the same order. CRDTs are more resilient to network partitions and do not require a central coordinator.

Why It Matters in Production

Real-time collaboration is critical for tools like Google Docs, Notion, and Figma. Users expect instant updates and no data loss. The choice between OT and CRDT affects latency, complexity, and offline support. OT is mature and used in Google Docs, but CRDTs are gaining popularity for their simplicity in peer-to-peer scenarios.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming that simple last-write-wins (LWW) semantics suffice. LWW can cause lost edits and user frustration. Proper conflict resolution must preserve all user intentions, which requires either OT or CRDT.

[{"question":"Why can't we use 'last write wins' for collaborative editing, and how do OT and CRDT solve this?","expectedPoints":["Last write wins loses concurrent edits from other users.","OT transforms operations to maintain consistency with a central server.","CRDT uses data structures that merge automatically without a central server.","Both ensure all clients converge to the same state without losing any edit.","Trade-offs: OT complex logic, CRDT higher metadata overhead."],"followUps":["How would you decide between OT and CRDT for a new collaborative app?","What are the implications of each approach for offline support?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you and a friend are coloring the same picture at the same time. If you both color the same spot, 'last write wins' means only the last person's color stays, and the other's disappears. OT and CRDT are smarter ways that make sure both colors appear together, like magic, so no one's work is lost.","detailedExplanation":"

In collaborative editing, multiple users edit the same document simultaneously. A naive approach like 'last write wins' would simply keep the most recent change, discarding any concurrent edits from other users. This is unacceptable for real-time collaboration because it loses data and breaks the user experience.

How OT and CRDT Solve This

Operational Transform (OT) works by transforming each operation (e.g., insert/delete) against concurrent operations from other users. It typically requires a central server to order operations and apply transformations so that all clients converge to the same state. OT is used by Google Docs.

CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) uses data structures that automatically merge concurrent changes without needing a central coordinator. Each client applies edits locally and syncs with peers; the data structure ensures that all replicas converge. CRDTs are used by Figma, Yjs, and Automerge.

Why It Matters in Production

Choosing between OT and CRDT affects architecture complexity, offline support, and scalability. OT's transform logic is complex and error-prone, while CRDTs are simpler in theory but require more metadata (e.g., unique IDs for each character). CRDTs naturally support peer-to-peer and offline-first scenarios, making them popular in modern apps.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming CRDTs are always simpler. In practice, implementing a correct CRDT for rich text (with formatting, images, etc.) is still challenging, and the metadata overhead can be significant. OT, while harder to implement correctly, can be more efficient for certain server-centric architectures.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User A edit] --> B{Conflict?} C[User B edit] --> B B -- Last Write Wins --> D[Lose one edit] B -- OT/CRDT --> E[Both edits kept] E --> F[Converged state]"}]

[{"question":"Design a real-time news feed for a social media platform like Facebook or Twitter.","expectedPoints":["Clarify requirements: user actions, feed types, real-time vs polling","High-level architecture: client, API gateway, feed service, database, cache","Data modeling: posts, users, follows, feed generation (push vs pull)","Real-time delivery: WebSockets, Server-Sent Events, or polling","Scalability: sharding, caching (Redis), CDN for media"],"followUps":["How would you handle feed personalization (ranking)?","What happens when a celebrity posts and millions of followers need updates?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a giant bulletin board where everyone posts notes. Your job is to design a system that shows each person a custom board with notes from their friends, updated instantly when someone posts. It's like having a personal assistant who runs around collecting new notes just for you.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a news feed is a classic system design problem that tests your ability to build a scalable, real-time, personalized content delivery system. The RADIO framework (Requirements, Architecture, Data, Integration, Optimization) helps structure the answer.

Requirements

Start by clarifying scope: users can post text/images/videos, follow others, and see a reverse-chronological feed of posts from followed users. Real-time updates are expected (e.g., new posts appear without refresh). Non-functional requirements: low latency (<500ms), high availability, eventual consistency, and ability to handle millions of users.

High-Level Architecture

Components: Client (mobile/web), API Gateway (routing, auth, rate limiting), Feed Service (generates and serves feeds), Post Service (handles post creation), Social Graph Service (manages follows), Database (SQL for users/posts, NoSQL for feeds), Cache (Redis for hot feeds), Message Queue (Kafka for async feed fan-out), CDN for media.

Data Model

  • Users: user_id, name, profile data
  • Posts: post_id, user_id, content, timestamp, media_urls
  • Follows: follower_id, followee_id
  • Feed: user_id, list of post_ids (precomputed or on-the-fly)

Feed Generation: Push vs Pull

Push (Fan-out on write): When a user posts, the system writes the post to all followers' feed caches. Good for celebrities? No—can cause write amplification. Pull (Fan-out on read): Generate feed on request by querying followed users' recent posts. Good for celebrities but slower for active users. Hybrid: Push for regular users, pull for celebrities (or use a threshold).

Real-Time Delivery

Use WebSockets for persistent connections to push new posts to active clients. Alternatively, Server-Sent Events (SSE) for one-way updates. For offline users, poll periodically.

Scalability & Optimization

Cache feeds in Redis with TTL. Shard databases by user_id. Use CDN for images/videos. For ranking, incorporate a machine learning model that scores posts based on relevance, recency, and engagement.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring the thundering herd problem when a celebrity posts: millions of followers' feeds need updating. Solution: use a message queue to batch updates, or switch to pull-based feed for that user.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR Client[Client App] -->|HTTP/WS| API[API Gateway] API --> FeedSvc[Feed Service] API --> PostSvc[Post Service] PostSvc --> Queue[Message Queue] Queue --> FanOut[Fan-out Worker] FanOut --> Cache[Redis Cache] FeedSvc --> Cache FeedSvc --> DB[(Database)] SocialGraph[Social Graph] --> FanOut"}]

[{"question":"Design a mobile-first social media feed with infinite scroll, offline resilience, and accessibility.","expectedPoints":["Covers functional requirements: feed, post creation, like/comment, media display, real-time updates.","Addresses non-functional requirements: fast load on 3G, smooth scroll, offline support, accessibility.","Proposes architecture: lazy loading, virtual scrolling, service worker for offline, ARIA roles.","Considers tradeoffs: infinite scroll vs pagination, image optimization, optimistic updates.","Includes error handling and loading states for poor network conditions."],"followUps":["How would you handle the 'new post available' indicator without disrupting scroll position?","What strategies would you use to ensure the feed is accessible to screen readers?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a never-ending photo album on your phone that works even when the internet is slow or goes out. You need to make sure pictures load quickly, scrolling feels smooth, and everyone—including people who use screen readers—can use it easily.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a complex, real-world frontend system. Start by clarifying functional requirements: infinite scroll feed, post creation, like/comment, media display (images/video), and real-time updates when new posts are available. Then discuss non-functional requirements: fast load on mobile + 3G, smooth scrolling, accessibility (a11y), and resilience to flaky networks.

Architecture Overview

Use a component-based architecture with a virtual scrolling library (e.g., react-window) to render only visible items, ensuring smooth performance. Implement lazy loading for images and videos with placeholders. For offline resilience, use a service worker to cache feed data and media, and implement optimistic updates for likes/comments so the UI feels instant even when the network is slow.

Key Design Decisions

  • Infinite scroll vs pagination: Infinite scroll improves engagement but can cause scroll position loss. Use a IntersectionObserver to trigger loading more items and maintain scroll anchor.
  • Real-time updates: Use WebSockets or Server-Sent Events for new post notifications. Show a subtle banner (e.g., 'New posts available') without disrupting the user's scroll position.
  • Accessibility: Add role='feed', aria-label, and proper focus management. Ensure keyboard navigation works for like/comment actions.
  • Network resilience: Cache feed responses in IndexedDB via service worker. Show skeleton loaders during fetch and retry on failure.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring scroll position restoration when new items are inserted above the current viewport. Always use a scroll anchor (e.g., store the first visible item's ID) to prevent jarring jumps.

[{"question":"In a social media feed, how would you design the data model to handle optimistic updates for a 'like' action while ensuring consistency between the local state and the server?","expectedPoints":["Normalize state to avoid duplication (e.g., separate posts and users entities).","Use a local update (e.g., increment likeCount, toggle likedByMe) immediately.","Handle rollback on server error or conflict.","Consider pagination and cursor for feed order.","Explain trade-offs between normalized and denormalized approaches."],"followUps":["How would you handle concurrent likes from multiple users?","What if the server returns a different like count due to race conditions?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a list of your favorite toys. When you like a toy, you want to see the 'like' count go up right away, even if the internet is slow. But you also need to make sure that if the internet fails, the count goes back to what it was before. This is like having a backup plan so your toy list stays correct.","detailedExplanation":"

When designing a data model for a social media feed with optimistic updates, the key is to normalize the state to avoid duplication and make updates easier. For example, store posts and users in separate entities with IDs, and maintain a feedOrder array for display order. This allows you to update a single post object when a like occurs, without affecting other parts of the state.

How it works

When a user clicks 'like', you immediately update the local state: increment likeCount and set likedByMe to true. Then you send the API request. If the request fails, you revert the local changes (rollback). This gives instant feedback to the user.

Why it matters in production

Optimistic updates improve perceived performance, making the app feel snappy. However, they introduce complexity: you must handle race conditions, server errors, and conflicts. For example, if two users like the same post simultaneously, the server might return a different count than expected. You need to reconcile the local state with the server response, possibly by using the server's authoritative count.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is not handling rollback properly. If the server fails, the local state becomes inconsistent. Always implement a mechanism to revert changes, and consider using a transaction-like pattern where you save the previous state before updating.

[{"question":"How would you implement a real-time feed update notification using SSE while maintaining cursor-based pagination?","expectedPoints":["Explain SSE connection to /feed/updates endpoint","Describe merging new posts into existing paginated feed","Handle cursor state and avoid duplicate posts","Discuss reconnection strategy on connection loss"],"followUps":["How do you prevent race conditions between SSE updates and manual pagination?","What if the server sends the same post twice?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're reading a book with bookmarks (cursors). Someone keeps adding new pages at the end. SSE is like a friend whispering 'new page added' while you read. You need to save your bookmark and later fetch only the new pages without losing your place.","detailedExplanation":"

Server-Sent Events (SSE) provide a unidirectional channel from server to client over HTTP. In this feed scenario, the client opens an SSE connection to /feed/updates and receives events like { newCount: 5 }. The client then decides when to fetch the new posts using the existing cursor-based pagination.

Implementation Steps

  • SSE Connection: Use EventSource to subscribe to the updates endpoint. On message, increment a 'new posts available' counter displayed to the user (e.g., '5 new posts').
  • Fetching New Posts: When the user clicks the notification, call GET /feed?cursor=<currentCursor>&limit=10. The server returns the latest posts and a new nextCursor. Prepend these posts to the feed list.
  • Cursor Management: Keep the latest cursor from the last paginated fetch. When merging, ensure no duplicates by comparing post IDs or using a Set. Update the cursor to the new nextCursor after fetching.
  • Reconnection: EventSource auto-reconnects on connection loss. On reconnect, the server may send a 'catch-up' event with the latest cursor or the client can re-fetch from the last known cursor.

Production Considerations

Use debouncing to avoid multiple rapid fetches if the user clicks the notification repeatedly. Handle race conditions where an SSE update arrives while a paginated fetch is in progress—queue the update and apply after the fetch completes. Memory management is crucial: limit the number of cached posts and implement virtual scrolling for long lists.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to close the EventSource when the component unmounts, causing memory leaks and unnecessary server load. Always clean up in useEffect return or componentWillUnmount.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR Client[Client] -->|SSE /feed/updates| Server Server -->|event: newCount| Client Client -->|GET /feed?cursor=C| Server Server -->|posts, nextCursor| Client Client -->|update feed list| UI[Feed UI] Client -->|store cursor| State[Cursor State]"}]

[{"question":"In a news feed, why should you show a 'X new posts' pill instead of automatically inserting new posts at the top?","expectedPoints":["Prevents layout shift (CLS) that disrupts reading position","Gives user control over when to view new content","Better user experience by avoiding involuntary scrolling","Does not reduce bandwidth; SSE can insert automatically"],"followUps":["How would you implement the pill using IntersectionObserver?","What if the user scrolls to the top – should we auto-insert then?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're reading a book and someone keeps shoving new pages in front of you, making you lose your spot. That's annoying. Instead, a little note says '3 new pages available' so you can choose when to look at them. That's the pill – it keeps your place and lets you decide.","detailedExplanation":"

In a news feed, automatically inserting new posts at the top causes Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS), a Core Web Vital metric. When new content pushes existing content down, the user loses their reading position, leading to frustration and accidental clicks. The pill pattern (e.g., '3 new posts') solves this by showing a non-intrusive notification that the user can tap when ready.

How it works

Use Server-Sent Events (SSE) or WebSockets to detect new posts. Instead of appending to the DOM, update a counter in a fixed pill element (e.g., at the top of the feed). When the user taps the pill, fetch and prepend the new posts smoothly, optionally scrolling to the top.

Why it matters in production

  • CLS prevention: The pill itself is a small, fixed element that doesn't shift layout. Only when the user acts do new posts appear, and you can use position: sticky or scroll anchoring to minimize shift.
  • User control: Users can finish reading the current content before seeing new items, improving engagement and satisfaction.
  • Performance: Avoids unnecessary DOM updates and reflows when the user is not ready.

Common pitfall

Some developers auto-insert new posts when the user is at the top of the feed. However, even then, a brief shift can occur if the user is mid-scroll. Always use the pill unless the user has explicitly scrolled to the top and no content is visible below – but even then, a pill is safer and more consistent.

[{"question":"Walk me through your step-by-step process for designing a frontend feature from scratch in an interview setting.","expectedPoints":["Clarify requirements and constraints first","Define component tree and data flow","Consider state management and API integration","Address performance, accessibility, and error handling","Discuss trade-offs and justify decisions"],"followUps":["How would you adjust your design if the feature needed to support offline mode?","What would you prioritize if you had only 30 minutes to design?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a new toy box. This is like designing a feature: you start by understanding what's needed, plan the parts, and make sure everything works smoothly.","detailedExplanation":"

In a frontend system design interview, the interviewer wants to see your structured thinking and ability to break down a complex problem. The process typically follows these phases:

1. Clarify Requirements

Start by asking questions to understand the feature's purpose, target users, and constraints. For example: “Is this a real-time dashboard or a static page? What devices should it support?” This prevents building the wrong solution.

2. Define Component Hierarchy

Sketch a component tree showing parent-child relationships. For instance, a search page might have SearchBar, ResultsList, and Pagination. Each component should have a single responsibility.

3. Plan Data Flow

Decide how data moves between components and the server. Will you use local state, Context API, or a library like Redux? Consider loading, empty, and error states for every API call.

4. Address Non-Functional Concerns

Discuss performance (lazy loading, memoization), accessibility (ARIA labels, keyboard navigation), and error handling (retry logic, fallback UI). These show senior-level awareness.

5. Trade-offs & Justification

Explain why you chose one approach over another. For example: “I chose local state over Redux because this feature is isolated and doesn't need global sharing.” This demonstrates practical experience.

Common pitfall: Jumping into code or UI details too early. Always start with requirements and architecture before diving into implementation.

This question tests your ability to architect a complex, real-time collaborative system under time constraints. The 60-minute sequence (5' requirements, 12' architecture, 8' data model, 8' interface, 12' optimization + Q&A) is a common framework.

Key Components

  • Real-time sync: Use WebSocket for bidirectional communication. Each client sends edits as they happen, and the server broadcasts to all other clients.
  • Conflict resolution: Choose between Operational Transformation (OT) (used by Google Docs) or CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types). OT transforms operations to maintain consistency; CRDT uses data structures that merge automatically. OT is more mature for text, CRDT simpler for decentralized systems.
  • State management: Use a library like Redux or Zustand to manage document state, with undo/redo stacks. Each edit is an action that gets sent to the server.
  • Performance: Implement virtual scrolling for large documents, debounced saves to reduce server load, and optimistic updates to make the UI feel instant.

Common Pitfall

Underestimating conflict resolution complexity. Many candidates propose a simple 'last write wins' strategy, which fails in real-time collaboration. You must discuss how to merge concurrent edits without losing data.

Production Considerations

  • Offline support: Use a local database (e.g., IndexedDB) to store pending edits, then sync when online.
  • Scalability: Consider using a message queue (e.g., Redis Pub/Sub) to handle many concurrent rooms.
  • Security: Validate operations server-side to prevent malicious edits.

[{"question":"How would you decide between using WebSockets and polling for real-time updates in a web application?","expectedPoints":["Mentions trade-offs: WebSockets offer low latency but higher complexity and server cost.","Considers use case: polling is simpler and sufficient for low-frequency updates.","Discusses scalability and resource usage.","Suggests measuring before optimizing.","Mention fallback strategies or hybrid approaches."],"followUps":["What metrics would you track to decide if polling is acceptable?","How would you handle connection loss with WebSockets?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to know when your friend posts a new drawing. Polling is like asking them every minute 'Did you draw something new?' – simple but wastes time if they draw rarely. WebSockets is like a phone call that stays open, so they tell you instantly when they draw – faster but harder to set up. You choose based on how often they draw and how much effort you want to spend.","detailedExplanation":"

This question probes your ability to balance real-time performance against implementation simplicity. WebSockets provide a persistent, full-duplex connection enabling instant updates with minimal overhead per message, ideal for applications like chat or live dashboards. However, they introduce complexity: managing connection state, handling reconnection, scaling with many concurrent connections, and requiring server-side support (e.g., WebSocket server, load balancer configuration).

When to choose polling

Polling (regular HTTP requests) is simpler to implement, works with any server, and is easier to debug. It's sufficient when updates are infrequent (e.g., every 30 seconds) or when real-time is not critical. The trade-off is higher latency and wasted bandwidth if polling too frequently. Stale-while-revalidate caching can help reduce server load.

Decision framework

  • Latency requirements: Sub-second updates? WebSockets. Acceptable 5+ second delay? Polling.
  • Update frequency: High frequency (many events per second) favors WebSockets to avoid HTTP overhead.
  • Server resources: WebSockets consume more memory per connection; polling uses more CPU/network if many clients.
  • Complexity budget: Small team or tight deadline? Polling is safer.

Common pitfall

Assuming WebSockets are always better. Many applications over-engineer with WebSockets when simple polling with proper caching would suffice. Always measure first – profile your actual update frequency and user tolerance for delay before committing to a solution.

[{"question":"How do you decide between using WebSocket and HTTP polling for real-time features in a frontend application?","expectedPoints":["Identify use cases requiring low latency bidirectional communication (chat, collab, trading).","Explain tradeoffs: WebSocket has lower overhead after connection, but more complex to manage reconnection and scaling.","Mention fallback strategies like long-polling or SSE for environments where WebSocket is blocked.","Discuss resource usage: persistent connections vs. repeated HTTP requests.","Consider team expertise and infrastructure support."],"followUps":["What would you do if WebSocket connections are blocked by a corporate firewall?","How would you handle reconnection and message ordering in a WebSocket-based chat app?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you're playing an online game with a friend. If you want to see their moves instantly, you need a direct line (WebSocket) that stays open. But if you only need to check for updates every few seconds, you can just call them on the phone (HTTP polling) – it's simpler but slower.","detailedExplanation":"

Choosing between WebSocket and HTTP polling is a classic tradeoff in frontend engineering, especially for real-time features. WebSocket provides a persistent, full-duplex communication channel over a single TCP connection. It's ideal for applications requiring low-latency, bidirectional data flow, such as live chat, collaborative editing, gaming, or financial trading platforms. Once established, WebSocket has minimal overhead per message, making it efficient for high-frequency updates.

HTTP polling (short or long polling) works over standard HTTP requests. Short polling sends requests at fixed intervals, while long polling holds the request open until new data is available. Polling is simpler to implement and works everywhere without special server support, but it introduces latency (up to the polling interval) and higher overhead due to repeated HTTP headers and connection setup.

Decision Factors

  • Latency requirements: If sub-second updates are critical, WebSocket is preferred. For updates every few seconds, polling may suffice.
  • Connection overhead: WebSocket reduces bandwidth after initial handshake, but requires managing reconnection, heartbeats, and scaling (e.g., sticky sessions or a pub/sub layer). Polling is stateless and easier to scale horizontally.
  • Network constraints: Some corporate firewalls or proxies block WebSocket. In such cases, fallback to long-polling or Server-Sent Events (SSE) is necessary.
  • Team expertise: WebSocket introduces complexity in error handling, reconnection logic, and message ordering. Polling is more straightforward to debug.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is over-engineering: using WebSocket for a feature that only needs occasional updates (e.g., a notification bell that checks every 30 seconds). This adds unnecessary complexity. Conversely, using polling for a real-time collaborative tool can lead to poor user experience and server load. Always profile your actual latency and throughput needs.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Real-time Feature] --> B{Latency Critical?} B -->|Yes| C[WebSocket] B -->|No| D[HTTP Polling] C --> E[Persistent Connection] C --> F[Low Overhead] D --> G[Simple Implementation] D --> H[Higher Latency]"}]

How would you handle conflict resolution and ensure low latency?","expectedPoints":["Use Operational Transformation (OT) or CRDT for conflict resolution","Client-server architecture with WebSocket for real-time sync","Optimistic UI updates with undo/redo support","Handle offline edits with local state and sync on reconnect","Consider scalability: sharding documents, load balancing"],"followUps":["What are the tradeoffs between OT and CRDT?","How would you implement cursor presence across users?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and a friend are coloring the same picture at the same time. To avoid messing up each other's colors, you need a rule book that decides whose color goes where. This is like how Google Docs lets many people edit together without losing changes.","detailedExplanation":"

Real-time collaborative document editing requires synchronizing edits from multiple users with low latency and no conflicts. The core challenge is conflict resolution, typically solved by Operational Transformation (OT) or CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types).

Architecture

  • Client-server model with WebSocket for bidirectional real-time communication.
  • Each client sends operations (insert/delete) to the server, which transforms them against concurrent operations and broadcasts to other clients.
  • Optimistic UI: apply changes locally immediately, then reconcile with server response.

Conflict Resolution

  • OT: transforms operations so they commute, preserving intent. Requires a central server for ordering.
  • CRDT: uses data structures that merge automatically without a central coordinator, better for peer-to-peer but more complex.

Production Considerations

  • Latency: use WebSocket with compression and batching.
  • Offline support: store pending operations locally, sync on reconnect.
  • Scalability: shard documents by ID, use load balancers for WebSocket connections.
  • Cursor presence: broadcast cursor positions as separate lightweight messages.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring undo/redo in a collaborative context. Undo must be aware of concurrent operations to avoid breaking other users' changes.

[{"question":"Design a real-time collaborative document editing system (like Google Docs). How would you balance consistency, availability, and partition tolerance?","expectedPoints":["Identifies CAP tradeoff: partition tolerance is mandatory, chooses CP or AP based on feature requirements.","Explains why strong consistency is needed for cursor sync and conflict resolution (CP).","Mentions eventual consistency for non-critical features like comments or version history (AP).","Discusses operational transformation or CRDT as consistency mechanisms.","Addresses scalability: horizontal scaling for WebSocket servers, sharding by document ID."],"followUps":["How would you handle network partitions between users in different regions?","What if a user has a poor connection—should they see stale data or nothing?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are drawing on the same digital whiteboard. If the internet goes down for a second, you have two choices: either stop drawing until the connection comes back (so you never see mismatched drawings), or keep drawing and fix the mess later. Google Docs chooses to stop drawing for important things like text changes, but lets you keep drawing for less important things like comments. This is the CAP tradeoff.","detailedExplanation":"

In a real-time collaborative document editor, the core challenge is the CAP theorem: you cannot have all three of Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance simultaneously. Since network partitions are inevitable, you must choose between CP (Consistency + Partition Tolerance) and AP (Availability + Partition Tolerance).

Why CP for core editing

For features like cursor synchronization and conflict resolution, strong consistency is critical. If two users see different versions of the same paragraph, they cannot collaborate effectively. Therefore, during a partition, the system should reject writes or pause editing to maintain a single truth. This is the CP approach, used by systems like Google Docs' operational transformation (OT) or CRDT-based editors.

Why AP for non-critical features

Features like comments, version history, or presence indicators can tolerate eventual consistency. Users can still see stale data or temporarily conflicting comments, and the system resolves them later. This improves availability and user experience during network issues.

Scalability and architecture

To scale horizontally, use WebSocket servers that handle real-time connections, sharded by document ID. Each document's state is managed by a single leader node to simplify consistency. For persistence, use a distributed database like Cassandra (AP) for metadata and a strongly consistent store like etcd (CP) for document locks.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is assuming all features need the same consistency level. Over-engineering for strong consistency everywhere hurts availability and latency. Instead, classify features by their consistency requirements and apply the appropriate CAP tradeoff per feature.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User A] -->|Edit| B[WebSocket Server] C[User B] -->|Edit| B B --> D{Partition?} D -->|Yes| E[CP: Block writes] D -->|Yes| F[AP: Allow stale reads] D -->|No| G[Strong Consistency] G --> H[Document State]"}]
qgen_v3:4b0f0c14d36a4c64:
[{"question":"How would you design a load balancing strategy for a real-time chat application that requires sticky sessions and low latency?","expectedPoints":["Use L4 load balancer for low latency","Use IP Hash algorithm for sticky sessions","Consider Least Connections for long-lived connections","Address LB as SPOF with active-passive pair","Mention rate limiting to prevent abuse"],"followUps":["How would you handle a server failure during an active chat session?","What rate limiting algorithm would you choose and why?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine a busy restaurant with many waiters. A load balancer is like a host who decides which waiter takes each order. For a chat app, we want the same waiter to handle the same customer (sticky session) and we want orders handled quickly (low latency). We also need a backup host in case the main one gets sick (SPOF).","detailedExplanation":"

Load balancing is critical for distributing traffic across servers to ensure availability and performance. For a real-time chat application, we need to balance low latency and sticky sessions (same server for a user's session).

L4 vs L7 Load Balancer

An L4 load balancer (e.g., AWS NLB, HAProxy TCP mode) operates at the transport layer, routing based on IP and TCP/UDP ports. It is faster and has less overhead, making it ideal for low-latency applications. An L7 load balancer (e.g., AWS ALB, Nginx) reads HTTP headers and can do content-based routing, but adds latency. For chat, L4 is preferred.

Load Balancing Algorithm

For sticky sessions, IP Hash ensures the same client IP always goes to the same server. However, for long-lived connections like chat, Least Connections is better because it sends new connections to the server with the fewest active connections, balancing load more evenly. A hybrid approach: use IP Hash for initial routing, then Least Connections for rebalancing if the server fails.

High Availability

The load balancer itself is a single point of failure (SPOF). Use an active-passive pair with a floating IP (e.g., keepalived/VRRP) or DNS-based load balancing with health checks.

Rate Limiting

To prevent abuse, implement rate limiting. Token Bucket allows bursts and smooths traffic, suitable for chat where users may send messages in bursts. Sliding Window Counter is more accurate but memory-intensive.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on IP Hash can cause uneven load if many users share the same IP (e.g., behind NAT). Combine with Least Connections or use a consistent hash ring.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client] --> B[L4 LB] B --> C[Server 1] B --> D[Server 2] B --> E[Server 3] subgraph Active-Passive F[Active LB] G[Passive LB] end F -->|floating IP| B G -.->|failover| F"}]

[{"question":"Design a database schema for a real-time chat application with 100 million users. Compare SQL vs NoSQL choices, and outline your sharding and replication strategy.","expectedPoints":["Choose NoSQL (e.g., Cassandra or MongoDB) for high write throughput and flexible message schema.","Use hash-based sharding on user_id or conversation_id for even distribution.","Implement master-slave replication for read scalability and failover.","Denormalize data (e.g., embed last message in conversation) to avoid joins.","Consider consistent hashing for dynamic shard addition."],"followUps":["How would you handle read-after-write consistency in a sharded NoSQL setup?","What isolation level do you need for message delivery guarantees?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a giant chat app like WhatsApp. You need to store messages for billions of users. SQL databases are like a strict filing cabinet—great for money stuff but slow to change. NoSQL is like a big box of sticky notes—fast and flexible. To handle all the messages, you split the data across many computers (sharding) and make copies (replication) so if one breaks, you don't lose anything.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a database for a real-time chat app with 100M users requires careful trade-offs. The primary workload is high write throughput (messages) and low-latency reads (conversation history).

SQL vs NoSQL Decision

Choose NoSQL (e.g., Cassandra or MongoDB) over SQL because:

  • Messages have flexible schema (attachments, reactions) – NoSQL is schema-less.
  • High write volume – NoSQL scales horizontally easily.
  • No complex joins – data is denormalized (e.g., embed last message in conversation document).
SQL (e.g., PostgreSQL) would be used for user accounts and payment where ACID is critical.

Sharding Strategy

Use hash-based sharding on conversation_id to distribute conversations evenly across shards. This avoids hotspots (range-based) and allows consistent hashing for adding shards with minimal data movement. Each shard is a separate database instance.

Replication

Implement master-slave replication per shard:

  • Master handles all writes (messages).
  • Slaves serve read queries (history).
  • If master fails, promote a slave (use tools like MHA or Orchestrator).
For read-after-write consistency, read from master for the user who just wrote, or use quorum reads in Cassandra.

Common Pitfall

Sharding too early. Start with indexing, caching (Redis), and read replicas before sharding. Most apps don't need sharding until 50M+ users.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Chat App] --> B[NoSQL Cluster] B --> C[Shard 1] B --> D[Shard 2] B --> E[Shard N] C --> F[Master] C --> G[Slave 1] C --> H[Slave 2] F --> I[Write Messages] G --> J[Read History] H --> K[Read History]"}]

[{"question":"Design a caching strategy for a high-traffic social media feed that shows the latest 20 posts per user. How would you handle cache stampede and cache avalanche?","expectedPoints":["Proposes cache-aside with TTL jitter to avoid avalanche","Uses mutex lock or early recompute for stampede prevention","Considers write-through or write-behind for feed updates","Mentions Redis Sorted Set for timeline ordering and pagination","Addresses cache penetration with null caching or Bloom filter"],"followUps":["How would you invalidate the feed cache when a user posts new content?","What if the feed is personalized? How does that affect caching?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a library where many people want the same popular book. Instead of everyone going to the basement to get it, you put a copy on the front desk (cache). But if the copy disappears and everyone rushes to the basement at once, it causes a mess. We add a random delay before fetching a new copy so not everyone goes at the same time. Also, if someone asks for a book that doesn't exist, we put a note on the front desk saying 'not here' so we don't keep checking the basement.","detailedExplanation":"

Caching is critical for reducing database load and improving read latency. For a social media feed, we need to serve the latest 20 posts per user quickly while handling high concurrency.

Strategy: Cache-Aside with Sorted Set

Use Redis Sorted Set (ZSET) to store post IDs with timestamps as scores. This allows efficient retrieval of the latest 20 posts via ZREVRANGE. The cache key could be feed:user:{userId}. On a cache miss, fetch from DB, populate the sorted set, and set a TTL.

Cache Stampede Prevention

When a key expires and multiple requests miss simultaneously, they all hit the DB. Use a mutex lock (e.g., SETNX) so only one request recomputes the cache; others wait or get stale data. Alternatively, use early recompute: if TTL is 60s, refresh when remaining TTL < 10s.

Cache Avalanche Prevention

Many keys expiring at the same time can spike DB. Apply TTL jitter: set TTL = base + random(0, 300) seconds to spread expiration times. Also, use a background refresh job to proactively update popular feeds before they expire.

Cache Penetration

Requests for non-existent user IDs bypass cache. Mitigate by caching null values with a short TTL or using a Bloom Filter to quickly reject invalid keys.

Write Strategy

When a user posts, use write-through to update both cache and DB synchronously for consistency, or write-behind for lower latency but risk of data loss. Invalidate the feed cache of the user's followers (or update it incrementally).

Common Pitfall

Ignoring cache invalidation for personalized feeds. If a user's feed is cached per user, any new post from a followed user must update many caches. Consider using a fan-out approach where the feed is precomputed and cached per user, and new posts are pushed to followers' caches.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Request] --> B{Cache Hit?} B -->|Yes| C[Return Feed] B -->|No| D[Acquire Mutex Lock] D --> E{Lock Acquired?} E -->|Yes| F[Read from DB] F --> G[Set Cache with Jitter TTL] G --> C E -->|No| H[Wait/Use Stale Data] H --> C"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a real-time event processing pipeline for a social media platform that tracks user clicks and must guarantee ordering per user while supporting replay for analytics?","expectedPoints":["Use Kafka as event streaming platform for durability and replay","Partition by user_id to maintain per-user ordering","Use at-least-once delivery with idempotent consumers","Separate consumer groups for real-time vs analytics","Consider retention period and compaction for replay"],"followUps":["How would you handle a consumer that falls behind and needs to catch up?","What if you need exactly-once semantics for financial transactions?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a giant notebook where you write down every click each person makes. You want to make sure that if you read the notebook later, you see the clicks in the right order for each person. Kafka is like that notebook, but it's shared by many people. You can give each person their own page (partition) so their clicks stay in order. And you can keep the notebook for a long time so you can read it again later.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your understanding of stream processing with Kafka in a real-world scenario. The key requirements are: real-time processing, per-user ordering, and replay capability for analytics.

Architecture Overview

Use Kafka as the event streaming backbone because it persists messages for a configurable retention period (e.g., 7 days) and supports multiple consumer groups reading independently. This satisfies both real-time and replay needs.

Partitioning for Ordering

To guarantee ordering per user, set the partition key to user_id. All events for a given user go to the same partition, where Kafka preserves order. Multiple partitions allow parallelism across users.

Delivery Semantics

Use at-least-once delivery combined with idempotent consumers. This is the most practical approach: the producer retries on failure, and the consumer deduplicates by checking a unique event ID or using a database upsert. Exactly-once is possible but adds complexity (Kafka transactions) and is rarely needed for click tracking.

Consumer Groups

Create separate consumer groups: one for real-time processing (e.g., update live dashboard) and one for analytics (e.g., batch job that replays from the beginning). Each group independently tracks its own offset.

Replay Strategy

For analytics, the consumer can reset its offset to the earliest available message (or a specific timestamp) and reprocess all events. This is possible because Kafka retains messages for a long time.

Common Pitfall

Assuming ordering across partitions. If you need global ordering, you must use a single partition, which limits throughput. Always design for per-key ordering.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR subgraph Producers A[User Clicks] end A -->|user_id as key| B[Kafka Topic: user-clicks] B --> C[Partition 0] B --> D[Partition 1] B --> E[Partition N] C --> F[Consumer Group: Real-time] D --> F E --> F C --> G[Consumer Group: Analytics] D --> G E --> G F --> H[Update Dashboard] G --> I[Batch Analytics Job]"}]

[{"question":"Compare REST, gRPC, and GraphQL for a real-time collaborative editing feature. Which would you choose and why?","expectedPoints":["Identifies WebSocket as the primary real-time transport.","Explains that REST/gRPC/GraphQL are API protocols, not real-time transports.","Notes that GraphQL subscriptions or gRPC streaming can work over WebSocket.","Considers browser compatibility, performance, and complexity.","Recommends WebSocket with a lightweight protocol (e.g., JSON) for low-latency sync."],"followUps":["How would you handle reconnection and conflict resolution in your chosen approach?","What are the trade-offs of using GraphQL subscriptions vs raw WebSocket for this use case?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and a friend are drawing on the same digital whiteboard. You need to see each other's changes instantly. REST is like sending a letter and waiting for a reply each time—too slow. gRPC and GraphQL are faster, but they still need a special connection to keep talking. The best way is to use a WebSocket, which is like a walkie-talkie that stays open so you can talk back and forth instantly.","detailedExplanation":"

For a real-time collaborative editing feature, the primary requirement is low-latency, bidirectional communication. REST is not suitable because it follows a request-response model over HTTP/1.1, requiring the client to poll or use long polling, which introduces latency and overhead. gRPC supports bidirectional streaming over HTTP/2, which can be used for real-time updates, but it requires a grpc-web proxy in the browser and has limited browser support. GraphQL offers subscriptions, which are typically implemented over WebSocket, allowing the server to push updates to the client. However, subscriptions are often an afterthought and may not be as performant as raw WebSocket.

Recommended Approach

Use WebSocket as the transport layer for real-time communication. It provides a persistent, full-duplex connection with minimal overhead. For the application protocol, you can use a lightweight format like JSON or a binary format like MessagePack. This combination offers the lowest latency and best browser support. You can also layer a protocol like Operational Transformation (OT) or CRDT on top to handle conflict resolution.

Why Not REST/gRPC/GraphQL?

  • REST: Requires polling or long polling, which is inefficient for real-time updates.
  • gRPC: Requires a proxy for browser support and adds complexity; better suited for server-to-server communication.
  • GraphQL: Subscriptions add complexity and may not be as mature as WebSocket; still relies on WebSocket under the hood.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is to assume that GraphQL subscriptions or gRPC streaming are drop-in replacements for WebSocket. They add overhead and may not be necessary if all you need is a simple real-time channel. Always evaluate the actual requirements before choosing a protocol.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client] -->|WebSocket| B[Server] B -->|Push updates| A subgraph API Protocols C[REST] -->|Polling| D[Server] E[gRPC] -->|Streaming| F[Server] G[GraphQL] -->|Subscriptions| H[Server] end A -.->|Not ideal| C A -.->|Needs proxy| E A -.->|Uses WebSocket| G"}]

[{"question":"Design a file storage and observability system for a video-sharing platform that handles user uploads and requires monitoring, logging, and tracing.","expectedPoints":["Use object storage (e.g., S3) for video files, not database BLOBs or local disk.","Implement structured logging with ELK stack, including trace IDs for correlation.","Use distributed tracing (e.g., Jaeger) to track requests across services.","Define metrics (RED/USE) and alerting (Prometheus + Grafana) for performance monitoring.","Consider log sampling (e.g., 100% ERROR, 1% INFO) to manage volume."],"followUps":["How would you handle large file uploads (e.g., >1GB) in terms of storage and processing?","What metrics would you alert on for the upload service?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big toy box (object storage) where you put all your video toys. You also have a notebook (logs) to write down what happens, a stopwatch (metrics) to see how long things take, and a map (tracing) to follow a toy's journey through different rooms. This helps you find problems quickly.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a scalable system combining file storage and observability—two critical aspects of production frontend systems that handle user-generated content.

File Storage

For a video-sharing platform, object storage (e.g., AWS S3, GCS, MinIO) is the default choice. Videos are stored as blobs with a unique key and metadata (e.g., user ID, upload date). Avoid storing files in a database (BLOB) or on local disk—both are stateful and don't scale horizontally. Use presigned URLs for secure uploads directly from the client to S3, reducing server load.

Observability: Three Pillars

  • Metrics: Use the RED method (Rate, Errors, Duration) for services and USE method (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) for resources. Tools: Prometheus + Grafana. Set alerts (e.g., error rate > 1%) to trigger PagerDuty.
  • Logging: Use the ELK stack (Logstash → Elasticsearch → Kibana). Logs should be structured JSON with fields like timestamp, level, service, trace_id, and message. Implement log sampling to reduce volume: log 100% of ERRORs, 1% of INFOs.
  • Tracing: Use distributed tracing (Jaeger, Zipkin, AWS X-Ray) to follow a request across services. Each request gets a trace ID, and each unit of work is a span. This helps find latency bottlenecks and cross-service errors.

Common Pitfall

Storing files in the database or on local disk leads to scalability issues and makes observability harder (e.g., no trace ID correlation). Always use object storage and ensure logs include trace IDs for full correlation across metrics, logs, and traces.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR User -->|Upload video| API API -->|Presigned URL| S3[Object Storage] API -->|Log event| Logstash API -->|Emit metric| Prometheus API -->|Create trace| Jaeger Logstash --> Elasticsearch --> Kibana Prometheus --> Grafana --> Alert Jaeger --> UI[Trace Viewer]"}]

[{"question":"Design a real-time news feed system like Twitter's timeline. Walk through the 4-step framework.","expectedPoints":["Clarifies functional (post tweet, view timeline) and non-functional (read-heavy, 99.9% uptime) requirements.","Estimates QPS (write ~1k, read ~10k) and storage (tweets ~30GB/day, media ~2TB/day).","Proposes high-level components: client, API gateway, tweet service, fanout service, timeline cache, DB, CDN.","Deep dives into bottlenecks: read-heavy → cache; fanout latency → async queue; hot users → sharding.","Discusses trade-offs: push vs pull fanout, consistency vs availability for timeline."],"followUps":["How would you handle a celebrity user with millions of followers?","What happens if the timeline cache fails?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a giant bulletin board where millions of people post notes and read notes from people they follow. You need to figure out how to store all those notes, show them quickly, and handle when lots of people post at once. This question tests if you can break down a big problem into small steps, like planning a huge party.","detailedExplanation":"

This question evaluates your ability to design a scalable, real-time news feed system using a structured 4-step framework. The framework ensures you cover all critical aspects: requirements, capacity, architecture, and trade-offs.

1. Clarify Requirements

Functional: Users can post tweets (text + media), follow others, and view a timeline of tweets from followed users. Non-functional: Read-heavy (10:1 read-to-write ratio), low latency for timeline loading (<500ms p99), high availability (99.9%), eventual consistency acceptable for timeline.

2. Capacity Estimation

Assume 100M daily active users, each posting 1 tweet/day → 100M writes/day. Write QPS: 100M / 86,400 ≈ 1,160/s. Read QPS: 10× writes ≈ 11,600/s. Peak: 3× average ≈ 35,000 reads/s. Storage: Tweet text (300B) → 30GB/day; media (10% tweets, 200KB avg) → 2TB/day. Yearly storage: ~11TB text + ~730TB media.

3. High-Level Design

Components: Client (mobile/web), API Gateway (rate limiting, auth), Tweet Service (handles post), Fanout Service (distributes tweets to followers' timelines), Timeline Cache (Redis, stores precomputed timelines), User Graph Service (follow relationships), Media Service (CDN for images/videos), Database (Cassandra for tweets, PostgreSQL for user metadata). Data flow: Post tweet → Tweet Service stores in DB → Fanout Service pushes tweet to all followers' timeline caches. View timeline → Client requests timeline from cache (miss → fallback to DB).

4. Deep Dive

Bottlenecks: Read-heavy → cache timeline to reduce DB load. Write-heavy for celebrities → use async fanout with a queue (Kafka) to avoid blocking. Hot users → shard by user ID. Edge cases: Server failure → replicate cache (Redis cluster). Network partition → handle with eventual consistency. Thundering herd → cache stampede prevention (locks, early recompute). Trade-offs: Push vs Pull fanout: Push gives low read latency but high write cost for celebrities; hybrid approach (push for normal users, pull for celebrities). Consistency vs Availability: Timeline can be eventually consistent; strong consistency for user profile updates.

Common Pitfall: Ignoring capacity estimation leads to underestimating storage for media or read QPS, causing system failure under load. Always calculate numbers early.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR Client -->|post tweet| API_Gateway API_Gateway --> Tweet_Service Tweet_Service --> DB[(Database)] Tweet_Service --> Fanout_Service Fanout_Service --> Queue[Message Queue] Queue --> Fanout_Worker Fanout_Worker --> Timeline_Cache[(Redis Cache)] Client -->|view timeline| API_Gateway API_Gateway --> Timeline_Cache Timeline_Cache -->|miss| DB"}]

[{"question":"Design a URL shortening service like TinyURL. How would you generate unique short URLs and handle redirects with analytics tracking?","expectedPoints":["Explain ID generator approach (Snowflake or auto-increment) with base62 encoding for unique short codes.","Discuss trade-off between 301 (cached, no analytics) vs 302 (server hit, trackable) redirects.","Describe analytics pipeline: click events → Kafka → Flink → aggregated writes to avoid write contention.","Mention storage estimation: ~91 TB for 5 years, 500 bytes per URL entry.","Address collision handling: deterministic hash vs unique ID generation."],"followUps":["How would you handle custom aliases?","What if the ID generator becomes a single point of failure?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a giant library with millions of books. You want to give each book a short code so people can find it quickly. You can either use a machine that gives each book a unique number (like a ticket counter) and then turn that number into a short code, or you can use a math trick to make a code from the book's title. But if two books end up with the same code, that's a problem. Also, when someone uses the code to find a book, you might want to know how many times it's used—like a librarian keeping track. You can either tell the person 'this book is here forever' (301) and they won't ask again, or 'it's here for now' (302) so they always ask you, letting you count every visit.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a scalable, real-world URL shortening service. The core challenge is generating unique short codes and handling redirects while tracking analytics.

Short URL Generation

The recommended approach is an ID generator (e.g., Snowflake or auto-increment) combined with base62 encoding. Snowflake provides unique, time-sorted IDs across distributed systems without collisions. The ID is converted to a 7-character base62 string using the alphabet 0-9a-zA-Z, yielding 62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion combinations. This is preferred over hashing (e.g., MD5 + truncation) because it guarantees uniqueness and avoids collision resolution overhead.

Redirect Strategy

Two HTTP status codes are commonly used: 301 Moved Permanently and 302 Found. A 301 redirect is cached by browsers, so subsequent clicks skip the server—saving bandwidth but losing analytics. A 302 redirect is not cached, so every click hits the server, enabling accurate tracking. For services like bit.ly that monetize analytics, 302 is the standard.

Analytics Pipeline

Directly updating a view_count column on every click causes write contention. Instead, use an event-driven pipeline: click events are sent to Kafka, then aggregated by Flink (or similar stream processor) every minute, and the summary is written to the database. This decouples writes from reads and scales horizontally.

Storage Estimation

With 100 million new URLs per month, 5 years of storage: 100M × 12 × 5 = 6 billion URLs. At 500 bytes per entry (including metadata like creation time, custom alias, expiration), total storage is ~3 TB. The original estimate of 91 TB assumed 100M per day, which is an order of magnitude higher—clarify assumptions during the interview.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is using a hash-based approach without handling collisions. Hash collisions require a retry mechanism (e.g., append a salt and rehash), which adds complexity and latency. The ID generator approach avoids this entirely.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Long URL] --> B[ID Generator] B --> C[Base62 Encode] C --> D[Short URL] D --> E[Store in DB] E --> F[Redirect: 301/302] F --> G[Click Event] G --> H[Kafka] H --> I[Flink Aggregate] I --> J[Write Summary]"}]

[{"question":"Design a news feed system for a social media platform like Twitter, handling both regular users and celebrities. How would you manage the fanout problem?","expectedPoints":["Explain fanout on write (push) and its trade-offs","Explain fanout on read (pull) and its trade-offs","Propose a hybrid approach with thresholds","Discuss storage choices (Redis, Cassandra, etc.)","Address caching and merging strategies"],"followUps":["How would you handle real-time updates for celebrity posts?","What happens if a regular user suddenly becomes viral?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a group of friends and a famous singer. When you post a photo, you want all your friends to see it quickly. One way is to send the photo to each friend's personal album right away (push). But if the singer posts, sending to millions of fans would take too long. So instead, fans check the singer's page when they open their app (pull). The best system mixes both: for regular users, push; for celebrities, pull.","detailedExplanation":"

Fanout is the process of delivering a post to all followers' feeds. There are two main strategies:

Fanout on Write (Push)

When a user posts, the system writes the post into every follower's feed cache (e.g., Redis Sorted Set). Read is fast because the feed is pre-built. However, for celebrities with millions of followers, this causes a write amplification problem: one post triggers millions of writes, wasting storage and compute.

Fanout on Read (Pull)

Feeds are built on-the-fly when a user requests them. The system fetches recent posts from all followed users and merges them. This is write-efficient (no fanout writes) but read-heavy: a user following 1000 people requires 1000 queries, causing high latency.

Hybrid Approach (Twitter/Facebook)

Most platforms use a hybrid model:

  • Regular users (followers < 1M): Fanout on write. Pre-built feed cached in Redis.
  • Celebrities (followers > 1M): Fanout on read. Their posts are fetched separately during feed load.

When loading the feed, the system reads the pre-built cache and merges it with celebrity posts, sorted by timestamp. This balances read/write costs.

Storage Choices

  • User profiles: MySQL + Redis cache for ACID and fast lookups.
  • Posts: Cassandra (wide-column) for high write throughput and time-series queries.
  • Feed cache: Redis Sorted Set (score = timestamp) for O(log n) insert and fast range queries.
  • Media: S3 + CDN for binary blobs with geo-distribution.
  • Search: Elasticsearch for full-text and hashtag search.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring the thundering herd problem: when a celebrity posts, many followers may request the feed simultaneously, causing a spike. Use caching and rate limiting to mitigate.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Posts] --> B{Followers > 1M?} B -- No --> C[Fanout on Write] C --> D[Write to All Followers' Redis] B -- Yes --> E[Fanout on Read] E --> F[Store Post in Cassandra] D --> G[Feed Cache Ready] F --> H[Fetch on Demand] G & H --> I[Merge & Sort by Timestamp] I --> J[Return Feed]"}]
