[{"question":"How would you design a real-time typeahead search system that supports low-latency prefix queries and handles frequent updates without blocking reads?","expectedPoints":["Uses Trie as core data structure for prefix matching","Caches top-K suggestions per node to avoid full subtree traversal","Separates read path (Redis cache + in-memory Trie) from async write path (Kafka + batch rebuild)","Employs blue-green deployment for atomic Trie swap during updates","Shards by alphabet range to distribute memory and load"],"followUps":["How would you handle hot shards where certain prefixes are extremely popular?","What tradeoffs exist between rebuilding the entire Trie vs incremental updates?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine a big dictionary where you want to suggest words as someone types. A Trie is like a tree of letters where each path spells a word. To make it fast, we store the top suggestions at each letter node so we don't have to search the whole tree. When new popular words appear, we collect them in a queue and rebuild the tree at night, swapping it in without stopping the service.","detailedExplanation":"

Typeahead search (e.g., Google autocomplete) requires returning top suggestions in under 50ms as the user types each character. The core data structure is a Trie (prefix tree), where each node represents a character and the path from root forms a prefix. To avoid traversing the entire subtree for every query, each node stores precomputed top-K suggestions (e.g., top 5 by frequency).

Read Path (Query Serving)

When a user types a prefix like syst, the system first checks a Redis cache keyed by prefix. On a cache miss, it queries the in-memory Trie service, which returns the top-5 suggestions from the node. The result is cached for subsequent identical queries.

Write Path (Trie Update)

Updates happen asynchronously to avoid blocking reads. User search completions are logged to Kafka. A batch job (e.g., MapReduce) runs hourly/daily to recompute frequencies from the logs. A new Trie is built offline and swapped in using a blue-green deployment — the old Trie remains serving reads while the new one is loaded, then a pointer swap makes the new Trie active.

Sharding

To fit the Trie in memory, shard by alphabet range: e.g., shard 1 handles prefixes a-m, shard 2 handles n-z. Each shard loads its Trie from a persistent store like RocksDB. A coordinator routes queries to the appropriate shard based on the first character.

Common Pitfall

Rebuilding the entire Trie can be expensive and may cause stale suggestions if done infrequently. An alternative is incremental updates (e.g., adding/removing individual suggestions), but that complicates consistency and may still require occasional full rebuilds to prune outdated entries.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR User[User types prefix] --> Cache[Redis Cache] Cache -- MISS --> Trie[Trie Service] Trie --> Suggestions[Top-5 Suggestions] Suggestions --> User User -- search event --> Kafka[Kafka Log] Kafka --> Batch[Batch Job] Batch --> NewTrie[New Trie] NewTrie --> Swap[Blue-Green Swap] Swap --> Trie"}]

[{"question":"In a messaging app like Zalo, why is a Snowflake ID (64-bit) preferred over a UUID (128-bit) for message ordering, and how does the ID structure enable time-sorting without a separate timestamp column?","expectedPoints":["Snowflake ID is time-sortable: sorting by ID equals sorting by time.","64-bit integer is smaller and faster for indexing than 128-bit UUID.","ID structure: timestamp + machine_id + sequence ensures global uniqueness without central coordinator.","High throughput: 4096 IDs per millisecond per machine.","Avoids separate created_at column for sorting, reducing storage and complexity."],"followUps":["What happens if two messages have the same timestamp on the same machine?","How would you handle clock skew in a distributed Snowflake ID generator?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a bunch of letters and you want to sort them by the time they were written. Instead of writing the time on each letter, you give each letter a special number that gets bigger as time passes. That way, you can just sort the numbers. Snowflake IDs are like those numbers—they're small and fast to sort, unlike big messy UUIDs that don't tell you anything about time.","detailedExplanation":"

Snowflake ID is a 64-bit identifier designed for distributed systems to generate unique, time-sortable IDs without a central coordinator. It is commonly used in messaging apps like Zalo for message ordering.

Structure

The 64-bit integer consists of: 41 bits for timestamp (millisecond precision, ~69 years), 10 bits for machine ID (supports up to 1024 machines), and 12 bits for sequence (4096 IDs per millisecond per machine).

Why Snowflake over UUID?

  • Time-sortable: Sorting by Snowflake ID directly orders messages by creation time, eliminating the need for a separate created_at column in queries.
  • Smaller size: 64-bit integer is half the size of a 128-bit UUID, leading to faster indexing and less storage in databases like Cassandra.
  • High throughput: Each machine can generate up to 4096 IDs per millisecond, sufficient for high-volume messaging.
  • Globally unique: The combination of timestamp, machine ID, and sequence ensures uniqueness without a central coordinator.

Production Importance

In a messaging app, messages must be displayed in order. Using Snowflake IDs allows the database to cluster rows by message_id (as shown in the schema: CLUSTERING ORDER BY (message_id DESC)), making range queries for chat history efficient. This avoids the overhead of sorting by a separate timestamp column.

Common Pitfall

Clock skew across machines can cause IDs to be out of order if a machine's clock jumps backward. Solutions include using a monotonic clock or a distributed coordination service like ZooKeeper to assign machine IDs and handle clock drift.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Message Sent] --> B[Snowflake Generator] B --> C[41-bit Timestamp] B --> D[10-bit Machine ID] B --> E[12-bit Sequence] C --> F[64-bit ID] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Sort by ID = Sort by Time]"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a real-time driver matching system for a ride-hailing app like Uber, focusing on geospatial indexing and state management?","expectedPoints":["Explains why brute-force distance calculation is infeasible for millions of drivers.","Describes at least one geospatial indexing approach (GeoHash, H3) with trade-offs.","Mentions Redis GEOADD/GEOSEARCH for radius queries.","Designs a trip state machine with transitions and distributed locks to prevent race conditions.","Considers idempotency and retry-safety in state transitions."],"followUps":["How would you handle the boundary problem in GeoHash?","What happens if a driver accepts a trip but the lock expires before updating the state?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you need to find the nearest ice cream truck to a kid. If you check every truck in the city, it takes too long. Instead, you can divide the city into hexagons (like a honeycomb) and only check trucks in the kid's hexagon and the six around it. That's what Uber's H3 system does. Also, when a driver accepts a ride, you need to make sure no other driver takes it by using a special lock, like putting a 'reserved' sign on the ride.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a scalable, real-time system that handles geospatial queries and state coordination under high concurrency.

Geospatial Indexing

A naive approach would compute the distance from the rider to every driver, which is O(N) and infeasible for millions of drivers. Instead, we use geospatial indexes like GeoHash or H3. GeoHash encodes lat/lng into a string prefix, but suffers from boundary problems where two nearby points may have different prefixes. Uber's H3 uses a hexagonal grid where each cell has exactly 6 neighbors, eliminating corner issues. In practice, we can use Redis with GEOADD to index driver locations and GEOSEARCH to find drivers within a radius, returning results sorted by distance.

Trip State Machine

The trip lifecycle is modeled as a state machine: requested → searching_driver → driver_accepted → driver_arrived → in_trip → completed (or cancelled). Each transition must be atomic to prevent race conditions. We use distributed locks (e.g., Redis SETNX) to ensure only one driver accepts a trip. The state machine should be idempotent — retrying a transition (e.g., due to network failure) must not cause duplicate state changes.

Production Considerations

In production, we must handle eventual consistency for driver location updates (e.g., batch updates every few seconds). The matching algorithm may also consider driver score, surge pricing, and fleet distribution. A common pitfall is lock expiration — if a driver's lock expires before they update the trip state, another driver might accept the same trip. Solutions include using a lease mechanism with automatic renewal or a two-phase commit pattern.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR R[Rider Requests] --> G[GeoIndex: H3/Redis] G --> D[Find Nearby Drivers] D --> L[Distributed Lock] L --> A[Driver Accepts] A --> S[Trip State Machine] S --> C[Completed or Cancelled]"}]

[{"question":"Design a real-time collaborative document editor like Google Docs. How would you handle conflict resolution and ensure low-latency updates?","expectedPoints":["Use Operational Transformation (OT) or CRDT for conflict resolution.","WebSocket for real-time bidirectional communication.","Client-side optimistic updates with server reconciliation.","Differential synchronization (diff-match-patch) for efficiency.","Consider offline support and reconnection strategies."],"followUps":["How would you scale the system to support millions of concurrent users?","What are the trade-offs between OT and CRDT?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and a friend are drawing on the same whiteboard at the same time. If you both try to erase the same line, you need a rule to decide whose action wins. This system uses special math (like OT or CRDT) to make sure everyone sees the same final picture, even if changes happen at the same moment. It also sends updates very fast using a technology called WebSocket, like a walkie-talkie that keeps the line open.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a real-time collaborative editor involves several key components: conflict resolution, real-time communication, and data synchronization.

Conflict Resolution

Two main approaches exist: Operational Transformation (OT) and CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types). OT transforms operations (insert, delete) so that concurrent changes are applied consistently. CRDTs use data structures that automatically merge without conflicts. OT is used by Google Docs, while CRDTs are used by Figma and some newer editors.

Real-time Communication

Use WebSockets for persistent, low-latency connections. Each client sends operations to a server, which broadcasts them to other clients. For large documents, differential synchronization (e.g., diff-match-patch) reduces bandwidth by sending only changes.

Client-side Optimistic Updates

Apply changes locally immediately for responsiveness, then reconcile with server state. If a conflict is detected, the server may roll back or adjust the local state.

Scaling Considerations

Use a pub/sub system (e.g., Redis Pub/Sub) to broadcast operations across multiple server instances. For persistence, store document versions in a database (e.g., PostgreSQL) with versioning. Consider sharding by document ID.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring offline support and reconnection. Users may lose connection; the system must queue operations locally and replay them upon reconnection, ensuring consistency.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client A] -->|WebSocket| B[Server] C[Client B] -->|WebSocket| B B --> D[Conflict Resolution] D --> E[OT or CRDT] E --> F[Broadcast] F --> A F --> C subgraph Storage G[(Database)] end B <--> G"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a cross-chain payment SDK that handles both card and crypto transactions while ensuring a consistent user experience across different blockchain networks?","expectedPoints":["Abstracts blockchain-specific logic behind a unified interface","Handles transaction lifecycle (init, confirm, retry, fail) consistently","Supports both fiat (card) and crypto payment flows","Considers gas estimation, slippage, and network fees","Provides clear error handling and fallback mechanisms"],"followUps":["How would you handle a scenario where a user's transaction fails due to insufficient gas on one chain?","What trade-offs would you consider between supporting many chains vs. optimizing for a few?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a magic wallet that can pay with either cash or different types of coins, but each coin works in a different country with its own rules. You need to build a system that lets people pay with any coin or cash, no matter which country they're in, and make it feel the same every time. That's what this SDK does—it hides all the complicated differences so users just see 'pay' and it works.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a cross-chain payment SDK like FunKit requires abstracting the complexities of multiple blockchain networks and payment methods into a single, consistent developer and user experience. The core challenge is that each blockchain (Ethereum, Solana, etc.) has its own transaction format, gas mechanics, and confirmation times, while card payments involve entirely different flows (PCI compliance, 3D Secure).

Unified Interface

The SDK should expose a high-level API like pay(amount, currency, chain) that internally maps to chain-specific adapters. Each adapter implements a common interface: estimateGas, submitTransaction, waitForConfirmation, handleError. This allows the core logic to remain chain-agnostic.

Transaction Lifecycle

A robust state machine manages the lifecycle: init (user confirms), pending (tx broadcast), confirming (waiting for blocks), success or failed. For card payments, the lifecycle includes authorization and capture steps. The SDK should emit events at each stage so the UI can show loading spinners, success animations, or error messages.

Cross-Chain Considerations

Gas estimation must be dynamic—fetch current gas prices and estimate required gas for the transaction. For EVM chains, use eth_estimateGas; for Solana, use getRecentBlockhash. Slippage tolerance is critical for token swaps; the SDK should allow configuration and display the estimated output to the user.

Error Handling

Common pitfalls include insufficient funds (gas or token balance), user rejection (wallet popup closed), and network congestion. The SDK should categorize errors and provide retry mechanisms (e.g., increase gas price, switch to a faster chain). For card payments, handle declines with specific codes (insufficient funds, fraud detection).

Production Importance

In production, a poorly designed SDK leads to failed transactions, lost revenue, and frustrated users. A well-designed one abstracts complexity, reduces integration time for merchants, and ensures high success rates. A common pitfall is over-engineering—trying to support every chain from day one. Instead, start with 2-3 major chains and add more based on demand.

[{"question":"As a senior frontend engineer, how would you design a real-time payment dashboard that displays transaction metrics (e.g., $100M payments in May 2026) with live updates, while ensuring performance and scalability for a rapidly growing user base?","expectedPoints":["Uses WebSocket or SSE for real-time data push instead of polling","Implements virtual scrolling or pagination for large transaction lists","Optimizes re-renders with React.memo, useMemo, or state management selectors","Considers lazy loading and code splitting for dashboard modules","Plans for horizontal scaling (e.g., CDN, load balancing) as user base grows"],"followUps":["How would you handle reconnection and data consistency if the WebSocket connection drops?","What trade-offs would you consider between WebSocket and Server-Sent Events for this use case?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a scoreboard for a video game that shows how many points players have earned, and it updates every second. You need to make sure the scoreboard doesn't slow down even if thousands of players are watching. You'd use a special fast connection (like a walkie-talkie) to get updates, and only show a few scores at a time so the screen doesn't get cluttered.","detailedExplanation":"

This question assesses your ability to architect a real-time data-heavy UI that scales. The key is to choose the right transport mechanism: WebSockets provide full-duplex communication ideal for live updates, while Server-Sent Events (SSE) are simpler for one-way data streams. For a dashboard with frequent updates, WebSockets are typically preferred for lower latency.

Performance Optimizations

To handle large datasets (e.g., thousands of transactions), implement virtual scrolling (e.g., react-window) to render only visible rows. Use React.memo and useMemo to prevent unnecessary re-renders. Consider a state management library like Zustand or Redux Toolkit with selectors to minimize component updates.

Scalability Considerations

As the company plans to scale from 20 to 50 engineers and user base grows, the frontend must handle increased data volume. Use lazy loading for dashboard widgets and code splitting via React.lazy and Suspense. Offload heavy computations to Web Workers. For real-time data, consider a message broker like Redis Pub/Sub or Kafka on the backend to fan out updates.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is polling the server every few seconds, which creates unnecessary load and delays. Another is not handling WebSocket reconnection gracefully—implement exponential backoff and a fallback to polling. Also, avoid storing all transaction data in memory; use pagination or infinite scroll with a fixed cache size.

This question assesses your ability to design a real-time, high-performance dashboard for a fintech context, which aligns with the candidate profile's emphasis on TypeScript, product obsession, and get-things-done attitude.

Key Considerations

  • Performance Optimization: Implement virtualization (e.g., react-window) for large lists, memoization (React.memo, useMemo) to prevent unnecessary re-renders, and lazy loading for non-critical components.
  • State Management: Use a global state library (e.g., Zustand, Redux Toolkit) with optimistic updates for instant UI feedback, and handle loading/error states gracefully.

[{"question":"How would you prepare for a multi-stage frontend interview process that includes a recruiter screen, hiring manager deep-dive, live coding assessment, and a final round with the founder?","expectedPoints":["Tailor preparation to each stage: behavioral for recruiter, technical depth for manager, coding for assessment, cultural fit for founder.","Research the company's tech stack and product to align answers with their needs.","Practice live coding under time constraints, focusing on clean code and communication.","Show commitment by accommodating time zone differences and avoiding rescheduling.","Prepare questions that demonstrate interest in the role and company culture."],"followUps":["What specific technical topics would you review for the hiring manager deep-dive?","How would you handle a live coding session if you get stuck?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"behavioral","childExplanation":"Imagine you have to go through several doors to get a special toy. You also have to show up on time, even if it's late at night, to prove you really want the toy.","detailedExplanation":"

This question assesses your ability to navigate a multi-stage interview process, which is common for senior roles. The key is to recognize that each stage evaluates different aspects: the recruiter screen focuses on basic fit and logistics; the hiring manager deep-dive probes your technical experience and problem-solving approach; the live coding assessment tests your coding skills under pressure; and the final round with the founder evaluates cultural alignment and long-term potential.

Preparation Strategy

  • Recruiter Screen: Prepare a concise summary of your experience, motivation for the role, and salary expectations. Be ready to discuss why you're interested in the company.
  • Hiring Manager Deep-Dive: Review your past projects in depth, especially those relevant to the role. Be prepared to discuss trade-offs, design decisions, and how you've handled challenges. Study the company's tech stack and be ready to compare it with alternatives.
  • Live Coding Assessment: Practice coding problems on platforms like LeetCode or HackerRank, focusing on algorithms and data structures common in frontend (e.g., arrays, trees, DOM manipulation). Simulate the environment by coding with a timer and verbalizing your thought process.
  • Final Round with Founder: Research the company's mission, culture, and recent news. Prepare thoughtful questions about the company's vision and your potential impact.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is treating all stages the same. For example, diving into technical details with the recruiter or being too casual with the founder. Tailor your communication style and content to each interviewer's role.

Time Zone Commitment

The note about strict rescheduling and NY timezone slots highlights the importance of flexibility. Showing willingness to accommodate unusual hours demonstrates commitment and reliability, which is especially valued in remote or global teams.

[{"question":"How would you design a compensation and benefits dashboard for a large tech company, considering both frontend architecture and user experience?","expectedPoints":["Discuss data visualization for salary ranges, equity, and benefits.","Address role-based access control (e.g., employees see own data, HR sees all).","Consider internationalization for multi-country compensation rules.","Mention performance optimization for large datasets (virtualization, caching).","Touch on integration with external benefits providers (APIs)."],"followUps":["How would you handle real-time updates when compensation data changes?","What trade-offs would you consider between using a charting library vs. building custom visualizations?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a special website for a big company where employees can see their pay and perks like free lunches. You need to make sure only the right people see the right info, and it works fast even for thousands of employees. It's like a school report card but for grown-ups at work.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a compensation and benefits dashboard for a large tech company involves several key considerations. First, data visualization is critical: you need to display salary ranges, equity grants, bonus structures, and benefits like free lunches or gym memberships. Use libraries like D3.js or Chart.js for interactive charts, but ensure they handle large datasets efficiently.

Access Control & Security

Implement role-based access control (RBAC) so employees see only their own data, managers see their team's, and HR sees all. Use JWT tokens with claims for roles, and enforce permissions on both frontend routes and API calls.

Internationalization

For global companies, compensation rules vary by country (e.g., different tax treatments, currency). Use i18n libraries like react-intl to localize numbers, dates, and legal disclaimers.

Performance

Large datasets require virtualization (e.g., react-window) for lists, and caching with React Query or SWR to avoid re-fetching. Consider lazy-loading chart data when scrolling.

Integration

Benefits like health insurance may come from third-party APIs. Use webhooks or polling to sync data, and handle loading/error states gracefully.

Common Pitfall: Over-engineering the dashboard with too many features initially. Start with a minimal viable product (MVP) showing core compensation data, then iterate based on user feedback.

[{"question":"How would you design a scalable frontend architecture for a multi-product B2B platform that must serve both internal admin portals and external merchant interfaces, while ensuring consistent UX and rapid feature delivery across teams?","expectedPoints":["Proposes a micro-frontend or module federation approach for independent deployability.","Advocates for a shared design system and component library to enforce consistency.","Discusses state management strategy (e.g., Redux Toolkit, Zustand) and cross-team communication patterns.","Addresses performance optimization (code splitting, lazy loading) and monitoring.","Considers security and compliance requirements for fintech/crypto contexts."],"followUps":["How would you handle versioning and breaking changes in the shared design system?","What trade-offs would you consider between a monorepo and multi-repo setup?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a giant Lego castle with many friends. Each friend builds a different tower (like a product), but you all need the same special bricks (design system) so the towers look like they belong together. You also need a way to put all towers together without breaking each other's work. That's what this question is about: planning how to build many web apps that share parts and work together smoothly.","detailedExplanation":"

This question assesses your ability to architect a frontend ecosystem for a large-scale B2B platform, similar to what the candidate experienced at VNG Zalo and Binance. The key challenge is balancing team autonomy with cross-product consistency.

Core Architecture

A micro-frontend approach using Module Federation (Webpack 5) or a custom shell allows each product team to develop and deploy independently. The shell provides navigation, authentication, and shared services. Each micro-frontend can be built with its own framework (React, Vue, etc.) but ideally sticks to a common one for consistency.

Shared Design System

A component library (e.g., Ant Design, Material-UI) with a custom theme ensures visual and behavioral consistency. This library should be versioned and published as an npm package. Use Storybook for documentation and visual regression testing.

State Management & Communication

Use a global state store (e.g., Redux Toolkit) for cross-cutting concerns like user authentication and notifications. For inter-micro-frontend communication, use a pub/sub event bus or shared context via the shell. Each micro-frontend manages its own local state.

Performance & Monitoring

Implement code splitting at the route level and lazy loading for micro-frontends. Use Web Vitals monitoring and centralized logging (e.g., Sentry). For fintech, ensure security headers and CSP are enforced.

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering the shared library with too many abstractions can lead to tight coupling and slow down teams. Keep the shared surface small and stable, and allow teams to extend locally.

[{"question":"How would you design a universal checkout module that works across multiple store platforms with different UI frameworks, while ensuring consistent error handling and conversion tracking?","expectedPoints":["Proposes micro-frontend or iframe-based architecture for cross-platform compatibility","Describes a shared error handling layer with self-resolution mechanisms","Integrates conversion tracking via event bus or analytics SDK","Addresses state management and payment flow orchestration","Considers platform-specific UI adaptations without duplicating logic"],"followUps":["How would you handle a scenario where one platform requires a custom UI component that the shared module doesn't support?","What metrics would you track to measure the checkout module's performance across platforms?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a single checkout system that works for different online stores, like a toy store and a book store. Inside the box, you have a smart helper that catches mistakes and fixes them automatically, and a counter that tracks how many people complete their purchase.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a universal checkout module that works across multiple store platforms, each potentially using different UI frameworks (React, Vue, Angular, etc.). The key challenge is achieving cross-platform compatibility while maintaining consistent behavior for error handling and conversion tracking.

Architecture Approach

A common solution is to use a micro-frontend or iframe-based architecture. The checkout module is built as a standalone application that communicates with the host platform via a well-defined API (e.g., postMessage for iframes, or a shared event bus). This isolates the checkout logic from the host's framework, allowing it to be embedded anywhere.

Error Handling

Implement a centralized error handling layer that categorizes errors (network, validation, payment) and provides self-resolution mechanisms. For example, retry logic for transient failures, user-friendly messages for validation errors, and fallback payment methods. This aligns with the candidate's experience improving error self-resolution by 65.4%.

Conversion Tracking

Use an analytics SDK or event bus to emit standardized events (e.g., checkout_started, payment_success, payment_failed). Each platform can subscribe to these events and forward them to their own analytics systems. This ensures consistent tracking without coupling.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is tightly coupling the checkout module to a specific framework or state management library, making it hard to integrate with platforms using different tech stacks. Always design for framework-agnostic integration.

[{"question":"Explain how React's reconciliation algorithm works and why the `key` prop is critical for list rendering performance.","expectedPoints":["Reconciliation compares virtual DOM trees to minimize real DOM updates.","Uses heuristics: same element type reuses DOM node, different type rebuilds.","Keys help identify which items changed, added, or removed in lists.","Without keys, React may incorrectly reuse components, causing bugs.","Stable, unique keys prevent unnecessary re-renders and preserve state."],"followUps":["What happens if you use array index as a key when the list can be reordered?","How does React handle key collisions or duplicate keys?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a row of toy blocks. When you swap two blocks, React wants to know which block moved. If you give each block a name tag (key), React can see that Block A moved to the right instead of rebuilding everything. Without name tags, React might think you removed Block A and added a new one, which is slower and could mess up your toys.","detailedExplanation":"

Reconciliation is React's algorithm for updating the DOM efficiently. When a component's state or props change, React creates a new virtual DOM tree and compares it with the previous one using a diffing algorithm. The goal is to determine the minimal set of changes needed to update the real DOM.

How It Works

React uses two key heuristics:

  • Different element types: If the root elements are of different types (e.g., <div> vs <span>), React tears down the old tree and builds a new one.
  • Same element type: React keeps the DOM node and only updates changed attributes or children. It then recursively reconciles the children.

For lists, React needs to match children from the old tree to the new tree. This is where the key prop becomes essential. A key is a unique identifier for each element in a list. It allows React to track which items have been added, removed, or reordered.

Why It Matters in Production

Without keys, React uses index-based matching, which can lead to incorrect component reuse and performance degradation. For example, if you have a list of input fields and you remove the first item, the remaining inputs will retain their state because React thinks they are the same components. Using stable keys (like a database ID) ensures that each component is uniquely identified, preserving state and avoiding unnecessary re-renders.

Common Pitfall

Using array index as a key when the list can be reordered or filtered. This can cause subtle bugs and performance issues because the index changes when items are added or removed, leading to incorrect component reuse.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Virtual DOM Tree] --> B[Diffing Algorithm] B --> C{Element Type Same?} C -->|Yes| D[Update Attributes] C -->|No| E[Replace Node] D --> F[Reconcile Children] F --> G{Has Keys?} G -->|Yes| H[Match by Key] G -->|No| I[Match by Index] H --> J[Efficient Update] I --> K[Potential Bugs]"}]

[{"question":"Explain the exact sequence of the useEffect cleanup lifecycle when dependencies change, and why failing to clean up an async fetch can cause a React warning.","expectedPoints":["Cleanup runs before the new effect on dependency change, not after unmount only.","AbortController or flag prevents setState after unmount.","Missing cleanup causes memory leaks and 'setState on unmounted component' warning.","Cleanup also runs on unmount, but no new effect runs after."],"followUps":["How would you implement cleanup for a WebSocket subscription?","What happens if you omit the dependency array entirely?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that plays music when you press a button. If you change the song, you first stop the old song before starting the new one. If you forget to stop the old song, it keeps playing even after you leave the room, wasting batteries. useEffect cleanup is like stopping the old song before starting a new one, and when you leave the room (component unmounts), it stops the song completely so nothing is left running.","detailedExplanation":"

The useEffect cleanup function runs in a specific order during the React component lifecycle. When a component mounts, the effect runs and optionally returns a cleanup function. When dependencies change, React first runs the cleanup function from the previous effect, then runs the new effect. When the component unmounts, the cleanup runs one final time, but no new effect is executed.

Why Cleanup Matters

Without proper cleanup, side effects like event listeners, timers, or async fetches can persist after the component unmounts, causing memory leaks or the infamous Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component warning. For async fetches, using AbortController is the recommended approach: create an AbortController in the effect, pass its signal to fetch, and call controller.abort() in the cleanup. This cancels the request if the component unmounts or dependencies change before the fetch completes.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is forgetting to include the cleanup function entirely, or incorrectly assuming cleanup only runs on unmount. Developers may also neglect to clean up observers like IntersectionObserver or MutationObserver, which can lead to detached DOM references and memory bloat. Always call .disconnect() in the cleanup for observers.

Production Impact

In production apps, especially with frequent re-renders or navigation, uncleaned effects accumulate. This degrades performance and can cause unpredictable behavior. A senior engineer ensures every effect with a persistent side effect has a corresponding cleanup.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Component Mounts] --> B[Effect Runs] B --> C[Returns Cleanup] C --> D[Deps Change] D --> E[Run Previous Cleanup] E --> F[Run New Effect] F --> C D --> G[Component Unmounts] G --> H[Run Final Cleanup] H --> I[No New Effect]"}]

[{"question":"When would you choose useCallback over useMemo, and what is a common pitfall when using these hooks?","expectedPoints":["useCallback returns a memoized function reference; useMemo returns a memoized value.","Use useCallback when passing callbacks to memoized child components to prevent unnecessary re-renders.","Use useMemo for expensive computations to avoid recalculating on every render.","Common pitfall: overusing both hooks without profiling, adding unnecessary memory and comparison overhead.","Rule of thumb: profile first, then optimize only when a measurable performance issue exists."],"followUps":["How would you debug a situation where a child component wrapped in React.memo still re-renders despite using useCallback?","Can you give an example where useMemo is more appropriate than useCallback?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that does a slow calculation every time you play with it. useMemo is like writing down the answer so you don't have to calculate again. useCallback is like keeping the same toy button that does the same action, so your friend (a child component) doesn't get confused and rebuild their toy every time you press it.","detailedExplanation":"

useMemo and useCallback are React hooks for performance optimization, but they serve different purposes. useMemo memoizes the result of a computation, returning a cached value until its dependencies change. It is ideal for expensive calculations or derived state, such as filtering a large array: const filtered = useMemo(() => items.filter(i => i.active), [items]);

useCallback memoizes the function reference itself, returning the same function instance across renders unless dependencies change. It is primarily used to prevent unnecessary re-renders of child components wrapped in React.memo that rely on reference equality for props. Example: const handleClick = useCallback(() => doSomething(id), [id]);

When to Choose Which

  • Choose useMemo when you need to avoid recalculating a value that is computationally heavy.
  • Choose useCallback when you need to pass a stable callback to a memoized child component to avoid breaking its memoization.

Common Pitfall: Over-Memoization

A frequent mistake is applying these hooks everywhere without profiling. Memoization itself has a cost: memory for caching and dependency comparison. Default React re-renders are usually fast enough. The rule is: profile first. Only add memoization when you observe a measurable performance problem, such as slow re-renders or a child wrapped in React.memo that keeps re-rendering due to new prop references.

In production, misuse can lead to increased memory usage and even slower performance if the comparison overhead outweighs the benefit. Always measure before and after optimization.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Component Render] --> B{Expensive Compute?} B -->|Yes| C[useMemo: Cache Value] B -->|No| D{Pass Callback to Memoized Child?} D -->|Yes| E[useCallback: Cache Function Ref] D -->|No| F[No Memoization Needed] C --> G[Value unchanged if deps same] E --> H[Function ref unchanged if deps same]"}]

[{"question":"How would you optimize a React list rendering 10,000 rows? Walk through your approach in order.","expectedPoints":["Start with virtualization to render only visible rows.","Add pagination or infinite scroll for UX and data chunking.","Use React.memo and stable refs to avoid unnecessary re-renders.","Apply useMemo for computed data like sort/filter.","Use stable keys (unique IDs, not array index)."],"followUps":["What if the list is sorted or filtered frequently? How would you handle that?","When would you consider using a Web Worker for this list?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge toy box with 10,000 toys. Instead of dumping them all out at once, you only take out the toys you can see at the front. That's like virtualization. You also put a label on each toy so you know which one is which (stable keys). And if you need to sort them, you do it in a separate helper (useMemo) so you don't slow down playing.","detailedExplanation":"

When rendering a large list (10,000+ rows) in React, the goal is to minimize DOM nodes and re-render work. The optimization stack should be applied in order, measuring impact after each step.

1. Virtualization

Use libraries like react-window or TanStack Virtual to render only the visible rows plus a small overscan. This is the biggest win because it reduces DOM nodes from thousands to tens.

2. Pagination / Infinite Scroll

Load data in chunks (e.g., 50-100 rows) using IntersectionObserver to trigger loading more. This improves initial load time and UX.

3. React.memo + Stable Refs

Wrap the row component with React.memo to prevent re-renders when props haven't changed. Ensure callbacks and objects passed as props are stable (use useCallback, useMemo).

4. useMemo for Sort/Filter

If the list is sorted or filtered, compute the derived data with useMemo to avoid recalculating on every parent render.

5. Stable Keys

Use unique, stable IDs as keys. Never use array index, as it causes incorrect state mapping and re-renders when the list changes.

6. CSS Containment

Add contain: layout style paint to each row to limit browser layout/style recalculations.

7. Defer Updates (React 18+)

Use useDeferredValue or startTransition to keep the input responsive while the list updates.

8. Web Worker

Offload heavy computation (e.g., sorting 10k items) to a Web Worker to avoid blocking the main thread. This is a last resort.

Common Pitfall: Applying optimizations without measuring. Always profile with React DevTools or browser performance tools to identify actual bottlenecks.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[10k Rows] --> B[Virtualization] B --> C[Render Visible Rows] C --> D[React.memo + Stable Keys] D --> E[useMemo for Sort/Filter] E --> F[CSS Containment] F --> G[Defer Updates] G --> H[Web Worker if needed]"}]

[{"question":"Implement a custom React hook `useDebounce` that debounces a value by a given delay. Then use it in a search input that fetches results from an API.","expectedPoints":["Correctly implements debounce with setTimeout and clearTimeout","Returns debounced value that updates only after delay","Handles cleanup on unmount or value change","Integrates with useEffect to trigger API call on debounced value change","Avoids stale closures by using ref or proper dependencies"],"followUps":["How would you modify this to debounce a callback instead of a value?","What are the trade-offs of using debounce vs throttle in a search input?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"debugging","childExplanation":"Imagine you're typing a message to a friend, but you don't want to send it after every letter—you wait until you stop typing for a moment. That's debounce. In our code, we wait a little while after the user stops typing before we search, so we don't overload the server with too many requests.","detailedExplanation":"

The useDebounce hook is a custom React hook that delays updating a value until a certain amount of time has passed since the last change. This is commonly used in search inputs to avoid making an API call on every keystroke.

Implementation

The hook uses useState to store the debounced value and useEffect to set a timer. When the input value changes, the effect clears any previous timer and sets a new one. After the delay, the debounced value is updated. Cleanup in the effect's return ensures no memory leaks.

Usage in Search

In the component, useDebounce returns the debounced search term. A separate useEffect watches the debounced term and triggers the API fetch. This reduces network requests and improves performance.

Why It Matters in Production

In fintech or SDK contexts, debouncing prevents excessive API calls that could lead to rate limiting or increased costs. It also improves user experience by avoiding flickering results.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is forgetting to clear the timer on unmount or when the value changes, causing state updates on unmounted components. Always include cleanup in the effect.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User types] --> B[Value changes] B --> C[Clear previous timer] C --> D[Set new timer] D --> E{Delay passed?} E -- No --> C E -- Yes --> F[Update debounced value] F --> G[Trigger API call]"}]

[{"question":"Explain the order of execution for the following code: console.log('1'); setTimeout(() => console.log('2'), 0); Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('3')); console.log('4');","expectedPoints":["Synchronous code runs first: logs 1 and 4","Microtask queue (Promise.then) runs before macrotask queue","Logs 3 before 2","Event loop drains microtasks entirely before next macrotask","Correct final order: 1, 4, 3, 2"],"followUps":["What would happen if there were multiple microtasks and macrotasks?","How does the render step fit into this cycle?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're playing a game where you have two lists of chores: urgent chores (like cleaning up a spill) and normal chores (like taking out the trash). You always finish all urgent chores before starting any normal chore, even if the normal chore was added first. In JavaScript, Promise callbacks are like urgent chores, and setTimeout callbacks are like normal chores.","detailedExplanation":"

The event loop is the mechanism that coordinates asynchronous operations in JavaScript. It continuously checks if the call stack is empty, then processes tasks from queues.

Execution Order

In the given code:

  • console.log('1') and console.log('4') are synchronous, so they execute immediately in order: 1, then 4.
  • setTimeout(..., 0) schedules a macrotask (callback) into the macrotask queue.
  • Promise.resolve().then(...) schedules a microtask into the microtask queue.

After the call stack empties, the event loop first drains the microtask queue entirely (executing the Promise callback, logging 3). Only then does it pick the next macrotask (the setTimeout callback, logging 2). So the final output is: 1, 4, 3, 2.

Why It Matters

This priority ensures that microtasks (like Promise reactions) are handled promptly, preventing unnecessary delays in async flows. In production, misunderstanding this can lead to subtle bugs where UI updates or data processing happen out of order.

Common Pitfall

Assuming setTimeout(..., 0) runs immediately after synchronous code. In reality, it runs after all microtasks, even if the delay is zero.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart TD A[Call Stack] -->|empty| B{Microtask Queue} B -->|drain all| C[Macrotask Queue] C -->|one task| D[Render Step] D --> A subgraph Queues B C end"}]

[{"question":"How would you choose between Promise.all, Promise.allSettled, Promise.race, and Promise.any for a dashboard that loads user data from three independent APIs, where one API is slow and you want the fastest successful response?","expectedPoints":["Identifies Promise.any for first successful response","Explains Promise.all rejects on any failure","Distinguishes race vs any: race settles on first result (could be reject)","Mentions AggregateError when all reject in Promise.any","Considers fallback pattern for dashboard resilience"],"followUps":["What if you need all results but one API is unreliable?","How would you implement a timeout with Promise.race?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're asking three friends for the answer to a question. Promise.any is like taking the first correct answer you get, ignoring wrong ones. Promise.race takes the first answer, even if it's wrong. Promise.all waits until everyone answers correctly, but if anyone is wrong, you stop. Promise.allSettled waits for all answers, right or wrong, and then looks at them all.","detailedExplanation":"

Promise combinators are methods on the Promise constructor that let you coordinate multiple asynchronous operations. Each combinator behaves differently, and choosing the right one is critical for building robust, performant applications.

What they do

  • Promise.all: Takes an iterable of promises and returns a single promise that resolves when all input promises resolve, with an array of their values. If any promise rejects, the returned promise rejects immediately with that reason.
  • Promise.allSettled: Returns a promise that resolves after all input promises settle (either resolve or reject). The resolved value is an array of objects, each with a status ("fulfilled" or "rejected") and either value or reason.
  • Promise.race: Returns a promise that settles (resolves or rejects) as soon as the first input promise settles. The outcome mirrors that first promise.
  • Promise.any: Returns a promise that resolves as soon as the first input promise resolves. If all input promises reject, the returned promise rejects with an AggregateError containing all rejection reasons.

How they work in production

For a dashboard loading user data from three independent APIs, you likely want the fastest successful response to display initial content. Promise.any is ideal because it resolves on the first success, ignoring failures. Promise.race would also return quickly, but it could return a rejection if the fastest API fails, leaving you with an error. Promise.all would wait for all three, failing entirely if one fails. Promise.allSettled waits for all, which is slower and gives you all results, but you'd still need to pick the first success manually.

Common pitfall

Using Promise.race when you mean Promise.any. Promise.race settles on the first settled promise, which could be a rejection. This often leads to unexpected errors in fallback patterns. Always use Promise.any when you want the first successful result.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[APIs] --> B[Promise.all] A --> C[Promise.allSettled] A --> D[Promise.race] A --> E[Promise.any] B --> F[All resolve: array] B --> G[Any reject: reject] C --> H[All settled: status array] D --> I[First settled: resolve/reject] E --> J[First resolve: value] E --> K[All reject: AggregateError]"}]

[{"question":"How would you implement an exponential backoff retry pattern with jitter for a payment API call in React, and what error types should you avoid retrying?","expectedPoints":["Formula: delay = baseDelay * 2^attempt + random jitter","Jitter prevents thundering herd problem","Do not retry 4xx errors or idempotency violations","Use a custom hook or wrapper for retry logic","Handle max retries and abort signal"],"followUps":["How would you test the retry logic to ensure it behaves correctly under network failures?","What if the payment API is not idempotent? How would you handle that?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're trying to call your friend, but the line is busy. Instead of calling again immediately, you wait a little longer each time (1 second, then 2, then 4). You also add a tiny random wait so that if many people are calling at once, they don't all try again at the same time. But if your friend says 'wrong number' (like a 4xx error), you stop calling because it won't help.","detailedExplanation":"

The exponential backoff retry pattern is a strategy for handling transient network failures in distributed systems, especially critical for payment SDK reliability. The core idea is to retry a failed request with increasing delays between attempts, following the formula: delay = baseDelay * 2^attempt + jitter. For example, with a base delay of 1 second, attempts wait 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, etc.

Why Jitter Matters

Jitter (a random offset, e.g., 0–100ms) prevents the thundering herd problem where many clients retry simultaneously after a server recovery, overwhelming the system. Without jitter, all clients would retry at the same moment, causing another failure.

Implementation in React

You can create a custom hook like useRetry that wraps an async function (e.g., a payment API call). The hook manages state for attempts, delays, and abort signals. Use setTimeout with the calculated delay and clearTimeout on cleanup. Ensure the hook respects a maximum retry count and aborts on component unmount.

What Not to Retry

  • 4xx errors (client errors like 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found) – these indicate a user or application bug, not a transient failure.
  • Idempotency violations – if a request is not idempotent (e.g., a payment that could be processed twice), retrying may cause duplicate charges. Use idempotency keys to prevent this.
  • Errors like tx already submitted – retrying could lead to double payment.

Common Pitfall

Not implementing jitter or using a fixed delay. This can cause cascading failures when many clients retry simultaneously. Always add jitter to spread out retry attempts.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Request] --> B{Fail?} B -->|Yes| C[Increment attempt] C --> D[Calculate delay: base*2^attempt + jitter] D --> E[Wait delay] E --> A B -->|No| F[Success]"}]

[{"question":"How would you handle the asynchronous nature of blockchain transactions in a React frontend, ensuring the UI updates correctly after a transaction is confirmed?","expectedPoints":["Explain transaction lifecycle: pending, confirmed, failed.","Use async/await with tx.wait() or event listeners.","Refetch data after confirmation to avoid stale state.","Handle errors (revert, out of gas) with user feedback.","Consider loading states and transaction hash display."],"followUps":["What if the user closes the browser before the transaction confirms?","How would you optimize for multiple pending transactions?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you send a letter (transaction) through the mail. You don't know when it arrives, so you keep checking the mailbox (blockchain) until you see a confirmation. In a web app, you need to show a 'sending' animation, then update the screen only when the letter is delivered, and tell the user if it got lost.","detailedExplanation":"

In Web3 frontend development, handling blockchain transactions is fundamentally different from traditional REST API calls because transactions are asynchronous and take an unpredictable amount of time (seconds to minutes) to be mined. The frontend must manage the entire lifecycle: pending (user signed, waiting for confirmation), confirmed (included in a block), or failed (reverted, out of gas).

Core Approach

Using libraries like ethers.js, you send a transaction via a signer object, which returns a TransactionResponse. You then call tx.wait() which returns a TransactionReceipt once the transaction is mined. This is typically done in an async function with try/catch for error handling.

State Management

After tx.wait() resolves, you must refetch any relevant on-chain data (e.g., token balances, NFT ownership) because the local state is now stale. Use React state or a caching library (like React Query) to invalidate and refetch queries. Also, listen for blockchain events (e.g., Transfer) via a provider to update the UI in real-time without polling.

Common Pitfall

Many developers forget to handle the case where the user switches networks or the transaction is dropped and re-sent. Always check the chainId before sending and provide clear error messages. Also, avoid blocking the UI during the wait—show a loading spinner and the transaction hash so users can track it on a block explorer.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User clicks] --> B[Send tx via signer] B --> C[tx.hash returned] C --> D[Show pending state] D --> E[Call tx.wait()] E --> F{Confirmed?} F -->|Yes| G[Refetch data] F -->|No| H[Show error] G --> I[Update UI]"}]

[{"question":"What are the key differences between an EOA and a smart contract wallet, and why would a dApp choose a smart wallet over an EOA?","expectedPoints":["EOA controlled by private key; smart wallet controlled by contract code.","Smart wallets support gas abstraction, batch transactions, and social recovery.","Smart wallets enable better UX: users can pay gas in ERC-20 tokens, combine multiple actions into one transaction, and recover accounts without seed phrases.","EOAs are simpler but limited; smart wallets are more flexible but require more gas to deploy and operate."],"followUps":["How would you implement gas abstraction in a smart wallet?","What are the security trade-offs of using a smart wallet vs an EOA?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a piggy bank (EOA) that only lets you put coins in or take them out one at a time, and if you lose the key, you lose everything. A smart wallet is like a safe with a computer inside that can follow rules, like letting your friends help you open it if you forget the code, or paying for the bus with a sticker instead of coins. That's why some apps build smart wallets—they make things easier and safer.","detailedExplanation":"

An Externally Owned Account (EOA) is a basic crypto wallet controlled by a private key. It can only sign and send transactions, must pay gas in ETH, and recovery is limited to the seed phrase. Batch transactions are not possible. Examples include MetaMask and hardware wallets.

A Smart Contract Wallet is controlled by contract code, enabling custom logic like multisig, session keys, spending limits, and social recovery. Gas can be sponsored by a paymaster (gas abstraction), allowing users to pay in USDC or other tokens. Batch transactions (e.g., approve + swap in one tx) are supported. Examples include Safe, Argent, and Fun.xyz.

Why Choose Smart Wallets?

Smart wallets dramatically improve user experience: users don't need to hold ETH for gas, can execute complex multi-step actions in one click, and recover accounts via guardians instead of seed phrases. This is critical for mainstream adoption.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is underestimating the higher gas costs for deploying and using smart wallets. Each transaction involves contract execution, which can be more expensive than a simple EOA transfer. Also, the contract code must be audited to avoid vulnerabilities.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[EOA] -->|Private Key| B[Sign & Send] A -->|Gas: ETH| C[Single Tx] A -->|Recovery: Seed| D[Lost = Lost] E[Smart Wallet] -->|Contract Code| F[Custom Logic] E -->|Gas: Paymaster| G[Any Token] E -->|Batch Tx| H[Multiple Actions] E -->|Recovery: Guardians| I[Social Recovery]"}]

[{"question":"How does EIP-4337 enable gasless transactions for users, and what role does the Paymaster contract play?","expectedPoints":["User sends a UserOperation to an alt-mempool instead of directly to the chain.","Bundler aggregates multiple UserOps into one on-chain transaction.","EntryPoint contract verifies signatures and executes each UserOp.","Paymaster contract optionally pays gas fees, allowing users to pay in ERC-20 tokens or have fees sponsored.","This enables UX improvements like gasless transactions, batch operations, and social recovery."],"followUps":["What are the security implications of using a Paymaster?","How does the Bundler get compensated for its work?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to buy a toy but you don't have the right coins to pay for the shipping. EIP-4337 is like a helper that lets you send a note saying 'I want this toy' to a special mailbox. Then a mail carrier (Bundler) collects many notes and delivers them all at once. If you don't have shipping coins, a friend (Paymaster) can pay for you, and you can pay them back later with different coins.","detailedExplanation":"

EIP-4337 introduces account abstraction without changing the Ethereum consensus layer. It allows users to interact with smart contract wallets using a new pseudo-transaction called a UserOperation.

How It Works

A user signs a UserOperation and sends it to an alt-mempool (a separate transaction pool). Off-chain nodes called Bundlers pick up multiple UserOps, aggregate them into a single Ethereum transaction, and submit it to the EntryPoint contract. The EntryPoint verifies each UserOp's signature, checks gas limits, and executes the desired action on the user's smart wallet.

Role of Paymaster

If the user lacks ETH for gas, a Paymaster contract can sponsor the transaction. The Paymaster pays the gas fee and can be reimbursed in ERC-20 tokens (e.g., USDC) or via a dApp subsidy. This enables gasless transactions for users.

Why It Matters in Production

EIP-4337 dramatically improves UX: users can pay gas in stablecoins, batch multiple actions (e.g., approve + swap) in one transaction, use session keys for gaming, and recover wallets without seed phrases. For dApps, it lowers onboarding friction.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming the Bundler is trustless. While the protocol is decentralized, Bundlers must be carefully implemented to avoid censorship or front-running. Additionally, Paymasters need to handle reentrancy and signature replay attacks.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR U[User] -- signs --> UO[UserOperation] UO --> AM[Alt-Mempool] AM --> B[Bundler] B -- submits 1 tx --> EP[EntryPoint] EP -- verifies --> SW[Smart Wallet] EP -- if no ETH --> PM[Paymaster] PM -- pays gas --> EP SW -- executes --> TX[Transaction]"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a frontend abstraction to handle cross-chain transactions while minimizing user friction and risk?","expectedPoints":["Abstracts bridging, swapping, and payment into a single SDK call","Handles error recovery and fund rollback on failure","Manages gas estimation and fee payment on multiple chains","Provides real-time status updates and fallback UIs"],"followUps":["How would you handle the case where the bridge succeeds but the swap fails?","What user feedback would you show during the 5-30 minute bridge wait?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have money in a US bank and want to pay a friend in Europe. Normally you'd have to exchange money, wait days, and pay fees. Our app does all that for you in one click, like a magic wallet that handles everything behind the scenes.","detailedExplanation":"

Cross-chain transactions involve moving assets from one blockchain to another, which typically requires bridging, swapping, and paying gas fees on both chains. The current user experience is fragmented and risky: users must manually bridge tokens (5-30 min wait), then swap on a DEX, and finally pay the merchant. This introduces multiple points of failure and high cognitive load.

Key Design Considerations

  • Abstraction Layer: A single SDK method (e.g., FunKit.pay()) that orchestrates bridging, swapping, and payment. The frontend only needs to call this method with the source chain, asset, and destination details.
  • Error Recovery: Implement a state machine that tracks each step. If a step fails (e.g., bridge succeeds but swap fails), the system should attempt to rollback or provide a recovery path (e.g., refund to source chain).
  • Gas Management: The SDK must handle gas fees on both chains. This can be done by estimating gas costs upfront and deducting from the user's balance, or using a relayer network that pays gas on behalf of the user.
  • User Feedback: During the bridge wait (5-30 min), show a progress indicator with estimated time remaining. Use optimistic UI updates to show the expected outcome, but clearly indicate that the transaction is pending.

Common Pitfall

Ignoring partial failure scenarios. For example, if the bridge succeeds but the swap fails, the user's funds may be stuck on the destination chain. The design must include a mechanism to either complete the swap automatically or return the funds to the user's original chain.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User has USDC on Polygon] --> B[FunKit SDK] B --> C[Bridge: Lock on Polygon, Mint on Ethereum] C --> D[Swap: USDC -> ETH on Uniswap] D --> E[Pay: Merchant receives ETH] B --> F[Error Recovery] F --> G[Rollback or Refund]"}]

[{"question":"Why does a failed transaction still consume gas under EIP-1559, and how would you handle this in an SDK to prevent user loss?","expectedPoints":["EVM executes computation before revert, consuming gas","Validators must be compensated for work done","Gas used is deducted from gas limit, not refunded","Use estimateGas and eth_call simulation before signing","Simulation catches reverts without burning user funds"],"followUps":["How would you simulate a transaction using eth_call?","What happens to the priority fee in a failed transaction?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you order a pizza but change your mind after the chef already started making it. You still have to pay for the ingredients used. Similarly, when a transaction fails on Ethereum, the network already did some work, so you pay for that work even if the transaction didn't succeed.","detailedExplanation":"

Under EIP-1559, a failed transaction still consumes gas because the EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine) executes computational steps before hitting a revert. The validator must be compensated for the work performed, even if the state is rolled back. The gas used is deducted from the gasLimit and is not refunded.

Gas Components

  • Base Fee: Set by network demand, burned (removed from supply).
  • Priority Fee (Tip): Paid to validator as incentive to include the transaction.
  • Max Fee Per Gas: Upper bound user is willing to pay per gas unit.
  • Gas Limit: Maximum gas units user authorizes.
  • Gas Used: Actual computation cost, ≤ gas limit.

Why Failed Transactions Cost Gas

The EVM consumes computational resources before encountering a REVERT opcode. Validators must be paid for this work. The state is rolled back, but the gas is not refunded. This is a critical consideration for SDK error handling.

SDK Implication

Always use estimateGas and eth_call simulation before asking the user to sign. eth_call simulates the transaction without broadcasting, catching reverts without burning user money. This prevents users from losing gas on failed transactions.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on estimateGas is insufficient because it may succeed while the actual execution reverts due to state changes. Always combine with eth_call simulation for robust error handling.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Signs Tx] --> B[EVM Executes] B --> C{Transaction Reverts?} C -->|Yes| D[Gas Used Deducted] C -->|No| E[State Updated] D --> F[Validator Paid] E --> F F --> G[Base Fee Burned] F --> H[Priority Fee to Validator]"}]

[{"question":"Design a JavaScript SDK that other developers embed on their websites to display user profile data from your service. How would you architect it for ease of use, performance, and maintainability?","expectedPoints":["Clear initialization API with configuration options","Lazy loading and async script loading for performance","Versioning and backward compatibility strategy","Error handling and graceful degradation","Minimal global scope pollution (e.g., single global object or module pattern)"],"followUps":["How would you handle multiple instances of the SDK on the same page?","What if the host page uses a different module system (AMD, CommonJS, ES modules)?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you build a special toy that other kids can put on their own toy shelves. You need to make sure it's easy to attach, doesn't break their shelf, and works even if they have other toys. That's what designing an SDK is like: making a tool that other developers can easily add to their websites without causing problems.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing an SDK for third-party embedding requires balancing developer experience, performance, and maintainability. The core is a simple initialization API that accepts configuration (e.g., API key, user ID, theme). Use a factory pattern or constructor that returns an instance with methods like render() or on().

Loading Strategy

Provide a single script tag that loads asynchronously. Use document.createElement('script') with async attribute to avoid blocking page rendering. The script should expose a global function (e.g., MySDK) that queues calls until the SDK is fully loaded.

Versioning & Compatibility

Use semantic versioning and serve multiple versions via CDN (e.g., https://cdn.example.com/v1/sdk.js). Avoid breaking changes within a major version. Provide a migration guide for major upgrades.

Error Handling

Wrap all operations in try-catch and emit events for errors. Use graceful degradation: if the SDK fails to load, the host page should not break. Provide a onError callback in the configuration.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is polluting the global namespace with multiple variables. Instead, expose a single global object (e.g., window.MySDK) and use a module pattern internally. Also, avoid overriding existing globals.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Host Page] -->|Script Tag| B[SDK Loader] B -->|async load| C[Core SDK] C --> D[Init API] D --> E[Render Widget] C --> F[Event System] F --> G[User Interactions] C --> H[Error Handler] H --> I[Graceful Degradation]"}]

[{"question":"You need to embed a checkout widget that must be PCI-compliant, support React and non-React sites, and allow automatic updates. Which embedding strategy would you choose and why?","expectedPoints":["Identifies iframe for PCI compliance due to sandbox isolation.","Suggests hybrid approach: script tag for simple sites, NPM for React, iframe for sensitive flows.","Explains trade-offs: iframe security vs. postMessage complexity and mobile UX.","Mentions automatic updates via CDN script tag or versioned NPM package.","Considers performance: lazy loading iframe or deferring script."],"followUps":["How would you handle communication between the iframe and the host page?","What if the client wants to customize the checkout UI? How would you support that with iframe?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy vending machine that other people can put inside their own toy stores. You need to make sure the money part is super safe (PCI compliance). One way is to put the vending machine in a glass box (iframe) so nobody can mess with it, but then you have to use walkie-talkies (postMessage) to talk to it. For stores that already have a lot of toys (React apps), you can give them a special kit (NPM package). For simple stores, you just give them a sticker (script tag) that automatically updates.","detailedExplanation":"

When embedding a checkout widget that must be PCI-compliant, the primary concern is security isolation for payment input fields. The iframe strategy is the gold standard because it creates a sandboxed environment that prevents the host page from accessing the iframe's DOM, thus protecting sensitive card data. However, iframes introduce complexity in communication via postMessage and can degrade mobile user experience due to sizing and scrolling issues.

Hybrid Approach (Stripe Pattern)

For maximum flexibility, use a hybrid embedding strategy:

  • Script tag injection for simple, non-build sites: easy integration, auto-updates via CDN, but pollutes global namespace and lacks type safety.
  • NPM package for React/SPA apps: provides type safety (TypeScript), version control, tree-shaking, and better integration with build tools like Webpack.
  • Iframe for the actual payment form: ensures PCI compliance by isolating sensitive input fields. The iframe communicates with the host via postMessage for events like tokenization or errors.

Key Considerations

  • Performance: Lazy-load the iframe or defer the script to avoid blocking the main thread. Use async or defer for script tags.
  • Versioning: For script tags, use a versioned URL (e.g., v1/sdk.js) to allow automatic minor updates but pin major versions. For NPM, use semantic versioning and provide migration guides.
  • Error Handling: Implement graceful degradation—if the iframe fails to load, show a fallback message or redirect to a hosted checkout page.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on an iframe for the entire checkout flow can lead to poor mobile UX and complex state synchronization. Instead, use the iframe only for the payment form and handle the rest (cart, shipping) in the host page. Also, avoid exposing API keys in client-side code; use backend proxies or tokenization.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client Site] --> B{Embedding Strategy} B --> C[Script Tag] B --> D[NPM Package] B --> E[Iframe] C --> F[Auto-updates] D --> G[Type-safe] E --> H[PCI Compliance] F --> I[Simple Sites] G --> J[React Apps] H --> K[Payment Form]"}]

[{"question":"How would you design a real-time transaction tracking system that handles chain reorganizations and cross-chain status updates?","expectedPoints":["Uses WebSocket for real-time updates with polling fallback","Handles reorgs by tracking confirmation count and reversing status gracefully","Tracks source and destination chains separately for cross-chain transactions","Implements reconnection logic with event replay on client disconnect"],"followUps":["How would you determine the required confirmation count for each chain?","What happens if the WebSocket connection drops and the client misses a status update?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're tracking a package that goes through different checkpoints. Sometimes the system makes a mistake and the package goes back to a previous checkpoint (reorg). You need to handle that gracefully. Also, if the package goes from one shipping company to another (cross-chain), you need to track both legs separately.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a distributed system that handles real-time updates, chain reorganizations, and cross-chain transactions. The architecture typically involves a client SDK with a useTxStatus hook that uses WebSocket for real-time updates and falls back to polling. The backend consists of an API Gateway for authentication and channel subscription, a Status Aggregator using pub/sub to manage status changes, and Chain Watchers per chain that connect to RPC nodes via WebSocket.

Handling Reorgs

Chain reorganizations occur when a block is removed from the chain. To handle this, you must track a confirmation count (e.g., 12 blocks for Ethereum, 15 for BSC). A transaction is only considered 'confirmed' after reaching this threshold. If a reorg happens before that, the status may revert to 'pending'. The system should emit a status reversal event and update the UI accordingly.

Cross-Chain Tracking

For cross-chain transactions, the source chain and destination chain are independent. You must track both sides separately. The source chain might show 'completed' while the destination chain is still 'pending'. The UI should display both statuses clearly.

Reconnection and Event Replay

If the client disconnects, the server should resubscribe the user to the relevant channels and replay missed events. This can be done by storing events in a buffer or using a persistent queue. The client should also implement exponential backoff for reconnection attempts.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is treating a transaction as final after a single block confirmation. This ignores reorgs and can lead to incorrect UI states. Always require a chain-specific confirmation count before marking a transaction as 'confirmed'.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR Client[Client SDK] -->|WebSocket| Gateway[API Gateway] Gateway -->|Subscribe| Aggregator[Status Aggregator] Aggregator -->|Chain Events| Watcher[Chain Watcher] Watcher -->|RPC| RPC[RPC Nodes] Aggregator -->|Store| DB[(TX Database)] subgraph Reorg Handling Watcher -->|Reorg| Revert[Status Reversal] end subgraph Cross-Chain Aggregator -->|Source| Source[Source Chain] Aggregator -->|Destination| Dest[Dest Chain] end"}]

[{"question":"Design a multi-layer cache strategy for a widget that fetches token prices and is used by 1000 concurrent users. How would you ensure freshness while minimizing latency?","expectedPoints":["Describes client memory cache with short TTL (e.g., 30s) for instant reuse.","Uses HTTP cache headers like Cache-Control: max-age=15, stale-while-revalidate.","Leverages CDN edge caching with stale-while-revalidate for geographic distribution.","Implements server-side cache (e.g., Redis) with region-aware TTL (5-15s).","Explains client-side deduplication to avoid duplicate in-flight requests."],"followUps":["How would you handle cache invalidation when a price update is critical (e.g., before swap submission)?","What trade-offs exist between using stale-while-revalidate vs. a shorter TTL?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a lemonade stand and many customers want to know the price. Instead of running to the store every time, you write the price on a board (client memory), check a note on your fridge (browser cache), ask a neighbor (CDN), or call a friend (server cache). You also make sure that if two customers ask at the same time, you only call the store once (deduplication).","detailedExplanation":"

A multi-layer cache strategy is essential for scaling a price-fetching widget to thousands of concurrent users. The goal is to minimize latency while maintaining reasonable freshness. The layers, from fastest to slowest, are:

1. Client Memory (Zustand)

Store the price in a state management library with a TTL of ~30 seconds. This gives ~0ms response for repeated requests within the same page session. However, it is per-tab and lost on refresh.

2. Browser HTTP Cache

Set Cache-Control: max-age=15, stale-while-revalidate. The browser serves cached data for 15 seconds, then can serve stale data while revalidating in the background (~5ms). This reduces network calls.

3. CDN Edge (Cloudflare)

CDN caches responses at edge locations with stale-while-revalidate and geographic awareness. Latency is ~20-50ms. This offloads origin traffic and speeds up global users.

4. Server Cache (Redis)

A shared Redis cache with TTL 5-15 seconds, region-aware. This is the source of truth for the CDN and backend. Latency ~5-10ms.

5. Origin (Aggregator)

Fetches from multiple price sources (Chainlink, CoinGecko) with ~100-500ms latency. This is the fallback when all caches miss.

Client-Side Deduplication

Use a Map to track in-flight requests. If two components request the same URL simultaneously, they share the same Promise. This prevents duplicate network calls.

Common Pitfall: Stale Cache on Swap

Always refresh the price right before a swap submission. A stale cache combined with price movement can cause user fund loss (slippage). Implement a forced refresh or use a short TTL for critical operations.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client Memory] -->|TTL 30s| B[Browser Cache] B -->|max-age=15| C[CDN Edge] C -->|stale-while-revalidate| D[Server Cache] D -->|TTL 5-15s| E[Origin Aggregator] F[Deduplication Map] --> A"}]
