tr_v2:93f0c1d26f16132a: Frontend System Design is a framework for designing large-scale frontend applications. The RADIO thinking framework includes: Requirements, Architecture, Data, Interface, and Optimization.
qgen_v4:6a33aca0badd3184:
[{"question":"How would you design a large-scale frontend application to ensure fast perceived performance and maintainability?","expectedPoints":["Starts with requirements gathering (user needs, constraints).","Discusses architecture choices (e.g., micro-frontends, component tree).","Covers data flow (state management, caching, API design).","Addresses interface design (lazy loading, code splitting, SSR).","Includes optimization strategies (performance budgets, monitoring)."],"followUps":["How do you decide between client-side rendering and server-side rendering?","What trade-offs would you consider when choosing a state management solution?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a giant Lego castle. You need to plan where each piece goes so it's strong and easy to change later. Frontend system design is like that: you decide how to organize the code, where to store data, and how to make the app feel fast to users, even if it's huge.","detailedExplanation":"

Frontend system design is a structured approach to building large-scale client applications, focusing on user experience, maintainability, and performance. Unlike backend design, which emphasizes server scalability and throughput, frontend design prioritizes perceived performance, data flow, and code organization.

Key Components

  • Requirements: Gather user needs, device constraints, and business goals. For example, a social media feed must load quickly on mobile networks.
  • Architecture: Choose between monolithic, micro-frontends, or module federation. Define component hierarchy and communication patterns (e.g., props, events, or a global store).
  • Data: Design state management (Redux, Zustand, or Context API), caching strategies (service workers, HTTP caching), and API contracts (GraphQL vs REST).
  • Interface: Implement lazy loading, code splitting, and server-side rendering (SSR) to reduce initial load time. Use skeletons and placeholders for smooth transitions.
  • Optimization: Set performance budgets, use Lighthouse audits, and monitor real user metrics (Core Web Vitals). Optimize images, fonts, and third-party scripts.

Why It Matters in Production

A well-designed frontend reduces bounce rates, improves SEO, and simplifies team collaboration. For instance, micro-frontends allow independent deployments, while SSR ensures fast first paint on slow connections.

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering: Adding complex state management or micro-frontends prematurely can increase bundle size and cognitive load. Start simple and iterate based on actual bottlenecks.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Requirements] --> B[Architecture] B --> C[Data Flow] C --> D[Interface Design] D --> E[Optimization] E --> F[Monitoring & Iteration] subgraph RADIO Framework A B C D E end"}]
tr_v2:6c716db6265196dd: RADIO Framework — the backbone of every answer
qgen_v4:e28ca499282a0cbf:
[{"question":"Walk me through how you would design a real-time collaborative document editor (like Google Docs) using the RADIO framework.","expectedPoints":["Clarifies requirements: multi-user, real-time sync, conflict resolution, offline support","Proposes architecture: WebSocket for real-time, CRDT/OT for conflict resolution, component tree","Defines data model: document state as sequence of operations, client vs server state","Specifies API: WebSocket messages for ops, REST for auth/metadata, component props","Discusses optimizations: debouncing, virtual scrolling, optimistic updates, accessibility"],"followUps":["How would you handle conflict resolution if two users edit the same word simultaneously?","What trade-offs would you consider between using Operational Transformation vs CRDT?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are drawing on the same digital whiteboard from different computers. The RADIO framework is like a checklist that helps you plan everything step by step: first, you decide what the whiteboard should do (Requirements), then you draw the big picture of how parts connect (Architecture), then you decide how to store the drawing data (Data Model), then you define how parts talk to each other (Interface), and finally you make it fast and smooth (Optimizations).","detailedExplanation":"

The RADIO framework is a structured approach to frontend system design interviews, ensuring you cover all critical aspects systematically. It stands for Requirements, Architecture, Data Model, Interface/API, and Optimizations.

Requirements

Start by clarifying the problem. For a collaborative editor, ask: Who are the users? What features are needed (real-time sync, offline support, version history)? What are the constraints (latency, device types)? Always confirm assumptions with the interviewer.

Architecture

Sketch high-level components: a WebSocket server for real-time communication, a client-side state manager (e.g., Redux), and UI components (toolbar, document canvas, cursor overlays). Show data flow: user action → local update → send operation via WebSocket → server broadcasts to other clients → apply operation.

Data Model

Define the document state. Use a sequence of operations (e.g., insert/delete characters) rather than a plain text string to enable conflict resolution. Distinguish between server state (authoritative) and client state (optimistic). Consider using CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) or Operational Transformation for merging edits.

Interface/API

Specify the contract between client and server: WebSocket messages for operations (e.g., {type: 'insert', position: 5, text: 'hello'}), REST endpoints for authentication and document metadata. Also define component props: .

Optimizations

Discuss performance: debounce local updates, use virtual scrolling for large documents, implement optimistic updates for low latency. Consider accessibility (ARIA labels for editor), security (validate operations server-side), and network resilience (retry logic, offline queue).

A common pitfall is jumping into architecture without clarifying requirements, leading to over-engineering or missing key constraints. Always start with R and iterate.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart TD R[Requirements] --> A[Architecture] A --> D[Data Model] D --> I[Interface/API] I --> O[Optimizations] O -.-> R"}]
qgen_v4:2bd3ba936158aae1:
[{"question":"What is the core difference between frontend and backend system design?","expectedPoints":["Frontend focuses on user experience, client architecture, rendering, and perceived performance.","Backend focuses on throughput, database, server scaling, and data processing.","Both require trade-offs and scaling considerations, but with different priorities.","Frontend design involves component organization, state management, network optimization, and user perception."],"followUps":["How would you approach scaling a frontend application for millions of users?","Can you give an example of a trade-off in frontend system design?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a lemonade stand. Frontend design is like making the stand look nice and serve customers quickly so they enjoy the experience. Backend design is like making sure you have enough lemons and sugar in the back to keep up with demand. Both are important, but they focus on different things.","detailedExplanation":"

Frontend system design and backend system design serve different primary goals, though both require trade-offs and scaling considerations.

Frontend System Design

Frontend design centers on the client-side experience. Key concerns include:

  • Component architecture: How to break UI into reusable, maintainable components.
  • State management: Efficiently handling data flow and UI state (e.g., Redux, Context API).
  • Rendering performance: Optimizing paint, reflow, and using techniques like virtualization or code splitting.
  • Network optimization: Reducing latency via CDN, caching, lazy loading, and minimizing bundle size.
  • Perceived performance: Using skeleton screens, optimistic updates, and smooth animations to make the app feel fast.

Backend System Design

Backend design focuses on server-side infrastructure and data handling:

  • Throughput: Handling many requests per second.
  • Database scaling: Sharding, replication, indexing.
  • Service architecture: Microservices vs monolith, load balancing, caching layers.
  • Data consistency: ACID vs BASE, distributed transactions.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming frontend design doesn't need to scale. In reality, frontend must handle millions of users via efficient asset delivery, state synchronization, and client-side performance. Ignoring frontend scaling leads to poor user experience even if the backend is robust.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Frontend Design] --> B[User Experience] A --> C[Client Architecture] A --> D[Rendering Performance] E[Backend Design] --> F[Throughput] E --> G[Database Scaling] E --> H[Server Infrastructure] B --> I[Perceived Performance] C --> J[Component & State] D --> K[Optimization]"}]
qgen_v4:7cd507043810df28:
[{"question":"In a frontend system design interview, how do you categorize requirements and why is this important?","expectedPoints":["Separate functional, non-functional, and constraints.","Functional: what the product does (e.g., post, like, comment).","Non-functional: performance, accessibility, SEO, offline support.","Constraints: device, browser, network, language, framework.","Categorization helps prioritize and make trade-offs."],"followUps":["Give an example of a trade-off between a functional and non-functional requirement.","How would you handle a constraint like supporting IE11?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a treehouse. You need to decide what it does (like a slide or a swing), how well it works (strong wood, safe), and what limits you have (only a small backyard, limited tools). Categorizing helps you plan and make smart choices.","detailedExplanation":"

In frontend system design, categorizing requirements into functional, non-functional, and constraints is a foundational step. It ensures you address all aspects of the system before diving into architecture.

Functional Requirements

These describe what the product does from the user's perspective. Examples: user can post, like, comment, infinite scroll, upload images. They define the core features.

Non-functional Requirements

These define how well the product performs. They include: load speed, smoothness (60fps), offline support, accessibility (a11y), SEO, security, and reliability. Often, these are the hardest to achieve and require careful trade-offs.

Constraints

These are boundaries you must work within: target devices (mobile vs desktop), supported browsers (e.g., IE11), network conditions (3G), multi-language support, framework choice (React, Vue), and team size.

Why It Matters

Categorization helps you prioritize and make informed trade-offs. For example, you might sacrifice some animation smoothness (non-functional) to support older browsers (constraint). It also guides your tech stack decisions and ensures you don't miss critical aspects like offline support or accessibility.

Common Pitfall

Mixing functional and non-functional requirements or ignoring constraints early can lead to a design that is either too vague or technically infeasible. Always list them explicitly and revisit during the design.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Requirements] --> B[Functional] A --> C[Non-functional] A --> D[Constraints] B --> E[Post, Like, Comment] C --> F[Speed, Offline, A11y] D --> G[Device, Browser, Network]"}]
tr_v2:52379e0b1133077f: The "golden" set of questions to ask
qgen_v4:1bdf52e08ed3b7fa:
[{"question":"How would you decompose a complex frontend application into components? Walk through your thought process for a real-time collaborative document editor.","expectedPoints":["Identifies core concerns: editing, collaboration, persistence, UI chrome.","Separates state management (e.g., document model, cursor positions) from rendering.","Defines component hierarchy with clear responsibilities (e.g., Editor, Toolbar, CollaborationOverlay).","Considers communication patterns: props, callbacks, or shared stores.","Mentions trade-offs like reusability vs. performance for real-time updates."],"followUps":["How would you handle the collaboration layer—WebSocket or polling? Why?","What if the document grows very large? How does your decomposition affect performance?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a giant Lego castle with your friends. You need to decide who builds the towers, who builds the walls, and how you'll share the bricks. In a collaborative document editor, we split the app into pieces like the typing area, the toolbar, and the part that shows where your friend is typing. Each piece has its own job, and they talk to each other through a central plan (state management).","detailedExplanation":"

When decomposing a complex frontend application like a real-time collaborative document editor, the goal is to achieve separation of concerns — each module has a single, well-defined responsibility. This makes the system easier to understand, test, and maintain.

Step 1: Identify Core Concerns

Start by listing the primary features: document editing (text input, formatting), real-time collaboration (multiple cursors, presence), persistence (auto-save), and UI chrome (toolbar, menus). Each concern should map to a distinct part of the architecture.

Step 2: Define High-Level Components

  • Editor Component: Handles the rich text editing surface, manages the contenteditable or uses a library like Slate/ProseMirror.
  • Collaboration Layer: Manages WebSocket connections, broadcasts local changes, and applies remote changes. Often implemented as a separate service or hook.
  • State Store: Holds the document model, cursor positions, and user presence. Could be a single store (Redux, Zustand) or multiple stores for different domains.
  • UI Components: Toolbar, sidebar, modals — these are mostly presentational and receive data via props.

Step 3: Communication Patterns

Components communicate via props (parent to child), callbacks (child to parent), or a shared state store. For real-time updates, a pub/sub pattern or a reactive store (e.g., MobX) works well to avoid prop drilling.

Why It Matters in Production

Proper decomposition allows teams to work in parallel, reuse components across features, and isolate performance bottlenecks. For example, the collaboration layer can be optimized independently without touching the editor UI.

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering: creating too many tiny components can lead to excessive re-renders and prop passing. Balance granularity with performance — use React.memo or selectors to prevent unnecessary updates.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart TD A[App Shell] --> B[Editor] A --> C[Toolbar] A --> D[Collaboration Overlay] B --> E[Document Model] D --> F[WebSocket Client] E --> G[State Store] F --> G G --> B G --> D"}]
qgen_v4:4a328f4299442d44:
[{"question":"How would you test a layered frontend architecture to ensure data flows correctly from the server to the UI and user interactions propagate back?","expectedPoints":["Identify each layer: network, store, view model, view.","Use integration tests for data flow across layers.","Mock external dependencies (API) to isolate layers.","Test event propagation from view back to store/server.","Verify that each layer can be tested independently."],"followUps":["How would you test the view model layer without rendering UI components?","What tradeoffs exist between mocking the store vs. using a real store in integration tests?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine a restaurant: the kitchen (server) sends food to the waiter (network layer), who brings it to the counter (store), then the chef (view model) prepares it for the customer (view). Testing means checking that each step works correctly—like making sure the waiter doesn't drop the food and the chef reads the order right.","detailedExplanation":"

In a layered frontend architecture, data flows unidirectionally from the server to the UI, while events (user interactions) flow in the opposite direction. Testing such an architecture requires a strategy that validates each layer's responsibility and the integration between them.

Key Testing Layers

  • Data/Network Layer: Test API client logic, caching, retry mechanisms, and token management. Use msw or similar to mock server responses.
  • State/Store Layer: Test reducers, selectors, and derived state. For server cache (e.g., React Query), test cache invalidation and refetching.
  • View Model / Controller: Test hooks and container components that orchestrate business logic. Use renderHook from React Testing Library to test hooks in isolation.
  • View / UI Components: Test presentational components with render and screen, focusing on rendering based on props.

Integration Testing

To ensure data flows correctly, write integration tests that exercise multiple layers together. For example, render a container component that uses a hook, which in turn calls an API client. Mock the network layer to return controlled data, then assert that the UI displays the expected output. Similarly, simulate user events (e.g., button clicks) and verify that the store updates and the correct API call is made.

Common Pitfall

Over-mocking can lead to tests that pass but don't reflect real behavior. For instance, mocking the store entirely might hide issues with selector logic or state shape. Prefer integration tests that use the real store (or a lightweight version) and only mock the network boundary.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR Server[Server/API] -->|Data| Network[Network Layer] Network -->|Normalized Data| Store[State/Store] Store -->|Derived State| ViewModel[View Model] ViewModel -->|Props| View[View/UI] View -->|Events| ViewModel ViewModel -->|Actions| Store Store -->|Mutations| Network Network -->|Requests| Server"}]
qgen_v4:a38b832ffc432942:
[{"question":"How does the Container/Presentational pattern improve testability and maintainability in a React application?","expectedPoints":["Container components handle data fetching, state, and logic; presentational components only render props.","Separation allows presentational components to be unit-tested independently of data sources.","Changes to data logic (e.g., API) don't affect UI components, and vice versa.","Presentational components are reusable across different containers or projects.","Easier to use with Storybook or visual regression testing for UI components."],"followUps":["What are the trade-offs of using this pattern with React Hooks?","Can you refactor a mixed component into container and presentational parts?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy car that can drive and make sounds. The 'smart' part is the remote control (container) that decides when to drive and beep. The 'dumb' part is the car itself (presentational) that just moves and beeps when told. If you want to change the car's color, you don't need to change the remote. This makes it easy to test the car separately and reuse it with different remotes.","detailedExplanation":"

The Container/Presentational pattern separates components into two categories: container (smart) components that manage data, state, and logic, and presentational (dumb) components that only render UI based on props.

How it works

Container components handle side effects like API calls, state management, and event handlers. They pass data and callbacks as props to presentational components, which are pure functions of their props. Presentational components contain no business logic or state beyond UI state (e.g., toggle open/close).

Why it matters in production

  • Testability: Presentational components can be unit-tested by simply passing props and asserting rendered output. No mocking of API or state is needed.
  • Reusability: The same presentational component can be used in different containers or even different projects, as long as the prop interface matches.
  • Maintainability: Changes to data fetching logic are isolated to containers; UI changes are isolated to presentational components. This reduces regression risk.
  • Tooling: Presentational components are ideal for Storybook, visual regression testing, and design systems.

Common pitfall

Over-engineering: With React Hooks, many developers now co-locate logic and UI in functional components, making the pattern less necessary. However, for complex components or when testability is critical, the separation still provides clear benefits. Avoid splitting every component; use it when you need reuse or isolated testing.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR subgraph Container A[API call] --> B[State] B --> C[Props] end subgraph Presentational D[Render UI] end C --> D"}]
qgen_v4:7de461833c05d85f:
[{"question":"What are the main trade-offs of using micro-frontends in a large-scale application?","expectedPoints":["Independent team deployment and codebase splitting","Increased complexity in build, integration, and bundle duplication","Risk of UX inconsistency across micro-frontends","Suitable for large organizations with multiple teams"],"followUps":["How would you handle shared dependencies to avoid bundle duplication?","What strategies can ensure UX consistency across independently developed micro-frontends?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a giant Lego castle that many friends build together. Micro-frontends let each friend build their own tower separately, so they can work at the same time. But then you have to make sure the towers fit together nicely, and you might end up with extra bricks because each tower has its own set. It's great for big teams but adds extra work to keep everything consistent.","detailedExplanation":"

Micro-frontends are an architectural pattern where a large frontend application is decomposed into smaller, independent pieces, each owned by a separate team. This approach is inspired by microservices on the backend and aims to solve scalability issues in large organizations.

Benefits

  • Independent deployment: Each team can deploy their part without coordinating with others, enabling faster release cycles.
  • Codebase splitting: Smaller, more manageable codebases reduce cognitive load and improve developer productivity.
  • Technology flexibility: Teams can choose different frameworks (e.g., React, Vue, Angular) for their micro-frontend, allowing gradual migration or experimentation.

Trade-offs

  • Bundle duplication: Shared dependencies (like React or utility libraries) may be duplicated across micro-frontends, increasing the overall bundle size and affecting performance.
  • Integration complexity: Combining multiple micro-frontends into a cohesive page requires careful orchestration, routing, and communication mechanisms (e.g., custom events, shared state).
  • UX inconsistency: Without a shared design system, each team may implement UI components differently, leading to a fragmented user experience.
  • Operational overhead: Managing multiple build pipelines, deployment strategies, and monitoring tools adds complexity.

When to Use

Micro-frontends are most beneficial for large organizations with multiple autonomous teams working on a single product. They are less suitable for small teams or projects where the overhead outweighs the benefits.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is adopting micro-frontends without a clear shared design system and integration strategy. Teams may end up with inconsistent UI and complex integration code that negates the benefits of independence.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Large App] --> B[Micro-frontend 1] A --> C[Micro-frontend 2] A --> D[Micro-frontend 3] B --> E[Team A] C --> F[Team B] D --> G[Team C] B --> H[Bundle Duplication] C --> H D --> H H --> I[Performance Impact]"}]
tr_v2:07e09a82a76d1e9a: Rendering Patterns: CSR · SSR · SSG · ISR
qgen_v4:15e1df33f4502cdf:
[{"question":"Compare CSR, SSR, SSG, and ISR in terms of when HTML is generated and the trade-offs for SEO, time-to-interactive, and server cost.","expectedPoints":["CSR: HTML generated client-side via JS; poor SEO, slow TTI, cheap server.","SSR: HTML generated per request on server; good SEO, faster FCP but slower TTI, higher server cost.","SSG: HTML generated at build time; excellent SEO, fast load, static hosting, no server cost.","ISR: Hybrid of SSG and SSR; revalidates pages incrementally; balances freshness and performance.","Trade-offs: CSR for apps needing rich interactivity, SSR for dynamic content, SSG for static content, ISR for large sites with frequent updates."],"followUps":["How would you decide between SSR and SSG for an e-commerce product page?","What caching strategies would you use with ISR to avoid stale data?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're baking cookies. CSR is like giving someone a recipe and ingredients and letting them bake at home (slow start). SSR is like baking fresh cookies for each customer (fast first bite but more work). SSG is like baking all cookies before the store opens (super fast but can't change). ISR is like baking most cookies ahead but refreshing a few when needed (best of both).","detailedExplanation":"

Rendering patterns determine where and when HTML is generated, impacting SEO, performance, and cost.

CSR (Client-Side Rendering)

HTML is generated in the browser using JavaScript. The server sends a minimal HTML shell, and JS fetches data and renders the page. Pros: Cheap server, rich interactivity. Cons: Poor SEO (crawlers may not execute JS), slow time-to-interactive (TTI). Used for web apps like dashboards.

SSR (Server-Side Rendering)

HTML is generated on the server for each request. The server fetches data, renders the page, and sends fully populated HTML. Pros: Good SEO, fast first contentful paint (FCP). Cons: Higher server load, slower TTI because JS still needs to hydrate. Common for e-commerce or news sites.

SSG (Static Site Generation)

HTML is generated at build time. All pages are pre-rendered as static files. Pros: Excellent SEO, blazing fast load, low server cost (can be served from CDN). Cons: Not suitable for dynamic content; requires rebuild for updates. Ideal for blogs, documentation.

ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration)

Combines SSG and SSR. Pages are pre-rendered statically but can be revalidated and updated after deployment. Pros: Fresh content without full rebuild, good performance. Cons: Complexity in cache invalidation, potential stale data during revalidation. Used for large e-commerce sites with frequent updates.

Common Pitfall

Choosing SSR for a mostly static site wastes server resources; prefer SSG. Conversely, using SSG for highly dynamic user-specific content leads to stale data. Always analyze content update frequency and interactivity needs.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Request] --> B{Pattern?} B -->|CSR| C[Minimal HTML] C --> D[Browser loads JS] D --> E[Fetch data] E --> F[Render HTML] B -->|SSR| G[Server renders HTML] G --> H[Full HTML sent] H --> I[Browser hydrates] B -->|SSG| J[Pre-built HTML] J --> K[CDN serves] B -->|ISR| L[Stale HTML served] L --> M[Revalidate in background] M --> N[Update cache]"}]
qgen_v4:78f4d114bd5f57c6:
[{"question":"Compare CSR, SSR, SSG, and ISR in terms of when HTML is generated, SEO impact, and FCP performance.","expectedPoints":["CSR: HTML generated on client after JS loads; poor SEO; slow FCP.","SSR: HTML generated per request on server; good SEO; fast FCP.","SSG: HTML generated at build time; best SEO; fastest FCP.","ISR: HTML generated at build + revalidated periodically; good SEO; fast FCP.","Trade-offs: CSR for interactive apps, SSR for dynamic content, SSG for static content, ISR for hybrid."],"followUps":["When would you choose ISR over SSG?","How does hydration affect interactivity in SSR?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy. CSR builds the toy only after you open the box and read instructions. SSR gives you a fully built toy but you need to add stickers later. SSG gives you a toy built before you even order it. ISR gives you a toy that updates itself every few days. Each has different speeds and freshness.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your understanding of rendering strategies in modern web development. The key difference lies in when and where HTML is generated.

CSR (Client-Side Rendering)

HTML is initially empty or minimal. The browser downloads a JavaScript bundle, executes it, fetches data, and then renders the UI. This leads to poor SEO because crawlers may not execute JS, and slow FCP (First Contentful Paint) as the user sees a blank page until JS runs. Best for authenticated dashboards where SEO is not critical.

SSR (Server-Side Rendering)

HTML is generated on the server for each request. The user receives a fully rendered page, improving SEO and FCP. However, interactivity requires hydration — attaching JS event handlers to the static HTML. TTFB (Time to First Byte) can be slower due to server processing. Ideal for personalized, dynamic content.

SSG (Static Site Generation)

HTML is pre-built at deploy time and served via CDN. This yields the fastest FCP and best SEO because content is static. However, data is stale after build. Perfect for blogs, documentation, and marketing pages.

ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration)

Combines SSG with periodic revalidation. Pages are built statically but can be regenerated on demand or at intervals. Offers a balance of speed and freshness. Suitable for e-commerce product pages that update occasionally.

Common pitfall: Assuming SSR always improves performance. In reality, it can increase server load and TTFB. Also, hydration can cause a flash of un-interactive content.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR subgraph CSR A[Empty HTML] --> B[Download JS] --> C[Fetch Data] --> D[Render UI] end subgraph SSR E[Request] --> F[Server Render HTML] --> G[Send Full HTML] --> H[Hydrate] end subgraph SSG I[Build Time] --> J[Generate HTML] --> K[CDN Cache] --> L[Instant Serve] end subgraph ISR M[Build] --> N[Static HTML] --> O[Revalidate Periodically] --> P[Update Cache] end"}]
qgen_v4:84e4f9a4d89669a2:
[{"question":"How would you decide between SSR, CSR, and Islands Architecture for a new e-commerce product page?","expectedPoints":["Considers SEO needs for product pages (SSR preferred).","Evaluates interactivity requirements (e.g., add-to-cart button vs. static description).","Mentions performance trade-offs like TTI and bundle size.","Proposes Islands Architecture for mostly static content with isolated interactive widgets.","Acknowledges user-specific content (e.g., personalized recommendations) may need CSR or RSC."],"followUps":["How would you handle a real-time stock indicator on the product page?","What if the page has a heavy 3D product viewer?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy store website. Some parts, like the toy name and picture, are the same for everyone and can be printed once on the server (SSR). But a 'buy now' button needs to work when clicked, so it needs JavaScript. Islands Architecture means you only add JavaScript to the interactive parts, like buttons, while the rest stays lightweight.","detailedExplanation":"

When deciding between SSR, CSR, and Islands Architecture for an e-commerce product page, you must evaluate SEO requirements, interactivity needs, and performance budgets.

SSR (Server-Side Rendering)

SSR sends fully rendered HTML from the server, which is great for SEO and first paint. However, hydration attaches JavaScript event handlers, which can delay Time to Interactive (TTI). Use SSR for the main product description, images, and price—content that must be indexed by search engines.

CSR (Client-Side Rendering)

CSR sends a minimal HTML shell and loads JavaScript to render content. This is suitable for user-specific sections like a personalized recommendation carousel that doesn't need SEO. But CSR can hurt initial load performance and SEO.

Islands Architecture

This pattern renders most of the page as static HTML (no JavaScript) and only hydrates isolated interactive components—called islands—like the add-to-cart button or a size selector. Tools like Astro or Qwik implement this. It reduces JavaScript payload and improves TTI.

Decision Framework

  • SEO-critical content → SSR or static generation.
  • Highly interactive widgets → Islands Architecture with selective hydration.
  • User-specific dynamic data → CSR or React Server Components (RSC).

A common pitfall is over-engineering: using SSR for everything when a static page with a few interactive islands would suffice. Always measure Lighthouse scores and Core Web Vitals to validate your choice.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[E-commerce Product Page] --> B{Content Type?} B -->|SEO-critical| C[SSR] B -->|Interactive Widget| D[Islands Architecture] B -->|User-specific| E[CSR / RSC] C --> F[Fast First Paint] D --> G[Minimal JS, Fast TTI] E --> H[Personalized, but heavier]"}]
qgen_v4:f965ce008984002c:
[{"question":"Explain the concept of hydration in the context of server-side rendering (SSR).","expectedPoints":["Hydration attaches JS event handlers to server-rendered HTML.","It transforms static HTML into an interactive client-side app.","Hydration is necessary for frameworks like React or Vue.","It can cause performance overhead if not optimized.","Mismatch between server HTML and client JS can cause bugs."],"followUps":["What happens if hydration fails or is skipped?","How does partial hydration (islands) differ from full hydration?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you get a coloring book with all the lines already drawn (that's the server-rendered HTML). Hydration is like adding the colors and making the pictures interactive—so you can click on them and they respond. Without hydration, the page would just be a static picture.","detailedExplanation":"

Hydration is the process in client-side JavaScript frameworks (like React, Vue, or Svelte) where the static HTML generated by the server is 'activated' by attaching event listeners, state, and interactivity. It bridges the gap between server-rendered content and a fully interactive single-page application.

How It Works

When a page is server-rendered, the server sends fully-formed HTML to the browser. The browser displays it immediately (good for SEO and perceived performance). Then, the framework's JavaScript bundle loads and hydrates the DOM: it re-creates the component tree in memory, matches it to the existing DOM nodes, and attaches event handlers. The page becomes interactive without re-rendering the entire DOM.

Why It Matters in Production

  • Performance: Hydration can be expensive—the framework must reconstruct the entire virtual DOM and reconcile it with the real DOM. This can block the main thread and delay interactivity (Time to Interactive).
  • User Experience: Users see content quickly (thanks to SSR) but may experience a delay before they can click buttons or type in forms.
  • SEO: SSR with hydration ensures search engines see the full content, while users get a rich app.

Common Pitfall

A frequent bug is hydration mismatch: the server-rendered HTML differs from what the client-side JavaScript expects (e.g., due to dynamic data like timestamps or browser-specific features). This can cause warnings, broken UI, or even full re-renders. Solutions include using deterministic data or suppressing hydration for dynamic parts.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Server] -->|Sends static HTML| B[Browser] B --> C[Display HTML immediately] C --> D[Load JS bundle] D --> E[Hydrate: attach events] E --> F[Interactive page]"}]
qgen_v4:6088cd054d473641:
[{"question":"How would you optimize a React app to improve its Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)?","expectedPoints":["Identifies LCP as render time of largest visible element.","Suggests optimizing images (compression, lazy loading, modern formats).","Mentions server-side rendering or static generation for faster initial HTML.","Discusses reducing render-blocking resources (CSS/JS).","Considers preloading critical assets like hero images or fonts."],"followUps":["How would you debug a slow LCP in production?","What tradeoffs exist between SSR and client-side rendering for LCP?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're baking a cake and the first thing people see is the frosting. LCP is how fast that frosting appears. To make it faster, you could pre-bake the cake (server-side rendering), use a smaller frosting layer (compress images), or start decorating while the cake is still baking (preload critical resources).","detailedExplanation":"

Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures the time it takes for the largest visible content element (e.g., hero image, heading) to render. It's a key Core Web Vital that impacts user perception of speed and SEO.

Optimization Strategies

  • Optimize images: Compress, use modern formats (WebP/AVIF), lazy-load below-fold images, and set explicit dimensions to avoid layout shifts.
  • Server-side rendering (SSR) or static generation (SSG): Deliver fully rendered HTML to the browser, reducing client-side work. Frameworks like Next.js or Nuxt.js help.
  • Reduce render-blocking resources: Inline critical CSS, defer non-critical JS, and use preload for key assets like fonts or hero images.
  • Preload critical resources: Use <link rel='preload'> for the LCP element (e.g., hero image) so the browser fetches it early.
  • Minimize JavaScript: Code-split, tree-shake, and defer third-party scripts that delay main thread work.

Common Pitfall

Over-optimizing LCP at the expense of other metrics. For example, aggressive SSR can increase Time to First Byte (TTFB) or hurt First Input Delay (FID) if the server is slow. Always measure the full user experience.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Request] --> B[Server] B --> C[SSR/SSG HTML] C --> D[Browser Render] D --> E[LCP Element] subgraph Optimizations F[Image Optimization] G[Preload Critical] H[Reduce Blocking] end F --> E G --> E H --> D"}]

qgen_v4:1b206bf9704db69e:
[{"question":"How does React.lazy with Suspense implement code splitting, and what are the trade-offs compared to manual dynamic imports?","expectedPoints":["React.lazy uses dynamic import() to split code at component level","Suspense handles loading state with fallback UI","Trade-offs: simpler API vs less control over chunk naming and preloading","Impact on bundle size and initial load time","Potential issues: flash of loading content, no SSR support without extra libraries"],"followUps":["How would you preload a lazy-loaded component before navigation?","What happens if the lazy component fails to load? How do you handle errors?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge toy box, but you only need one toy at a time. Instead of carrying the whole box, you just grab the toy you need. React.lazy lets your app load only the parts it needs right now, making it start faster. Suspense shows a placeholder (like a loading spinner) while the toy is being fetched.","detailedExplanation":"

Code splitting is a technique that breaks your JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks, loading only what's needed for the current view. React.lazy is a built-in React function that enables component-level code splitting using dynamic import() syntax. It returns a component that can be rendered inside a Suspense wrapper, which shows a fallback (e.g., a skeleton) while the chunk is loading.

How it works

When React encounters a lazy component, it triggers a dynamic import. The import returns a promise that resolves to the module. React.lazy wraps this promise and provides a component that Suspense can await. The chunk is fetched on-demand, reducing the initial bundle size.

Trade-offs vs manual dynamic imports

  • Simplicity: React.lazy integrates seamlessly with Suspense, requiring less boilerplate than manual import() with state management.
  • Control: Manual imports give you finer control over chunk naming (via webpack magic comments) and preloading strategies (e.g., prefetch).
  • Error handling: With React.lazy, you need an error boundary to catch load failures; manual imports allow try/catch.
  • SSR: React.lazy does not work with server-side rendering out of the box; you need libraries like @loadable/components.

Why it matters in production

Large bundles degrade First Contentful Paint (FCP) and Largest Contentful Paint (LCP). Code splitting ensures users only download code for the current route, drastically improving perceived performance. For example, a dashboard with heavy charting libraries can be split so that the login page loads in under 1 second.

Common pitfall

Over-splitting can lead to too many small HTTP requests, causing network overhead. Also, forgetting to wrap lazy components in Suspense will throw an error. Always test with slow network throttling to ensure the fallback UI appears smoothly.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User navigates] --> B[React.lazy triggers] B --> C[Dynamic import()] C --> D[Chunk loaded?] D -- No --> E[Suspense fallback] D -- Yes --> F[Render component] E --> C"}]
tr_v2:f181dbde5df1e1be: List Virtualization — the secret to handling long lists
qgen_v4:3be41944359adaed:
[{"question":"How would you implement list virtualization for a chat application that displays 10,000 messages, and what are the key considerations?","expectedPoints":["Only render items within viewport plus overscan buffer","Reuse DOM nodes as user scrolls (windowing)","Handle variable item heights (e.g., dynamic measurement)","Maintain scroll position when new items are added","Use a library like react-window or @tanstack/virtual"],"followUps":["How would you handle items with dynamic heights?","What happens to scroll position when prepending messages?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge list of toys, but you can only see a few at a time through a small window. Instead of building all the toys, you only build the ones you can see right now, and when you move the window, you swap them out. That's list virtualization—it makes scrolling fast and saves memory.","detailedExplanation":"

List virtualization (also called windowing) is a performance technique that renders only the visible portion of a long list, plus a small buffer (overscan). This drastically reduces DOM nodes, memory usage, and layout cost, enabling smooth scrolling even with thousands of items.

How It Works

The virtualizer calculates which items are within the viewport based on scroll position and item heights. It then renders only those items, reusing DOM nodes as the user scrolls. Libraries like react-window, react-virtuoso, or @tanstack/virtual abstract this logic.

Key Considerations

  • Item height estimation: Fixed heights are simple; variable heights require measuring after render or using a resize observer.
  • Scroll position preservation: When items are added/removed (e.g., prepending messages), the scroll offset must be adjusted to avoid jumping.
  • Overscan count: Extra items rendered above/below viewport to reduce blank areas during fast scrolling.
  • Accessibility: Ensure keyboard navigation and screen readers work correctly with virtualized lists.

Why It Matters in Production

Without virtualization, rendering 10,000 DOM nodes causes jank, high memory, and long layout times. Virtualization keeps the DOM small (~10–20 nodes), making the UI responsive and reducing CPU/GPU load.

Common Pitfall

Assuming all items have the same height. If heights vary, the virtualizer may misalign scroll positions, causing flickering or blank spaces. Use dynamic measurement or a consistent height estimation strategy.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Long List 10,000 items] --> B[Virtualizer Calculates visible range] B --> C[Render only viewport items] C --> D[+ Overscan buffer items] D --> E[DOM nodes ~10-20 items] E --> F[Scroll event updates range] F --> B"}]
qgen_v4:19b36dda6ad1379c:
[{"question":"How would you optimize a page that renders 50,000 products and lags on scroll?","expectedPoints":["Identify DOM overload as root cause","Propose list virtualization (windowing)","Explain that only visible items are rendered","Mention memory and scroll performance benefits","Reject CSS-only or hardware solutions"],"followUps":["What library would you use for virtualization?","How does virtualization handle dynamic item heights?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge toy catalog with 50,000 pages. Instead of holding all pages in your hands (which is heavy and slow), you only keep the page you're looking at and the next few. When you turn a page, you swap it in. That's what list virtualization does for a web page: it only keeps a few items in memory, making scrolling fast and smooth.","detailedExplanation":"

What is List Virtualization?

List virtualization (also called windowing) is a technique where only the items currently visible in the viewport (plus a small buffer) are rendered in the DOM. As the user scrolls, items that leave the viewport are removed, and new items are added. This keeps the DOM size small (e.g., 10–20 nodes) regardless of the total list length (e.g., 50,000 items).

How It Works

The library (e.g., react-window, react-virtualized) calculates which items should be visible based on the scroll position and item heights. It uses absolute positioning or transforms to place items correctly, and recycles DOM nodes to avoid creating new elements. The total scroll height is simulated so the browser shows a proper scrollbar.

Why It Matters in Production

Rendering 50,000 DOM nodes causes layout thrashing, high memory usage, and janky scrolling. Virtualization reduces memory footprint by 99%+ and keeps the main thread responsive. It's essential for infinite scroll, large tables, chat logs, and any data-heavy list.

Common Pitfall

Assuming all items have the same height. If heights vary, you must measure or estimate them, or use a library that supports dynamic heights. Incorrect height estimation leads to scroll jumps and broken layout.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Scroll Event] --> B[Calculate Visible Range] B --> C[Render Only Visible Items] C --> D[DOM ~20 nodes] D --> E[Smooth Scroll] subgraph Virtualization B C end F[50,000 items] -.->|not rendered| G[Memory Saved]"}]
qgen_v4:c964cbea71a9ff7b:
[{"question":"In a frontend-heavy application with complex data relationships, how would you decide between REST and GraphQL?","expectedPoints":["Recognizes REST's over/under-fetching problem in complex UIs","Understands GraphQL's single endpoint and declarative data fetching","Considers caching complexity and tooling support","Evaluates team expertise and API maturity","Mentions trade-offs in versioning and performance optimization"],"followUps":["How would you handle caching in a GraphQL client?","What are the downsides of using GraphQL for simple CRUD apps?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you're ordering a pizza. REST is like calling a specific number for each topping—you might get extra toppings you don't want or miss some. GraphQL is like ordering a custom pizza from one place where you list exactly what you want. It's more flexible but harder to keep track of leftovers.","detailedExplanation":"

When deciding between REST and GraphQL for a frontend-heavy application, the key is to evaluate the data fetching patterns and the complexity of the UI.

REST Advantages

REST uses multiple fixed endpoints, each returning a predefined data structure. This makes caching simple because HTTP caching works per URL. It's ideal for simple APIs where the client always needs the same data. However, REST often leads to over-fetching (getting extra data) or under-fetching (needing multiple requests), which can hurt performance in complex UIs.

GraphQL Advantages

GraphQL exposes a single endpoint and lets the client specify exactly which fields it needs. This eliminates over/under-fetching, making it perfect for UIs with many interconnected components (e.g., dashboards, social feeds). It also supports schema evolution without versioning—deprecated fields can be gradually removed.

Trade-offs

GraphQL's flexibility comes at a cost: caching is more complex because queries vary. Clients often use normalized caches (e.g., Apollo Client) to deduplicate data. Additionally, GraphQL requires more server-side effort (resolvers, batching) and can lead to N+1 query problems if not optimized. REST is simpler to implement and debug, but may require multiple versions (/v1, /v2) as the API evolves.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is choosing GraphQL for a simple CRUD app where REST would suffice, adding unnecessary complexity. Conversely, using REST for a highly interactive UI can result in many round trips and poor performance.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Client] -->|REST| B[Endpoint /users] A -->|REST| C[Endpoint /posts] A -->|GraphQL| D[Single Endpoint /graphql] B --> E[Fixed Data: over/under fetch] C --> E D --> F[Custom Query: exact fields] E --> G[Simple caching] F --> H[Complex caching]"}]
qgen_v4:4b812387ecc059b9:
[{"question":"For a social media feed with constantly arriving new posts, which pagination strategy would you choose and why?","expectedPoints":["Identifies cursor-based pagination as the best fit","Explains that offset pagination causes duplication or missing items when new posts are inserted","Mentions that cursor-based pagination uses a stable pointer (e.g., timestamp or ID) to avoid shifting","Notes that infinite scroll is a UI pattern often backed by cursor-based pagination","Acknowledges trade-off: cursor-based pagination does not support arbitrary page jumps"],"followUps":["How would you implement cursor-based pagination for a feed that shows posts from followed users?","What happens if the cursor points to a deleted item?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're reading a story that keeps getting new pages added at the beginning. If you use page numbers, after a new page is added, page 2 now shows what used to be page 1, so you might miss or see the same content twice. Instead, you use a bookmark (cursor) that marks your exact spot, so even if new pages are added, you always continue from where you left off.","detailedExplanation":"

For a social media feed where new posts are constantly inserted at the top, cursor-based pagination is the recommended strategy. This approach uses a unique, sequential token (e.g., a timestamp, post ID, or a combination) to mark the position of the last item fetched. The client sends this cursor in subsequent requests to retrieve the next set of items.

Why not offset pagination?

Offset pagination (?page=2&limit=20) relies on a fixed offset from the beginning of the dataset. When new items are inserted, the offset shifts: what was on page 2 might now be on page 3, causing duplicate items (if the user scrolls down and new items push old ones to the next page) or missed items (if items are deleted). This is unacceptable for a real-time feed.

How cursor-based pagination works

In cursor-based pagination, the API returns a cursor (e.g., after=cursor123) that points to the last item in the current page. The next request includes this cursor, and the server returns items that come after it. Since the cursor is a stable pointer to a specific record, new items inserted before it do not affect the result set. This ensures consistency even as data changes.

Relation to infinite scroll

Infinite scroll is a UI pattern that automatically loads more content when the user scrolls to the bottom, typically using IntersectionObserver. Under the hood, it often uses cursor-based pagination to fetch the next batch of items without page jumps.

Trade-offs

  • No arbitrary page jumps: Users cannot skip to page 5 directly because there is no concept of page numbers. This is acceptable for feeds where sequential browsing is natural.
  • Cursor expiration: If the cursor points to a deleted item, the server must handle it gracefully (e.g., return items after the next valid ID).

Common pitfall

A common mistake is using a cursor that is not unique or sortable. For example, using a simple timestamp may cause issues if two items have the same timestamp. Always use a combination of fields (e.g., timestamp + ID) to guarantee uniqueness and stable ordering.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User scrolls] --> B[IntersectionObserver triggers] B --> C[Send request with cursor] C --> D[Server queries items after cursor] D --> E[Return new items + next cursor] E --> F[Append items to feed] F --> A"}]

[{"question":"How would you handle race conditions in a search autocomplete component when the user types quickly?","expectedPoints":["Use AbortController to cancel previous in-flight requests.","Combine with debounce (e.g., 300ms) to reduce request frequency.","Check if the response corresponds to the latest query before updating state.","Handle abort errors gracefully (e.g., ignore AbortError)."],"followUps":["What happens if the user clears the input? How would you handle that?","How would you test this behavior in a unit test?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're asking a friend to fetch a book from the library. If you change your mind and ask for a different book before they come back, you need to tell them to stop looking for the first one. In a search box, when you type fast, each keystroke sends a request. AbortController is like a walkie-talkie to tell the old request to stop, so only the latest one matters.","detailedExplanation":"

Race conditions in autocomplete occur when network responses arrive out of order: a response for an earlier query arrives after a later query's response, overwriting the correct result. This is a common pitfall in search UIs.

Solution: AbortController + Debounce

The primary solution is to use the AbortController API to cancel the previous in-flight request before sending a new one. This ensures only the latest request completes. Additionally, a debounce (e.g., 300ms) reduces the number of requests fired during rapid typing.

Implementation pattern:

  • Declare a mutable controller variable outside the search function.
  • At the start of the search function, call controller?.abort() to cancel any pending request.
  • Create a new AbortController and pass its signal to fetch.
  • In the response handler, check if the query is still the latest (e.g., compare with a ref storing the latest query).
  • Catch AbortError and ignore it, as it's intentional cancellation.

Why It Matters in Production

Without this, users see flickering or incorrect results, degrading UX. In high-traffic apps, it also reduces unnecessary server load. This pattern is essential for any real-time search, filtering, or typeahead component.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to handle the AbortError in the catch block can cause unhandled promise rejections. Always check error.name === 'AbortError' and ignore it.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User types 'rea'] --> B[Send request A] B --> C[User types 'react'] C --> D[Abort request A] D --> E[Send request B] E --> F[Response B arrives] F --> G[Update UI with B] subgraph AbortController D end"}]
qgen_v4:d0d88a760fac238d:
[{"question":"How would you design a resilient autocomplete search component that handles network failures and race conditions?","expectedPoints":["Uses debounce to limit request frequency","Cancels stale requests with AbortController","Implements retry with exponential backoff for transient failures","Shows cached or fallback results on error (graceful degradation)"],"followUps":["How would you ensure idempotency for the search requests?","What timeout value would you choose and why?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're typing a search term and each letter sends a request. If the network is slow, old results might appear after you've typed more. To fix this, we wait a tiny bit after you stop typing (debounce), cancel old requests, and if a request fails, we try again with longer waits (backoff) or show a friendly message.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a resilient autocomplete component requires handling both network failures and race conditions. The core strategy combines debouncing, request cancellation, retry with backoff, and graceful degradation.

Debounce and Request Cancellation

Use debounce (e.g., 300ms) to reduce the number of requests. When a new keystroke arrives before the debounce timer fires, reset the timer. Once the timer fires, send the request using fetch with an AbortController. Store the current controller; if a new request is about to be sent, call abort() on the previous controller to cancel the stale request. This prevents old responses from overwriting newer ones.

Retry with Exponential Backoff

For transient failures (e.g., 503), implement exponential backoff: retry after 1s, then 2s, then 4s, up to a maximum (e.g., 3 retries). Use a jitter to avoid thundering herd. Ensure idempotency by including a unique request ID or using the search query itself as an idempotency key, so retries don't cause duplicate entries on the server.

Graceful Degradation

If all retries fail, show a friendly error message (e.g., “Network error, showing cached results”) and fall back to the last successful cached results. Never show a blank screen. Also set a timeout (e.g., 5s) per request to avoid hanging.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is forgetting to cancel the previous request, leading to race conditions where an old response arrives after a new one, causing incorrect suggestions. Always abort the previous request before sending a new one.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User types] --> B[Debounce 300ms] B --> C{New keystroke?} C -->|Yes| B C -->|No| D[Abort previous request] D --> E[Send fetch with AbortController] E --> F{Success?} F -->|Yes| G[Update suggestions] F -->|No| H[Retry with backoff] H --> I{Max retries?} I -->|No| E I -->|Yes| J[Show cached/error]"}]
qgen_v4:46fe60de8e3fa586:
[{"question":"How would you design state management for a real-time collaborative document editor like Google Docs?","expectedPoints":["Categorizes state into server, UI, and local ephemeral state","Chooses appropriate tools: WebSocket for sync, Zustand/Redux for global UI, useState for local","Handles optimistic updates and conflict resolution","Considers performance: avoids unnecessary re-renders, uses selectors or atomic stores","Discusses offline support and state persistence"],"followUps":["How would you handle cursor positions of multiple users?","What tradeoffs exist between using a global store vs. multiple local stores?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are drawing on the same whiteboard. If you both draw at the same time, you need a way to combine your drawings without messing up. State management is like deciding who keeps track of what: the server remembers the final drawing, your local app remembers your own strokes, and you use a smart system to update everyone's view smoothly.","detailedExplanation":"

State management is critical in frontend applications, especially for real-time collaborative tools. The key is to categorize state and choose the right tool for each category.

State Categories

  • Server State: Data that lives on the backend (e.g., document content, user list). Use WebSocket or a library like React Query to sync.
  • UI State: Global UI controls (e.g., theme, sidebar open/close). Use a lightweight global store like Zustand or Redux.
  • Local Ephemeral State: Transient data like form inputs or cursor position. Use useState or useReducer.

Real-Time Sync

For a collaborative editor, use WebSocket for bidirectional communication. Implement optimistic updates to show changes instantly, then reconcile with server responses. Conflict resolution can use Operational Transformation (OT) or CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types).

Performance Considerations

Avoid storing everything in a single global store. Use selectors to prevent unnecessary re-renders. For cursor positions, keep them in a separate store or local state to avoid re-rendering the entire document.

Common Pitfall

Putting all state into one global store leads to over-rendering and complex debugging. Instead, keep state as close to where it's used as possible, and only lift shared state to a global store when necessary.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Action] --> B[Local State] B --> C[Optimistic Update] C --> D[WebSocket Send] D --> E[Server] E --> F[Broadcast] F --> G[Other Clients] G --> H[Conflict Resolution] H --> I[Final State]"}]
qgen_v4:b1ef2f0a173a305d:
[{"question":"How would you decide which state management tool to use for a new feature: useState, Context, Redux, or React Query?","expectedPoints":["Categorizes state into local, global UI, server, and URL state.","Matches useState for local component state (e.g., form inputs).","Recommends React Query for server state (async, caching, refetching).","Suggests Context or Redux only for truly global UI state (theme, auth).","Avoids putting server data into Redux; uses dedicated tools instead."],"followUps":["What problems arise if you store server data in Redux?","How would you handle optimistic updates with React Query?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have different kinds of toys: some you keep in your pocket (local state), some you share with friends (global state), and some you borrow from the library (server state). You wouldn't store a library book in your pocket—you'd use a library card to keep track of it. Similarly, in React, we use different tools for different types of state to avoid mess and keep things fast.","detailedExplanation":"

When designing state management for a feature, the first step is to categorize the state into one of four types: local state, global UI state, server state, and URL state. Each type has a recommended tool.

Local State

Use useState or useReducer for state that only affects a single component or its children, like form inputs or dropdown toggles. This keeps the code simple and avoids unnecessary re-renders.

Global UI State

For state shared across many components (e.g., theme, authentication status, cart), use Context for simple cases or a library like Zustand, Redux, or Jotai for more complex needs. These tools provide a centralized store and optimized re-renders.

Server State

Data from an API (e.g., user profiles, article lists) is asynchronous and can become stale. Use dedicated libraries like React Query, SWR, RTK Query, or Apollo to handle caching, background refetching, deduplication, and loading/error states. Avoid putting server data into Redux or Context, as that forces you to manually write caching logic and leads to bugs.

URL State

State that should be shareable via URL (e.g., selected tab, search query, filters) belongs in the router (e.g., React Router query params). This enables deep linking and browser navigation.

Common Pitfall

Many developers mistakenly store server data in Redux and write custom thunks for loading/error states. This duplicates effort and often results in stale data, race conditions, and poor performance. Instead, let React Query manage server state and keep Redux for truly global client-only state.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart TD A[Feature State] --> B{Type?} B -->|Local| C[useState/useReducer] B -->|Global UI| D[Context/Zustand/Redux] B -->|Server| E[React Query/SWR] B -->|URL| F[Router Query Params]"}]
qgen_v4:742ce970bb52d484:
[{"question":"Explain how unidirectional data flow in Redux helps with debugging, and describe the role of each component in the cycle.","expectedPoints":["Unidirectional flow means data moves in one direction: Action → Reducer → Store → View.","Every state change is triggered by a dispatched action, making it traceable.","Reducers are pure functions that compute new state from previous state and action.","Store holds the single source of truth; view re-renders on store updates.","Debugging is easier because you can log actions and inspect state transitions.","Common pitfall: mutating state directly in reducers instead of returning new objects."],"followUps":["How would you debug a state change that produces an unexpected result?","What happens if a reducer has side effects like API calls?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy train that only goes forward on a track. Every time you want to change something, you press a button (action), which tells the engine (reducer) to change the train's cargo (state). The train then shows the new cargo to everyone (view). Because the train only goes one way, you can always check the button you pressed to know why the cargo changed.","detailedExplanation":"

Unidirectional data flow is a core principle of Redux and Flux architectures. It enforces a strict one-way cycle: Action → Reducer → Store → View. This means data never flows backward; views cannot directly modify the store.

How it works

  • Action: A plain object describing what happened (e.g., { type: 'ADD_TODO', payload: 'Learn Redux' }).
  • Reducer: A pure function that takes the current state and an action, and returns the next state. It must not mutate the previous state.
  • Store: Holds the entire state tree. It dispatches actions to reducers and notifies views of changes.
  • View: Subscribes to store updates and re-renders accordingly.

Why it matters in production

Unidirectional flow makes state changes predictable and traceable. In large applications, you can log every action and inspect state before/after, making debugging straightforward. Tools like Redux DevTools leverage this to enable time-travel debugging.

Common pitfall

Mutating state inside a reducer (e.g., state.todos.push(newTodo)) breaks the flow because Redux relies on reference equality to detect changes. Always return a new object or array.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Action] --> B[Reducer] B --> C[Store] C --> D[View] D --> A"}]
qgen_v4:943bf0349993a009:
[{"question":"Design the props API for a reusable Modal component that supports different sizes, custom close behavior, and accessibility.","expectedPoints":["Defines clear, minimal props like isOpen, onClose, size, children.","Handles accessibility with aria attributes and focus trapping.","Supports customization via render props or slots for header/footer.","Considers edge cases like nested modals and body scroll locking.","Uses TypeScript for type safety and documentation."],"followUps":["How would you handle multiple modals open at the same time?","What if the user wants to close the modal by clicking outside or pressing Escape?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a pop-up box that can show messages or forms. A good Modal component is like a well-designed door: it should be easy to open and close, fit different rooms (sizes), and be safe for everyone (accessibility). You need to decide what buttons and settings to give to other developers so they can use it correctly without breaking things.","detailedExplanation":"

What it is: A Modal component is a UI overlay that displays content on top of the main page, often used for confirmations, forms, or alerts. Designing its props API means deciding what properties (props) the component accepts to control its behavior and appearance.

How it works

The core props include isOpen (boolean to show/hide), onClose (callback when user dismisses), size (e.g., 'sm', 'md', 'lg'), and children for content. For accessibility, add aria-labelledby and aria-describedby to link the modal to its title and description. Focus trapping ensures keyboard navigation stays within the modal. Use ReactDOM.createPortal to render the modal at the document root to avoid z-index issues.

Why it matters in production

A well-designed Modal API prevents misuse: developers can't forget to handle close events or miss accessibility. It also ensures consistency across the app. Common pitfalls include not managing body scroll (page scrolls behind modal) and not cleaning up event listeners on unmount. Use useEffect to add/remove scroll lock and escape key listener.

Common pitfall

Over-engineering: adding too many props like showCloseButton, closeOnOverlayClick, closeOnEsc can be overwhelming. Instead, provide sensible defaults and allow customization via a closeButtonProps or render prop pattern for advanced cases.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Modal Component] --> B[Props API] B --> C[isOpen] B --> D[onClose] B --> E[size] B --> F[children] B --> G[aria attributes] A --> H[Portal] A --> I[Focus trap]"}]
qgen_v4:56d2a71378dbb755:
[{"question":"Design a reusable dropdown component API that supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes, with sensible defaults and built-in accessibility.","expectedPoints":["Exposes both value+onChange (controlled) and defaultValue (uncontrolled) props.","Uses composition (e.g., Dropdown, DropdownItem) rather than a single massive config object.","Provides sensible defaults like isOpen=false, placeholder text, and keyboard navigation.","Includes ARIA roles (listbox, option), aria-expanded, and focus management out of the box.","Single responsibility: dropdown only handles selection, not styling or layout."],"followUps":["How would you handle keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Enter, Escape) in this component?","What if a user wants to customize the dropdown's trigger button appearance?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box with many toys. A good dropdown is like a toy box that can either be told exactly which toy to show (controlled) or can decide by itself (uncontrolled). It should work right out of the box with just a few toys inside, and it should be easy for everyone to use, even if they can't see well.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your ability to design a component API that balances flexibility, usability, and accessibility. A well-designed dropdown component should follow the Single Responsibility Principle: it only handles selection, not styling or layout.

Controlled vs Uncontrolled

Provide both value + onChange for controlled mode (parent manages state) and defaultValue for uncontrolled mode (component manages its own state). This gives consumers choice without forcing a pattern.

Composition over Configuration

Instead of a single component with 30 props, use composition: <Dropdown> wraps <DropdownItem> children. This makes the API extensible and easy to customize.

Sensible Defaults

The dropdown should work with minimal props: default isOpen=false, a placeholder like 'Select an option', and keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Enter to select, Escape to close).

Accessibility Built-in

Include ARIA roles (listbox, option), aria-expanded on the trigger, and focus management (auto-focus first item when opened, trap focus while open). This ensures screen reader users can interact.

Common Pitfall

Over-engineering: adding too many props for every possible customization. Instead, rely on composition and allow consumers to pass custom render props or slots for the trigger or list items.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Dropdown Component] --> B[Controlled] A --> C[Uncontrolled] B --> D[value + onChange] C --> E[defaultValue] A --> F[Composition] F --> G[DropdownItem] A --> H[Accessibility] H --> I[ARIA roles] H --> J[Focus management]"}]
tr_v2:607d87dbea9e816b: Example: Design an API for an Autocomplete component
qgen_v4:9bb9c02da78626d7:
[{"question":"Design the API for an Autocomplete component that handles async data fetching, keyboard navigation, and accessibility.","expectedPoints":["Props include fetchOptions, renderOption, getOptionLabel, debounceMs, minChars, value/onChange, loading, emptyMessage.","Handles debounce (default 300ms) and race conditions (AbortController).","Supports keyboard navigation (ArrowUp/Down, Enter, Escape) and ARIA attributes (role='combobox', aria-activedescendant).","Considers caching results and virtualization for long lists."],"followUps":["How would you implement caching to avoid redundant network requests?","What ARIA attributes are essential for screen reader support?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a search box that suggests things as you type, like Google. You need to wait a tiny bit after each keystroke (debounce) so you don't overload the server. Also, if you type fast, you must cancel old requests (race condition). And you must let people use arrow keys to pick suggestions and tell screen readers what's happening.","detailedExplanation":"

An Autocomplete component is a text input that shows a dropdown of suggestions based on user input. Designing its API requires careful consideration of performance, user experience, and accessibility.

Core Props

The primary prop is fetchOptions, a function that takes the current query string and returns a promise of options. getOptionLabel extracts the display text from each option. renderOption allows custom rendering. debounceMs (default 300ms) delays the fetch until the user stops typing. minChars (default 1) sets the minimum characters before fetching. Controlled props value and onChange manage the selected item. loading and emptyMessage handle states.

Race Conditions & Caching

Without handling race conditions, a slow response from an earlier query might overwrite a later one. Use AbortController to cancel in-flight requests when a new query starts. Caching results by query string prevents redundant network calls. A simple in-memory cache (e.g., Map) works well.

Keyboard Navigation & ARIA

Support ArrowUp/Down to move focus through options, Enter to select, and Escape to close the dropdown. Use role='combobox', aria-expanded, aria-activedescendant to indicate the active option, and aria-autocomplete='list'. Ensure the dropdown has role='listbox' and options have role='option'.

Virtualization

For large lists (e.g., thousands of options), use virtualization (e.g., react-window) to render only visible items, improving performance.

Common Pitfall

Forgetting to clean up timeouts and abort controllers on unmount, causing memory leaks or state updates on unmounted components. Always cancel debounce timers and abort fetch requests in a useEffect cleanup.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User types] --> B[Debounce timer] B --> C[Abort previous request] C --> D[Fetch options] D --> E[Cache result] E --> F[Render dropdown] F --> G[Keyboard nav] G --> H[Select option]"}]
qgen_v4:c28a79279713ca1c:
[{"question":"Design a compound component for an Autocomplete that supports keyboard navigation and async data fetching. How would you structure the components and share state?","expectedPoints":["Uses Context to share state between parent and children implicitly","Exposes sub-components like Autocomplete.Input, Autocomplete.List, Autocomplete.Option","Handles keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Enter, Escape) and ARIA attributes","Manages async fetching with debounce, race condition handling, and caching","Allows flexible composition without prop explosion"],"followUps":["How would you handle race conditions when the user types quickly?","What ARIA roles and attributes are essential for accessibility?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box with different compartments. The compound components pattern is like having a main box (Autocomplete) that gives you smaller boxes (Input, List, Option) that all share a secret tunnel (Context) to talk to each other. This way, you can arrange the smaller boxes however you like, and they still work together perfectly.","detailedExplanation":"

The compound components pattern is a design technique in React that allows you to create a set of components that work together implicitly, sharing state via Context rather than passing props through every level. This pattern is ideal for complex UI widgets like Autocomplete, Tabs, or Menu because it provides flexibility in composition while keeping the API clean.

How it works

The parent component (e.g., Autocomplete) holds the shared state (e.g., input value, list visibility, selected index) and provides it via a Context provider. Child components like Autocomplete.Input, Autocomplete.List, and Autocomplete.Option consume that context to read and update state. This way, the user can compose the UI in any order or structure, and the components still communicate seamlessly.

Why it matters in production

  • Flexibility: Users can rearrange or style sub-components without breaking functionality.
  • Clean API: Avoids prop drilling and keeps the number of props manageable.
  • Reusability: Each sub-component can be used independently or in different contexts.

Common pitfall

Over-abstracting: creating too many sub-components can make the API confusing. Also, forgetting to handle keyboard navigation and ARIA attributes (like role="combobox", aria-activedescendant) can break accessibility. Additionally, async operations require careful handling of race conditions (e.g., using an abort controller or a unique request ID) and debouncing to avoid excessive API calls.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart TD A[Autocomplete] -->|provides Context| B[Autocomplete.Input] A -->|provides Context| C[Autocomplete.List] A -->|provides Context| D[Autocomplete.Option] B -->|updates value| A C -->|reads options| A D -->|selects option| A subgraph Shared State E[inputValue] F[options] G[highlightedIndex] end A --> E A --> F A --> G"}]
qgen_v4:51ce451de3d9457f:
[{"question":"How would you design a caching strategy for a news feed that updates frequently, ensuring users see fresh content while minimizing network requests?","expectedPoints":["Use a cache-first then network strategy with stale-while-revalidate pattern.","Implement cache invalidation via timestamps or versioning.","Consider offline support with service workers and IndexedDB.","Handle race conditions between cache updates and network responses.","Balance freshness vs performance with TTL and background sync."],"followUps":["How would you handle cache invalidation when a user posts a new item?","What tradeoffs exist between using localStorage vs IndexedDB for this scenario?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box where you keep your favorite toys. When you want a toy, you first check your box (cache) to get it quickly. But sometimes your mom buys new toys, so you need to update your box. You can set a rule: if the toy is older than 5 minutes, go ask mom for the new one. That way you always have the newest toys without asking mom every time.","detailedExplanation":"
