Cache-first with stale-while-revalidate is a common pattern for frequently updated content like a news feed. The app first serves data from the cache (fast), then in the background fetches fresh data from the network and updates the cache. This ensures users see content immediately while staying reasonably fresh.

Implementation Details

Use a service worker to intercept network requests. For each feed request, the service worker checks the cache. If a cached response exists, it returns it immediately and then fetches the network. If the network succeeds, it updates the cache. If the network fails, the stale cache is still available.

Cache Invalidation

To avoid serving stale data, use versioning (e.g., include a version number in the cache key) or timestamps (e.g., store a lastFetched time and compare with a TTL). When the user posts a new item, you can purge the feed cache or mark it as stale so the next request triggers a network fetch.

Storage Choice

For large amounts of data (e.g., many feed items), IndexedDB is better than localStorage because it supports asynchronous access, larger storage, and complex queries. localStorage is synchronous and limited to ~5MB, which can block the main thread.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is over-caching without proper invalidation, leading to users seeing outdated content. Always define a TTL and provide a way to force refresh (e.g., pull-to-refresh). Also, beware of race conditions where multiple cache updates happen concurrently; use a mutex or queue writes.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Request] --> B{Cache Hit?} B -->|Yes| C[Serve from Cache] C --> D[Fetch in Background] D --> E[Update Cache] B -->|No| F[Fetch from Network] F --> G[Cache Response] G --> H[Serve to User]"}]
qgen_v4:6ee9705119ea86ef:
[{"question":"Describe the different client-side cache layers a frontend request passes through, from fastest to slowest, and explain how invalidation strategies differ across them.","expectedPoints":["Identifies memory/React Query cache as fastest, then localStorage/IndexedDB, Service Worker Cache API, HTTP cache (CDN/browser), and server.","Explains that closer-to-app caches are faster but more volatile and require explicit invalidation (e.g., React Query's staleTime).","Discusses trade-offs: memory cache is ephemeral, localStorage persists but is synchronous, Service Worker enables offline, HTTP cache relies on headers.","Mentions invalidation strategies: TTL, manual eviction, cache-busting via versioning or ETags.","Notes that stale data risk increases as you move away from the app layer."],"followUps":["How would you invalidate a Service Worker cache after a new deployment?","What are the pros and cons of using localStorage vs IndexedDB for caching API responses?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy you love. You keep it in your pocket (memory cache) so you can grab it fast, but it might get lost. You also have a toy box in your room (localStorage) that holds more toys but takes a bit longer to open. If you want to play outside, you might put toys in a backpack (Service Worker) so you can play even without internet. And if you order a new toy, the delivery truck (HTTP cache) might bring it quickly if it's already nearby. Each place has rules for when to throw away old toys so you don't play with broken ones.","detailedExplanation":"

Client-side caching is critical for performance and offline resilience. A request can be served from multiple layers, each with different speed, persistence, and invalidation characteristics.

Cache Layers (Fastest to Slowest)

  • App/Memory Cache (e.g., React Query cache): In-memory JavaScript objects. Extremely fast, but lost on page refresh. Invalidation via staleTime or manual invalidateQueries.
  • Storage (localStorage / IndexedDB): Persistent across sessions. localStorage is synchronous and limited to ~5MB; IndexedDB is async and larger. Invalidation requires explicit TTL or version checks.
  • Service Worker Cache (Cache API): Programmable cache for offline support. Lives in a separate thread. Invalidation often done by deleting old caches during activate event.
  • HTTP Cache (Browser & CDN): Controlled by Cache-Control, ETag, Expires headers. Fast but less flexible. Invalidation via cache-busting URLs or header changes.
  • Server: Origin data. Slowest but most authoritative.

Why It Matters in Production

Choosing the right layer balances speed vs. freshness. For example, a news feed might use React Query with a 5-minute stale time, while static assets use HTTP cache with long max-age and versioned filenames.

Common Pitfall

Over-relying on localStorage for API responses without invalidation leads to serving stale data. Always implement a version or timestamp check.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[App/Memory] --> B[localStorage/IDB] B --> C[Service Worker] C --> D[HTTP Cache] D --> E[Server] style A fill:#c8e6c9 style E fill:#ffcdd2"}]
qgen_v4:82bfcce1335f2a7f:
[{"question":"A developer stores a JWT token in localStorage for API authentication. What are the security risks, and how would you mitigate them?","expectedPoints":["localStorage is accessible via JavaScript (XSS vulnerability).","Cookies with HttpOnly flag prevent JS access, reducing XSS risk.","Use Secure and SameSite flags to protect against CSRF and MITM.","Consider token refresh patterns or short-lived tokens.","Evaluate trade-off between convenience and security."],"followUps":["How would you handle token refresh with HttpOnly cookies?","What if the app needs to read the token client-side for role-based UI?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"tradeoff","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a secret key to your treehouse. If you keep it under the doormat (localStorage), anyone who sneaks into your yard (XSS attack) can grab it. But if you give it to your parent (server) and they keep it in a locked box (HttpOnly cookie), the sneaky person can't take it because they can't open the box. The trade-off is that you can't easily show the key to your friends (client-side code) when it's locked away.","detailedExplanation":"

Storing a JWT token in localStorage is convenient but introduces a significant security risk: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting). If an attacker injects malicious JavaScript into your page, they can read localStorage directly via window.localStorage.getItem('token') and steal the token. This grants them full access to the user's session.

Mitigation with HttpOnly Cookies

A more secure approach is to store the token in a cookie with the HttpOnly flag. This flag tells the browser to prevent JavaScript from accessing the cookie, so even if XSS occurs, the attacker cannot read the token. Additionally, use the Secure flag to ensure the cookie is only sent over HTTPS, and SameSite=Strict or Lax to mitigate CSRF attacks.

Trade-offs

Using HttpOnly cookies means the client-side JavaScript cannot read the token. This complicates scenarios like role-based UI rendering where you need to decode the token to get user roles. Workarounds include:

  • Storing a separate, non-sensitive indicator (e.g., a role in a separate cookie or a lightweight API call).
  • Using a token refresh pattern: a short-lived access token in memory (or a cookie) and a long-lived refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie.

Common Pitfall

Many developers assume localStorage is safe because they trust their own code, but third-party scripts, browser extensions, or even a single missed sanitization can introduce XSS. Always treat localStorage as insecure for sensitive data.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR subgraph Client A[localStorage] -->|JS accessible| B[XSS attacker] C[HttpOnly Cookie] -->|JS blocked| B end subgraph Server D[Issue token] end D -->|Store in| A D -->|Store in| C B -->|Steal token| E[Unauthorized access]"}]
qgen_v4:5b10ea278530e6a8:
[{"question":"How would you implement a cache invalidation strategy for a dashboard that fetches user data every 30 seconds, but also needs to show stale data immediately on page load?","expectedPoints":["Uses stale-while-revalidate pattern to serve cached data first","Sets appropriate TTL (e.g., 30s) for cache freshness","Handles background revalidation and updates UI asynchronously","Considers ETag/Last-Modified for conditional requests","Invalidates cache on user mutation (e.g., after form submit)"],"followUps":["How would you handle the case where the background revalidation fails?","What if the user mutates data while a revalidation is in progress?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box. When you want a toy, you first grab one from the box (even if it's a bit old) so you can play immediately. Meanwhile, you ask your parent to get a newer toy from the store. When the new toy arrives, you swap it. This way you never wait—you always have something to play with, and eventually you get the freshest toy.","detailedExplanation":"

Cache invalidation is a critical challenge in frontend applications, especially for dashboards that require both freshness and performance. The stale-while-revalidate pattern is a core strategy used by libraries like SWR and React Query.

How it works

On page load, the app immediately returns cached (stale) data to the UI, ensuring instant rendering. Simultaneously, it triggers a background fetch to the server. When the fetch completes, the cache is updated and the UI re-renders with fresh data. This avoids a loading spinner and provides a smooth user experience.

Key components

  • TTL (Time-To-Live): Defines how long cached data is considered fresh. For a 30-second refresh interval, set TTL to 30s.
  • Stale-while-revalidate: Serves stale data while revalidating in the background.
  • Cache-then-network: A variant where the app shows cached data first, then updates from network.
  • ETag / Last-Modified: Used in conditional requests to avoid re-downloading unchanged data.
  • Invalidate on mutation: After a user action (e.g., form submit), the cache for that resource should be invalidated immediately to force a re-fetch.

Why it matters in production

Users expect instant feedback. Without this strategy, every navigation would show a loading state, degrading perceived performance. It also reduces server load by avoiding unnecessary fetches when data hasn't changed.

Common pitfall

Failing to invalidate cache on mutations leads to stale data being shown after a user updates a record. Always clear or update the cache for the affected resource after a successful mutation.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Page Load] --> B{Check Cache} B -->|Cache Hit| C[Show Stale Data] B -->|Cache Miss| D[Fetch from Server] C --> E[Background Revalidate] E --> F[Update Cache & UI] D --> G[Cache & Show Fresh Data] F --> H[Fresh UI] G --> H"}]
tr_v2:1fa3295287368f48: Optimistic UI provides a real-time feel.
qgen_v4:a298208df1a9e2ce:
[{"question":"How would you implement an optimistic UI update for a 'like' button, and what are the trade-offs?","expectedPoints":["Immediately update UI before server response","Rollback on error with original state","Handle race conditions and stale data","Consider user experience vs. data consistency"],"followUps":["How would you handle multiple rapid likes/unlikes?","What if the server returns a different state than expected?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're playing a game where you press a button to score a point. Instead of waiting for the referee to confirm, you immediately add the point to your score. If the referee says it didn't count, you take it back. That's optimistic UI—it makes the app feel fast even if the network is slow.","detailedExplanation":"

Optimistic UI is a pattern where the user interface is updated immediately in response to a user action, before the server confirms the change. This creates a perception of speed and responsiveness, especially on high-latency networks.

How It Works

When a user clicks 'like', the client immediately updates the UI (e.g., increment like count, change button color) and sends the request to the server. If the server responds with success, the optimistic update is confirmed. If the server returns an error, the client rolls back the UI to its previous state and optionally shows an error message.

Why It Matters in Production

In real-world apps, network delays can be hundreds of milliseconds. Without optimistic UI, users would see a loading spinner or delayed feedback, making the app feel sluggish. Optimistic UI is critical for user engagement in social features (likes, comments, follows) and collaborative tools.

Common Pitfall

Race conditions can occur when multiple optimistic updates happen in quick succession. For example, if a user likes and then unlikes before the first request completes, the rollback logic might restore an incorrect state. To mitigate this, use request IDs or local state snapshots to track pending operations and apply updates in order.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User clicks Like] --> B[Update UI immediately] B --> C[Send request to server] C --> D{Server response} D -- Success --> E[Keep optimistic state] D -- Error --> F[Rollback UI to original] F --> G[Show error toast]"}]
qgen_v4:4c5e57891aa84f6e:
[{"question":"How would you design a frontend system to support both accessibility (a11y) and internationalization (i18n) for a global e-commerce platform?","expectedPoints":["Uses semantic HTML and ARIA attributes for screen readers.","Supports keyboard navigation and focus management.","Implements i18n with locale detection, translation files, and RTL support.","Ensures a11y and i18n work together (e.g., translated ARIA labels).","Considers cultural differences (date formats, currencies, colors)."],"followUps":["How would you handle dynamic content updates for screen readers?","What tradeoffs exist between client-side and server-side i18n?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine building a toy store website that kids all over the world can use. Some kids can't see well, so the website must talk to them (screen reader). Some kids speak different languages, so the website must show words in their language. You need to plan how to make the website work for everyone, like having a special helper that reads the page and a magic translator that changes words.","detailedExplanation":"

Designing a frontend system for both accessibility (a11y) and internationalization (i18n) requires a holistic approach. Start with semantic HTML (e.g., <nav>, <button>) and ARIA attributes (e.g., aria-label, role) to ensure screen readers can interpret the page. For keyboard users, implement visible focus indicators and logical tab order.

Internationalization

Use a library like react-intl or i18next to manage translations. Detect user locale via navigator.language or a cookie, and load the appropriate message files. Support right-to-left (RTL) layouts using CSS logical properties (e.g., margin-inline-start). Format dates, numbers, and currencies with Intl APIs.

Combining a11y and i18n

Translate ARIA labels and alt texts. For example, an image of a flag should have alt text in the user's language. Use aria-live regions to announce dynamic content changes (e.g., “Item added to cart”) in the correct language. Ensure that translated strings do not break layout (e.g., German words are longer).

Production Considerations

Test with real screen readers (NVDA, VoiceOver) and keyboard-only navigation. Use automated tools like axe-core and Lighthouse. A common pitfall is forgetting to update ARIA labels when the language changes, leading to mismatched announcements. Also, avoid hardcoding text in JavaScript; always use translation keys.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Request] --> B{Locale Detection} B -->|en| C[Load English Translations] B -->|ar| D[Load Arabic Translations] C --> E[Render Semantic HTML + ARIA] D --> E E --> F[Apply RTL/LTR CSS] F --> G[Format Dates/Currencies] G --> H[Accessible UI with i18n]"}]
qgen_v4:611c0e4619381cf6:
[{"question":"How would you ensure a complex single-page application is keyboard accessible, and what are the most common pitfalls you'd watch for?","expectedPoints":["Logical tab order using tabindex and DOM order","Visible focus indicators (e.g., :focus-visible)","No focus traps; manage focus for modals/dialogs","All interactive elements reachable and operable via keyboard","Use semantic HTML to get keyboard support for free"],"followUps":["How would you handle a custom dropdown component's keyboard navigation?","What's the difference between tabindex="0" and tabindex="-1"?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you can only use the Tab and Enter keys to play a video game. The game must let you reach every button and show you which one is selected. Keyboard accessibility means building websites so people who can't use a mouse can still do everything using just the keyboard.","detailedExplanation":"

Keyboard accessibility ensures that all functionality is operable through a keyboard interface. This is critical for users with motor disabilities, power users, and screen reader users who rely on keyboard navigation.

Core Requirements

  • Logical tab order: The DOM order should match the visual order. Use tabindex="0" to make a normally non-interactive element focusable, and tabindex="-1" to make it programmatically focusable but not in the tab order. Avoid positive tabindex values as they disrupt the natural order.
  • Visible focus indicators: Always provide a clear, visible focus ring (e.g., :focus-visible outline). Never use outline: none without a replacement.
  • No focus traps: Users must be able to tab away from any component. For modals, trap focus within the modal but allow closing via Escape and ensure focus returns to the trigger element on close.
  • All interactive elements reachable: Every button, link, input, and custom widget must be reachable via Tab or arrow keys (for composite widgets like tabs, menus, grids).

Common Pitfall

Using <div onClick> with ARIA roles instead of a native <button>. A native button provides keyboard support, focus management, and role semantics for free. Adding ARIA to a <div> is error-prone and often incomplete. Remember: No ARIA is better than bad ARIA.

Production Importance

Keyboard accessibility is a legal requirement (e.g., WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 2.1.1). It also improves UX for all users—power users often prefer keyboard shortcuts. Testing with only a keyboard (no mouse) is a quick way to catch issues.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User presses Tab] --> B[Focus moves to next element] B --> C{Is element interactive?} C -->|Yes| D[Perform action via Enter/Space] C -->|No| E[Skip to next focusable] D --> F[Update UI / trigger event] F --> G[Focus may move to new element] G --> A"}]
qgen_v4:2e62a88c0c11cd2e:
[{"question":"How would you implement i18n in a large React application to support multiple locales, including RTL languages, while keeping bundle size minimal?","expectedPoints":["Use string externalization with translation keys (e.g., JSON files).","Lazy-load locale bundles to avoid including all translations in initial bundle.","Use Intl API for locale-aware formatting (dates, numbers, currencies).","Handle pluralization and gender with ICU MessageFormat.","Support RTL via logical CSS properties (e.g., margin-inline-start)."],"followUps":["How would you handle dynamic locale switching without a full page reload?","What are the trade-offs of using a library like react-intl vs. a custom solution?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy that speaks different languages. Instead of building a new toy for each language, you store all the words in separate boxes. When a child wants to play, you only open the box for their language. That way, the toy stays small and fast. Also, for languages that read right-to-left, you use special arrows that automatically flip the direction.","detailedExplanation":"

Internationalization (i18n) is the process of designing an application so it can be adapted to various languages and regions without engineering changes. In a large React app, a robust i18n strategy involves several key practices.

String Externalization

All user-facing strings should be extracted into translation files (e.g., JSON) keyed by a unique identifier. This avoids hardcoding text and allows translators to work independently. For example: { "greeting": "Hello" }.

Pluralization and Gender

Different languages have complex plural rules (e.g., “1 item” vs “2 items”). Use ICU MessageFormat to handle these cases: { "items": "{count, plural, one {# item} other {# items}}" }.

Locale-Aware Formatting

Use the built-in Intl API for formatting dates, numbers, and currencies according to the user’s locale. Example: new Intl.NumberFormat('vi-VN', { style: 'currency', currency: 'VND' }).format(1500000) outputs "1.500.000 ₫".

RTL Support

For right-to-left languages like Arabic or Hebrew, use logical CSS properties (e.g., margin-inline-start instead of margin-left) so the layout automatically flips. Avoid hardcoding directional values.

Lazy-Loading Locales

To keep the bundle size small, load only the locale data for the current language. Use dynamic imports (e.g., import('intl/locale-data/jsonp/vi.js')) or split translation files per locale. This prevents shipping all translations upfront.

Common Pitfall

A frequent mistake is relying solely on CSS for RTL (e.g., direction: rtl) without using logical properties. This can break layouts when components are reused or nested. Always prefer logical properties for true RTL support.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User selects locale] --> B[Load locale data] B --> C{Locale loaded?} C -- Yes --> D[Render with Intl API] C -- No --> E[Lazy-load translation JSON] E --> D D --> F[Apply logical CSS for RTL] F --> G[Display localized UI]"}]
qgen_v4:f84abacd9c821576:
[{"question":"How would you design a frontend security strategy to mitigate XSS and CSRF attacks in a React application that handles sensitive user data?","expectedPoints":["Explain XSS (e.g., sanitizing user input, using Content Security Policy) and CSRF (e.g., anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies).","Propose React-specific measures: JSX escaping, avoiding dangerouslySetInnerHTML, using libraries like DOMPurify.","Discuss secure cookie attributes (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite) and token-based authentication (e.g., JWT with short expiry).","Mention CSP headers to restrict script sources and report violations.","Address sensitive data exposure: avoid storing secrets in localStorage, use environment variables for API keys."],"followUps":["How would you handle third-party scripts that need to run on your page?","What if the anti-CSRF token is stolen via an XSS vulnerability?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a secret diary that you keep in your room. You want to make sure no one sneaks in and writes fake entries (XSS) or tricks you into sending a letter that you didn't write (CSRF). So you lock your diary in a safe (CSP), only use pens that can't be erased (React's JSX), and always check that a letter you receive has a special stamp that only you and your friend know (CSRF token).","detailedExplanation":"

Frontend security is critical because client-side code runs in an untrusted environment. The two most common attacks are Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF). A senior engineer must design a defense-in-depth strategy.

Mitigating XSS

XSS occurs when an attacker injects malicious scripts into your page. In React, JSX automatically escapes values, but you must avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML. For user-generated content, use a sanitization library like DOMPurify. Implement a Content Security Policy (CSP) header to restrict allowed script sources (e.g., script-src 'self') and enable reporting for violations.

Mitigating CSRF

CSRF tricks the user's browser into making unwanted requests. Use anti-CSRF tokens (sent as a custom header or cookie) validated by the server. Set cookies with SameSite=Strict or Lax, and use HttpOnly and Secure flags. For APIs, require a custom header like X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest.

Protecting Sensitive Data

Never store secrets (API keys, tokens) in localStorage or sessionStorage because they are accessible via XSS. Use HttpOnly cookies for authentication tokens. For third-party integrations, use backend proxies to avoid exposing keys.

Common Pitfall

Relying solely on frontend validation. Attackers can bypass client-side checks. Always validate and sanitize on the backend. Also, CSP can be complex to configure; a too-restrictive policy may break legitimate functionality, so test thoroughly.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Input] --> B{Sanitize?} B -- Yes --> C[DOMPurify] B -- No --> D[React JSX Escapes] C --> E[Render Safely] D --> E E --> F[CSP Header] F --> G[Block Malicious Scripts] subgraph CSRF Protection H[Request] --> I[Check CSRF Token] I --> J[Validate Cookie SameSite] end G --> K[Secure App]"}]
qgen_v4:0caaa6b35153d48d:
[{"question":"In a React app, when is it mandatory to sanitize user-provided HTML (e.g., using DOMPurify) to prevent XSS?","expectedPoints":["React auto-escapes JSX expressions, so {userInput} is safe","dangerouslySetInnerHTML bypasses React's escaping","Sanitization is required only when using dangerouslySetInnerHTML with untrusted content","Server-side validation is still necessary; client-side sanitization is not sufficient alone"],"followUps":["What other defenses beyond sanitization can mitigate XSS?","How would you handle XSS in a non-React frontend (e.g., vanilla JS)?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy that shows messages. React is like a toy that automatically wraps every message in a safe bubble so bad words can't escape. But if you use a special 'dangerous' button to show raw HTML, you must clean the message first with a tool like DOMPurify, just like washing dirty fruit before eating.","detailedExplanation":"

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) is the most common frontend security vulnerability. It occurs when an attacker injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. In React, the framework provides automatic protection by escaping values rendered inside JSX expressions ({userInput}). This means any HTML tags or script code in the input are converted to safe text, preventing execution.

When Sanitization Is Required

The only scenario where React's default escaping is bypassed is when you use dangerouslySetInnerHTML. This API tells React to render raw HTML directly into the DOM. If the content comes from an untrusted source (e.g., user comments, rich text editors), an attacker can inject <script> tags or event handlers. Therefore, you must sanitize the HTML string before passing it to dangerouslySetInnerHTML. A popular library for this is DOMPurify, which strips dangerous elements and attributes while preserving safe markup.

Why It Matters in Production

Failing to sanitize can lead to cookie theft, session hijacking, or defacement. Even with React's built-in protection, developers often need to render rich content (e.g., blog posts, chat messages). A common pitfall is assuming that server-side validation alone is enough. However, client-side sanitization is a defense-in-depth layer; the server must also validate and sanitize input because client-side code can be bypassed (e.g., via DevTools).

Additional Defenses

  • Content Security Policy (CSP): Restricts which scripts can execute, even if injected.
  • HttpOnly cookies: Prevents JavaScript from accessing sensitive cookies.
  • Input validation: Reject or encode special characters on the server.
","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Input] --> B{React JSX?} B -- Yes --> C[Auto-escaped Safe] B -- No (dangerouslySetInnerHTML) --> D{Sanitized?} D -- Yes --> E[Safe HTML] D -- No --> F[XSS Vulnerability] F --> G[Attacker steals cookie]"}]
tr_v2:62d58534c6529a5a: How does the browser render a page?
qgen_v4:5a5658e8e0948402:
[{"question":"Explain the browser's rendering pipeline and how it affects web performance.","expectedPoints":["Describes the main steps: DOM construction, CSSOM, render tree, layout, paint, compositing.","Identifies that layout and paint are expensive and can be triggered by style changes.","Mention that transform/opacity only trigger compositing, not layout or paint.","Explain layout thrashing and how to avoid it by batching DOM reads/writes.","Discuss content-visibility as a way to skip rendering off-screen elements."],"followUps":["How would you debug a layout thrashing issue in a real application?","Can you give an example where using transform instead of top/left improves performance?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a LEGO castle. First you read the instructions (DOM), then you sort the bricks (CSSOM), then you put them together (layout), then you color them (paint), and finally you take a photo (compositing). If you keep changing the position of a brick, you have to rebuild the whole castle each time, which is slow. Using transform is like moving the photo of the brick instead of the real brick—it's much faster.","detailedExplanation":"

The browser rendering pipeline is a sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into pixels on the screen. Understanding this pipeline is crucial for optimizing web performance because each step can be a bottleneck.

Steps in the Pipeline

  • DOM Construction: The browser parses HTML to create the Document Object Model (DOM).
  • CSSOM Construction: CSS is parsed into the CSS Object Model (CSSOM).
  • Render Tree: The DOM and CSSOM are combined to form the render tree, which includes only visible elements.
  • Layout: The browser calculates the geometry (position and size) of each element. This is also called reflow.
  • Paint: The browser fills in pixels for each element (colors, images, text).
  • Compositing: The painted layers are combined and drawn on the screen.

Performance Implications

Not all CSS properties trigger the same steps. Changing width or top triggers layout and paint, which are expensive. Changing transform or opacity only triggers compositing, which is handled by the GPU and is much faster. This is why modern animations prefer transform over top or left.

Layout Thrashing

A common pitfall is layout thrashing, where JavaScript repeatedly reads and writes to the DOM, forcing the browser to recalculate layout multiple times. For example, reading element.offsetHeight forces a layout recalculation if there are pending style changes. To avoid this, batch all DOM reads together, then batch writes.

Content-visibility

The content-visibility property allows the browser to skip rendering of off-screen elements, improving initial load time. It works by deferring layout and paint for elements outside the viewport until they are needed.

In production, understanding the rendering pipeline helps identify performance bottlenecks. Tools like Chrome DevTools' Performance panel can show you exactly which steps are taking time.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[HTML] --> B[DOM] C[CSS] --> D[CSSOM] B & D --> E[Render Tree] E --> F[Layout] F --> G[Paint] G --> H[Compositing] H --> I[Screen]"}]
qgen_v4:04fe80961819d97c:
[{"question":"Explain the Critical Rendering Path and identify which steps are most expensive when changing an element's geometry vs. its opacity.","expectedPoints":["Lists the 6 steps: HTML→DOM, CSS→CSSOM, Render Tree, Layout, Paint, Composite.","Explains that changing geometry (width, top) triggers Layout, Paint, and Composite.","Explains that changing transform/opacity only triggers Composite (GPU-accelerated).","Recognizes that Layout is the most expensive step because it recalculates positions/sizes.","Understands that minimizing Layout/Paint reflows is key for performance."],"followUps":["How would you use `will-change` to optimize animations?","What tools can you use to diagnose CRP bottlenecks?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine building a LEGO castle. First you read the instructions (HTML→DOM), then you sort the bricks (CSS→CSSOM), then you combine them (Render Tree). Then you measure where each brick goes (Layout), color them (Paint), and finally put them on a baseplate (Composite). If you move a big wall (geometry), you have to remeasure and recolor everything. But if you just spin the castle (transform), you only need to move the baseplate—much faster!","detailedExplanation":"

The Critical Rendering Path (CRP) is the sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into pixels on the screen. Understanding it is crucial for optimizing page load and runtime performance.

The 6 Steps

  • HTML → DOM: Parse HTML to build the Document Object Model (DOM) tree.
  • CSS → CSSOM: Parse CSS to build the CSS Object Model (CSSOM) tree.
  • Render Tree: Combine DOM and CSSOM into a render tree containing only visible elements.
  • Layout: Calculate the exact size and position of each render tree node (also called reflow).
  • Paint: Fill in pixels for each node (e.g., colors, images, text).
  • Composite: Merge layers into the final screen image, often done on the GPU.

Performance Implications

Changing an element's geometry (e.g., width, height, top) forces the browser to rerun Layout, then Paint, then Composite. Layout is the most expensive step because it recalculates positions for potentially many elements. In contrast, changing transform or opacity only triggers Composite, which is cheap and runs on the GPU. This is why CSS animations using transform and opacity are recommended for smooth 60fps performance.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is animating properties like left or margin instead of transform: translateX(). The former triggers Layout on every frame, causing jank. Always prefer compositor-only properties for animations.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR HTML-->DOM CSS-->CSSOM DOM+CSSOM-->RenderTree RenderTree-->Layout Layout-->Paint Paint-->Composite Composite-->Pixels"}]
tr_v2:51865a0cd0d2ee36: Reflow vs Repaint — why it matters
qgen_v4:fcf0d4fb5a1a1b01:
[{"question":"How do reflow, repaint, and composite-only updates differ in the browser rendering pipeline, and why is it important to prefer transform and opacity for animations?","expectedPoints":["Reflow recalculates layout geometry and is expensive; repaint redraws pixels without layout change; composite-only updates skip layout and paint.","Reading layout properties (e.g., offsetHeight) after DOM writes forces synchronous reflow (layout thrashing).","Animating transform/opacity runs on the compositor thread, avoiding main thread blocking and achieving 60fps.","Batch reads before writes or use requestAnimationFrame to prevent layout thrashing."],"followUps":["How would you refactor a loop that reads offsetTop and sets style.left to avoid layout thrashing?","What CSS properties besides transform and opacity trigger only compositing?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a LEGO tower. Reflow is like rebuilding the whole tower when you change a block's size. Repaint is just coloring a block without moving it. Composite is like sliding a block on a smooth track—the track (GPU) handles it without touching the tower. For smooth animations, you want to slide blocks (transform/opacity) instead of rebuilding or repainting.","detailedExplanation":"

The browser rendering pipeline consists of Layout (reflow), Paint (repaint), and Composite. Reflow occurs when geometric properties change (width, height, position, adding/removing DOM). It recalculates the layout tree, which is expensive because it affects descendant and ancestor elements. Repaint happens when visual properties change without geometry (color, visibility, box-shadow). It redraws pixels but skips layout. Composite-only updates (transform, opacity) skip both layout and paint; the browser moves layers on the GPU via the compositor thread.

Why It Matters in Production

Animating transform or opacity ensures 60fps because the compositor thread runs independently of the main thread. Animating width or top triggers reflow every frame, causing jank. Layout thrashing occurs when you read a layout property (e.g., offsetHeight) after writing to the DOM, forcing a synchronous reflow. To avoid it, batch all reads first, then writes, or use requestAnimationFrame.

Common Pitfall

Developers often assume will-change: transform alone solves performance issues, but overusing it can waste GPU memory. Only promote elements that will actually animate.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[DOM Change] --> B{Property Type?} B -->|Geometry| C[Reflow] B -->|Visual only| D[Repaint] B -->|Transform/Opacity| E[Composite] C --> F[Paint] D --> F F --> G[Composite] E --> G"}]
qgen_v4:2e866e10511a08b3:
[{"question":"Explain the order of execution in the event loop when a setTimeout, a Promise, and a synchronous console.log are called. Provide a code example.","expectedPoints":["Synchronous code runs first, then microtasks (Promise.then), then macrotasks (setTimeout).","Promise callbacks are microtasks and execute before the next macrotask.","setTimeout callback is a macrotask and runs after all microtasks are cleared.","Correctly predicts output order: sync, Promise, setTimeout.","Mentions event loop phases: call stack, microtask queue, macrotask queue."],"followUps":["What happens if a microtask queues another microtask?","How does async/await affect the event loop compared to raw Promises?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're a single waiter at a restaurant. You take orders (synchronous tasks), then immediately handle special requests (microtasks like adding extra napkins), and only after that do you handle regular tasks (macrotasks like bringing the main course). This order ensures urgent things get done first.","detailedExplanation":"

The event loop is the mechanism that allows JavaScript, a single-threaded language, to handle asynchronous operations without blocking the main thread. Understanding the order of execution between synchronous code, microtasks, and macrotasks is crucial for debugging unexpected behavior.

Execution Order

When JavaScript runs, it first executes all synchronous code in the call stack. After the stack is empty, it processes the microtask queue (e.g., Promise.then, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver). Only when the microtask queue is empty does it pick the next macrotask from the macrotask queue (e.g., setTimeout, setInterval, I/O events).

Code Example

console.log('1'); // sync
setTimeout(() => console.log('2'), 0); // macrotask
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('3')); // microtask
console.log('4'); // sync
// Output: 1, 4, 3, 2

Why It Matters in Production

Misunderstanding this order leads to bugs where UI updates or data fetches happen out of sequence. For example, a setTimeout(fn, 0) does not run immediately after synchronous code; it waits for all microtasks. This can cause race conditions if you rely on timing.

Common Pitfall

Assuming setTimeout(fn, 0) runs before a Promise callback. In reality, Promises (microtasks) always execute before any macrotask, even if the timeout delay is 0.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart TD A[Call Stack] -->|Empty| B{Microtask Queue?} B -->|Yes| C[Process Microtask] C --> B B -->|No| D[Macrotask Queue] D --> E[Pick Next Macrotask] E --> A"}]
qgen_v4:81ae82ff3f2c3711:
[{"question":"Explain how the event loop processes a click event that triggers a Promise resolution and a setTimeout callback.","expectedPoints":["Describes call stack execution for synchronous code","States microtask queue (Promise.then) is emptied before next macrotask","Explains macrotask queue (setTimeout) processes one task per event loop iteration","Mentions rendering step occurs after microtask queue is cleared","Correctly orders: click macrotask → Promise microtask → setTimeout macrotask"],"followUps":["What happens if a microtask queues another microtask?","How would requestAnimationFrame fit into this cycle?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're doing homework (call stack). When you finish a page, you check your phone for messages (microtask queue) before starting the next page. If a friend texts back, you reply immediately. Only after all messages are handled do you start a new page (macrotask). The browser also updates the screen (render) after messages but before the next page.","detailedExplanation":"

The event loop is the core mechanism that enables JavaScript's non-blocking concurrency despite being single-threaded. It coordinates three main components: the call stack, microtask queue, and macrotask queue.

How it works

Each iteration of the event loop follows a strict order:

  • Execute all synchronous code on the call stack until it is empty.
  • Process the entire microtask queue (e.g., Promise.then, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver). If a microtask queues another microtask, it is also executed before moving on.
  • Take one task from the macrotask queue (e.g., setTimeout, DOM events, I/O) and execute it.
  • Optionally perform rendering (UI update) if needed.
  • Repeat.

Why it matters in production

Understanding this order is critical for avoiding performance pitfalls. For example, a long-running microtask can block rendering and cause jank. Conversely, deferring work to a macrotask (e.g., setTimeout) can allow the browser to paint between tasks, improving perceived performance.

Common pitfall

A common mistake is assuming setTimeout(fn, 0) runs immediately after the current code. In reality, it is placed in the macrotask queue and will only execute after the call stack and all microtasks are cleared. This can lead to unexpected ordering when mixing Promises and timers.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart TD A[Call Stack] -->|Empty| B{Microtask Queue} B -->|Process All| C[Render] C --> D[Macrotask Queue] D -->|Take 1 Task| A subgraph Event Loop A B C D end"}]
qgen_v4:c331a92ca3d5ca03:
[{"question":"What is the output of the following code? console.log('1'); setTimeout(() => console.log('2'), 0); Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('3')); console.log('4');","expectedPoints":["Recognizes synchronous code runs first: 1, 4","Identifies microtask queue (Promise.then) runs before macrotask (setTimeout)","Correct output order: 1, 4, 3, 2","Explains event loop phases: sync → microtasks → macrotasks"],"followUps":["What would happen if the Promise.then recursively creates another microtask?","How would you prevent UI blocking if you have a heavy microtask chain?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have three chores: homework (synchronous), cleaning your room (microtask), and taking out the trash (macrotask). You always do homework first, then clean your room completely before taking out the trash. Even if you set a timer for trash, cleaning comes first.","detailedExplanation":"

The event loop processes tasks in a specific order: synchronous code first, then microtasks, then macrotasks. In the given code:

  • console.log('1') and console.log('4') are synchronous, so they execute immediately in order: 1, 4.
  • Promise.resolve().then(...) queues a microtask. After the synchronous code finishes, the event loop empties the entire microtask queue before moving to the next macrotask. So console.log('3') runs next.
  • setTimeout(() => console.log('2'), 0) queues a macrotask. It runs only after all microtasks are done. So '2' is printed last.

Thus the output is: 1, 4, 3, 2.

Why it matters in production

Understanding this order prevents bugs where you expect a timeout callback to run before a promise resolution. It also helps avoid microtask starvation: if a microtask continuously queues new microtasks, the browser never gets to render or process user input, causing a frozen UI.

Common pitfall

Assuming setTimeout(fn, 0) runs immediately after synchronous code. In reality, it's a macrotask and must wait for all microtasks (e.g., promise callbacks, mutation observers) to complete.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Sync Code: 1,4] --> B[Microtask Queue: Promise.then] B --> C[Macrotask Queue: setTimeout] C --> D[Output: 1,4,3,2]"}]
qgen_v4:3efe5541da50b57a:
[{"question":"How does the evolution from HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/3 affect frontend bundling strategies?","expectedPoints":["HTTP/1.1 head-of-line blocking and need for bundling","HTTP/2 multiplexing reduces bundling necessity","HTTP/3 further improves with QUIC and no TCP HOL blocking","Trade-offs: bundling still useful for compression and caching","Real-world impact on build tools like Webpack"],"followUps":["When would you still bundle files in an HTTP/2 world?","How does HTTP/3's connection migration benefit mobile users?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're sending letters to a friend. HTTP/1.1 is like sending one letter at a time and waiting for a reply before sending the next. HTTP/2 lets you send many letters at once in the same envelope. HTTP/3 is like using a faster, more reliable postal service that doesn't get stuck if one letter is delayed. So, with newer versions, you don't need to combine all your letters into one big envelope (bundling) as much.","detailedExplanation":"

The evolution from HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/3 fundamentally changes how frontend assets should be bundled and served. Understanding this helps explain why older best practices (like concatenating all JS into one file) are no longer optimal.

HTTP/1.1: The Bottleneck

HTTP/1.1 suffers from head-of-line (HOL) blocking at the connection level: only one request can be in flight per TCP connection. Browsers typically open 6–8 parallel connections per domain. To maximize throughput, developers bundled many small files (e.g., CSS, JS) into fewer large files to reduce the number of requests. This was a workaround for the protocol's limitations.

HTTP/2: Multiplexing

HTTP/2 introduces multiplexing, allowing multiple concurrent requests over a single TCP connection. This eliminates the need for bundling to reduce request count. In fact, bundling can be counterproductive because it prevents granular caching: changing one line of code invalidates the entire bundle. With HTTP/2, serving many small, independent files (e.g., individual components) is often better for cache efficiency.

HTTP/3: QUIC and No TCP HOL

HTTP/3 uses QUIC over UDP instead of TCP, eliminating TCP-level HOL blocking. It also offers faster connection establishment and connection migration (e.g., switching from Wi-Fi to mobile data without interruption). For frontend, this means even more reliable and faster loading, especially on poor networks. Bundling becomes even less critical.

Why Bundling Still Matters

Despite these improvements, bundling remains useful for compression (larger files compress better) and caching (fewer cache lookups). However, the optimal strategy is often a hybrid: bundle critical code into a few chunks (e.g., vendor, app) and lazy-load non-critical components individually. Tools like Webpack support code splitting and dynamic imports to leverage HTTP/2 multiplexing.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is blindly applying HTTP/1.1 bundling strategies to HTTP/2 sites, resulting in large, monolithic bundles that hurt caching and load performance. Instead, developers should audit their network waterfall and adjust chunk sizes based on actual protocol behavior.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[HTTP/1.1] -->|HOL blocking| B[Bundle files] C[HTTP/2] -->|Multiplexing| D[Small files] E[HTTP/3] -->|QUIC| F[Even fewer bundles] B --> G[Fewer requests] D --> H[Better caching] F --> I[Faster connections]"}]
tr_v2:61363869cf956e4b: TLS handshake & why HTTPS is "slow on the first connection"
qgen_v4:daf1a81650fa6217:
[{"question":"Why is HTTPS slower on the first connection, and how can you mitigate that?","expectedPoints":["Explains TLS handshake adds extra round trips beyond TCP","Mentions connection reuse, preconnect, HTTP/3 as mitigations","Distinguishes preconnect vs dns-prefetch for third-party domains","Quantifies impact (hundreds of ms) and real-world relevance"],"followUps":["How does HTTP/3 reduce the number of round trips?","When would you use dns-prefetch instead of preconnect?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to visit a friend's house for the first time. You need to look up their address (DNS), walk there (TCP), and then knock and be let in (TLS). That takes time. But if you've visited before, you can just walk straight in. HTTPS is slow the first time because of all those steps. To speed it up, you can start looking up the address and knocking before you even leave, like using preconnect.","detailedExplanation":"

The TLS handshake adds multiple round trips on top of the TCP 3-way handshake, making the first HTTPS connection noticeably slower than HTTP. The full sequence is: DNS lookup, TCP handshake, TLS handshake (key exchange, certificate verification), then the actual request. Each step costs one or more round trips, leading to a high TTFB (Time to First Byte).

How it works

During the TLS handshake, the client and server exchange cryptographic keys and verify the server's certificate. This typically requires 2 round trips for TLS 1.2, or 1 round trip for TLS 1.3. Combined with TCP's 1 round trip, the total can be 3-4 round trips before any application data is sent.

Why it matters in production

For users on high-latency networks (e.g., mobile), these extra round trips can add hundreds of milliseconds to page load time. This directly impacts user experience and conversion rates. Optimizing the initial connection is critical for performance.

Mitigations

  • Connection reuse: Keep-alive allows multiple requests over the same connection, avoiding repeated handshakes.
  • Preconnect: Use <link rel="preconnect"> for critical third-party origins (CDN, API) to perform DNS, TCP, and TLS early.
  • dns-prefetch: Use <link rel="dns-prefetch"> for less critical domains to resolve DNS only.
  • HTTP/3: Uses QUIC, which reduces handshake overhead by combining transport and security setup into fewer round trips.

Common pitfall

Overusing preconnect on many domains can waste browser resources and delay other critical work. Reserve it for the most important origins; use dns-prefetch for the rest.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[DNS] --> B[TCP handshake] B --> C[TLS handshake] C --> D[Request] D --> E[TTFB] subgraph Mitigations F[preconnect] G[dns-prefetch] H[HTTP/3] end A -.-> F B -.-> F C -.-> F A -.-> G"}]
tr_v2:284c9b5f97e0e45d: JavaScript Engine: How V8 Works Internally
qgen_v4:ffccf7da10919334:
[{"question":"Explain how V8's JIT compilation pipeline works, including the role of Ignition and TurboFan.","expectedPoints":["V8 parses source code into an AST, then Ignition generates bytecode.","Ignition is the interpreter that executes bytecode and collects profiling data.","Hot functions are optimized by TurboFan (JIT compiler) using type feedback.","Optimized code may be deoptimized if assumptions (e.g., types) change.","This pipeline balances fast startup (interpretation) with peak performance (compilation)."],"followUps":["What is deoptimization and why does it happen?","How does inline caching improve property access performance?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a chef who first reads a recipe quickly (interpreter) to serve food fast, but if a dish is ordered many times, he writes a special optimized recipe (compiler) to cook it super fast. If the ingredients change, he goes back to the quick method.","detailedExplanation":"

The V8 JavaScript engine uses a Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation pipeline to execute JavaScript efficiently. The pipeline consists of two main components: Ignition (the interpreter) and TurboFan (the optimizing compiler).

How It Works

When V8 parses JavaScript source code, it first generates an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). Ignition then compiles the AST into bytecode, which is a compact, platform-independent representation. Ignition executes this bytecode directly, providing fast startup. During execution, it collects profiling data (e.g., types of variables, function call frequencies).

When a function is identified as hot (executed many times), V8 marks it for optimization. TurboFan takes the bytecode and profiling data to generate highly optimized machine code. This optimized code assumes certain type information (e.g., a variable is always an integer). If those assumptions break (e.g., a string is passed), the engine performs deoptimization, falling back to bytecode execution.

Why It Matters in Production

Understanding this pipeline helps developers write engine-friendly code. For example, keeping function arguments monomorphic (same types) avoids deoptimization. Avoiding try/catch in hot paths prevents TurboFan from bailing out. This knowledge leads to predictable performance without blind micro-optimizations.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is assuming all code gets optimized. In reality, only hot functions are compiled. Overusing features like eval or with can prevent optimization entirely. Also, changing object shapes (adding/removing properties) can break hidden classes and inline caches, causing performance degradation.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Source Code] --> B[Parser] B --> C[AST] C --> D[Ignition Interpreter] D --> E[Bytecode] E --> F{Is function hot?} F -- No --> E F -- Yes --> G[TurboFan Compiler] G --> H[Optimized Machine Code] H --> I{Assumptions hold?} I -- Yes --> J[Execute fast] I -- No --> K[Deoptimize] K --> E"}]
