[{"question":"Explain how the V8 compilation pipeline optimizes JavaScript execution and what happens when an optimization assumption is invalidated.","expectedPoints":["Describes the pipeline: Parser → Ignition → Profiler → TurboFan","Explains that Ignition generates bytecode and Profiler identifies hot code","Describes TurboFan JIT compilation for hot code with type assumptions","Explains deoptimization when assumptions fail, reverting to bytecode","Mentions performance impact and how V8 re-optimizes later"],"followUps":["How does V8 decide when to re-optimize after a deoptimization?","What are common patterns that cause deoptimization in production code?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a chef who first reads a recipe (Parser), then makes a quick version (Ignition), and watches which dishes are ordered most (Profiler). For popular dishes, the chef creates a super-fast recipe (TurboFan). But if a customer changes their order (deoptimization), the chef has to go back to the quick version. This helps the chef cook faster overall, but changing orders slows things down temporarily.","detailedExplanation":"

The V8 compilation pipeline is a multi-tier system that transforms JavaScript source code into optimized machine code. It consists of four main stages: Parser, Ignition, Profiler, and TurboFan.

How it works

First, the Parser converts source code into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). Then Ignition, the interpreter, generates bytecode from the AST and executes it. While executing, the Profiler monitors the code and identifies hot code—functions that are executed frequently. For hot code, V8 triggers TurboFan, the optimizing JIT compiler, which compiles the bytecode into highly optimized machine code. TurboFan makes assumptions about types and object shapes based on observed behavior.

Deoptimization

If an assumption is later invalidated (e.g., a variable changes type), V8 performs deoptimization: it discards the optimized machine code and falls back to the slower bytecode execution. This ensures correctness but incurs a performance penalty. V8 may later re-optimize the function if it becomes hot again.

Why it matters in production

Understanding this pipeline helps developers write optimization-friendly code. Avoiding type changes, using monomorphic functions, and keeping object shapes consistent reduce deoptimizations and improve performance.

Common pitfall

A common pitfall is polymorphic functions that accept multiple types (e.g., a function that sometimes receives a number and sometimes a string). This causes TurboFan to generate less optimized code or frequent deoptimizations, hurting performance.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR Source[Source Code] --> Parser Parser --> AST[AST] AST --> Ignition Ignition --> Bytecode[Bytecode] Bytecode --> Profiler Profiler -->|Hot Code| TurboFan TurboFan --> MachineCode[Machine Code] MachineCode -->|Deoptimize| Bytecode"}]
qgen_v4:e849db56805dcf6e:
[{"question":"How do hidden classes and inline caching affect JavaScript object property access performance, and what coding patterns can degrade them?","expectedPoints":["Hidden classes are internal shapes V8 assigns to objects based on property order and types.","Objects with the same hidden class share optimized code paths via inline caching.","Adding properties out of order, deleting properties, or using dynamic property names breaks hidden class consistency.","Inline caching caches the result of property lookups based on the hidden class, speeding up repeated access.","Best practice: initialize all properties in constructor, avoid delete, and maintain consistent property order."],"followUps":["What happens when you use Object.assign() to copy properties? Does it preserve hidden classes?","How would you refactor code that frequently adds properties after object creation to improve performance?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy box where each toy has a specific shape. If all your toys are the same shape, you can quickly grab them without looking. But if you keep changing the shape of a toy or add a new part, you have to stop and figure out where everything is again. That's what happens with hidden classes in JavaScript—they help the computer find properties fast, but only if objects keep the same 'shape'.","detailedExplanation":"

Hidden classes (also called shapes or maps) are an internal optimization used by V8 (and other JavaScript engines) to represent the structure of objects. When you create an object, V8 assigns it a hidden class that tracks the property names and their order. If two objects have the same properties in the same order, they share the same hidden class. This allows the engine to generate efficient machine code for property access.

How Inline Caching Works

Inline caching (IC) is a technique where the engine caches the result of a property lookup based on the hidden class of the object. For example, when you write obj.x, V8 first checks the hidden class of obj. If it matches a previously seen class, it directly uses the cached offset to retrieve x without a full lookup. This makes repeated property access extremely fast.

What Breaks Optimization

  • Adding properties after construction in a different order than other objects creates a new hidden class, breaking sharing.
  • Using delete forces the engine to create a new hidden class because the shape changes.
  • Dynamic property names (e.g., obj[someVar]) prevent the engine from predicting the property at compile time, degrading IC.

Production Impact

In hot code paths (e.g., rendering loops, data processing), hidden class mismatches can cause significant slowdowns. A common pitfall is initializing objects with missing properties and adding them later, which creates multiple hidden classes. Always initialize all properties in the constructor or object literal, avoid delete, and keep property order consistent across objects.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Create obj1] --> B[Hidden Class HC1] C[Create obj2 same shape] --> D[Same HC1] B --> E[Inline Cache: offset for x] D --> E F[Add property z to obj1] --> G[New Hidden Class HC2] G --> H[Cache miss for obj1] H --> I[Slower access]"}]
qgen_v4:7f46cf993070fe99:
[{"question":"How does HTTP caching work with ETag and Cache-Control headers, and how can it cause issues with CORS preflight requests?","expectedPoints":["Explains ETag as a validator for conditional requests (If-None-Match).","Describes Cache-Control directives like max-age, no-cache, must-revalidate.","Identifies that CORS preflight (OPTIONS) responses are often not cached, causing extra latency.","Mentions Access-Control-Max-Age header to cache preflight responses.","Notes that misconfigured caching can lead to stale CORS headers or failed requests."],"followUps":["How would you debug a CORS error that only happens intermittently?","What is the difference between no-cache and no-store?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a library book (a web resource). ETag is like a stamp that tells if the book has changed. Cache-Control is like a due date for how long you can keep the book without checking. CORS is like a security guard that checks if a visitor from another school is allowed to read the book. Sometimes the guard's permission slip (preflight) expires quickly, so the visitor has to ask again and again, slowing things down.","detailedExplanation":"

HTTP caching and CORS are two critical mechanisms that often interact in production. Caching reduces server load and speeds up page loads by reusing previously fetched resources. The Cache-Control header specifies caching policies: max-age sets a freshness lifetime, no-cache forces revalidation, and no-store prevents caching entirely. ETag is a validator: the client sends If-None-Match with the ETag value, and the server responds with 304 Not Modified if the resource hasn't changed, saving bandwidth.

How CORS interacts with caching

CORS preflight requests (OPTIONS) are sent for cross-origin requests with non-simple methods or custom headers. These preflight responses can be cached using the Access-Control-Max-Age header, which tells the browser how long to cache the preflight result. Without this header, the browser sends a preflight for every request, adding latency. However, caching the preflight response can cause issues if the server's CORS policy changes: the browser may use a stale cached preflight, leading to unexpected failures.

Common pitfalls

  • Stale CORS headers: If the server changes allowed origins but the preflight is cached, new origins may be blocked.
  • Cache-Control misconfiguration: Using no-store on API responses can break caching entirely, increasing server load.
  • ETag with CORS: Conditional requests (If-None-Match) still require proper CORS headers on the 304 response; otherwise, the browser may block the cached response.

In production, always set Access-Control-Max-Age appropriately (e.g., 600 seconds) and ensure your caching strategy aligns with your CORS policy. Debug using browser DevTools Network tab to inspect cache hits and preflight requests.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Browser] -->|Request with If-None-Match| B[Server] B -->|304 Not Modified| C[Cache] C -->|Use cached resource| A A -->|Cross-origin request| D[Preflight OPTIONS] D -->|Access-Control-Max-Age| E[Cache preflight] E -->|Subsequent requests skip preflight| A"}]
qgen_v4:ecf7eb31e6dcad2d:
[{"question":"Explain the difference between Cache-Control: no-cache and Cache-Control: no-store, and when would you use each?","expectedPoints":["no-cache forces revalidation with server before using cached response","no-store prohibits any caching entirely","no-cache used for dynamic content that changes frequently","no-store used for sensitive data like banking info","Common pitfall: confusing no-cache with 'no caching'"],"followUps":["How does ETag work with no-cache to reduce bandwidth?","What is stale-while-revalidate and how does it improve performance?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a library book. 'no-cache' means you must check with the librarian before using it to see if a newer version exists. 'no-store' means you cannot keep the book at all; you must return it immediately. For example, a news article might use no-cache, but your bank statement must use no-store.","detailedExplanation":"

Cache-Control: no-cache does not mean 'do not cache'. Instead, it instructs the browser to revalidate the cached response with the server before using it. The browser may store the response, but every time it needs to serve it, it must ask the server (via If-None-Match or If-Modified-Since) whether the content has changed. If the server responds with 304 Not Modified, the cached version is used. This ensures freshness while still allowing caching to reduce bandwidth.

Cache-Control: no-store is the strictest directive: it forbids any caching at all. The browser must not store the response in any cache (disk, memory, etc.). This is critical for sensitive data like banking transactions, medical records, or authentication tokens, where even a stale cached copy could be a security risk.

When to use each

  • no-cache: For dynamic pages that change often but where you still want to save bandwidth (e.g., news feed, stock prices).
  • no-store: For any response containing private or sensitive information (e.g., API returning credit card numbers).

Common pitfall

Developers often mistakenly use no-cache when they mean 'never cache'. This leads to unexpected revalidation requests and can degrade performance. Always use no-store if you truly want to prevent caching.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Browser Request] --> B{Cache-Control?} B -->|no-cache| C[Check Server via ETag] C --> D{304?} D -->|Yes| E[Use Cached Response] D -->|No| F[Fetch New Response] B -->|no-store| G[Do Not Cache] G --> H[Fetch from Server Always]"}]
qgen_v4:5fcf246890ed9826:
[{"question":"Why does the browser block a cross-origin PUT request even though the server receives the request and responds successfully?","expectedPoints":["CORS is a browser-enforced mechanism, not a server-side protection.","The browser sends a preflight OPTIONS request for non-simple requests.","The server must respond with appropriate CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin, etc.).","If preflight fails, the actual request is never sent by the browser.","The server may still process the request if it doesn't check origin, but browser blocks JS from reading the response."],"followUps":["What would happen if the server does not send CORS headers but the request is still processed?","How would you fix a CORS error for a PUT request in production?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to send a letter to a friend in another country, but your mom (the browser) checks first if that friend's parents (the server) allow letters from your country. If they say no, your mom stops you from sending the letter, even though the friend's parents might still receive it. CORS is like that mom—it protects you, not the friend.","detailedExplanation":"

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser-enforced security mechanism that controls how web pages from one origin can request resources from a different origin. It is not a server-side protection; the server may still receive and process the request, but the browser blocks the JavaScript from reading the response if the server does not explicitly allow it via CORS headers.

How it works

For non-simple requests (e.g., PUT, DELETE, or requests with custom headers), the browser first sends a preflight request using the HTTP OPTIONS method. This preflight asks the server: "Are you willing to accept this cross-origin request?" The server must respond with appropriate headers like Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, and Access-Control-Allow-Headers. If the preflight succeeds, the browser sends the actual request. If it fails, the browser never sends the actual request, and the client-side code receives a CORS error.

Why it matters in production

In production, CORS errors are common when frontend and backend are hosted on different domains. The fix must be on the server side (or via a proxy), not in client-side JavaScript. A common pitfall is trying to set CORS headers on the client, which is impossible—CORS is enforced by the browser based on server responses.

Common pitfall

Developers often confuse no-cache with no-store in HTTP caching, but for CORS, a frequent mistake is assuming that adding mode: 'cors' in fetch options fixes the issue. In reality, the server must send the correct headers; the client cannot bypass CORS.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Browser] -->|1. OPTIONS preflight| B[Server] B -->|2. CORS headers| A A -->|3. Actual request| B B -->|4. Response| A A -->|5. JS reads response| C[Client Code] subgraph CORS Check A -->|If headers missing| D[Block response] end"}]
qgen_v4:e531428ecd46a016:
[{"question":"How would you design a component library that ensures UI consistency across multiple teams while allowing for team-specific customizations?","expectedPoints":["Single source of truth with a shared design token system","Component composition vs. configuration for customization","Versioning and release strategy (e.g., semantic versioning)","Testing and documentation for adoption","Governance process for contributions"],"followUps":["How do you handle breaking changes in the component library?","What if a team needs a component that doesn't exist yet?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friends are building a giant Lego castle. A design system is like a rulebook that says 'all red bricks must be the same shade of red' so the castle looks consistent. The component library is a shared box of pre-built Lego pieces (like doors and windows) that everyone can use, but you can still add your own special decorations if you follow the rules.","detailedExplanation":"

A design system is a collection of reusable components, guidelines, and design tokens that serve as the single source of truth for UI consistency. When designing a component library for multiple teams, you need to balance uniformity with flexibility.

Core Architecture

Start with design tokens (colors, spacing, typography) that are platform-agnostic. Components consume these tokens via a theme provider. Use a composition over configuration approach: allow teams to override styles via props or slots, but not modify core behavior. For example, a Button component exposes variant and size props, but not arbitrary CSS.

Versioning & Distribution

Publish the library as an npm package with semantic versioning. Use a monorepo (e.g., Nx, Lerna) to manage multiple packages (core, themes, utilities). Each team can pin a specific version, and breaking changes are communicated via changelogs and codemods.

Customization Strategy

Allow teams to extend components via render props or slots (e.g., React's children or Vue's slots). For more advanced customization, provide a theme override mechanism where teams can redefine tokens for their section of the app. However, enforce a governance process: any new component or major change must go through a design review and be added to the shared library.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is over-engineering the library with too many props, making it hard to maintain. Instead, start with a minimal set of well-designed components and iterate based on real usage. Also, avoid tight coupling to a specific framework; consider using web components for framework-agnosticism.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Design Tokens] --> B[Theme Provider] B --> C[Core Components] C --> D[Team A App] C --> E[Team B App] D --> F[Custom Overrides] E --> G[Custom Overrides] subgraph Governance H[Component Review] I[Versioning] end C --> H H --> I"}]
tr_v2:cd18e87db4d21805: The layers of a Design System
qgen_v4:c5b937bd15861d81:
[{"question":"How would you structure a design system to ensure consistency across multiple products while allowing for product-specific customization?","expectedPoints":["Layered architecture: tokens → primitives → patterns → products","Tokens as single source of truth for colors, spacing, etc.","Primitives are stateless, token-driven, no business logic","Patterns compose primitives into reusable UI patterns","Products consume patterns, override tokens for branding"],"followUps":["How do you handle versioning and breaking changes across layers?","What if a product needs a completely new pattern not in the system?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine building with LEGO. Design tokens are the basic brick colors and sizes. Primitives are simple pieces like a 2x4 brick or a wheel. Patterns are pre-built structures like a car or house. Products are the final sets you sell. Each layer builds on the previous one, so you can change colors (tokens) without rebuilding the car (pattern).","detailedExplanation":"

A design system is typically organized in layers to separate concerns and promote reuse. The four common layers are:

1. Design Tokens

These are the atomic variables that define the visual foundation: colors, spacing, typography, shadows, etc. They are stored as key-value pairs (e.g., --color-primary: #0055FF). Tokens are the single source of truth for brand identity and theming.

2. Primitives / Base Components

These are stateless, presentational components like Button, Input, Text. They consume tokens directly (e.g., background: var(--color-primary)) and have no business logic. They are the building blocks for higher-level components.

3. Patterns / Composite Components

These are compositions of primitives that solve common UI problems: Form, Modal, DataTable. They may include layout, validation, or accessibility logic but remain product-agnostic.

4. Products

The actual applications consume patterns and primitives. Products can override tokens for their own branding (e.g., different primary color) without modifying the underlying components.

In production, this layered approach enables scalability and consistency. A common pitfall is leaking business logic into primitives, which makes them less reusable. Always keep primitives pure and push logic up to patterns or products.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR T[Design Tokens] --> P[Primitives] P --> C[Patterns] C --> A[Product A] C --> B[Product B] subgraph Layers T P C end"}]
tr_v2:5ae1ab4ecdaf965d: Design Tokens — the heart of theming
qgen_v4:00025d83e4fae1d1:
[{"question":"Why separate semantic tokens from primitive tokens in a design token system?","expectedPoints":["Semantic tokens abstract intent (e.g., --color-primary) from values (e.g., #3b82f6).","Theme changes require only remapping semantic tokens to different primitives.","Components reference only semantic tokens, avoiding per-component updates.","Enables consistent theming across the entire component library.","Reduces risk of hardcoded colors and simplifies maintenance."],"followUps":["How would you handle a new brand that needs a completely different color palette?","What are the trade-offs of using CSS custom properties vs. JavaScript variables for tokens?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a set of crayons with specific colors like 'sky blue' (primitive). You also have labels like 'sky color' (semantic). When you want to change the sky from blue to orange, you just change what 'sky color' points to, instead of recoloring every picture. That's why we separate them.","detailedExplanation":"

Design tokens are the atomic values that represent visual design decisions (colors, spacing, typography). A robust token system separates primitive tokens (raw values like --blue-500: #3b82f6) from semantic tokens (intent-based aliases like --color-primary: var(--blue-500)).

Why separate them?

  • Theme switching: Changing a theme (e.g., light to dark) only requires remapping semantic tokens to different primitives. For example, --color-primary points to --blue-500 in light mode and --blue-300 in dark mode. Components using var(--color-primary) update automatically.
  • Brand customization: To support a new brand, you redefine the semantic-to-primitive mapping without touching component code.
  • Consistency: All components reference the same semantic tokens, ensuring a unified look and preventing hardcoded colors.

How it works in production

In a component library, each component uses only semantic tokens. The theme is applied via a data-theme attribute on a root element, and CSS custom properties cascade down. This decouples design decisions from implementation, making the library highly maintainable and scalable.

Common pitfall

Developers sometimes skip semantic tokens and use primitives directly in components. This leads to duplication and makes theme changes painful—every component must be updated individually. Always introduce a semantic layer to keep theming flexible.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR P[Primitive Tokens] --> S[Semantic Tokens] S --> C[Components] T[Theme] --> S subgraph Theme Layer T end subgraph Token Layer P S end subgraph UI Layer C end"}]
qgen_v4:4ae16cfac7bd98b8:
[{"question":"How do you decide what to test in a frontend application, and at which layers?","expectedPoints":["Focus on testing behavior over implementation details","Use the testing trophy (or pyramid) to prioritize integration tests","Test critical user flows and edge cases, not 100% coverage","Balance cost of maintenance with bug-catching value"],"followUps":["How would you test a complex form with validation and API submission?","What would you do if a test is flaky due to async operations?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're building a toy car. You don't test every single screw; you test that the wheels turn and the car moves forward when you push it. Similarly, in frontend testing, we test the important parts that users interact with, not every tiny detail. This helps us fix problems faster and change things without breaking everything.","detailedExplanation":"

Testing strategy in frontend is about maximizing confidence while minimizing maintenance cost. The goal is not 100% coverage, but to catch the most impactful bugs efficiently.

Key Principles

  • Test behavior, not implementation: Focus on what the user sees and does, not internal state or method calls. This makes tests resilient to refactoring.
  • Prioritize integration tests: The testing trophy model (popularized by Kent C. Dodds) emphasizes integration tests over unit tests because they test how pieces work together, catching more real-world bugs.
  • Critical user flows: Identify the most important paths (e.g., login, checkout) and test those thoroughly. Edge cases and error states are also high value.

Layers of Testing

  • Unit tests: For pure functions, utilities, and isolated logic. Cheap but catch fewer integration issues.
  • Integration tests: Test components with their dependencies (e.g., API calls, state management). Use tools like React Testing Library to simulate user interactions.
  • E2E tests: For critical end-to-end flows (e.g., Cypress, Playwright). Expensive but catch real browser issues.

Common Pitfall

Over-testing implementation details (e.g., testing internal state or private methods) leads to brittle tests that break on refactoring, reducing confidence. Instead, test from the user's perspective.

Production Relevance

A good strategy enables frequent deploys with confidence. Teams can refactor aggressively because tests guard against regressions. The cost of maintaining tests is offset by reduced debugging time and faster release cycles.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Actions] --> B[Integration Tests] B --> C[Component + API] B --> D[State Management] E[Unit Tests] --> F[Pure Functions] G[E2E Tests] --> H[Critical Flows] subgraph Testing Trophy B E G end"}]
qgen_v4:95a090b738a17665:
[{"question":"Explain the Testing Pyramid and the Trophy variant. Which one do you prefer for a modern frontend application and why?","expectedPoints":["Describes the classic Testing Pyramid: unit tests at base, integration in middle, E2E at top.","Explains the Trophy variant: emphasizes integration tests as the primary focus.","Justifies preference with trade-offs (speed, cost, confidence, maintenance).","Mentions real-world frontend concerns like component interaction and user flows.","Acknowledges that both models aim for balanced test coverage."],"followUps":["How would you decide the right ratio of unit to integration tests in a React app?","What specific tools would you use for each layer in the Trophy approach?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine building a LEGO castle. Unit tests check if each brick is strong. Integration tests check if two bricks fit together. E2E tests check if the whole castle stands and the drawbridge works. The Trophy says: spend most time making sure bricks fit well together, because that's where most problems happen.","detailedExplanation":"

The Testing Pyramid is a classic model that organizes automated tests into three layers: unit tests at the base (fast, cheap, many), integration tests in the middle, and end-to-end (E2E) tests at the top (slow, expensive, few). The pyramid suggests writing many unit tests, fewer integration tests, and even fewer E2E tests.

The Trophy variant, popularized by Kent C. Dodds, flips the emphasis: it places integration tests as the largest layer, with unit and E2E tests as smaller complements. The rationale is that integration tests provide the best confidence per cost — they test how components work together without the brittleness of E2E tests or the isolation of unit tests.

Why it matters in production

In modern frontend applications, most bugs arise from interactions between components (e.g., a button click updating a list). Unit tests alone miss these; E2E tests catch them but are slow and flaky. The Trophy approach prioritizes integration tests, which run quickly and give high confidence in user-facing behavior.

Common pitfall

Developers often over-invest in unit tests (testing implementation details) or E2E tests (testing everything end-to-end). The Trophy reminds us to focus on the sweet spot: testing the integration of real components with mocked network calls, using tools like React Testing Library and Mock Service Worker.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR subgraph Classic Pyramid Unit[Unit Tests] --> Integration[Integration Tests] --> E2E[E2E Tests] end subgraph Trophy Variant Unit2[Unit Tests] -.-> Integration2[Integration Tests] -.-> E2E2[E2E Tests] Integration2 -->|Largest| Focus[Sweet Spot] end"}]
tr_v2:881c4fa6fb10cc1d: Each type of test catches specific bugs:
qgen_v4:ad0c74adb515de97:
[{"question":"How would you test a login form component using React Testing Library?","expectedPoints":["Uses getByRole or getByLabelText to find elements","Simulates user interactions (fireEvent or userEvent)","Asserts expected behavior (e.g., success callback called)","Avoids testing internal state or implementation details"],"followUps":["How would you test a form submission that triggers an API call?","What would you do if the test becomes flaky due to async operations?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you want to check if a vending machine works correctly. Instead of opening the machine to see its wires, you press the buttons and see if the candy comes out. React Testing Library does the same: it clicks buttons and reads text on the screen, just like a real user would.","detailedExplanation":"

React Testing Library (RTL) encourages testing components from the user's perspective. For a login form, you would:

1. Query by accessible roles/labels

Use getByRole('textbox', {name: /username/i}) or getByLabelText(/password/i) to find inputs. This ensures your test mirrors how a real user (or assistive technology) interacts with the form.

2. Simulate user interactions

Use userEvent.type() (preferred over fireEvent) to type into fields, then userEvent.click() on the submit button. This fires realistic events like input and click.

3. Assert on visible outcomes

Check that the form calls the expected callback (e.g., onSubmit) with correct data, or that a success message appears. Avoid checking internal state or class names.

Why it matters in production

Tests written this way are resilient to refactoring — you can change internal implementation (e.g., rename a state variable) without breaking tests. They also catch real bugs: if a button is invisible or has wrong label, the test fails.

Common pitfall

Testing implementation details like wrapper.find('input').props().value or checking component state. This makes tests brittle and gives false confidence. Always query by what the user perceives.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User types username] --> B[getByLabelText] B --> C[userEvent.type] C --> D[Form updates state] D --> E[User clicks submit] E --> F[userEvent.click] F --> G[Assert callback called]"}]
qgen_v4:0afa2914dbfee72e:
[{"question":"How would you design a monitoring and observability system for a large-scale single-page application to detect real user performance issues and errors?","expectedPoints":["Proposes Real User Monitoring (RUM) with tools like Web Vitals, error tracking, and session replay.","Discusses metrics: Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS), API latency, error rates, and user satisfaction.","Explains data pipeline: client-side instrumentation, aggregation, alerting, and dashboards.","Addresses sampling, privacy, and performance overhead of monitoring itself.","Mention integration with backend logs and distributed tracing for full picture."],"followUps":["How would you handle monitoring for single-page application route changes?","What metrics would you prioritize for a critical user journey like checkout?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you built a toy car track. After the race, you want to know which parts were bumpy or where cars fell off. Monitoring is like putting sensors on the track to measure bumps and crashes, so you can fix them and make the next race smoother.","detailedExplanation":"

Monitoring & Observability is the practice of collecting, analyzing, and acting on data from your application to understand its health and performance in production. For a single-page application (SPA), this involves instrumenting the client-side code to capture real user interactions, performance metrics, and errors.

Key Components

  • Real User Monitoring (RUM): Collects data from actual user sessions, including page load times, route transitions, and user interactions. Tools like the web-vitals library measure Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) directly in the browser.
  • Error Tracking: Captures JavaScript exceptions, promise rejections, and network failures. Services like Sentry or Rollbar aggregate errors with stack traces and context (user, browser, action).
  • Performance Metrics: Beyond Web Vitals, track API call durations, resource loading times, and custom business metrics (e.g., time to interactive, first paint).
  • Session Replay: Records user interactions (clicks, scrolls, inputs) to replay and diagnose issues visually.

Data Pipeline

Data flows from the browser to a backend collector (e.g., via beacon API to avoid blocking page unload), then to a processing pipeline (e.g., Kafka, Spark) for aggregation, and finally to storage (e.g., Elasticsearch, BigQuery) and visualization (e.g., Grafana, Datadog). Alerts are configured for thresholds like error rate > 1% or LCP > 4 seconds.

Production Considerations

Monitoring must be lightweight to avoid degrading performance. Use sampling for high-traffic sites (e.g., 1% of sessions) and privacy controls (anonymize IPs, avoid PII). Integrate with backend tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to correlate frontend slowness with slow API calls or database queries.

Common Pitfall

Collecting too much data without clear alerts or dashboards leads to alert fatigue. Focus on actionable metrics tied to user experience and business goals. Also, avoid relying solely on synthetic monitoring (e.g., Lighthouse) as it doesn't reflect real user conditions.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[User Browser] -->|Beacon API| B[Collector] B --> C[Aggregation Pipeline] C --> D[(Storage)] D --> E[Dashboard] D --> F[Alerting] E --> G[Engineer] F --> G subgraph Client A end subgraph Backend B C D end"}]
qgen_v4:f0e096aea07a5ec3:
[{"question":"How would you set up a monitoring strategy for a production frontend application, and what are the three key pillars you would focus on?","expectedPoints":["Identifies error tracking (JS runtime, promise rejections, API errors) with tools like Sentry.","Explains Real User Monitoring (RUM) for Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) from real users.","Describes analytics and funnel tracking to connect performance with user behavior and business metrics.","Distinguishes between lab tests (Lighthouse) and RUM data.","Mentions grouping errors with stack traces and breadcrumbs for debugging."],"followUps":["How would you prioritize which errors to fix first?","What would you do if RUM data shows poor LCP but Lighthouse scores are good?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're a detective watching a busy playground. You want to know if any kids fall (errors), how fast they can run (performance), and which slides they use most (analytics). The three pillars help you catch problems, measure real speed from actual kids, and see what's popular so you can make the playground better.","detailedExplanation":"

When monitoring a production frontend, you need a strategy that covers three critical pillars: Error Tracking, Real User Monitoring (RUM), and Analytics & Funnel. Each pillar addresses a different aspect of application health and user experience.

1. Error Tracking

This captures JavaScript runtime errors, unhandled promise rejections, and API failures. Tools like Sentry or Rollbar group similar errors, provide stack traces, and include breadcrumbs (user actions leading to the error). This helps developers quickly identify and fix bugs in production.

2. Real User Monitoring (RUM)

RUM measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) from actual users across different devices, networks, and regions. Unlike lab tests (e.g., Lighthouse), RUM reflects real-world conditions. It helps you understand performance bottlenecks that affect real users.

3. Analytics & Funnel

This tracks user behavior—where users drop off in a funnel, which features are used, and how performance correlates with business metrics like conversion or revenue. Connecting performance data with analytics helps prioritize improvements that impact the bottom line.

Why it matters in production: Without these pillars, you're blind to issues that degrade user experience and business outcomes. Error tracking ensures you catch bugs early; RUM gives you real performance data; analytics ties everything to business value.

Common pitfall: Relying solely on lab tests (Lighthouse) and ignoring RUM. Lab tests are synthetic and may not reflect real user conditions. Always complement them with RUM data.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Monitoring Strategy] --> B[Error Tracking] A --> C[Real User Monitoring] A --> D[Analytics & Funnel] B --> E[Sentry/Rollbar] C --> F[Core Web Vitals] D --> G[User Behavior] F --> H[LCP, INP, CLS]"}]
tr_v2:5438b9e7b08593fa: Performance Budget — set limits to prevent "scope creep"
qgen_v4:ddb94c273f50485a:
[{"question":"How do you set up a performance budget in a CI/CD pipeline, and how do you decide between using lab data versus field data for your thresholds?","expectedPoints":["Define performance budget as limits on metrics (e.g., JS size, LCP).","Explain how to enforce budgets in CI (e.g., webpack plugin, Lighthouse CI).","Distinguish lab data (stable, debug) vs field data (real users, SEO impact).","Mention using both: lab for early detection, field for final decision.","Note that Google uses field data (CrUX) for Core Web Vitals ranking."],"followUps":["What would you do if field data shows a metric is over budget but lab data is fine?","How would you handle source maps in production to avoid exposing code?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a backpack for a trip. A performance budget is like a weight limit for your backpack. You check the weight in a perfect room (lab data) and also on the actual trail (field data). The trail weight matters more because it's real. You also hide your map (source maps) so others can't copy your route.","detailedExplanation":"

A performance budget is a set of limits on web performance metrics (e.g., JavaScript bundle size, Largest Contentful Paint) to prevent regressions during development. It acts as a guardrail against 'scope creep' that degrades user experience.

How to set up in CI/CD

Use tools like webpack-bundle-analyzer or Lighthouse CI to measure metrics in CI. For example, a lighthouserc.json can define budgets for resource sizes and performance scores. If a build exceeds the budget, the pipeline can warn or fail, forcing the team to optimize before merging.

Lab data vs. Field data

Lab data (e.g., Lighthouse) is collected in a controlled environment—consistent network, device, and location. It's great for debugging and catching issues early. Field data (e.g., Chrome User Experience Report, Real User Monitoring) comes from actual users with varying devices and network conditions. Google uses field data (CrUX) for Core Web Vitals ranking, so field data is the ultimate truth for SEO.

Why both matter in production

Relying only on lab data can miss real-world bottlenecks (e.g., slow 3G on a low-end phone). Conversely, field data alone may be noisy. A common pitfall is setting budgets based solely on lab data and being surprised when field metrics are worse. Always monitor both: use lab data for immediate feedback in CI, and field data for ongoing production monitoring.

Source maps in production

Source maps help debug minified code in error tracking tools like Sentry. However, deploying them publicly exposes your original source code. Instead, upload them privately to your error monitoring service and never serve them to the public.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Dev commits code] --> B[CI runs Lighthouse] B --> C{Within budget?} C -- Yes --> D[Deploy to production] C -- No --> E[Fail build / warn] D --> F[Collect field data (CrUX)] F --> G{Real users OK?} G -- Yes --> H[Keep monitoring] G -- No --> I[Optimize further]"}]
qgen_v4:5336abf2f92533fb:
[{"question":"How would you design a deployment pipeline for a large-scale React application that supports feature flags and zero-downtime rollbacks?","expectedPoints":["Describes CI/CD pipeline with build, test, bundle, and deploy stages.","Explains use of feature flags to toggle features without redeployment.","Discusses rollback strategy (e.g., blue-green deployment or canary releases).","Mentions bundling optimization (code splitting, tree shaking) for faster loads.","Addresses monitoring and alerting for deployment health."],"followUps":["How would you handle A/B testing in this pipeline?","What metrics would you track to decide whether to rollback?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a toy factory. You write instructions (code) to make toys, but the factory machines (browsers) can't read your instructions directly. A bundler is like a translator that turns your instructions into a format the machines understand. It also helps pack the toys into smaller boxes so they ship faster. Feature flags are like secret switches that let you turn new toy features on or off without rebuilding the whole factory. Rollback is like having a backup plan to quickly switch back to the old toy design if something goes wrong.","detailedExplanation":"

When designing a deployment pipeline for a large-scale React application, the goal is to ensure fast, safe, and reliable releases. The pipeline typically includes stages: build, bundle, test, and deploy.

Build & Bundle

The bundler (e.g., Webpack, Vite) transforms your React code into optimized static assets. Key techniques include code splitting (lazy loading routes) and tree shaking (removing unused code) to reduce bundle size. This directly impacts load time and user experience.

Feature Flags

Feature flags allow you to toggle features on/off without redeploying. In production, you can use a service like LaunchDarkly or a simple config file. This enables gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and instant kill switches. The flag state is fetched at runtime, often via an API or environment variable.

Deployment & Rollback

For zero-downtime rollbacks, use blue-green deployment (two identical environments, switch traffic) or canary releases (route small % of users to new version). Rollback is triggered by monitoring metrics like error rates, latency, or user feedback. A common pitfall is not testing feature flags in production-like environments, leading to unexpected behavior when toggled.

Production Considerations

  • CI/CD automation: Every merge to main triggers build, test, and deploy.
  • Monitoring: Track bundle size, load time, and error rates post-deploy.
  • Security: Use signed artifacts and immutable deployments to prevent tampering.

In summary, a robust pipeline combines bundling optimization with feature flags and rollback strategies to deliver features quickly and safely.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Code Commit] --> B[Build & Bundle] B --> C[Run Tests] C --> D[Deploy to Staging] D --> E[Feature Flag Config] E --> F[Canary Deploy] F --> G[Monitor Metrics] G -->|Healthy| H[Full Rollout] G -->|Issues| I[Rollback] I --> J[Old Version]"}]

qgen_v4:fb903d8bb5a1a97e:
[{"question":"How does tree-shaking work in a bundler, and what are its prerequisites?","expectedPoints":["Tree-shaking relies on static analysis of ES module import/export syntax.","It removes unused exports from the final bundle.","CommonJS modules cannot be tree-shaken due to dynamic require().","Bundlers like Webpack, Rollup, and esbuild support tree-shaking.","Side effects in modules can prevent tree-shaking; use 'sideEffects' flag."],"followUps":["How would you debug tree-shaking not working for a specific module?","Can tree-shaking remove unused CSS? How?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big box of LEGO bricks, but you only need a few pieces to build a car. Tree-shaking is like a smart robot that looks at your instructions and throws away all the bricks you don't need, so you only get the ones you actually use. This makes your car lighter and faster to build.","detailedExplanation":"

Tree-shaking is a dead-code elimination technique used by modern JavaScript bundlers (Webpack, Rollup, esbuild) to remove unused exports from the final bundle. It relies on the static structure of ES modules (import/export), which allows the bundler to analyze which exports are actually imported elsewhere.

How it works

The bundler parses the module graph, marks each export as used or unused, and then excludes unused exports during the final output generation. For example, if a module exports foo and bar but only foo is imported, bar is removed.

Prerequisites

  • ES module syntax (import/export) — CommonJS (require) is dynamic and cannot be statically analyzed.
  • No side effects — if a module has side effects (e.g., modifies a global variable), the bundler must keep it. Use "sideEffects": false in package.json to hint safety.
  • Minification — often combined with tree-shaking to further reduce size (e.g., Terser).

Why it matters in production

Tree-shaking can reduce bundle size by 20–60%, directly improving load time and Time to Interactive (TTI). It's especially important for large libraries like Lodash or Moment.js, where you might only use a few functions.

Common pitfall

Developers often assume tree-shaking works automatically with CommonJS or with modules that have side effects. Always verify with a bundle analyzer (e.g., Webpack Bundle Analyzer) and set "sideEffects": false appropriately.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Source Modules] --> B[Static Analysis] B --> C{Export Used?} C -->|Yes| D[Keep in Bundle] C -->|No| E[Remove (Tree-shake)] D --> F[Final Bundle] E --> F"}]
qgen_v4:d6a08d263bd3f30f:
[{"question":"How would you design a deployment pipeline that allows you to release a new frontend feature to only 5% of users, monitor for errors, and instantly disable it without redeploying?","expectedPoints":["Use feature flags to decouple deploy from release","Implement canary release with gradual traffic shifting","Include monitoring and automatic rollback triggers","Handle stale client versions with versioned assets and reload on chunk load failure"],"followUps":["How would you handle a user who has the old JavaScript bundle cached and encounters a 'chunk load failed' error after the canary is rolled out?","What metrics would you monitor to decide whether to increase the canary percentage or roll back?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you're baking cookies and want to test a new recipe. Instead of giving it to everyone at once, you first give it to just a few friends (5%). If they like it, you give it to more people. If they don't, you stop and go back to the old recipe. Feature flags are like having a switch to turn the new recipe on or off without baking new cookies.","detailedExplanation":"

This question tests your understanding of modern frontend deployment strategies that minimize risk and enable rapid iteration. The key concepts are feature flags, canary releases, and versioned assets.

Feature Flags

Feature flags (or toggles) allow you to decouple deployment from release. You can deploy code containing a new feature to production but keep it hidden behind a flag. The flag can be toggled on or off in real time without redeploying. This enables gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and instant kill switches.

Canary Releases

A canary release routes a small percentage of users (e.g., 5%) to the new version while the rest see the old version. This limits blast radius. If errors spike, you can roll back by shifting traffic back to the old version. Combined with feature flags, you can also disable the feature at the flag level without a full rollback.

Handling Stale Clients

A common pitfall is users who loaded the old JavaScript bundle before the canary was active. If they navigate to a new route that requires a chunk from the new version, they may get a chunk load failed error. To handle this, use versioned assets with content hashes (e.g., app.abc123.js) and keep old files available for a grace period. Additionally, implement a service worker or a version check that triggers a page reload when a new version is detected.

Monitoring and Rollback

Monitor error rates, latency, and user engagement. Set up automated alerts. If error rate exceeds a threshold, automatically disable the feature flag or shift traffic back to the old version.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Commit] --> B[CI: lint, test, build] B --> C[Deploy to production] C --> D{Feature flag off?} D -- Yes --> E[Old version serves all] D -- No --> F[Canary: 5% users get new feature] F --> G[Monitor errors] G -- Error spike --> H[Disable flag / rollback] G -- All good --> I[Gradually increase % to 100%]"}]
tr_v2:cec1b5fe192c6f6f: Bundler comparison: Webpack · Vite · esbuild · Rollup
qgen_v4:424a3a6ce85a7e3e:
[{"question":"Why is Vite faster than Webpack in development mode, and what trade-offs does it introduce?","expectedPoints":["Vite uses native ES modules for dev, avoiding full bundling.","Webpack bundles everything upfront; Vite lazy-loads on demand.","Vite leverages esbuild for pre-bundling dependencies.","Trade-off: Vite may have compatibility issues with non-ESM libraries.","Trade-off: Vite's production build uses Rollup, not esbuild."],"followUps":["How would you handle a legacy library that doesn't support ESM in Vite?","When would you still choose Webpack over Vite?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine building a Lego castle. Webpack puts all the bricks together first before you can play. Vite gives you bricks only when you need them, so you start playing faster. But sometimes Vite can't find a special brick, so you have to help it.","detailedExplanation":"

Vite achieves faster development speeds by leveraging native ES modules in the browser. Instead of bundling the entire application upfront like Webpack, Vite serves code as individual ES modules during development. The browser loads only the modules it needs, on demand, which drastically reduces cold-start time and enables instant hot module replacement (HMR).

How it works

Vite uses esbuild to pre-bundle dependencies (node_modules) into a single ES module, which is fast because esbuild is written in Go. Application source code is served as native ESM, so the browser handles module resolution. For production, Vite uses Rollup for tree-shaking and optimization, which is more mature than esbuild for production builds.

Why it matters in production

Faster dev cycles improve developer productivity. However, Vite's approach introduces trade-offs: it requires modern browsers that support ESM, and some CommonJS-only libraries may need shimming. Additionally, Vite's production build (Rollup) may produce slightly different output than Webpack, potentially affecting compatibility with older tools.

Common pitfall

Assuming Vite works out-of-the-box with all npm packages. Some packages rely on Node.js APIs or CommonJS patterns that don't translate directly to ESM. You may need to configure optimizeDeps or use plugins to polyfill.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Dev Start] --> B{Choose Tool} B -->|Webpack| C[Bundle All Modules] B -->|Vite| D[Pre-bundle deps with esbuild] C --> E[Full bundle served] D --> F[Serve source as ESM] F --> G[Browser loads on demand] E --> H[Slow HMR] G --> I[Fast HMR]"}]
qgen_v4:d6131c4300165362:
[{"question":"Compare Webpack, Vite, esbuild, and Rollup in terms of their primary strengths and ideal use cases.","expectedPoints":["Webpack: complex apps, huge plugin ecosystem, module federation","Vite: fast dev with native ESM + esbuild, production via Rollup","esbuild: extremely fast build speed (Go), limited advanced features","Rollup: library builds, excellent tree-shaking, clean ESM/CJS/UMD output"],"followUps":["When would you choose esbuild over Vite for a production build?","How does Webpack's module federation compare to Vite's approach for micro-frontends?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have different tools for building a Lego castle. Webpack is like a huge toolbox with every piece you might need, but it takes time to set up. Vite is like a super-fast helper that builds your castle instantly while you watch, then packs it neatly for the final show. esbuild is a rocket-powered glue gun that sticks pieces together incredibly fast, but it only does simple jobs. Rollup is like a master sculptor who carves a perfect statue from a block of marble, removing any extra bits to make it clean and lightweight.","detailedExplanation":"

This question compares four major JavaScript build tools: Webpack, Vite, esbuild, and Rollup. Each has distinct strengths and ideal use cases.

Webpack

Webpack is the most mature and feature-rich bundler. It excels in complex applications with a massive ecosystem of loaders and plugins. Key features include code splitting, lazy loading, and Module Federation for micro-frontends. However, configuration is verbose and build times can be slow for large projects.

Vite

Vite focuses on developer experience. During development, it serves modules via native ESM and uses esbuild for pre-bundling dependencies, resulting in near-instant hot module replacement. For production, it uses Rollup for optimized builds. Vite is ideal for modern frameworks like Vue, React, and Svelte, but may lack some advanced Webpack plugins.

esbuild

esbuild is an extremely fast bundler written in Go, claiming 10–100× speed improvements over JavaScript-based tools. It's great for simple projects, quick prototypes, or as a transpiler/minifier. However, it lacks advanced features like code splitting and extensive plugin support, making it unsuitable for large, complex apps.

Rollup

Rollup specializes in library builds. It produces clean, optimized output with excellent tree-shaking and supports multiple output formats (ESM, CJS, UMD). It's the go-to tool for publishing npm packages. However, it's less suited for applications with many dynamic imports.

Common Pitfall

A common mistake is choosing a tool based solely on speed or popularity without considering the project's needs. For example, using esbuild for a large enterprise app may lead to missing features, while using Webpack for a small library can be overkill.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Webpack] --> B[Complex Apps] A --> C[Module Federation] D[Vite] --> E[Fast Dev: ESM+esbuild] D --> F[Prod: Rollup] G[esbuild] --> H[Speed: 10-100x] G --> I[Limited Features] J[Rollup] --> K[Library Builds] J --> L[Tree-shaking]"}]
tr_v2:1f398ac0f78f4744: Why is Vite fast during development?
qgen_v4:3b2bc5f10179df54:
[{"question":"Why is Vite's dev server nearly instant even for large projects?","expectedPoints":["Vite uses native ES modules in the browser","No bundling during development","Only transforms requested modules via esbuild","Contrasts with Webpack's full bundle on startup"],"followUps":["How does Vite handle Hot Module Replacement differently from Webpack?","What trade-offs does Vite make by not bundling in dev?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a huge Lego set. Webpack builds the entire castle before you can play, which takes a long time. Vite lets you play with just the pieces you need right now, and only builds those pieces when you ask for them. That's why Vite starts up super fast.","detailedExplanation":"

Vite achieves near-instant dev server startup by leveraging the browser's native ES Module (ESM) support. Instead of bundling the entire application upfront like Webpack, Vite serves modules on-demand. When the browser requests a module, Vite transforms it using esbuild (a blazing-fast bundler written in Go) and sends it directly. This means only the modules actually needed for the current page are processed, drastically reducing startup time.

How it works

  • During development, Vite acts as a simple file server that responds to ESM import statements.
  • It pre-bundles dependencies using esbuild (for CommonJS-to-ESM conversion and performance) but leaves application code unbundled.
  • When a file changes, Vite performs Hot Module Replacement (HMR) by invalidating only that module's chain, again using native ESM.

Why it matters in production

For large projects, Webpack's full-bundle approach can cause startup delays of minutes. Vite's on-demand strategy keeps the dev server responsive regardless of project size, improving developer experience and iteration speed. However, for production builds, Vite switches to Rollup for optimized bundling.

Common pitfall

A common misconception is that Vite is always faster than Webpack. While Vite excels in development, its production build uses Rollup, which may be slower than esbuild for simple tasks. Also, Vite's dev approach relies on modern browser ESM support, which may not work in older browsers without polyfills.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Browser requests module] --> B[Vite dev server] B --> C{Module cached?} C -->|No| D[Transform with esbuild] D --> E[Send ESM to browser] C -->|Yes| E E --> F[Browser imports next module] F --> B"}]
qgen_v4:7120977b1ca722d1:
[{"question":"How would you test a React component that fetches data from an API on mount and displays a loading state, then the data or an error? Walk through your approach including mocking, async handling, and what you'd verify.","expectedPoints":["Uses jest.mock or MSW to mock the API call, not real network","Tests loading state before data resolves","Tests successful data rendering and error state","Uses waitFor or findBy to handle async updates","Avoids testing implementation details like setState calls"],"followUps":["How would you test a component that makes multiple API calls in sequence?","What if the component uses a custom hook for data fetching?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're testing a vending machine. You don't want to actually connect it to the real soda company to see if it works—that's slow and unreliable. Instead, you pretend to be the soda company and give it fake responses. You check that the machine shows 'loading' while waiting, then shows the soda name when it gets a good response, or an error message if the fake response is an error. That's what mocking and async testing do for code.","detailedExplanation":"

Testing async components is a common senior-level challenge because it involves coordinating mocking, async handling, and assertion timing. The goal is to write tests that are fast, deterministic, and catch real bugs.

Approach

  • Mock the API: Use jest.mock to replace the fetch call or use MSW (Mock Service Worker) to intercept network requests. This avoids real network calls and gives you control over responses.
  • Test loading state: Render the component and immediately assert that a loading spinner or text appears.
  • Test success state: Use waitFor or findBy to wait for the mocked promise to resolve, then assert that the data renders correctly.
  • Test error state: Mock the API to reject, then assert that an error message appears.

Why it matters in production

Poorly written async tests often pass even when the component is broken because they don't wait for the right state. For example, a test that checks for data immediately after render will pass if the mock resolves synchronously, but fail in real async scenarios. Using proper async utilities ensures tests mirror real user behavior.

Common pitfall

Testing implementation details like internal state or lifecycle methods. Instead, test what the user sees: loading, data, or error. Also, avoid over-mocking—only mock the network layer, not the entire component.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Component Mounts] --> B[Show Loading] B --> C{API Mock} C -->|Resolve| D[Render Data] C -->|Reject| E[Render Error] D --> F[Assert Data Text] E --> G[Assert Error Text]"}]
tr_v2:01b032b3fc33f536: Mock vs Stub vs Spy — don't confuse them.
qgen_v4:9fe275753c1397e7:
[{"question":"You are testing a function that calls an external API and then logs the result. How would you use a stub, a spy, and a mock to test this scenario, and what are the trade-offs of each approach?","expectedPoints":["Stub replaces API call with fixed return value to control test input.","Spy wraps real logger to verify it was called with correct arguments.","Mock sets expectations on API call and logger, failing if not met.","Trade-off: stub is simple but doesn't verify interactions; spy is less intrusive; mock is strict but brittle.","Prefer stubs/spies for external dependencies, mocks for critical contracts."],"followUps":["When would you choose a mock over a stub for the API call?","How would you avoid over-mocking in this test?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you're testing a robot that fetches a toy from a shelf and then tells you what it got. A stub is like pretending the shelf always has a red ball, so you don't need the real shelf. A spy is like watching the robot's mouth to see if it says 'red ball' correctly. A mock is like a strict teacher who expects the robot to say exactly 'red ball' and gets upset if it doesn't. Each helps you check different things about the robot.","detailedExplanation":"

Stubs, spies, and mocks are test doubles used to isolate the unit under test from its dependencies. Understanding their differences is crucial for writing effective, maintainable tests.

Stub

A stub replaces a function with a fixed return value. In your scenario, you would stub the external API call to always return a known response (e.g., { status: 200, data: 'ok' }). This ensures the test is not dependent on network availability and the test input is deterministic. Trade-off: Stubs do not verify that the API was called correctly; they only provide data.

Spy

A spy wraps the real implementation and records how it was called (e.g., number of calls, arguments). You would spy on the logger to assert that it was called with the expected log message. Trade-off: Spies allow the real code to execute, which can be useful but may introduce side effects. They are less intrusive than mocks.

Mock

A mock is a fake object with pre-programmed expectations. You would mock the API call and the logger, setting expectations like expect(api.fetch).toHaveBeenCalledWith('/endpoint') and expect(logger.log).toHaveBeenCalledWith('success'). The test fails if the interactions don't match. Trade-off: Mocks are strict and can make tests brittle, as they break on implementation changes (e.g., refactoring internal calls).

Production Considerations

In real projects, over-mocking is a common pitfall. Mocking every dependency leads to tests that only verify mock interactions, not actual behavior. Follow the mock at the boundary principle: use mocks for external systems (network, I/O) and stubs/spies for internal logic. For this scenario, a stub for the API and a spy for the logger is often the sweet spot—it tests the function's behavior without over-specifying internal calls.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR subgraph Test A[Test Function] end subgraph Dependencies B[External API] C[Logger] end A -- stub --> B A -- spy --> C A -- mock --> B & C"}]
qgen_v4:13f3cc3af681d3c8:
[{"question":"How would you test a debounced function without waiting real time?","expectedPoints":["Use fake timers (vi.useFakeTimers) to control time","Call debounced function multiple times in quick succession","Advance time by the debounce delay (vi.advanceTimersByTime)","Assert the function was called only once","Restore real timers after test"],"followUps":["What happens if you advance time by less than the debounce delay?","How would you test that the debounced function receives the latest arguments?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a doorbell that only rings once if you press it many times quickly. To test it without waiting, you use a magic clock that you can fast-forward. You press the bell twice, then fast-forward 300 milliseconds, and check that it rang only once.","detailedExplanation":"

Testing asynchronous code like debounce requires controlling time to avoid real delays. Use fake timers (e.g., vi.useFakeTimers()) to replace setTimeout and clearTimeout with mock implementations.

How it works

Create a debounced function with a delay (e.g., 300ms). Call it multiple times in quick succession — each call resets the timer. Then advance time by exactly the delay using vi.advanceTimersByTime(300). This triggers the pending timer, executing the debounced function only once.

Why it matters in production

  • Performance: Real waits slow down test suites; fake timers make tests fast and deterministic.
  • Reliability: No flakiness from timing races or environment differences.
  • Isolation: Tests don't depend on actual clock speed.

Common pitfall

Forgetting to restore real timers after the test (vi.useRealTimers()). If not restored, other tests may use fake timers unexpectedly, causing timeouts or broken async behavior.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Call debounced fn] --> B[Start timer 300ms] C[Call again quickly] --> D[Reset timer] D --> B B --> E[Timer fires after 300ms] E --> F[Execute fn once] subgraph Fake Timers G[vi.useFakeTimers] H[vi.advanceTimersByTime] end G --> B H --> E"}]
tr_v2:b0c3429b6b1bb3b2: Contract Testing — preventing "API changes without anyone knowing"
qgen_v4:4deda920412d54b2:
[{"question":"How would you implement a contract test between a frontend and a backend team to catch API changes before production?","expectedPoints":["Describes using a tool like Pact or a shared schema (e.g., OpenAPI) to define expectations.","Explains that the frontend declares expected response shape, backend verifies it matches.","Mentions that contract tests run in CI and fail if either side violates the contract.","Highlights that this catches breaking changes early, before deployment.","Notes that both teams must agree on the contract and update it collaboratively."],"followUps":["What happens if the backend changes a field that the frontend doesn't use? Should the contract test fail?","How do you handle optional fields or versioning in contract tests?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you and your friend are building a Lego castle together. You build the left tower, and your friend builds the right tower. You both need to agree on how the towers connect—like the shape of the bricks at the joint. A contract test is like checking that your bricks still fit together before you try to connect them. If your friend changes their bricks, the test tells you right away, so you don't find out when the castle falls apart.","detailedExplanation":"

Contract testing is a technique to verify that two independent systems (e.g., a frontend and a backend) can communicate correctly. It ensures that the API contract—the expected request/response format—is respected by both sides.

How it works

Typically, the frontend (consumer) defines its expectations for an API endpoint using a tool like Pact or a shared OpenAPI specification. The backend (provider) then runs a test to confirm it can satisfy those expectations. If the backend changes a field name, type, or removes an endpoint, the contract test fails immediately.

Why it matters in production

In microservices or team-separated environments, frontend and backend evolve independently. Without contract tests, a backend change might break the frontend silently, causing runtime errors in production. Contract tests catch these issues in CI/CD pipelines, before deployment.

Common pitfall

One common mistake is treating contract tests as full integration tests. They should only verify the shape of the data, not business logic. Also, both teams must agree on the contract and update it when requirements change—otherwise, tests become stale or ignored.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR FE[Frontend] -->|Declares expectations| CT[Contract Test] BE[Backend] -->|Verifies it matches| CT CT -->|Pass/Fail| CI[CI Pipeline] CI -->|Fail| Alert[Alert Team] CI -->|Pass| Deploy[Deploy to Production]"}]
qgen_v4:edcbcf2d412e69fb:
[{"question":"How would you design a component documentation system using Storybook for a large-scale design system?","expectedPoints":["Component isolation from app for independent development and testing","Integration with design tokens and theming","Automated visual regression testing and accessibility checks","Versioning and release process for component library","Collaboration between designers and developers via stories"],"followUps":["How do you handle component variants and states in Storybook?","What strategies do you use to keep Storybook in sync with the production app?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"system_design","childExplanation":"Imagine you have a big box of LEGO bricks. Storybook is like a special table where you can build and show each brick separately, without the rest of the castle. This helps you test if each brick works well and looks good, and you can also write instructions for others to use them.","detailedExplanation":"

Storybook is a frontend workshop that allows you to develop and document UI components in isolation, separate from your main application. For a large-scale design system, it serves as a living documentation that evolves with the codebase.

Key Design Considerations

  • Component Isolation: Each component is developed in its own sandbox, enabling focused testing and debugging without app dependencies.
  • Design Tokens Integration: Use addons like @storybook/addon-themes to apply design tokens (colors, spacing) dynamically, ensuring consistency.
  • Visual Regression Testing: Integrate tools like Chromatic or Percy to catch unintended visual changes automatically.
  • Accessibility Checks: Use @storybook/addon-a11y to audit components for accessibility issues during development.
  • Versioning & Release: Maintain a separate package for the component library (e.g., via Lerna or Nx) and version it independently. Storybook can be deployed to a static site (e.g., Netlify) for easy access.
  • Collaboration: Stories serve as both documentation and test cases. Designers can review components in Storybook before they are integrated into the app.

Production Importance

In a large team, Storybook ensures that components are reusable, tested, and documented. It reduces integration issues and speeds up onboarding for new developers.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is not keeping Storybook in sync with the actual app. If components are updated in the app but not in Storybook, the documentation becomes outdated. Use automated CI pipelines to rebuild Storybook on every commit.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Component Library] --> B[Storybook] B --> C[Isolated Stories] C --> D[Development] C --> E[Testing] C --> F[Documentation] B --> G[Addons] G --> H[Theming] G --> I[Accessibility] G --> J[Visual Regression]"}]
qgen_v4:afba35d22efdc5c3:
[{"question":"How does developing components in isolation improve the testing and maintainability of a frontend application?","expectedPoints":["Enables testing each component state (loading, error, empty) without running the full app.","Acts as living documentation with real examples for designers and developers.","Supports visual regression testing to catch UI breakage.","Allows accessibility audits per component state.","Reduces dependencies and simplifies debugging."],"followUps":["What trade-offs might you face when using isolated component development?","How would you integrate isolated component testing with end-to-end tests?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine building a toy car. Instead of testing the whole car at once, you test just the wheels, then just the doors, etc. That way, if something breaks, you know exactly which part is broken. Isolated components let developers test each piece of a website separately, making it easier to find and fix problems.","detailedExplanation":"

Isolated component development is a practice where each UI component is built and tested independently, without requiring the full application or complex data setups. This approach is central to tools like Storybook and is widely adopted in modern frontend workflows.

What It Is

Developers create a separate environment for each component, rendering it in various states (e.g., loading, error, empty, full data). These states are often called stories. Each story is a self-contained example that shows how the component behaves with different props.

How It Works

Using a tool like Storybook, you define stories for each component. For instance, a UserCard component might have stories for Default, Loading, Error, and Empty. Each story is a function that returns the component with specific props. The tool renders these stories in an isolated iframe, so they are unaffected by other parts of the app.

Why It Matters in Production

  • Faster development: You can build and tweak components without starting the entire app or mocking complex data.
  • Living documentation: Stories serve as executable documentation that designers and other developers can browse to understand all component variants and usage.
  • Visual regression testing: Automated screenshot comparisons of stories catch unintended UI changes that unit tests might miss.
  • Accessibility audits: Add-ons can check contrast, ARIA attributes, and focus order for each state, ensuring a11y compliance early.

Common Pitfall

One common mistake is over-isolating components to the point where they don't reflect real integration behavior. For example, a component might look perfect in isolation but break when placed inside a layout with global styles. To mitigate this, supplement isolated tests with integration or end-to-end tests that verify the component in context.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Component] --> B[Stories] B --> C[Loading] B --> D[Error] B --> E[Empty] B --> F[Full Data] C --> G[Visual Test] D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H[Catch UI Bugs]"}]
qgen_v4:66b27f35500c3836:
[{"question":"What are the three roles a single Storybook story serves in a design system?","expectedPoints":["Serves as living documentation for other developers","Acts as an isolated development playground","Provides input for visual regression testing","Write once, use for three purposes","Demonstrates platform/infra thinking for maintainability"],"followUps":["How would you structure stories to maximize these three benefits?","What happens if a story is not kept in sync with the actual component?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you build a Lego castle. A 'story' is like a photo of the castle from one angle. That photo helps others see what it looks like (documentation), lets you try building it again (playground), and later you can compare new photos to see if anything changed (testing). One photo does three jobs!","detailedExplanation":"

A Storybook story is a single exported function that renders a component in a specific state. It serves three critical roles simultaneously, which is why it's a cornerstone of scalable design systems.

1. Living Documentation

Each story is a runnable example that shows exactly how a component looks and behaves with given props. Unlike static documentation, it stays accurate because it uses the actual component code. Developers can browse stories to understand usage without reading source code.

2. Isolated Development Playground

Stories render components in isolation, free from app context. This allows developers to focus on one component at a time, test edge cases, and iterate quickly. The args mechanism lets you tweak props interactively via the Storybook UI, making it a live sandbox.

3. Visual Regression Test Input

Stories are automatically captured as screenshots by tools like Chromatic or Percy. On each commit, these screenshots are compared against baselines to detect unintended visual changes. This catches CSS regressions, layout shifts, and accessibility issues before they reach production.

Why It Matters in Production

For teams building a design system, this 'write once, use three ways' approach drastically reduces maintenance overhead. A single story replaces separate documentation pages, manual testing environments, and visual test fixtures. It enforces consistency and encourages developers to think in terms of component states rather than pages.

Common Pitfall

Treating stories as mere documentation and not keeping them updated. If a component's API changes but its stories aren't updated, the stories become misleading. Always pair component changes with story updates, ideally enforced by code review or CI checks.

","diagramMermaid":"flowchart LR A[Component Code] --> B[Story File] B --> C[Living Docs] B --> D[Dev Playground] B --> E[Visual Test Input] C --> F[Team Understanding] D --> G[Fast Iteration] E --> H[Regression Detection]"}]
qgen_v4:1b6def1407ad50a5:
[{"question":"How does Module Federation enable independent deployment and runtime code sharing between micro-frontends?","expectedPoints":["Each micro-frontend is a separate build with its own webpack config.","The host app declares remotes and loads remote modules asynchronously at runtime.","Shared dependencies (e.g., React) are configured to avoid duplication.","Version mismatches are handled via shared singleton or fallback strategies.","Communication between micro-frontends typically uses custom events or shared state."],"followUps":["How would you handle version conflicts for a shared library like React?","What are the trade-offs of using Module Federation vs iframe-based micro-frontends?"],"difficulty":"senior","type":"conceptual","childExplanation":"Imagine you have several Lego sets that each build a different part of a castle. Module Federation lets each set be built separately, but when you put them together, they can share special bricks (like the same color blocks) without having to rebuild everything. This way, each part can be updated independently without breaking the whole castle.","detailedExplanation":"

Module Federation is a webpack feature that enables a micro-frontend architecture where multiple independently built and deployed applications can share code and render each other's components at runtime. Each micro-frontend is a separate webpack build with its own configuration. The host application declares remote modules via the ModuleFederationPlugin, specifying the remote entry URLs. At runtime, the host asynchronously fetches the remote entry and loads the required modules, allowing seamless integration.

How It Works
